HxGN SDx2 Help
HxGN SDx2 is a web-based software that helps manage projects and assets throughout their lifecycle. As a central hub that stores all your important product information, HxGN SDx2 connects everyone involved in the project, from
designers and engineers to operators and contractors.
A primary goal of HxGN SDx2 is to provide a modular digital backbone for asset lifecycle information management and integration. For more information, see Exploring HxGN SDx2.
HxGN SDx2 is cloud-native software as a service solution (SaaS). What does this mean?
HxGN SDx2 marks a new way for Hexagon's Asset Lifecycle Intelligence division to deliver software.
You'll now access HxGN SDx2 from a cloud computing environment, instead of installing software on-premises at your location. Hexagon handles all the physical and software resources, including updates. Updates will be continuously
delivered to you, much like the smartphone in your pocket.

Exploring HxGN SDx2
HxGN SDx2 helps you organize your latest plans, designs, and other project information for easy access. This makes collaboration easier, so your teams can share information and discuss ideas throughout the project lifecycle. The
platform handles repetitive tasks for you, providing insights and reports in real-time to help understand the project and make informed choices.
In HxGN SDx2, you can create and manage a digital twin - a digital copy - of your real-world asset, which helps monitor performance, predict maintenance needs, and improve overall efficiency.
By streamlining workflows and reducing errors, HxGN SDx2 helps you finish projects faster and under budget. HxGN SDx2 gives you the data you need to make informed choices about your projects and assets.
HxGN SDx2 is a powerful and versatile tool that can help your organization achieve success throughout the project lifecycle. The HxGN SDx2 platform delivers superior, innovative connectivity, comprehensive data management, and
collaboration and analytics across diverse, project-driven industries, for example in oil and gas, pharmaceuticals, biochemicals, power generation, petrochemicals, and so on.
HxGN SDx2 integrates data from engineering design tools, as well as operations and maintenance tools, for a complete view of your asset lifecycle. Why integrate?
Data Take-On in HxGN SDx2 is a controlled process for importing, validating, and contextualizing engineering and asset-related information. What is Data Take-On?
Why integrate?
A primary goal of HxGN SDx2 is to provide a modular, digital backbone for asset lifecycle information management and information integration. What exactly is meant by “Integration”?
Hexagon identifies five different forms of integration - referred to as tiers to indicate increasing levels or steps of capability - evolving as suiting for the business. While the following solutions are situation-dependent, the product platform,
tools, and architecture deployed support a wide range and mix of these options. This is a requirement if they are to address more than one problem in a given business.
Tiers of integration
Presentation integration
The simplest tier is integrating the presentation of information. Information from multiple sources is accessible and provided side-by-side within a single interface, such as that of a web site.
For example, a user could navigate from data within one application, such as a plant tag, to corresponding data in other systems - for example, to a maintenance procedure, to associated records, or to real-time data - and have it all
presented in the same client interface to promote the decision support process.
Information from two source applications is presented side-by-side within the same interface. To the end-user, it appears that the data behind that information may in fact be integrated (supplied by one integrated system), when in reality it
is not.
This type of integration is most beneficial to users for whom information creation is not their primary role, such as the managerial, clerical, and manual personnel. They can see information presented side-by-side with little operability.
Data integration
The second tier is integrating data by aggregating and consolidating data from different sources together into a single common storage mechanism. Applications provide the data as exports, either with the content already mapped to the
receiving system’s data model during export, or via an external transformation mechanism to then be loaded into the target system, a process of Export/Transform/Load (ETL). In this environment, the applications providing the data do not
care, nor do they need to know, that the data integration (receiving) system exists.
A classic example of a data integration environment is document management. Documents, drawings, models, files, and "containers" of all sorts are brought together and loaded into a common classification indexing or librarian system for
storage and retrieval.
Another more granular form of data integration is the engineering data warehouse. "Content" from multiple disparate applications is brought together and harmonized to form a single uniform view of the "truth." This more granular data
integration also forms the foundation of the other tiers of integration. It supports the unidirectional movement of data between systems and requires the data to be mapped to the data model of the target system.
In point-to-point integrations, this is invariably a direct translation. But when multiple systems are required to share the same common data, such as pressures, temperatures, units of measure, and so forth, it is more advantageous to
translate/map this data to a shared data architecture, agnostic and neutral in form. Such a data architecture underlies the Common Schema in HxGN SDx2. The Common Schema reduces the number of transformations required to support
integration and offers an extensible schema for the HxGN SDx2 database.
HxGN SDx2 manages these two different levels of data granularity, containers and contents, natively: documents (containers) define the boundary condition/scope for exchanges and provide the deliverable record, while the data (content)
is extracted and aggregated together with that from other exchanges.
When data is being imported from multiple sources, it is possible some duplication exists. If the different data sources do not possess information management capabilities, most tools importing data overwrite data as it changes. Revision
management capabilities do exist in some data sources, but this is not common outside of document management and control systems. Additionally, data integration should deal with the provenance, status, and security of the data. It is for
these reasons that such capabilities are considered essential for a project data handover application.
Application integration
Application integration extends the data integration capabilities by adding transportation of the data to the correct location for the receiving application, and then importing it via an application-specific protocol.
This is different from data integration, because the data integration mechanism does not assume that anything more than a "file parser or file loader" capability exists for the receiving system. Application integration in this way results in
reduced time and cost of deployment but requires that the integrated tool supports some form of validation (correctness) of the data.
Many tools today provide sophisticated Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) or other methodologies for data acquisition which ensure quality and integrity of the resulting data. But they do require more effort to deploy. Typically, this
route is chosen if the applications are going to exchange data bi-directionally, on a frequent basis, and the user is engaged in the export and import process. Such examples include high-value, high-frequency point-to-point exchanges - for
example, between a 3D design tool and a stress analysis program.
Another key difference is in the scope of the content being exchanged as represented by the data overlaps.
The primary goal for data integration is to remove the overlaps so that the receiving system has the total sum of the data - or, in other words, to "enforce consistency."
Conversely, the primary goal for application integration is to exchange only the common/shared data between the applications. So, the goal is to manage the data overlaps - or, in other words, to "manage inconsistency."
Why are these two methodologies different or required? To answer that question, one needs to look at the business process being executed. Consider two examples:
1. The engineering data about an instrument has been checked and approved. The data is pushed to the procurement system for purchasing.
In this example, there is no conversation - the movement and translation of the data is non-negotiable.
2. A dialog is going on between a process engineer and an instrument engineer during the definition of an instrument.
In this second example, there is a back-and-forth exchange of evolving data. The iterative refinement of data and knowledge is negotiated between multiple parties.
Business process integration
Application integration on its own takes no account of the business process involved - application integration can be set up, the user can push a button, and data flows from application A to application B. It can be executed in a point-to-
point fashion. This works fine if one of the following apply:
There are only two applications involved.
Integration occurs in a small workgroup where interpersonal communication is good.
Milestones between disciplines can be aligned for the exchange to occur.
Expectations for data are the same between participants.
But, application integration alone does not integrate business practices, and data integration does not speed up human communication about complex data.
A business process integration is normally required when one of the following occurs:
The user must interact with the data externally to their working application to decide what to accept or reject. This would be a negotiated transaction - engineers want to be notified of change but decide not to accept for many reasons.
The projects extend beyond a workgroup (enterprise or extended-enterprise).
Milestones between disciplines or partners do not neatly align. For example, engineering is not a real-time activity.
Data and documents must be controlled, approved, released, and recorded.
This requires an electronic workflow execution, involving the actions to notify, store, deliver, consume, and move on.
Integrating the business process helps:
Determine or predict the synchronicity of processes, exchanges, and tasks between business functions.
Control the flow of the data between these functions.
Gain a comprehensive knowledge of progress.
Notify and warn of change while avoiding unnecessary stoppages.
For business process integration to succeed, not only are data integration and application integration required, but also the interactions that business processes have with the data and the applications managing that data:
Modeling and execution of the workflow processes between disparate business functions.
Identifying the timing and scope of the handover/exchange tasks.
Interjecting into the application itself or providing a notification mechanism to warn the user of potential change.
Storing the change until the user is ready to receive and absorb the change.
Delivering the change (while maintaining a record of the change).
Optionally with HxGN SDx2, the user can choose what to retrieve now, what to retrieve at a later date, and what to reject. This may sound like a lot of effort, but it is necessary to provide true concurrency of project execution tasks without
the problem of data changing "under the feet" of the end-user (a potential source of human errors).
Composite applet implementation
The final tier of integration offers the ability to create entirely new applications (or applets) that may or may not have a data store of their own, and that utilize the high-quality, high-integrity data stores that have been integrated together in
the previous tiers.
HxGN SDx2 offers an extensible integration that can break down the data silos of competing applications and proprietary tools and platforms.
As the digital backbone for Hexagon’s Smart Digital Reality for asset lifecycle management, HxGN SDx2 can integrate business processes for operations, management, design, and construction with their connected applications, data,
reports, and third party platforms - all communicating with each other as part of a “role-based” application.
HxGN SDx2 features increasing integration with Hexagon’s engineering and design software, as well as Hexagon’s operations and management applications. Integration breaks down the walls of digital data silos so that a business
administrator can produce reports from the same contextualized data that a facility technician accesses to maintain assets. By integrating data, HxGN SDx2 offers a unified digital platform for real-time asset information, historical
documents for compliance, and capital project design and documentation.
Integration Engine
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | What is the Integration Engine? <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/65d7e802c69393a960f0206f>
Within HxGN SDx2, the Integration Engine enforces consistency on the integrated data and allows HxGN SDx2 to achieve all five of the tiers of integration with Smart Digital Reality solutions from Hexagon. The Integration Engine offers
configurable rules for determining the validity of incoming data so that you can determine which information is true while recording the discrepancies for any business-to-business or business-to-administration record-keeping. HxGN SDx2
also offers a document review and revision function, streamlining communication over both data and documents.
The benefits of data integration include the same benefits as presentation integration, plus the abilities to:
Consolidate or aggregate the data, removing overlaps and providing a clean, high-integrity data exchange and storage.
Translate the data to a common form for ease of access, such that the source applications (which could be many) are not required for the information consumer to install, learn, or pay for and support, and the data appears as a
seamless whole.
The Integration Engine process has five steps: cumulate, calculate, correlate, consolidate, and compare.
Cumulate
The cumulate step collects the database objects necessary to perform the remaining steps (calculate, correlate, consolidate, and compare) on the group of objects being processed by the engine. These might include things such as:
Other objects and relationships within the group being processed, since these objects are the ones moving through the Integration Engine.
Information about what calculations will be used, so that the correct information can be collected from the design tool.
Details about the types of objects (tags, ports, and so forth) in the group of objects to be processed by the engine, so that the appropriate correlation rules can be applied later.
Information about the application the data is being shared from, so that the appropriate precedence rules can be applied during the consolidation step.
Calculate
The calculate step performs various calculations on the data that are required before the data moves to the remaining steps.
Other similar calculations that must be performed, where applicable, before the group of objects can proceed to the next step of the engine include:
Naming rule calculations
Correlate
The objects that have been accumulated and, when necessary, had calculations performed on them, are correlated before being consolidated. This step determines correlation IDs for each object being processed. A correlation ID is a
string that uniquely identifies an object across the integrated design tools. This value combined with the type hierarchy allows each application to independently describe the same object, while the system recognizes that they are
describing the same object from different perspectives.
 Although the correlation ID is unique to only one object, it is different from the GUID for the object.
The system uses a set of correlation rules, defined in the Common Schema, to create the correlation IDs. Each set of correlation rules contains a correlation rule definition, which uses if and else statements to determine if the set of rules
apply to an object, and an ordered set of rules to be applied once a match is made.
Consolidate
After a group of objects completes the correlate steps, those with correlation IDs enter the consolidate step. Since the correlation ID and type hierarchy are unique to a specific object, this step uses them to locate the existing object (if it
already exists) for each object in the group being consolidated. Objects that have graphics are consolidated even if the correlation ID is not generated.
 The type hierarchy does not have to match exactly between objects to consolidate. Incoming objects must share a subset of the highest or top levels of the hierarchy to consolidate, and the consolidated object retains the most
specific hierarchy from the incoming objects.
If no consolidated object exists, the object is copied directly into the consolidated design component. If a consolidated object exists already, precedence rules determine how the data for that object is updated in the consolidated design
component. The precedence rules control whether incoming data for correlated objects replace the existing data in the consolidated design component.
Compare
The compare step determines the difference between objects that come out of the correlate step and objects that are already in the consolidated design component. The compare step also creates exception reports to tell other design
components what they need to do to update their data.
For each object that is consolidated, one of the following four things happen:
The object is added to the consolidated design component as the initial consolidated object.
The object is terminated in (or deleted from) the consolidated design component.
One or more properties of the object in the consolidated design component are updated with the new information.
No changes are made to the consolidated object.
If the consolidation step
The compare step
Creates a new consolidated object
Performs a check, based on the class of the new object, to determine what design tools
includes objects of that class.
Creates an exception record for each of these design tools (except for the design tool that the
new consolidated object just came from), indicating that the design component needs to add
that object or mark it as an exception to be ignored.
Terminates (deletes) the consolidated object
Creates an exception record for each design tool associated with that consolidated object indicating
that they should also terminate or delete that object.
Updates the consolidated object
Performs a comparison for each object tied to the consolidated object (including the object
currently being processed).
Creates an exception report that includes any common properties that were updated for each
design component that needs to update their property values to match the consolidated object.
Made no updates to the consolidated object
Compares the consolidated object with the object used for the consolidation to determine if there are
any differences. (This comparison may be done as part of the consolidate step rather than the
compare step.)
Smart Digital Reality solutions
As the digital backbone for Hexagon’s Smart Digital Reality solutions, HxGN SDx2 is designed to offer a “single pane of glass” access to Hexagon tools for operations and projects. Using HxGN SDx2, you can leverage integrated data
about your planned and as-built industrial facilities across the asset life cycle.
Project execution
Intergraph Smart® P&ID
Provides an asset-centric, rule-driven, engineering solution that creates and maintains the plant configuration and its related process, equipment, instrumentation, and piping data for the life of the plant.
Helps create, manage, store, and access piping and instrumentation diagram (P&ID) data - the "roadmap" of the plant - to keep it accurate and up to date for the efficient design, construction, operations, and maintenance of a plant.
Intergraph Smart® 3D
Offers a next-generation, data-centric, rule-driven software solution.
Streamlines engineering design processes while preserving existing data and making it more usable and reusable.
Breaks through the constraints imposed by traditional plant design systems by providing advanced automation and integration capabilities.
Delivers proven, multi-discipline, cross-functional environment for streamlining ship and offshore design, production, and life-cycle management.
Provides automated, configurable engineering rules built within the software to ensure design accuracy and consistency while reducing errors, changes, and revisions.
Enforces design rules, resulting in increased productivity, quality, and reliability by enabling faster and more efficient creation, transfer, and review of the design and fabrication phases of the project as it develops.
Intergraph Smart® Electrical
Addresses the electrical needs of the entire life cycle of the plant, from concept to detailed design, and through operations and maintenance, including start-up, continuous operation, emergencies, and shutdowns.
Generates single-line diagrams and schematics automatically, creating graphical reports based on data provided by the engineers.
Reduces wasted material and saves construction costs by conducting proper modeling and design while ensuring projects are completed on time and within budget by improving the timing and efficiency of procuring the required
materials, such as electrical equipment, cables, and panels.
Properly sizes the project's electrical network by determining the correct cable lengths for all electrical equipment and panels, such as motors and transformers.
Ensures compliance with regulations by requiring traceability of data changes and document management to track changes and revisions of various documents and deliverables.
Intergraph Smart® Instrumentation
Prevents failure by better planning maintenance, and by managing and storing the history of the control system.
Facilitates plant expansions and control system modernizations.
Offers a single source of truth for all instrumentation requirements and settings, ensuring consistency across instrument tasks and deliverables.
Allows plant engineers and operators to quickly replace instruments for a variety of functions, minimizing downtime, and enabling a more agile approach to facility operations.
Operating and maintaining plants
HxGN EAM
Tracks assets, digitizes maintenance operations, and optimizes operational efficiency to extend asset life, increase safety, and improve profitability.
Produces a more comprehensive picture of the operational environment by linking your work orders and position records to your digital twin Info Map to view the radial diagram for the referenced tag and its related objects, including
plants, functional locations, and documents.
j5 Operations Management Solutions
Provides a tried-and-tested operations management solution with spreadsheet-like configurability and enterprise scalability.
Connects the material assets of your facility to the human assets of your enterprise for day-to-day operations, shift-to-shift communications, and task scheduling.
Ecosys®
Brings together previously siloed project data such as schedules, budgets, expenditures, commitments, timesheets, invoices and more.
Manage project portfolios with insights from actual project data.
Data cooperation
HxGN SDx2 offers presentation, data, or application integration for many popular maintenance, design, and operations systems.
AspenTech®
Provides the HxGN SDx2 user with access to real time and historical information that comes from a variety of sensors in the IP21 system.
Automatically creates, updates, and terminates the corresponding assets in the AspenTech® Manufacturing Master Data Manager.
Maximo®
Automatically creates, updates, and terminates the corresponding locations in Maximo® for data that is manipulated in HxGN SDx2.
Seamlessly navigate and view engineering documents from Maximo® in HxGN SDx2.
OSIsoft PI™
Automatically creates, updates, and terminates the corresponding asset framework elements in OSIsoft PI™ when they are manipulated in HxGN SDx2.
SAP® Plant Maintenance
Ensures that the engineering changes executed in HxGN SDx2 are implemented in the plant by maintenance teams using SAP®.
Provides the detailed engineering data in HxGN SDx2 for maintenance and inspection planners.
Smart Digital Reality Integration Overview
Currently, Smart 3D, Smart Electrical, Smart Instrumentation, and Smart P&ID can share data with HxGN SDx2 using the delivered web API. Additionally, other structured data can be sent to HxGN SDx2 using the same web API. The data
is sent through the web APIs directly to the HxGN SDx2 database, where it is separated based on the tool's assigned design component and processed by the Integration Engine, which consolidates the data, creating a "golden record."
The changes are compared to data from other sources.
Other forms of data, such as tags and documents from other applications, can be loaded to HxGN SDx2 using the delivered Data Take-On Service.
What is Data Take-On?
The HxGN SDx2 Data Take-On feature facilitates seamless loading of data provided in different input file formats into our digital platform.
 Data Take-On is now available in HxGN SDx2 on request. Submit a support case with details, including how you intend to use Data Take-On for your unique business scenario and the specific tenant or tenants for which you want
this feature enabled.
Supported CSV load sizes in Data Take-On currently
The following parameters must be kept under consideration while running a Data Take-On job. However, these parameters will change regularly as we add more features and continuously enhance our solution. We recommend you to
revisit the documentation frequently to keep track of changes to these parameters.
The maximum number of rows in your comma separated values (CSV) file must not exceed 50,000, in case of documents and tags. The application notifies you if the number of rows in your CSV file exceeds this limit.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document or all the documents that can be created as part of a Data Take-On job is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 4GB.
The maximum number of files that can be attached as a zip file to documents must not exceed 10,000. Even when you are uploading multiple zip files, the total number of files in all the zip files must not exceed 10,000.
Data Take-On can run a single job at a time.
Before you start running any Data Take-On jobs, ensure that the client application is registered for your tenant. Otherwise, the jobs may not move to the next stage after Stage processing. Contact your system administrator for more
information. Also, see Configure a client application.
You must also ensure that any PBS items such as Area, Unit, and so on, listed in your CSV file, also exist in the domain specified in the job definition. For more information, see Load Configurations tab in Create a job definition.
For running a Data Take-On job, you must be having a valid organization and security code assigned to you by your administrator.
For more information on running Data Take-On jobs, see How do I use Data Take-On to load documents and data?
Input file format - CSV files
Data Take-On feature helps you control how tag and document data is imported, validated, and loaded into the HxGN SDx2 database during project handover, brownfield data take-on, and data loads.
Comma Separated Value (CSV) files contain tags and documents.
Data Take-On is especially useful to do bulk creation of objects and documents.
Also, there are cases where HxGN SDx2 is not used as a collaboration platform and the contractors work offline in their own systems. They hand over their data and documents using CSV format. The CSV files are loaded through
Data Take-On using the DTO Loader workflow. For more information, see Converting the HxGN SDx2 object to a Data Take-On job using the DTO Loader workflow.
Several loader mappings are used for setting up the system. For example, tag classifications, material classifications, site-plant-area-unit, and so on.
Loader mappings are delivered for documents, tags, and so on to load data. This can be for migrating data into HxGN SDx2 or even frequently loading data that doesn’t have to be put into a review process through Submittals. For
more information, see Loading data into your system.
Data Take-On is required to submit Submittals and to put the documents into the Review process.
Data Take-On Administration
Using Data Take-On, data files are imported using an import definition, which specifies how the data is mapped to the database using existing class definitions, property definitions, and relationship definitions. The imported data is validated
against defined validation rule sets that can use the database as their basis, and the load mapping ensures that the data is mapped correctly into the database format. Job definitions combine all these components into one set of steps.
Available in HxGN SDx2, the Data Take-On feature set can be used to manage the movement of data using the following set of components:
Import definition - Maps incoming data specified in the input files against appropriate schema for loading the data into staging area. It consists of multiple mappings to match the database schema.
Validation rules and rulesets - Evaluates imported data against a defined set of rules, grouped within rulesets, to ensure the validity of the data.
Load mapping - Uses a defined mapping, based on the structure of the database, to manage loading data into the database.
Job definition - Links together various import, validating, and load components to define how a set of data will be processed by Data Take-On.
 If you are creating one object at a time, we recommend creating the mappings interactively rather than using the delivered mappings. To create maps interactively, you use the Import Definition feature in the Data Take-On feature
set.
For more information, see How do I configure Data Take-On?
Data Take-On Job Management
You can manage the Data Take-On jobs by accessing the different Data Take-On job methods through the Data Take-On feature set in the Web Client. Using job definitions, you can create jobs that manage the automation of the import,
validation, and loading of data into the database.
For more information, see How do I use Data Take-On to load documents and data?
Data Take-On Basics
Introduction to Data Take-On
Data Take-On is a comprehensive data validation, transformation, and migration platform that enables companies to ensure the quality of information and significantly reduce the time and costs associated with data take-on.
Data Take-On supports the staging, validation, and loading of data, providing consistency and traceability of the entire data migration process. It ensures the quality of data delivered from contractors, suppliers, and vendors, creating a
high-quality data basis for operations.
Data Take-On enables Engineering, Procurement, Construction companies and owner operators to:
Check the quality of information being transferred
Keep a record of which checks have been performed and results obtained
Load approved information into their databases
Route unapproved information back to responsible parties for remediation
Data Take-On monitors and maintains the data quality when data is being moved from one source to another, during project handover, brownfield data take-on, data loads, and migrations. Data Take-On manages the data for quality
assurance purposes, and it provides reports that can be used for both greenfield and brownfield environment data to help minimize quality issues.
Data Take-On Workflows
The validation of data imported into the staging area and the loading of the objects into the database are configured using workflows. Data Take-On is delivered with the DTO Loader workflow templates. The workflow brings each
component together as a process step and ensures that the imported data is validated before loading into the database.
Data Take-On in a snapshot
In the following graphic, you’ll see a high level representation of the core functionalities of Data Take-On. This includes:
Translate - 3D model files from various third-party tools are translated and parsed into graphic data tables and engineering data tables.
 This functionality is currently not supported and will be available in a future update.
Stage – Column headers of the input file are mapped to the staging objects.
Validation – Imported data in the staging area is verified for accuracy with the validation rules.
Load – After the approval of the validation report, the data is loaded into the database.
Data Take-On components
The Data Take-On process relies on several main components that you can configure to your business practices:
Import mappings
Validation rules and rule sets
Load mappings
Job definitions
For more information about configuring Data Take-On, see How do I configure Data Take-On?
For more information about running Data Take-On jobs, see Loading documents and data using Data Take-On.
Import
Data Take-On imports files in comma separated values (CSV) format into the staging area where the validation takes place. Import definitions are used to map the column headers in the input file to the correct column headers for objects,
properties, and relationships in the staging area. The import mapping not only uses the physical column headers that already exist in the input data file, but it also supports other column headers that are computed or provided by the user
during the import process.
 We recommend using a text editor like Notepad to modify your CSV files to avoid any unexpected results.
You can define the column headers for each import definition to map the column data from the imported input files to the existing columns in the database. You can create, edit, and modify each column header mapping to match the class
definitions, objects, properties, relationships, and relationship properties between the imported data objects and database objects. For more information about creating and editing import definitions, see Create an import definition.
Column headers
Column headers are used to map the data in the input file to the correct locations in the staging area and database, and they support a number of different column types. The column type you select when defining the column header
determines the appearance of the Column Header and Mapping dialog boxes that you use later in the import mapping process. Data Take-On supports the following types of columns:
Physical - Maps to a specific column in the input data source.
Computed - Generates a new column using data from other columns or sources, such as environment type variables. This provides the ability to use functions. For example, you can enter a function in the Computed API box to
replace original entries with new entries.
Constant - Allows you to use a string constant.
In the following graphic, you’ll see that column headers mapped from the input CSV file to the staging objects. Additional column mappings allow users to enter values and the application to compute values during the import process.
Explanation of the mapping in the graphic:
Column 1 of input CSV file is mapped to the object called Tag and the Name property of it.
Column 2 of input CSV file is mapped to the Description property of the tag object.
Column 3 of input CSV file is not mapped directly, but is used in a computed column which is used to map to the object called FDWModel and the Name property of it.
The constant value “EQUIPMENT” is mapped to the Tag Category property of the tag object.
A computed column takes the input value from the Column 3 of the input CSV file and changes it to upper case. This is mapped to the object called FDWModel and the Name property of it.
You must create column mappings for each column header in the import definition so that imported data matches the corresponding data column format in the staging area or database. You can search for the objects and properties found
locally or add new objects and properties, and then you can create the new mappings to match the database objects and properties.
You can create three types of column header mappings for each column header.
Object mapping
Property mapping
Relationship mapping
For more information about column header mappings, see Define import mapping.
Actions
When you define a column header in Data Take-On, you assign it an action. This action indicates what Data Take-On will do with the data imported from the related column in the input data. The kinds of operations supported varies,
depending on whether the column header is mapped to an object, a property, or a relationship.
Object actions
Action
Description
Check Exists
Checks to see if the object exists in the database.
Create Update
Creates a new object or updates the object if it already exists in the database.
Delete
Deletes an object in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the object in the database.
Terminate
Terminates the object in the database.
Update
Updates the object in the database.
Validate Only
Validates the data only. No action is taken on the object in the database, but if errors are found then they are propagated to the related objects.
Property actions
Action
Description
Check Staging File Exists
Checks to see if the staging property file exists in the staging area.
Compare Create
Invokes the compare value rule, which creates an error if the database value is different. The database property is overwritten if the value is not set.
Compare Value
Invokes the compare value rule, which never changes the value in the database.
Create Update
Creates a new property and updates the existing property in the database.
Delete
Deletes a property in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the property in the database.
Terminate
Terminates the property in the database.
Validate Only
Validates the property only. No action is taken on the object in the database, but if errors are found then they are propagated to the related objects.
 Using Data Take-On, you cannot delete or terminate the required properties of an object that exists in the database.
Relationship actions
Action
Description
Check Exists
Checks to see if the relationship exists in the database.
Create Update
Creates a new relationship and updates the existing relationship in the database.
Delete
Deletes a relationship in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the relationship in the database.
Terminate
Terminates the relationship in the database.
Relationship property actions
Action
Description
Create Update
Creates a new relationship property and updates the existing relationship property in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the relationship property in the database.
Compare Create
Invokes the compare value rule, which creates an error if the database value is different. The database relationship property is only overwritten if
the value is not set.
Compare Value
Invokes the compare value rule, which never changes the value in the database.
Validation
Validation is performed on the data that is imported into the staging area. Validation ensures the imported objects meet the criteria mentioned in the selected rule sets.
Validation rules verify that the data in an imported record meets the data standards specified for the database. Each validation rule contains a formula that evaluates the imported data and verifies that it matches the existing criteria and
hierarchy found in the database. Each validation rule includes an error message that is recorded in a validation report when the rule returns an invalid value.
All the validation rules that will be used in a job are grouped into a set. You can create a validation rule set, and then create the individual rules that will make up that set.
 When a validation rule is terminated, any staging schema items, such as class definition, relationship definition, or property definition referenced by the rule, which are no longer used by Data Take-On import mappings or load
mappings, are automatically removed from the database.
In the following graphic, you’ll see the schema validation and the business/data logic rules validate the imported data in the staging database. Some of the rules also check the database schema. The validation report displays errors found
in the imported data.
 Data Take-On automatically generates validation rules when you create your import mapping.
Validation rules and their descriptions
The following table lists all the validation rules and their descriptions delivered with Data Take-On.
Rule name
Description
Check Claimed to Sub Configuration
Checks to see if a plant-level item has been claimed to the project.
Check Duplicate Issue Dates
Checks to see if there are revisions with duplicate issue dates for the same master document.
Check File Exists
Checks to see if a file exists in the specified path and that security has been set to configure access permissions.
Check File Exists for Document Revision
Checks to see if any file is attached to the document revision if the document revision is not a planned revision.
Check For Cyclic Relationships
Checks to determine if the same object is set on both End 1 and End 2 of a relationship definition.
Check Is Claimable
Checks to see if the data object is claimable into the HxGN SDx2 collaboration project and prevents the object from being
loaded.
Check Is Revisable
Checks to see if the document is revisable to the HxGN SDx2 collaboration project and prevents the document revision
from being loaded.
Check Object Exists
Checks to see if the object exists in the database already.
Check Parallel Claim
Checks to see if the item is claimed to a parallel project under the same plant.
Check Revised to Sub Config
Checks to see if a plant-level document has been revised to the project.
Check Revised to Parallel Config
Checks to see if the document item has been revised to a parallel project under the same plant.
Check Properties Against Schema
Checks to see if the database property definitions are valid for the database object type and if the values are valid for this
property, including whether required values are present.
Check Relationship Exists
Checks to see if the relationship exists in the database.
Check Unique Key
Checks to see if a specific unique key already exists elsewhere in the system and cannot be implicitly claimed. This check
runs only on the database and detects duplicates in the source files.
Check Value and UOM
Checks to see if the UOM of the object property is the same as the UOM configured in the database. It also checks to see
if the object property value is defined within a specified range of minimum and maximum values.
Compare All Property Values For Object
Checks to see if all the staging area object property values are the same as the database property values.
Compare Value
Checks to see if the staging area property value is the same as the database property value.
Compare Value Against Environment Variable
Checks to see if the staging area property value specified in the CSV file is available in the database using the
environment variable.
Date Time
Checks to see if an object property must validate against some date and time, YMD, or DMY.
Double
Checks to see if an object property value has a minimum and a maximum value.
Integer
Checks to see if an object property value is defined within a specified range by a minimum and maximum value.
Regular Expression
Checks to see if an object property value adheres to a specified pattern.
Staging System Cardinality
Checks for the cardinalities between the related objects.
String Does Not Start With
Checks to see that the value does not start with the given value.
String Length
Checks to see if the length of an object property is within a specified minimum and maximum limit or adheres to a
specified pattern.
String Not Equal To
Checks to see that the value is not equal to the given value.
Target System Cardinality
Checks for the minimum and maximum cardinality values between related objects from the database.
Configure the automatic generation of validation rules
When you create your import mappings from the database, validation rules are automatically generated and set on the import definition. The system determines which rules it runs for each action, saving configuration time. You can
configure which rules are automatically generated for each action in the Data Take-On system options, see Using Data Take-On Administration. The actions and rules are configurable.
Load
The HxGN SDx2 data model forms the basis for the load mapping process in Data Take-On. The load process uses a defined mapping, based on the structure of the database, to manage loading data into the database.
The data to be loaded must match the data in the database using any one of three parameters:
Unique ID (UID)
Unique key
Query definition
By default, Data Take-On identifies objects in the database using the UID constructed in the staging area and the class definition specified in the load map. Sometimes, however, the UIDs for a specific type of object in a database cannot
be constructed or predicted from the data provided in a comma separated values (CSV) file. In that case, you can use a database unique key or a query definition to specify how to identify the object in the database.
You can load data to a database by mapping the imported staging area data objects to the existing objects, properties, and relationships found in the database. The load mapping can be further refined for each stage object to target object
mapping by selecting specific property-to-property, relationship-to-property, and relationship-to-relationship mappings.
 If you have two databases that use the same model or schema, they can share the same load mapping.
Load mapping actions
Load mappings are restricted by what actions you select when creating the object, as there are permitted combinations of actions. For example, the actions available for the Delete class definition mapping are Compare Value and No
Action.
The following table shows the supported combination of default actions available in Data Take-On.
 Only one Check Staging File Exists property action is allowed per object. If there is more than one, an error is raised and the loading is blocked. For more information, see Review load stage errors.
Job Definition
A job definition is a combined set of components configured into a template for the staging, validation, and loading of data into the database. The job definition manages the quality of the data to be loaded into the database. You can define
the import mappings, load mappings, validation rule sets, and delete rule sets.
When a job definition has been configured, Data Take-On uses it to load Data Take-On jobs containing data into the database.
Each job definition is configured using the following components:
Basic Details - The job description name and description, as well as the workflow used to load data into the database.
Workflow - The defined set of steps that drive the behavior of the job running through Data Take-On. The following workflow templates are delivered with Data Take-On: DTO Loader and Translate Loader workflows.
Database - The system used to validate and load the data.
Load Configuration - The load configuration used for creation of new document versions and file management.
Import Definitions - The definitions used to import the data.
Rule Sets - The rule sets used to validate and verify the imported data.
Validation Report Definitions - The report definition used to create the validation report.
Method Details - The method details used to create a method to run the Data Take-On job.
Access Groups - The access groups that have permission to use the job definition to create a new job in Data Take-On.
Terms and acronyms
Use the following topics to learn more about key terms and acronyms in Data Take-On.
Terms
Term
Meaning
Actions
An indicator of what Data Take-On will do with the object data in the validation and export process, such as update and delete. These kinds of
operations vary, depending on whether the column header is mapped to an object, a property, or a relationship.
Brownfield data
An existing project or area that has constraints imposed due to prior work and contains existing data.
Column Headers
They are used in Data Take-On as the basis for mapping new data in columns to match an existing structured database.
Load Mapping
The process that maps the imported objects, classes, and properties to the existing structure of the objects, classes, and properties found in a
database.
Load Process
Manages the loading of the data into the database, using a defined mapping, based on the structure of the database.
Functions
A computed functional code run at import, where the output value of a function depends only on the arguments that are input to the function.
Greenfield data
A project or area that is completely new and does not have any constraints imposed by prior work or existing data.
Import Definition
A defined mapping of imported file objects, properties, and relationships from existing column headers to objects, properties, and relationships
found in the staging area during the import process.
Import Mapping
The process that maps the imported objects, classes, and properties to the existing structure of the objects, classes, and properties found in a
staging area.
Import Process
The process that manages the import of data in the data file or files to the staging area using a defined mapping.
Job
A defined object that carries information for the progress of data through a selected workflow in Data Take-On, such as when data is imported,
validated, and loaded into a database.
Job Definition
A combined set of components configured for the import, validation, and loading of data to a specific database.
Mapping
A defined process where data is correlated from existing column headers for the objects, properties, and relationships in the imported data to the
correct column headers for the objects, properties, and relationships found in the database, such as the staging area or the database.
Rules
A logical formula used to evaluate and verify whether the data in an imported record meets the requirements and data standards specified for the
database.
Rule Sets
A combined set of rules that can be run as a set during job processing.
Staging Area
The staging area in the database used is the first part of a combined set of processes in Data Take-On, where imported data is held and
validated before being loaded into a HxGN SDx2 database.
Database
The system or database used as the destination for loaded data that has been validated before loading. It is also used as the basis for creating
import mappings and validation rules.
Terminate
To change the status of an object to terminated without removing it from the database. Terminating objects, instead of deleting them, allows you
to continue to see the history of the object after termination.
Unique Key
A set of values guaranteed to be unique for each object in a relation, and can be used to identify objects in the database.
Validation Process
A process that evaluates the imported data against a defined set of rules to ensure the validity of the data.
Validation Rule
A logical formula used to evaluate the imported data in one or more fields to determine whether it matches the existing criteria and hierarchy
found in the database. There are two types of validation rules:
Rules that determine if the data is valid for the database schema (these rules can be auto-generated).
Rules that determine if the data meets specific business criteria, such as naming format.

Common tasks in HxGN SDx2
This section details common tasks available across HxGN SDx2.
Understanding information access
What you see and can do in HxGN SDx2 depends on how your administrator configures your system and your security permissions. Administrators use the security model to do the following:
Create users, access groups, and role assignments
Assign users to roles and associate roles with access groups, which determine the access to features, commands, plants and projects
Create scopes for plant or projects known as configurations, which determine what objects and data you can view, create, and modify
 Configurations are used to manage controlled change to data. The top-level configurations are usually plants with projects underneath.
Create and enforce security rules and security codes
How do I know which roles and configurations I can use?
You can see which roles and configurations you can use two different ways:
You can see which roles and configurations are currently being used in the upper right corner. Select Query, Create, and Role to view the options available to you.
Select Settings 
, and then Scope to view your configurations, or select Roles to view which roles are available to you.
I can create an object in Plant A, but not in Plant B. Why?
You can only create objects in a single configuration at a time selected as your Create Scope. For example, if you select Plant A as your create scope, you can only create objects in Plant A. You can query for data and objects in other
configurations, such as Plant B, but you cannot create objects in Plant B until you switch your create scope to Plant B.
Which security features determine what I can see or do in the system?
What you can see or do is determined by which objects you need to see and which actions you need to perform to do your job.
Your administrator can use many different components of the security model to control what you can do or see in the system.
For more information on the security model, see Security model overview.
Security model feature
What it does
Role and role assignment
Determines your level of access to data and functionality in a specific plant or project
configuration. You can belong to more than one role per configuration. Roles are
associated with related access groups, domains, and owning groups.
Access group
Determines what actions you can perform in the system and which functional
components you can access in your system. Users are related to roles, which are
related to access groups.
Security rule
Limits the types of data that you can query in the system. Your associated access
groups are used to determine which security rules are relevant.
Security code
Sets different security levels for users and data in the system. Security codes are
used by security rules, which determine your access to documents and data items
that can be secured by these codes. You may be assigned different sets of security
codes in different plants or projects.
Owning group
Sets up ownership of data, typically by department or discipline, as well as controls
your access to an object or parts of an object based on its ownership. Objects can be
owned by a user or by an owning group. Owning groups are associated to roles. The
default owning group is engineering to which everyone has access.
Configuration
You can be assigned different roles in different plants and projects.
You can create and manipulate data in a single plant or project. This is also known as
the create scope. Objects created in projects are not visible from parallel projects or
its parent plant.
You can query across multiple plants and projects. This is also known as the query
scope.
I can’t find a document that I’m supposed to work on. Where is it?
There might be several reasons why you can’t find the document in your system:
A security rule might be applied that limits access to specific organizations.
The document might be in a plant or project that you don’t have access to.
You might not have the appropriate role to view or edit that specific document.
It’s important to communicate with your administrator to see if you’re allowed to access that document.
Which security codes are delivered?
There are three security codes delivered: Unclassified, Proprietary, and Classified; however, your administrator can configure any security code that meets your business's needs.
Security codes can be associated with both objects in the system and user roles - setting different security levels and controlling access and visibility of objects.
Understanding scopes
You access data in your system found within plants or project configurations using scopes. The scopes determine what data you can view, query, create, or modify in the selected plants or project configurations.
What is the difference between a create scope and a query scope?
The Create Scope is the one plant or project configuration, that you select from a list of query configurations, in which you can create and modify data or objects.
The Query Scope displays an access-defined set of plants or project configurations that you can search and query for data and objects. When you select the plant or project configurations, this selection determines what data and objects
are returned and displayed after you run a search or query.
When you select multiple plants or project configurations as your query scope, the returned data is displayed in the results grid under separate tabs that correspond to each configuration. Each tab is shown in alphabetical order.
When you query a project configuration that exists under a plant, the query returns objects found in both the plant and the selected project configuration. For example, if you set your query scope as PlantA and Project2, your query
will return objects that exist in both PlantA and Project 2.
How many plants or projects can I select for each scope?
You can only select one plant or project as your Create Scope.
You can select multiple plants or project configurations as your Query Scope. The returned data is displayed in the results grid under separate tabs that correspond to each configuration. Each tab is shown in alphabetical order.
When you query a project configuration that exists under a plant, the query returns objects found in both the plant and the selected project configuration. For example, if you set your query scope as PlantA and Project2, your query will
return objects that exist in both PlantA and Project 2.
 The maximum number of scopes you can select as your Query Scope is restricted to ninety nine (99). This is a system limitation. To reduce the amount of data returned, you should refine your query scopes to a more targeted
selection.
How does filtering features by design component work?
You can control the display of specific features in HxGN SDx2 based on the integrated design component selected in the Data Filter section when you configure your scopes. The Consolidated option is selected by default, so you need to
select the specific design component to see any of the design component features, such as actions, objects and methods.
Setting the data filter option to a design component limits your scope to display only the data assigned to that corresponding design component for a specific tool, such as 3D. This ensures that only the relevant design component features,
such as methods, entry points, reports, and forms are visible to you based on the design component you set for your scopes.
To control the visibility of each design component feature, relationship definitions are defined between the type of object you want to control and the specific design component. They are:
SPFMethodDesignComponent - Relationship between a method and the design component.
SPFFormDesignComponent - Relationship between a form and the design component
SPFAPIEntryPointDesignComponent - Relationship between an entry point and the design component.
SPFAdhocReportDesignComponent - Relationship between a report and the design component.
Methods
As you create or update a method, you can select the design component you want the form to be related to by setting the IDF. This configures the SPFMethodDesignComponent relationship definition between the method and a design
component so that the method is only displayed when you select the IDF for that design component in scopes. The following example image shows that the View Item in Drawing method is associated with both the 3D and the
Consolidated design components using the SPFMethodDesignComponent relationship.
The same approach applies to any method actions, shortcuts, entry points, reports, and forms. The only difference is the relationship definition created is used to retrieve the related design component features.
If the selected IDF for the method is both 3D and Consolidated, the View Item in Drawing method is available for both design components.
If the method is not associated with any specific design component, it is only available in the Consolidated design component.
Forms
As you create or update a form, you can select the design component you want the form to be related to by setting the integration data filter. This configures the SPFFormDesignComponent relationship definition between the form and a
design component, so that the form is only displayed when you select the IDF for that design component in scopes.
If a Class Definition is linked to a form that has a relationship with a design component, the form is retrieved if the selected design component matches.
If a Class Definition is linked to a form that is not associated with any design component, the form is retrieved for the default Consolidated design component.
In cases where both Class Definition and Classification have forms, the most specialized form linked to the classification is selected. The filtering for the design component is unchanged.
If you selected the data filter as Consolidated, the To Do List displays both Non-SIO tasks and Consolidated tasks through the relationship between the form's class definition to the selected design component.
If you select another data filter different from Consolidated, the To Do List icon is hidden in the sidebar.
 For more information, see Working with scopes.
Understanding roles
Your user role determines what features or commands you can view in each plant or project. Your business administrator assigns you one or more role within the plants or projects. You can select multiple roles from the list to access
different functionality in different configurations. As you select each role, the different features or commands are made available, or are removed.
 For more information, see Change your roles.
Which roles do I have access to?
You can check the list of roles assigned to you in the selected plant or project in Settings. As you select each role, different features will become available.
Why do my roles change when I change configuration?
Your business administrator assigns you one or more roles within plants and projects. They can assign the same roles to you across multiple plants or projects, so different roles can be assigned to you depending on your responsibilities
for each plant or project. When different roles are assigned, some configuration options might not be available to you because of your role permissions.
When the same roles are assigned to you across the multiple plants or projects, you can access the same features in each configuration.
The list of plants or projects available can also change depending on your selected role because some roles might not have access to certain plants or projects.
The list of roles to which you have access is configured by your system administrator. For more information on your role assignments, contact your system administrator.
Users assigned a contractor role will not have access to certain features in HxGN SDx2.
What happens when I select all roles?
When Select All is applied to the Roles list, all your role permissions are applied at the same time.
If you have been assigned a contractor role and the Select All option is selected, some features are hidden and unavailable to you even if another one of your roles has the necessary permissions to access the features because the
limited access permissions for contractor role overrides the other role permissions. To access the features you would normally have access to, clear the contractor role selection. For more information on your role assignments, contact your
system administrator.
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
