After logging into HxGN SDx2, you can quickly and easily navigate to your documents and data through the feature sets and functions on the Home page and perform your tasks from the sidebar.
Select
To see
What it does
Actions menu
Performs common operations on one or more selected objects as indicated.
 Select Open in new tab 
 against an action to display the results in a new browser tab. Keep in mind that
logging out in one tab automatically logs you out in all other open tabs. You will lose any unsaved changes.
Pins
Provides quick access to items that you have saved, or pinned as indicated.
Notifications
Alerts you when a change has been made to an object that you have registered interest in.
Feature sets
Each contains a configured set of functions that you can use to quickly access data or run reports and
commands. You can also open a feature set and access the functions from the sidebar.
To Do List
Lists all your tasks.
Advanced Search
Performs a complex search using specific search criteria that includes classifications, their properties, and
parameters.
 You can save a complex advanced search query, as well as rename an existing query or save an edited
query with a new name.
Search Bar
Searches for objects in the system based on object types, parameters, or wildcards that you type in the Search
Bar.
Settings
Shows the controls you can use to:
Choose a different configuration or role.
Reassign your workflow tasks to other users.
Change user preferences to customize your experience. For example, you can customize your display and
email notifications.
Search for data and objects in your system
The easiest ways you can search for data and objects are to:
Use the Search Bar for the quick retrieval of information from the system, such as tags, documents, assets, and so on, as described in Search for objects.
Use the Multiple Item Search option in the Search Bar for the retrieval of specific information that is known by name, as described in Search for objects.
Run a predefined search function tile found in a feature set configured by your system administrator.
For more advanced searches, you can use Advanced Search 
 to create more complex queries that you can save for future use, as described in Querying for data.
All search results are shown in the results grid, see View search results.
For more information on configuring search function tiles, see Relate a method to a feature set.
Search for objects
You can search for specific objects in the search bar.
1. In the toolbar, click Search.
2. In the Search Menu, select the data type, and select the search type.
For example, select Documents as the data type, and select All Documents as the search type.
3. In the Search Bar 
, type any defining criteria, parameters, or wildcards, and click Search 
.
 You can control the default search criteria that appears in the Search bar for each object type selected in the Search menu. For more information, see Configure the default search bar criteria.
How can I improve my search?
You can improve your search by:
Selecting FILTER on the Search menu so you can refine the search to a targeted set of objects. You can change the search filters by selecting other column set items from the list.
 You can choose up to a maximum number of five (5) column set properties from the list. This helps improve your search performance by restricting the number of properties you can select. If you select more than five (5), a
message warns you that too many filters are selected. If you still go ahead and run the search with six or more filters selected, peek search is disabled, and the search just uses Name as the default or the search filter option
configured at the top.
Selecting a property or relationship as a filter, and entering a wildcard (*) will only return the objects which have the column set item (property or relationship) instantiated.
Using the search operator Is equal to instead of the default search operator Contains. The Is equal to operator returns more targeted searches for data that is an exact match and helps improve search performance each time you
search.
Changing the default search filters to other items available in the objects column set, such as Description or Classification, so they are preselected each time you search. The default search filter is Name.
 For more information on configuring search options for the Search menu, see Configure search menu items.
The search results are displayed in a results grid. For more information, see View search results.
As you type in the search bar, a peek search returns examples and the number of objects that match the typed characters. You can configure the number of characters that must be typed before a peek search request is invoked. For
more information, see Configure peek search request value.
Search for multiple items
You can search for multiple items directly in the search bar.
1. In the toolbar, select Search.
2. In the Search Menu, search for the required data type.
For example, search for Tag Types.
3. In the Search bar 
, select Multiple Object Search 
.
4. In the Multiple Object Search dialog, type the name of each search object in a separate row, along with any wildcards. For example:
 You can also copy and paste line entries directly into the multiple object search dialog from an existing list, such as from Microsoft Excel. This is limited to one hundred (100) line entries per search.
5. Select Search 
, and the results are displayed in a Results grid.
Multiple Object Search only supports searching the Name field using the Is equal to search operator, which returns results that are an exact match. Multiple object search does not support listing matches using a peek search.
When you select Search, the contents of the Multiple Object Search dialog automatically removes any duplicate entries, empty lines, or spaces from any search terms. There is no need to use pipe | special character to separate
line entries.
If a multiple object search request is too large, an error may occur. Reduce the number of search objects and retry.
Export object data to Excel
After you have run a search for objects, the returned objects and their property column set data is displayed in a results grid. You can then download the named objects and their column set properties to a Microsoft Excel® XLSX file using
the EXPORT ALL TO EXCEL command. You can open the downloaded spreadsheet directly from your browser or you can download it to your designated folder and open it there.
 We do not recommend you export large amounts of data using the Export All to Excel command. The maximum export limit is set to 10,000 objects by default. If the number of objects to be exported exceeds this default,
the export may fail. We advise you to run reports that have been specifically developed to handle the export of large amounts of data.
The EXPORT ALL TO EXCEL command is also available to other functionality, such as opening a pins list in a results grid, and so on.
Configured reports return and display large amounts of data in a variety of formats. For more information on running reports, see Execute saved reports.
The property columns you see, and the order in which the columns appear in the results grid are determined by the column set based on the object classification.
Use the Actions menu to manage objects
The Actions menu displays a list of shortcuts to command functions that are specific to the selected object(s). To display the Actions menu command shortcuts:
Select the object in a row, and select Actions 
 in the row, or select Actions 
 in the sidebar.
If you select multiple objects, the number of objects selected is displayed in the Actions sidebar. Only actions applicable to all of the selected objects are displayed in the Actions menu.
Some actions are available in the shortcut ribbon when you hover on an object row.
You can also open the Actions menu from the central node on the Info Map and from an object's hotspot bubble in an opened 2D or 3D file.
For more information on adding Actions menu command shortcuts, see Configure Actions menu items.
How can I open action results in a new tab?
Select Open in new tab 
 to open the results in a new browser tab.
 Logging out in one tab automatically logs you out in all other open tabs. You will lose any unsaved changes.
 You can use the Suppress Open in New Tab in Web Client option in a method to hide Open in Web Client 
 icon from display in the Actions menu. This ensures that the action does not open into a separate browser tab.
Displaying actions as shortcut icons
Some Actions menu functionality can be accessed using shortcut icons that display in a ribbon when you select an object. Only action shortcut icons in the ribbon relevant to the selected object are displayed. The shortcut icon ribbon
displays when:
You hover over an object's description in the Results grid, and the icon ribbon opens beneath the object row.
You open the Actions menu for an object, the icon ribbon displays at the top of the Actions menu.
Apple iPads and Android Tablets
You interact differently with the UI on Apple iPads or Android tablets as there is a variation in functionality for displaying the actions shortcut icons. This is due to the different devices that can be used to view HxGN SDx2:
On an Apple iPad or Android tablet, on the object row, select the ribbon option , and the action shortcut ribbon opens beneath the row.
Default ribbon icons
The following action shortcut icons are configured to display in the ribbon by default.
Icon
Action
Description
Show Info Map
Displays the selected object's relationships as clear, organized user-focused information.
Add to Pinboard
Adds the selected object to a list in the Pinboard.
Show the detail form
Displays the Object Details form showing the current value of the object's properties.
Share Data URL
Displays the object's URL address path, which can be sent to anyone you are collaborating with.
Other object shortcut icons
Other action shortcut icons are displayed depending on the type of object selected in the Results grid and how your system is configured. For example, the following action shortcut icons can be displayed dependent on the document type.
Icon
Action
Description
View and Markup
Displays the selected object in View and Markup.
View drawing
Displays the object as a drawing in a document.
View 3D Model
Displays the object as an item in a three dimensional model.
View laser scan
Displays the object in a laser scan.
Show inbox for object
Displays the To Do List task items for the selected object.
View workflow
Displays the Workflow Viewer, which shows the current status and position of the object in the attached workflow.
Add objects to the Pinboard
You can bookmark your favorite objects by adding them to a list in your Pinboard, where they can be easily managed and recalled for future use.
You access your pinned items by clicking Pins 
 in the sidebar to open the Pins menu to see the pinned items in your Pinboard. For more information, see Using the Pinboard.
 When you create a new object, it is automatically added to New Items in the Pins menu. However, new objects must be added to a list in the Pinboard 
 to save them, as they are only available in a single session.
Review your work in the To Do List
The To Do List 
 displays your workflow tasks that have been assigned to you or your roles that need to be actioned or completed. You can see the number of tasks available in each selected configuration, the task's details including
attached files, and see a brief view of your task step in the workflow.
Select To Do List 
, and select a slice in the pie chart or a task type in the list to view all the tasks in a results table.
Use the Info Map to visually navigate information
Use the Info Map to quickly visualize all the objects related to a single object. The selected object is displayed as a central node surrounded by all the related objects, such as file types, plants, tags, contracts, suppliers, and so on.
Selecting a different node in the view displays that node as the central node with its related objects expanded.
Select an object in the Results table row, and select Info Map 
.
How can I interact with the Info Map?
Drag and reposition individual object nodes in the window using the mouse or touch screen control.
Zoom and resize objects in the display using the mouse wheel or touch screen control.
You interact with the Info Map and change how objects are presented using the following tool-bar commands:
Name
Icon
Description
Single Mode
Displays a single node surrounded by its related objects and relationships.
Follow Mode
Displays multiple nodes each surrounded by their related objects and relationships, which allows you to follow the
expansion and grow a relationship tree.
 The follow mode is disabled when you view the Info Map from the object details form.
Reset
Resets the node positions back to their original position.
Clear History
Clears the Info Map expansion history and returns the display the selected node and its related objects.
Hide Node Names
Hides the names of each node displayed in the window.
Show Node Names
Shows the names of each node displayed in the window.
Zoom to Fit
Fits all the displayed object nodes into the window.
Undo
Undoes the last selected node expansion.
Redo
Redoes the last selected node expansion.
 Info Map 
 is also displayed in the actions menu or in the object details page.
Change your user settings and personalize your experience
You can change your configuration scopes, roles, display, and email preferences in the Settings 
 menu, as well as reassign workflow tasks to other users. For more information, see Working with scopes and User preferences.
Working with scopes
You can choose your create and query scopes from the list of available plant and project configurations in the Scope Settings page. The selected scope configurations determine what objects you can view, create, or modify after a search
or query displays the results in a results grid.
You can quickly view your selected query and create scopes in a list directly in the scopes header bar. For more information, see View the scopes in the header bar.
Only the workflow tasks that are relevant to your selected query scope plant and project configurations are displayed in your To Do List. For more information, see .
For more information about access, see Understanding information access.
Change the query scope
1. Select Query in the header bar to open the Scope Settings page to set your query scopes.
2. Select the plants or project configurations from the list to add them to your query scope. The selected configurations are displayed in a list in the Query Scopes column.
 You can expand each plant or configuration node to reveal other related configurations or projects. Select LOAD MORE to display more configurations in large systems.
Each loaded list of plants and configurations is displayed in batches of 25 to improve performance.
The selected number of configurations can also be seen in the Query scope header bar. For more information, see View the scopes in the header bar.
If you query for the project configurations that exist under a plant, by default the query returns objects found in both the plant and the project configurations.
For example, if you set your query scope as Project 2 of Plant A, your query returns objects that exist in both Project 2 and Plant A.
The maximum number of configurations you can select as your Query scope is restricted to 99. For more information, see Understanding information access.
You can also access the Scope Settings page by selecting Settings 
 in the sidebar.
You can narrow the query to return data from a selected set of configurations and the plant using the Query by selected configurations only option. For more information, see Query by selected configurations only.
Change the create scope
1. Select Create in the header bar to open the Scope Settings page to set your create scope.
2. In the Query Scopes list, select the Create 
toggle key for the configuration in which you want to create or modify objects.
 You can only choose one configuration at a time to use as your Create Scope from the configurations added to your Query Scopes list.
View the scopes in the header bar
You can view a summary of your selected scopes in the header bar by selecting the arrow located next to the Create scope option. All the selected query scopes are displayed and the create scope configuration is indicated.
The scope header bar can also indicate when a specific design component data filter has been applied to the query scope. This narrows the view to the design component data shared from a single design authoring tool registered with
HxGN SDx2.
 Select the arrow to reveal the selected scope configurations.
 For more information, see Filter data by design component.
Query by selected configurations only
By selecting the Query by selected configurations only option, your query only returns data or objects from the selected configurations (projects) and does not return data from the higher configuration (plant).
For example, if you set your query scope to Project1 and Project 2 of Plant A and select the Query by selected configurations only option, your query only returns data or objects found in Project1 and Project 2 and returns no data
from the higher configuration, Plant A.
Filter data by design component
After you have selected a query scope to specify the plant or configurations that you want to view data from, you can filter the data by the integrated authoring tool to see only the shared design components from authoring tools registered
with HxGN SDx2.
The Integration Data Filters section of the Settings window contains a list of all the authoring tools that have registered with HxGN SDx2 for integration.
1. Select Scope 
 to select your query scope.
2. From the list, select the authoring tool that you want to view design component data from. For example, 3D:
The list only displays the integrated authoring tools that are registered with HxGN SDx2 and have shared their design components. For example, if you select the 3D data filter option, you only see the 3D design component objects shared
to the selected query scope configurations in HxGN SDx2.
By default, the Consolidated option is always selected first to display all shared design component objects found in the configurations and shows the most recent data. If you change the selection to the 3D option, you only see the 3D
design component objects shared to HxGN SDx2.
 You can only view the data from one design component at a time, such as 3D.
How does filtering features by design component work?
You can control the display of specific features in HxGN SDx2 based on the integrated design component selected in the Data Filter section when you configure your scopes. The Consolidated option is selected by default, so you need to
select the specific design component to see any of the design component features, such as actions, objects and methods.
Setting the data filter option to a design component limits your scope to display only the data assigned to that corresponding design component for a specific tool, such as 3D. This ensures that only the relevant design component features,
such as methods, entry points, reports, and forms are visible to you based on the design component you set for your scopes.
To control the visibility of each design component feature, relationship definitions are defined between the type of object you want to control and the specific design component. They are:
SPFMethodDesignComponent - Relationship between a method and the design component.
SPFFormDesignComponent - Relationship between a form and the design component
SPFAPIEntryPointDesignComponent - Relationship between an entry point and the design component.
SPFAdhocReportDesignComponent - Relationship between a report and the design component.
Methods
As you create or update a method, you can select the design component you want the form to be related to by setting the IDF. This configures the SPFMethodDesignComponent relationship definition between the method and a design
component so that the method is only displayed when you select the IDF for that design component in scopes. The following example image shows that the View Item in Drawing method is associated with both the 3D and the
Consolidated design components using the SPFMethodDesignComponent relationship.
The same approach applies to any method actions, shortcuts, entry points, reports, and forms. The only difference is the relationship definition created is used to retrieve the related design component features.
If the selected IDF for the method is both 3D and Consolidated, the View Item in Drawing method is available for both design components.
If the method is not associated with any specific design component, it is only available in the Consolidated design component.
Forms
As you create or update a form, you can select the design component you want the form to be related to by setting the integration data filter. This configures the SPFFormDesignComponent relationship definition between the form and a
design component, so that the form is only displayed when you select the IDF for that design component in scopes.
If a Class Definition is linked to a form that has a relationship with a design component, the form is retrieved if the selected design component matches.
If a Class Definition is linked to a form that is not associated with any design component, the form is retrieved for the default Consolidated design component.
In cases where both Class Definition and Classification have forms, the most specialized form linked to the classification is selected. The filtering for the design component is unchanged.
If you selected the data filter as Consolidated, the To Do List displays both Non-SIO tasks and Consolidated tasks through the relationship between the form's class definition to the selected design component.
If you select another data filter different from Consolidated, the To Do List icon is hidden in the sidebar.
The iHub option allows you to view data from the Consolidated domain by default.
The Consolidated option allows you to view all design component data from the Consolidated domain, which includes information from all authoring tools registered with HxGN SDx2 that have shared design components.
While other authoring tools or design components are supported, only those that are registered with HxGN SDx2 and assigned a tool signature will appear in this list.
As you create or update any delivered or custom form, such as for methods or entry points, you can select an authoring tool in those forms that you require a relationship with a specific design component. For more information, see
Relate design components to forms.
Change your roles
 Your roles are not set until you select your query scope configuration.
1. Select Role in the header bar to open the Settings page.
 You can also select Settings 
 in the sidebar to open the Settings page to select your roles.
2. Select 
 and choose the roles you want to use to access data from the Roles list.
What happens when I select all?
When Select All is applied to the Roles list, all your role permissions are applied at the same time.
If you have been assigned a contractor role and the Select All option is selected, some features are hidden and unavailable to you even if another one of your roles has the necessary permissions to access the features because the
limited access permissions for contractor role overrides the other role permissions. To access the features you would normally have access to, clear the contractor role selection. For more information on your role assignments, contact your
system administrator.
If you select more than one configuration, the roles you select are combined in the Roles list.
Some roles may not have access to the same functionality in different configurations.
For more information on your roles and access permissions, contact your system administrator.
User preferences
Use the User Preferences section in the Settings to personalize your user settings and viewing experience.
Set the user options
Select Options 
 to customize your display and email notifications to match your workflow requirements.
Option
Details
Picklist Display
Choose how the object details in a list are displayed in the Results table by selecting Name, Description, or Both.
Nested Reference File Level
When viewing files, this limits how many levels of reference files are displayed by a master document, as the files may
also reference other files. For example, if a master document was uploaded with four levels of reference files and the
option is set to two, only the first two levels of reference files are displayed.
Show All Items On Update References Dialog
You can use this option to choose the display of files in the Update Reference Relationships window based on their
relationship with the master document. By default, the toggle is turned off, to show only the files with out-of-date
reference relationships with the master document in the Update Reference Relationships window.
You can turn on the toggle to show all files for a document with reference relationships such as Always Update,
Prompt Update and Always Update Working Revisions. They are displayed in the Update Reference
Relationships window as active or inactive depending on their reference relationships with the master document.
Format type for email digest
Select the format for the email digest to be HTML or Text.
 Select HTML to switch on any links in the emails in the email digest.
Email digest duration in hours
Set the number of hours you want to receive notifications in an email digest. For example, setting 4 means an email
digest message is received every 4 hours - the digest combines all notifications from the previous 4 hours.
Notification type
Choose the type of notification you want to receive, Notify, Email, or Notify and Email.
Notify indicates messages appear as a software notification.
Email indicates messages are delivered using your email.
Group email per project
Indicates that notifications included in an email digest message should be grouped according to the project with which
they are associated.
Info Map node type display name
Sets the object type to show the relationship of an Info Map satellite node with the main node. Choose how the node
type is displayed by selecting Class display name or Relationship display name.
Use dashboard as default home page
Uses your available home screen dashboards as your default home page instead of using feature sets.
Open Info Map in a new browser tab
Indicates that the Info Map is displayed in a new browser tab when it is opened from an Impact Analysis, Conflict
Resolution, Data Book Management, or Communication Management page.
When possible, open to markups view first
View and Markup preferences are designed to view first and then markup as required. By default, the button is
switched OFF allowing you to view first.
You can switch ON the button to open to the markup option first when possible.
For more information about what file formats are supported or how to markup your files, see Visualizing your data.
Reassign your tasks
Select Task Reassignment 
 to reassign your workflow tasks to a deputy when you are out of the office for a designated period. You can select individual projects and choose the roles and assignees to manage the tasks.
For more information, see Reassign work to a different user.
Resize your menu panes
You can resize your menu panes by dragging or double-clicking the menu sizing handle  to alternate between the maximum and minimum pane size.
 Not all menu panes can be resized.
Setting your language
You can access HxGN SDx2 in any of the supported languages you prefer.
 This feature is now available on request. Please submit a support case with details, including your preferred language or languages and the specific tenant or tenants for which you want this feature enabled.
What are the supported languages?
English
Chinese
Dutch
German
Japanese
Korean
Portuguese
Spanish
Select [User Name] > Languages 
, choose your preferred language from the available drop-down list.
By default, English is set as the preferred language.
The language choice sets the default language used on the pages of the application. This includes all text that forms part of the interface, for example option names, box labels, and error messages.
After logging out and logging in again, the application will remember your choice and retains the selected language as default for the subsequent sessions.
This feature generates output using the software's existing automatic translation capability. If you believe the translation quality could be improved, please work with your regional representatives to address any issues.
Editing object data
To edit object details in the results grid, select Actions 
 > List Editing. For more information, see Using List Edit.
Why can't I edit data in a column?
The columns you see and the order they are displayed is determined by the access to the object's column set. The object values displayed in columns in the List Edit window can be configured to be viewable, editable, mandatory, or read-
only. Some objects may be configured to be only viewable by a specific role but may be editable by another role. You might not have access to columns due to the column set access set for your role.
 For more information on your role's access to editing values in columns, contact your system administrator.
Using an InsightView dashboard
An InsightView dashboard displays real-time data from various sources (including third-party data sources) in a single page. The data can be related to a selected object, allowing you to easily visualize plant information relevant to that
object. There are three types of InsightView dashboard:
Dashboards
These display data related to a selected object, allowing you to easily visualize plant information relevant to that object. For example, a dashboard can be opened from a tag's Actions 
 menu and can display information related to
that tag.
Home Screens
These can be used as a user's default home page instead of the feature sets, providing users with easy access to their tasks and a role-based view of their assets and critical information. They can also be displayed by selecting 
.
Context Panels
These display data related to a selected object in a split screen view. For example, when you select an object in a Results page, a context panel can open alongside the results and display its properties and other widget data.
An InsightView dashboard contains several smaller windows (known as widgets), each of which can display data from a different data source. For example, a dashboard could be configured to show tag, asset, and model information, and
recent change requests (from HxGN SDx2), and active work permits (from an external permit to work system such as j5) each in a separate widget.
 Your administrator will create various dashboards as required by your organization. The Context Panel dashboards are automatically enabled for all users. If you require a specific dashboard, please contact your system
administrator.
Learn more about an InsightView dashboard
The following is an example of an InsightView dashboard where the dashboard is shown in a pop-up window. It contains three types of widget. All available widget types are explained below.
 A dashboard can also be configured to open as a page rather than as a pop-up window.
What types of widget are available?
Widget type
Description
IFrame (1)    
An iframe widget displays another web page.
Form (2)    
A form widget displays returned data in a form. In the example above, the form widget shows details of the selected
tag.
Grid (3)    
A grid widget displays returned data in columns similar to the Results page. In the above example, the grid widget
shows work orders related to the tag.
Chart
A chart widget displays returned data in a chart. A chart can be configured so that clicking on a section of the chart
displays the related data in a grid in a second page.
Count
A count widget displays a count of the number of results returned, with the details in a grid in a second page.
Property Inspector
A property inspector widget displays the names and values of the properties associated with a selected object. It
shows all populated properties for the object apart from DomainUID, SDxByCustomer, OBID, UniqueKey, UID,
ClassDef, and LastUpdatedDate.
Spotlight
A spotlight widget displays one item of returned data scaled to fit in the widget.
Tag Existence
A tag existence widget displays the Tag Existence Dashboard in a widget. For more information on the Tag
Existence Dashboard, see View the Tag Existence Dashboard.
To Do List
A To Do List widget displays a summary of your To Do List. You can switch between displaying tasks by
configuration or object type, at the bottom of the widget. For more information, see Using a To Do List widget.
3D Quick Access
A 3D quick access widget displays a list of the filters that you can access. Click > View and Markup next to a filter
to open the related 3D model.
In a grid widget, you can browse through the pages of results in the same way as in a results page. In a small widget, click on 
, to access the paging controls.
In grid and count widgets, the Actions 
 menu is available for returned objects if the widget is displayed in a full page dashboard (rather than a pop-up window), and if configured by your administrator.
In a form widget, you can change the display to show the data in a list or in a tile. You can also sort and filter the data displayed.
Set a home screen dashboard as your home page
Home screen dashboards can be displayed as your default home page instead of the feature sets. This can provide you with a role-based view of your assets and critical information, together with easy access to your day-to-day tasks.
To use home screen dashboards as your default home page, select the Use dashboard as default home page option in your user options. For more information, see Set the user options.
When enabled, click on Home 
 to display the home screen dashboards that you can access. You can scroll through your available home screens, or search for a specific home screen, using the controls at the bottom of the page.
 On a tablet, you can also scroll through your available home screens by swiping left or right.
Can I access the feature sets if a dashboard is my default home page?
Yes. Click on 
 to display the feature set home page when your default home page is set to use home screen dashboards. This does not affect your default home page, which can be accessed by clicking on 
.
Can I access home screen dashboards without setting them as my home page?
Yes. Click on 
 to display your available home screen dashboards when they aren't set as your default home page. This does not affect your default home page, which can be accessed by clicking on 
.
Working with widgets
When a widget is displayed in a home screen, its default scope will be your create scope. If it is displayed in a dashboard, its default scope will be the selected object's scope. The default scope may be overridden by the administrator.
Some widgets are configured to show data for a specified scope which you can then change. To change this scope, click 
 at the top of the widget, and select the scope required.
 If the Query by selected configurations only setting is set to False, when you select a project or sub-configuration that exists under a plant, the query returns objects found in both the selected item and the scope above. For
example, if you select Project 2 of PlantA, then the widget displays data for both Project 2 and PlantA. If the Query by selected configurations only setting is set to True, when you select a project or sub-configuration that exists under
a plant, the query returns only objects found in the selected item.
Some widgets can be related to another widget on the dashboard and may not display data until a selection is made in the other widget. These are indicated by 
 in the bottom right of the widget. Select an object in the linked widget to
display related information. When you select a different object in the linked widget, the related information is updated.
Data displayed in a grid widget can be filtered in the same way as a results grid. For more information, see Filter or sort the results. If the widget is connecting to a remote system, the remote system may require the filter to be case-
sensitive.
You cannot use Is equal to or Is not equal to to filter a column containing both date and time information.
The number of objects displayed in each page of a widget grid may differ from the number of objects displayed in each page of the results grid depending on how it has been configured by your administrator.
Hover over the widget to display a popup menu:
Select 
 to expand the widget. Select 
 to return it to the original size.
You can also double-click the widget title to expand and collapse it. On an Apple iPad, you can double-tap to expand or collapse the widget and to zoom in.
Select 
 to reload the data in the widget.
Click 
 to open the iframe widget in a new window.
 On a tablet, the above items are only available when you click 
.
How do I view the status of a widget?
When viewing the dashboard, either from the home screen dashboard or from the Actions 
 menu of the related object, select 
 to see the status of a widget.
If any of the widgets do not display correctly, possible solutions can be found in InsightView dashboard issues.
You can also preview a widget when creating or updating a dashboard. To do this, hover over the widget and select 
.
The status details returned depend on the type of widget, and can include the entries shown in the following table.
Name
Displays
API
The Web API used to access the data.
Timer
The time taken to return the results.
Records
The number of items returned for the widget.
Status
Whether or not the widget successfully returned data.
Query
The selected scope for the widget.
Query by selected configurations only
Whether this option is set to restrict the scope to just that selected. For more information, see Query by selected configurations only.
Error
An explanation for the error, if one occurred.
Information on why the widget may not display its contents correctly can be found in the troubleshooting topic Widget content is not
displayed.
 indicates that an error has occurred.
A user name and password might be required to log on to an external web page in an iframe widget. If the widget contents don't display, and you aren't prompted for these details, select 
 to log on to the external web page in a new
window.
Select 
 to expand the widget. Select 
 to return it to the original size.
You can also double-click the widget title to expand and collapse it. On an Apple iPad, you can double-tap to expand or collapse the widget and to zoom in.
Select 
 to reload the data in the widget.
Why has my widget been disabled?
A widget is disabled if, the last time the dashboard was displayed, the widget request either took too long to return the results or returned too many results. The amount of time, and number of results allowed, are configured by your
administrator.
You can manually enable the widget by clicking 
. However, if the widget continues to run slowly, it will be disabled again, and you should ask your administrator to look into the issue.
Using a To Do List widget
The To Do List widget displays a summary of your To Do List which contains all of the workflow objects assigned to you or your role which have tasks that you need to action or complete. For more information on the To Do List, see Using
the To Do List.
In the To Do List widget, your tasks are displayed by either configuration or object type. You can switch between these views at the bottom of the widget.
 The To Do List widget ignores the Query by selected configurations only setting. However, it does respect your selected life cycle Status Filters. For more information, see Working with scopes.
Click on an area in the To Do List chart to open the selected items in the To Do List.
 Only tasks that can be carried out by your currently selected roles are displayed in the To Do List. Therefore, if your To Do List appears empty, or has fewer tasks than shown in your To Do List widget, change your selected roles as
described in Change your roles.
To filter the tasks included in the widget, use the following buttons:
Select this
To include
Tasks that are available to you or your role
Tasks previously claimed by you or explicitly assigned to you for action
Tasks that are overdue and require your immediate action or completion
Tasks that you are eligible to action, but which you must explicitly claim first. These are tasks assigned to a role or
group for which only a subset of people need to take action
Using a Context Panel dashboard
A Context Panel dashboard displays the properties and widgets when a specific object is selected in a Results grid, the Info Map, or the Details page. To view an object Context Panel:
Select an object, and click anywhere on the selected objects row or on an Info Map node to expand the related Context Panel.
Context Panels are configured for specific object types and are automatically enabled for all users. For more information, contact your system administrator.
The Context Panel always appears collapsed by default and displays when an object is selected.
Multiple dashboards with different items and formats can be supported on the Context Panel carousel.
If you select another object in a row, the Context Panel remains open and the panel details are updated with the new object information.
Using the Pinboard
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Using the Pinboard <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6617ed65cd6838249b9283f1>
You can bookmark your favorite items by adding them as pins to your Pinboard, where they can be managed and recalled in pins lists for future use.
You access your pinned items by clicking Pins 
 in the sidebar to open the Pins menu, where all your saved pins are displayed under two columns:
New Items - where any newly created items are displayed. These must be added to a pins list in your Pinboard to be saved for future use, as new items are only available for a single session.
Pinboard - where any pinned items are saved to a pins list. The pinned items can be easily managed in pins lists and are saved for future use in the specified configuration.
 If a pinned item has properties that contain a URL, selecting the property opens the URL in a new browser window.
Can I save my pins and manage them in lists?
Yes you can save your items by saving them to a pins list so they can be used anytime in the future. You can also modify the lists you create and copy or move saved items between different lists. For more information, see Modify the pins
lists and Move or copy pins.
What happens if you create new items?
Items newly created in HxGN SDx2 are automatically added to the New Items column in the Pins menu. These can then be added to any list in the Pinboard so they are saved for future use, as new items are only available for a single
session.
Can I create objects directly from a saved pin?
Yes you can create new objects directly related to your selected pinned item using the appropriate create form for the object type. For more information, see Create an object related to a pin.
Adding items to your Pinboard
You can add items to a pins list in your Pinboard in the following ways:
Hover over an item in a results grid, select Pin 
 from the shortcut menu ribbon. The item is added to the pins list selected.
Select items in the results grid, and click the Pin to 
 command to add the pins to a list in the 
 drop-down menu.
Select the items, click Actions 
 > Pin 
 to add the pins to a list in the 
 drop-down menu.
Can you add a pin from an image or drawing?
You can add a pin to the Pinboard directly from an object's open image or drawing file:
Select Pin 
 on the item's hotspot bubble to add the item to a list in the 
 drop-down menu.
 You can only unpin item from a list using the Pins menu unpin action. See Pins menu shortcut icons.
Group your pins in lists
All your pinned items are saved in to a pins list so they can be managed in groups and saved for future use. Each pins list is displayed in the list 
 drop-down menu. You can add, modify, copy , and move your pins in each list. For more
information, see Pins menu shortcut icons.
 You can create a new pins list by selecting NEW LIST in the list 
 drop-down menu.
Every pins list is displayed in alphabetical order and under tabs for each configuration to which you have access.
If a pins list does not exist in your system, your pins are saved to the Default List. When you first use the Pinboard, any pinned items are added to the default list until you create and select a new pins list.
Pins menu shortcut icons
The Pins menu displays the following action shortcut icons in a ribbon when you select pins.
Icon
Action
Description
View In Grid
Displays the pinned items in a new results grid.
Unpin
Unpins the items from the selected pins list.
Move or copy selected pins
Moves or copies the selected pinned items to another target list.
Create an object from the selected pins
Creates a new object related to the pinned items type, such as a change request.
 Unpinned items are only removed from the selected pins list, and remain in other lists if present.
Pinned item actions
Additional action shortcut icons display when you select a single pinned item in a list, and click More Actions .
Icon
Action
Description
UNPIN
Unpins the item from the selected pins list.
ACTIONS
Opens the Actions menu for the pinned item.
SHOW INFO MAP
Displays the pinned item's relationships as clear, organized user-focused information.
SHOW THE DETAIL FORM
Displays the Object Details form for the pinned item showing the current value of its properties.
MOVE OR COPY PIN
Moves or copies the selected pinned item to another target list.
Modify the pins lists
You can modify a pins list in the Pinboard:
Select the pins list from the 
 list drop-down menu in the Pinboard, and click MODIFY LISTS to add, rename, or delete a pins list.
 The Default List cannot be modified.
Move or copy pins
You can easily move or copy selected pins to another pins list.
1. Select the Move or copy selected pins 
 command.
2. Select the pins list from the 
 drop-down menu to which you want to add the items.
 You can also create a new pins list in the current configuration to which you can move or copy pins by selecting Enter a name for the new list.
You can use the following options:
Retain pins in current list - the selected pins are left in the current pins list and copied to the target pins list.
Open target list after move - the target pins list opens so you can verify that the pins have been moved or copied.
 You can only move or copy pins to a target list in a configuration related to the selected create scope.
Create an object related to a pin
You can create new objects related to selected pinned items by clicking Create an object from the selected pins 
in the Pins menu ribbon. The create method applicable to the object type selected creates a new object related to the
pinned item using the appropriate create form, such as a change request.
 If multiple pins are selected, the list of create methods represents only those that apply to all the items. If only one create method matches the pinned items type, it is the default.
Managing documents and files
HxGN SDx2 provides you with the following document management features:
Document revision and version control
Life cycle control including revise, check out, check in, and sign off
Document, file, revision, and version user access and security
Create, update, and copy documents
Send documents through workflows
Comment on documents and related files
Generate PDF copies
Upload and download of documents and files
Attach, edit, replace, and manage files
 All document management features are configurable and can be edited or hidden depending on your own required documentation life cycle.
Working with documents
You work and manage documents in the document life cycle using actions which are configured out of the box on documents for basic functionality.
How do you use the different document commands?
The following table shows the different document commands and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision schema.
Action
What it does
Revise
The CURRENT document is revised, which creates a new WORKING version.
Edit Files
A new WORKING document version is created and marked as checked out. Files attached to the previous working version can be downloaded to a
client device for editing. You must manually check in the new document version after editing.
Check In
Check in the new document version, created when the Edit Files command was used, along with any files that were checked out for editing.
Sign Off
Signs off a complete document and changes the status of the document revision from WORKING to CURRENT.
Sign off with form
Signs off a complete document revision and allows you to update properties and revision notes. The document revision status changes from WORKING
to CURRENT.
Update Document
Update the master properties of the document.
Update Latest Revision
Update the properties of the latest revision of the document.
Copy
Creates a new copy of the document with a new name, and allows you to select the required property details and values on the Copy form.
Document Controller role only
Action
What it does
Undo Sign Off
Changes the status of the document revision from CURRENT to WORKING.
Cancel
Cancels a document when it is in the RESERVED state.
Documentation life cycle
All revisable documents follow a controlled document life cycle using a revision scheme that includes current revisions, working revisions, versions, and their attached files.
What is the revision scheme?
A revision scheme is a sequence of major and minor revisions, such as A1 and B1, that are used while creating or revising a document to keep track of its progress as it undergoes change. This, in turn, also reflects any changes made to
attached files that the document manages. All revisable documents in the revision scheme are composed of document masters, revisions, and versions along with any attached files.
Document master
A document master represents the top revision-independent information, such as the document title.
Document revision
A document revision contains revision-specific information. As a document is created and revised, new revisions are created and attached to the document master. In a single configuration, such as a plant or a project, there can be only
one current revision and one working revision at one time.
Document version
A document version contains version-specific information. A version of a document can be made for each project in a plant. Files are attached to the document version.
What is the document life cycle?
The standard documentation life cycle has stages, such as create, sign off, revise, check out, check in, and sign off. There can be some variation of this depending on the number of revisions and versions needed and which edit command
you use, such as Edit Files or Replace Files.
The revise, modify, and sign off process can be continually repeated.
New documents are brought into the system as Working versions.
How do I view all revisions and versions of a document?
Show all revisions of a document
The Show All Revisions command allows you to display all revisions of a selected document in a results grid. This command is available in the Actions menu of a document.
On a document's Actions menu, select Master Relationships > Show All Revisions.
 If a collaborating organization user selects a document created by an originating organization user, Show All Revisions displays the signed off current revisions of the selected document by default.
Show all versions of a document
The Show All Versions command allows you to display all versions of a selected document revision in a results grid. If you use this command from the current revision of the document, then all the versions associated with the current
revision are returned. However, if you navigate to a superseded revision, the command returns all the versions associated with that revision.
On a document's Actions menu, select Revision Relationships > Show All Versions.
 If a collaborating organization user selects a document created by an originating organization user, Show All Versions displays the latest signed off current versions of the selected document by default.
What do the different document icons mean?
The document revision state is indicated by its icon.
Icon
Document Revision State
Notes
Current revision
The Current revision. The working
revision is signed off. No claim state.
Current revision superseded
A Current revision has been revised for
further editing. No claim state.
Current revision result of a claim
The Current revision is the result of a
claim from a higher configuration.
Current revision source of a claim
The current revision is the source of a
claim to a lower configuration.
Current revision result of a claim and source of a claim
The Current revision has been claimed
from a higher configuration and is the
source of a claim to a lower
configuration. Result of a three tier-
hierarchy scenario.
Superseded revision
The revision that was previously signed
off as the Current revision, but has been
superseded by another signed off
revision. No claim state.
Superseded revision source of a claim
The revision is the source of a claim to a
lower configuration and superseded by a
newer Current revision.
Working revision/version
The working revision or version. Either a
previous Current revision has been
revised to create a new working revision
and version, or this is the first revision of
the document. No claim state.
Working version superseded
The previous working version has been
superseded by a check in. No claim
state.
Working version checked out
A new working version has been
checked out for editing. No claim state.
Working version checked out superseded
The new working version checked out
for editing has been superseded. No
claim state.
Working version result of a claim
A new working version is the result of a
claim from a higher configuration by
revising the Current revision.
Working revision source of a claim
The working version that is the source of
a claim to a lower configuration.
Submitted revision
A submitted revision is created when you
submit a document using an MDR
deliverable submittal with the Initial
Submittal Review flag enabled on the
project.
Rejected submitted revision
The rejected submitted revision is
created when a user with a document
controller role rejects an incorrect
submitted revision.
 These documents are not
visible in a normal search and can only
be found in the ToDo list or with
relationship expansion.
Superseded rejected submitted revision
The superseded rejected submitted
revision is created when you reject a
submitted revision and submit a new
version. The earlier version is
superseded.
Planned revision
Using an MDR planning submittal, you
can create a placeholder document.
Such a document is reserved and has
full document representation (master,
revision, and version), but does not have
a file attached. A typical revision is ‘—’.
Superseded planned revision
The planned revision has been
superseded by the submission of the
actual document. The document is
revised to a normal revision scheme
from a planned revision scheme.
The following description and image details how a document progresses through the Document Life Cycle:
You create a new document from a master template as a new Working Revision. After sign off, the document is locked and set as the Current Revision (A1).
The Current Revision (A1) is revised to create a new Working Revision (B1, Version 1), so you can edit the files in a document. Only working revisions can be edited.
The Working Revision (B1, Version 1) is checked out to edit files, which creates Working Revision (B1, Version 2). Any requested attached files are downloaded to your local machine for editing.
When you finish making edits to the Working Revision (B1, Version 2) or any later version, and it is complete, you can sign off the Working Revision to create a new Current Revision (B1), which is locked to prevent any further
change.
The document life cycle revision process in the image above is summarized by the following:
Create
A new document is created from a master and enters the system as the WORKING REVISION A1.
Sign off
Files are attached to the document and ready to be issued and locked, sign off the revision to create a CURRENT
REVISION A1.
Current Revision
The status of the revision that was signed off is changed to CURRENT, and the document is considered Issued.
Revise
The CURRENT REVISION A1 is revised, which creates a new WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 1.
The CURRENT REVISION A1 is still the Current revision and the most recently approved and issued revision, but it is
marked as undergoing change.
Edit Files
The Edit Files command is used, which creates WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2, and the attached files are
downloaded to a client device for editing.
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 1 marked as undergoing change.
Check In
The document's attached files are uploaded and checked in as a new WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2.
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 1 is set to SUPERSEDED.
Replace Files
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2 is updated using the Replace Files command, which replaces existing
files with files of the same name. This creates a new WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 3.
The WORKING REVISION B1, VERSION 2 is set to SUPERSEDED.
Sign Off
The WORKING VERSION B1, VERSION 3 is signed off and is set as the new CURRENT REVISION B1.
The previous existing CURRENT REVISION A1 is set to SUPERSEDED.
The example shown is for demonstration of the overall process only. This process can vary depending on your system, the revision scheme, access permissions, and the commands used as shown.
Each time a working revision document is edited, a new version is created and the previous version is set as SUPERSEDED. However, all superseded revisions and their versions are retained and can be viewed in the system.
Create a document
1. In the Documents feature set, select Create Document.
 You select the document type to create depending on the configured method.
2. On the Create document form, enter the details and values required for the document in the sections and tabs available.
When assigning classification to the object, you can use the Classification search bar to search and filter for the appropriate classifications based on their Name and Description.
If your system is configured to use ENS, you can create multiple documents with ENS using the create or copy forms, and review them in the Summary pane. For more information, see ENS preview summary.
 You can also assign the collaborating organizations from the Collaboration tab. This option is not available for Administration document classifications.
3. In New Files pane,select the files you want to attach to the new document from the tabs.
In the LOCAL FILE SYSTEM tab, select CHOOSE FILES or drop files onto the area from a selected location.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 10GB. The application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these
limitations.
If the file being attached reference other files and they are either already in the system attached to other documents or also selected to be attached here, the software automatically creates a relationship between the master file
and the referenced files along with the relationship between the master file and the document version. For more information, see Working with reference files and relationships.
4. Click FINISH, and the document is created as a working version ready for sign off.
If the create document method has been configured to display the TEMPLATE DOCUMENTS tab.
Select the correct template document for the document classification type that have a set of predefined files. These files appear in the Template Files pane.
You can see the revision, revision state, check out, checked in, and version of the template document so you can choose the correct template document.
 The Rename template files with document name option allows the renaming of template files with the new document suffix. If ENS is enabled, this option stays selected when you create more than one document.
You can select only a single owning discipline to relate it to a document. The primary or owning disciplines are mainly used in the security rules or the document review matrix. The Discipline box displays the disciplines a user or a
role is related to based on the following scenarios:
If a user or a role is not linked to any discipline, all the disciplines related to the current configuration are displayed in the box.
If a user or a role is linked to a few disciplines, all these disciplines are displayed in the box.
If a user or a role is linked to a single discipline, that discipline is displayed and automatically selected in the box.
You can select multiple secondary disciplines to relate them to a document. Secondary disciplines (or review disciplines) are mainly used in the review assignment rules, where multiple disciplines can review a document. The
Secondary disciplines box displays all the disciplines related to the current configuration. If the current configuration has no selected disciplines, then the selected disciplines of the higher configuration is displayed in the box.
 The owning and secondary disciplines are not dependent. When applying the secondary disciplines for review, the owning discipline is not automatically added to the list of secondary disciplines.
Copy a document
 The Copy Document command does not copy any files attached to the original document. Files must be added to the new document using the Manage Files or Replace Files commands. This is by design to avoid file
duplication in the system.
You can copy any document master or revision. This copies the original document details, type, and values to create a new document.
1. Select a document, and select Actions 
 > Copy Document.
2. On the Copy form, and give the document a unique name. Asterisks (*) indicate required information.
 The new document cannot have the same name as the original document. If you use ENS, the system will apply the next available number as per your ENS settings.
3. Select the revision scheme and addition information values as required.
 You can also use the Copy Revision command, which copies the document and revision scheme, but allows you to edit the revision scheme as required.
Revise a document
 To revise a document in the current scope configuration, the document must be signed off to set the document revision state to CURRENT.
To make any edits to a document, you must revise the document to create a new WORKING version in the project.
1. Select a current revision document to revise and select Actions 
 > Edit > Revise.
2. If required, type a new title, revision scheme, or issue purpose.
3. If required, select CHOOSE FILES, and select additional files to attach to the new working version.
 If you want to bring markups related to the previous version of the attached files forward and include them on the new working version, select Copy Markups.
4. Select REVISE.
 To attach a file that references other files, you must ensure that the nested reference file level is less than or equal to the maximum number of nested reference files allowed as set in User Preferences. For more
information, see Set the user options.
 If the document to be revised has attached to it a master file that is associated with out-of-date reference files, after clicking REVISE, an Update Reference Relationships window appears. In the window, you can update the
reference relationship of the attached reference files. For more information, see Update reference relationships.
If a master file references a file that is attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching the appropriate reference file in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see
Attach master file which references other files.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
When you revise a document, the revision scheme creates the document as the next major revision by default. However, you can change the revision scheme so that it revises a document to the next minor revision. For more
information, see parameter 12 in the Revise client API.
When revising a document at the plant level, existing document relationships can be auto-inherited and assigned to the new document revision. Your administrator can configure this behavior. For more information, see Configure
Revise method to inherit existing document relationships to the new document revision.
Revise multiple documents
1. Select multiple documents that you want to revise, and select Actions 
 > Edit > Revise.
The Bulk Revise page displays a list of selected documents which are valid or invalid for revise.
The documents selected for revise must be signed off.
The documents valid for revise are listed in the Valid Documents for Revise section.
If documents are not valid for revise, a validation error message is displayed in the Invalid documents for revise section.
 You can choose to either proceed with only the revisable documents, or cancel the operation to address the validation errors.
2. Select Revise.
 Revise notifications are created for each document in the Notifications 
 list. You can check the status of the revise process by refreshing the revise notification.
 When you revise multiple documents simultaneously, the next major revision is created for all revised documents.
See the Bulk Revise page
Edit and check in a document
When you edit a WORKING version document, it is checked out and set as reserved for edit, and then downloaded to your device for editing. After a document has been edited, you must check in the document as a new WORKING
version.
1. Select Actions 
 > Edit Files.
2. On the Select files to download page, select the file or files you want to edit, and select Save.
 Depending on the browser used, you can either open the document directly or locate it saved in the download location on your device.
3. Edit the files locally.
4. To check in the updated files, select the document, and select Actions 
 > Check In.
5. On the Select Document(s) to Check In window, select a master document you want to check in from the list of documents displayed, you cannot select its reference documents as the software displays them in the view mode.
6. Select Choose Files to locate the files to be checked in, and select FINISH.
You must select the files from the files displayed in the Files that are being edited section
To check in a document by attaching a file that references other files, including their nested reference files, you must ensure that the nested reference file level is less than or equal to the maximum number of nested reference
files allowed. For more information, see Set the user options.
The modified document must be uploaded with its original name. You can see the check in details in Object Details > History tab, which lists the object's history details, such as the original name or creation date.
When checking in a document and a master file with its referenced files:
If a file is attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
For files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
When you edit a document, a new WORKING version of the document is created based on the revision scheme. The previous document version is set to Superseded but is kept for history.
Edit Files allows you to download master files and copy files referenced by it, along with their nested reference files. As an administrator, you can prevent the copying of nested reference files by configuring methods of client API
EditFiles.
Replace the files for a document
You can attach and replace files that are attached to a document in the Working revision state.
1. Select a document to revise, and select Actions 
 > Replace Files.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Select CHOOSE FILES to select the files to attach to the new revision.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 10GB. The application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these
limitations.
The file names being attached need to exactly match the name of the files being replaced.
If the new master file references other files, the reference files along with master file are attached to the document.
When replacing files for a document, a Reference Files Details window is displayed in the following scenarios:
If a master file references a file that is attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching the appropriate reference file. For more information, see Attach master file which references
other files and and Working with reference files and relationships.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the system, the missing files are shown in the Reference Files Details window. In such cases, the file upload processing continues or aborts, but you can change the default
behavior of file attach by adjusting the respective method parameters. For more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
The following image shows an example of the Reference Files Details window which displays the duplicate and missing reference files information.
Show Reference Files Details window
Sign off a document
When edits to a document are complete, you sign off the working revision, which sets the document as the CURRENT released revision and prevents users from creating further working versions for that revision.
What happens when I sign off a document?
Signing off a document finalizes it:
The status for the revision is set to CURRENT. Any previous released revisions are SUPERSEDED.
The document is set to be the current released version and is made official.
The status of the document master is changed to ISSUED.
Document revisions that have been signed off and current are frozen and cannot be checked out.
Signing off a document in a project does not update the as-built revision status to SUPERSEDED.
1. Select the document revision to sign off.
2. Select Actions 
 > Sign Off to display the Select Document(s) to Sign Off window.
3. If the document revision to be signed off is a:
Master document, then the Select Document(s) to Sign Off window displays the list of master documents along with their reference documents. You can select a document revision from the list of documents to sign off, but you
cannot select its reference documents as the software displays them in the view mode.
Reference document, then the Select Document(s) to Sign Off window displays the document revision.
4. Select OK.
You cannot sign off a document revision with attached reference files in the SUPERSEDED or WORKING revision state. However, you can proceed with the sign off, if the updated revisions of reference files associated with this
document exist, but are not associated with this document. In such scenario, you can update the reference relationships for its reference documents by following the steps given in Update reference relationships.
For a reference document, if the associated master document refers an out-of-date revision of the reference document, after signoff, an Update ReferenceBy Relationships window is displayed. You can use the window to relate the
master document with the latest revision of reference document. For more information, see Update referred by relationships.
Before you sign off a document, it must be checked in.
To make changes to a signed off document, the document must be revised.
Select Undo Sign Off to cancel signing off the document.
You can update the collaborating organizations using the Update Collaborating Organizations method available in the Actions menu. This option is not available for Administration document classifications.
Update a document
You can update the details of the document when it has a working version..
1. Select a document.
2. Select Actions 
 > Update Document.
 You can also use Update Latest Revision command.
3. On the Update form, select the document properties, and values to update. Any related items are listed in separate tabs.
You can update the collaborating organizations in the Collaboration tab on the Update form. This option is not available for Administration document classifications.
If you rename a document, the unique identifier definition (UID) or unique key definition, as well as the UIDs used in relationships, are also updated, as necessary.
How do I assign an organization to multiple documents?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple documents:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the documents to the organization.
Sign off with form
The Sign Off with Form allows you to sign off on documents using a form in which you can update properties and add revision notes.
What happens when I use sign off with form?
When you use the sign off with form for a document:
The revised document allows you to update properties and add revision notes using a form.
The document is set to be the current released revision.
The status for the latest revision is set to CURRENT and the revise state is set to blank.
The status of the document master is changed to ISSUED.
The signed off revision of the document supersedes any previously released revisions.
Document revisions that have been signed off are frozen and cannot be checked out.
Signing off a document in a project does not update the as-built revision status to SUPERSEDED.
1. Select the document to sign off, and update the properties or add revision notes as necessary.
2. Select Actions 
 > Sign Off with Form.
You can sign off multiple selected documents and any changes made are applied to all of the selected documents.
You cannot sign off a document that has been reserved for editing without checking it in first.
To make changes to a signed off document, you must revise it and create a new Working version.
Undo sign off
When you undo sign off on a document, the latest WORKING revision is reverted back to the previous WORKING revision.
 This an administration command for document controllers only.
Select Actions 
 > Undo Sign Off
Insert a historical revision
The Insert Historical Revision command allows you to insert a superseded revision in a document between the current and superseded revisions or between two superseded revisions. The revision is inserted with a date that falls before
the selected revision and after the previous revision.
 Revised documents must be signed off before they can have a historical revision inserted.
1. Select a document revision before which you want to insert a historical revision, and select Actions 
 > Insert Historical Revision.
What can you see in the Insert Historical Revision page?
In the Insert Historical Revision page, you can see the following sections:
Historical revision information - displays the revision scheme, the major revision, the minor revision, and allows you to choose the issue date before the selected revision date.
Previous revision information - displays the major revision, the minor revision, and the issue date of the SUPERSEDED revision (read only).
Selected revision information - displays the major revision, the minor revision, and the issue date of the CURRENT or SUPERSEDED revision (read only).
2. In the Insert Historical Revision > Historical revision information section, select an issue date for a revision before the creation date of the selected revision of the document, and click NEXT.
3. In the New Files page, choose the revision(s) to insert in the document, and select FINISH.
Cancel a document
You can only cancel a document when it is in the RESERVED state.
 This an administration command for document controllers only.
1. Select Actions 
 > Cancel.
2. Enter the reason for canceling the document in the Cancel Document(s) dialog box.
When you cancel a document, any steps attached to a workflow are also terminated.
You can see the details of the canceled document on the History tab of the Object Details form.
Working with files
You can work with files in the document life cycle.
