The viewing, conversion, hot spotting, printing, and redlining of files secured with Microsoft Azure Information Protection (AIP), or Information Protection in general (MIP), is not supported. You can attach, store, and manage those
protected files with HxGN SDx2.
The viewer does not support Office 95 or earlier files. You must save such files in later Office versions before working with them.
How do you use the different edit commands?
The following table shows the different edit commands and their capability, which is dependent on the object's state in the revision schema.
Action
What it does
Edit Files or Download and Edit Files
Creates a new document version. Checks out the new version, and downloads it to a client device along with copies of attached files for editing.
Check In
Checks in the files after being checked out for editing using Edit Files.
Replace Files or Revise and Replace Files
Creates a new document version, and allows you to update the document by replacing any attached files.
Manage Files
Updates the existing document version so you can manage the attached files using remove, add, or delete.
Save Target As
Downloads and saves a master file and any files referenced, including any nested reference files, to a client device.
Undo Edit Files
Removes any new version created by the check out. Sets the most previous version back to Working
 This option is available for the Document Controller role only.
Edit files
When you want to edit a file attached to a document, the file is saved to the download location on your device for editing.
1. Select Actions 
 > Edit Files.
2. On the Select files to download page, select the file you want to edit.
The download operation can vary depending on the browser used. You can open the file and select Save, or save the file directly to your local device.
The document the file is attached to is set as undergoing change and the file is set as reserved for edit.
When you edit a file attached to a document, a new WORKING version of the document is created based on the selected revision scheme.
When you cancel an edit, the files on the local device may not be automatically deleted.
Edit Files allows you to download master files and copy files referenced by it, along with their nested reference files. As an administrator, you can prevent the copying of nested reference files by configuring methods of client API
EditFiles.
 You can view the related files by selecting a document from a results grid, and then selecting Actions 
 > Show All Files.
Replace files
You can replace files in a document with files with the same name that are already attached to a WORKING version.
1. Select a WORKING revision document, and click Actions 
 > Replace Files.
2. In New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Select CHOOSE FILES, and select the files to attach to the revision.
4. Select Copy Markups to copy existing markups on to the attached files, and select FINISH.
The file names being attached must exactly match the name of the files being replaced.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 10GB. The application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these
limitations.
Replace Files creates a new WORKING version for the revision. The previous WORKING version is set to superseded.
When attaching a master file, if it references a file attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching the appropriate reference file displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For
more information, see Attach master file which references other files and Working with reference files and relationships.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
Manage files
This command allows you to manage the files on an existing WORKING version without creating a new one. You can add new files or delete existing files.
1. Select a row item, and click Actions 
 > Manage Files.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system tab.
3. Click CHOOSE FILES, select files, and click FINISH.
 You can access other local systems from which to upload files.
If you close the File Management page before files have successfully uploaded, any files still in the queue are not uploaded.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 10GB. The application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these
limitations.
When attaching a master file, if it references a file attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references
other files and Working with reference files and relationships.
If a master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Attach master file which references other files.
When downloading files that are attached to multiple documents, if more than one file has the same name in the downloaded zipped folder, the following scenarios apply:
If the duplicate file does not have any reference files, it is automatically downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the zipped folder. For example, if 2 copies of the file MyDoc.rtf attached to multiple documents are being
downloaded, then 1 copy of the file is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
If the duplicate file has reference files attached, then the duplicate file along with its reference files are downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the zipped folder. For example, if 2 copies of the Master.dgn along with Ref1.dgn and
Ref2.dgn are being downloaded, then 1 copy of the Master.dgn along with Ref1.dgn and Ref2.dgn is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
You can view the related files by selecting a document from a results grid, and then selecting Actions 
 > Show All Files.
 If you want to search a Hexagon 3D model, you must attach the files in a specific order so that the label database file (.mdb2) is automatically generated. You must attach the corresponding .xml file to the document before
attaching the 3D WebGL viewer file (.binz).
Check in files
When a document has been checked out to edit the files, the check in allows you to copy the edited files from the local system back to the server for storage.
1. Select the document version you want to check in, and click Actions 
 > Check In.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Click CHOOSE FILES, and select the files to be checked in from the list and its displayed reference documents.
You can only select a checked out document version to check in.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 10GB. The application notifies you if the number of files or upload size exceeds these
limitations.
4. Unselect Copy Markups if you do not want to attach markups on files in the previous working version to the updated files in the new working version.
5. Click FINISH.
The modified file must be uploaded with its original object name. You can see the check in details in Object Details > History tab, which lists the object's history details, such as creation date.
When checking in master files, if the files referenced is attached to multiple documents, the information about the duplicate files is displayed in the Reference Files Details window.
When checking in, if the master file references files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing reference files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window.
Undo edit files
 This is administration command for document controllers only.
If the files attached to a WORKING version are being edited, you can select Undo Edit Files to revert the version and its attached files back to the previous version iteration.
1. Select Actions 
 > Undo Edit Files.
2. Select Yes to undo the edit.
You do not see the Undo Edit Files action for any document revision with only one version, as this action is controlled by a condition in the method so the system will not delete the revision.
You can change the behavior of Undo Edit Files command by configuring the UndoEditFiles client API. For more information, see UndoEditFiles client API.
 You can view the related files by selecting a document from a results grid, and then selecting Actions 
 > Show All Files.
Revise and replace files
When a selected document is a CURRENT revision, this creates a new WORKING version of the document. You can either replace the files that are attached to the previous revision or attach new files to the new version.
1. Select a row item, and click Actions 
 > Revise and Replace Files.
2. In the New Files pane, select the file system, such as Local File System.
3. Click CHOOSE FILES, select files, and click REVISE.
When replacing a file for a document, the Reference Files Details window is displayed if:
A master file references a file attached to more than one document, you can resolve the duplicates by selecting and attaching appropriate reference file displayed in the Reference Files Details window. For more information,
see Attach master file which references other files and and Working with reference files and relationships.
A master file references the files that do not exist in the database, the names of the missing files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window. In such cases, the file upload processing continues or aborts based on the
configuration of the method for the ReplaceFiles client API. For more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
Download attached files
You can download selected files and save them to a location of your choice.
1. Select Actions 
 > Save Target As.
2. In the Select files to download page, select the files you want to download. The files are displayed in pages based on the total number of files returned.
 Non-viewable files are also displayed, which you can also download as required.
Save Target As returns all the files attached to the object independent of the default query page limit, such as the downloaded master file, any reference files, and any nested reference files. For each document, the master file and its
corresponding reference files are downloaded together in a single zipped file.
Single files without any reference files are downloaded individually without any folder structure.
The maximum number of nested reference files that can be downloaded is set in the User Preferences. For more information, see Set the user options.
If you are not prompted to select a location when saving a file, the file will be saved to your personal Downloads folder or to the default download location defined in your domain user profile.
When downloading files that are attached to multiple documents, if more than one file has the same name in the downloaded zipped folder, the following scenarios apply:
If the duplicate file does not have any reference files, it is automatically downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the zipped folder. For example, if 2 copies of the file MyDoc.rtf attached to multiple documents are being
downloaded, then 1 copy of the file is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
If the duplicate file has reference files attached, then the duplicate file along with its reference files are downloaded in the GUID folder, inside the zipped folder. For example, if 2 copies of the Master.dgn along with Ref1.dgn and
Ref2.dgn are being downloaded, then 1 copy of the Master.dgn along with Ref1.dgn and Ref2.dgn is automatically saved in the GUID folder.
Generate PDF renditions of files
 You can generate PDF renditions from one or more files attached to one or more documents. If configured, any associated reference files, markup layers, watermarks, or labels are included.
There are two ways you can generate a PDF rendition:
Select a document in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate PDF Copies.
Select a file in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate PDF Copy.
You can consolidate two or more files into a single merged PDF rendition from the results grid using the following three ways:
Select a single document with two or more attached files, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate Merged Rendition For Files.
Select two or more documents with attached files, and select Actions 
 > Files > Generate Merged Rendition For Files.
Select two or more files, and select Actions 
 > Generate Merged Rendition For Files.
Generate thumbnail images of files
You can generate thumbnail images for 2D or 3D files attached to a document by completing the following steps:
How can I generate thumbnails for a document?
 Before generating thumbnails for a document, check with your administrator to ensure that following method FileCompositionGenerateThumbnail is assigned to your username. This setting will display the Generate
Thumbnail method in the document's Actions menu.
1. Select a document and click Actions 
 > Show Files.
2. Select a file and click Actions 
 > Generate Thumbnails to generate thumbnails for the supported file types.
 To view the generated thumbnails for a file, you can click Show Thumbnails under the file's Relationship menu.
By default, you cannot generate a thumbnail for a file which already has one. However, if you are an administrator you can change the default behavior to allow the regeneration of thumbnails, by updating the GenerateThumbnail
client API.
If you are an administrator, you can enable auto generate thumbnails whenever a document is created, revised and checked in.
The thumbnails of a file are especially useful when you want to view the content of the file without opening it.
To view the status of thumbnail generation, you can view the List of Notifications.
Working with reference files and relationships
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | How to work with reference files <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/66348b14eaa969016ab22d0c>
In HxGN SDx2, certain file types, such as MicroStation files, AutoCAD files, and Word files, are called master files, and when attached to a document, can reference other files. The files referenced by the master file can be viewed and
edited separately and can reference other files, which creates a nested reference relationship.
The relationships are maintained between the master files and their referenced files using reference relationships during check-in, editing, viewing, and so on. These reference relationships are created between the master files and the
referenced files when a document is created and has files attached or when a master file is checked back in after editing. These reference relationships are created and updated if you add, edit, or delete any reference files when you edit a
master file.
When a master file is attached or checked in, the software reads the headers of all the files. The software first searches for the reference files attached to the master file and then searches for any referenced files that are attached to other
documents to create any reference relationships. If the software does not find the reference files, or finds duplicate reference files, you must resolve these issues to create the reference relationship by default.
Your administrator can change the default behavior to stop, report any issues, or continue with the file attachment and resolve any issues later. For more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files.
The following diagram shows various reference relationships between documents and files.

associated with master files of a specific type (listed along the top of the table). For some types of files, specific versions of the software or configurations are required.

Attach master file which references other files
You can create, revise, check-in, signoff or replace a file for a document with a master file that references:
an existing file in the database which is attached to more than one document.
more than one file with the same name that is attached to different documents or revisions.
When attaching the master file, a Reference Files Details window is displayed in which you can resolve the duplicates as follows:
a. In New Files pane, select a master file to attach to the document.
b. Click CHOOSE FILES, select files.
c. In the Reference Files Details window, select one or more reference files to resolve duplicates, and attach the master file.
d. Select Apply
e. Click FINISH in the New Files pane to attach the master file by resolving duplicates and creating or resolving reference relationships.
Which Microsoft Office file formats are supported for reference files?
The latest Microsoft Office file formats such as docx, docm, pptx, and pptm are supported for reference files.
In the Reference Files Details window, you can:
Click View and Markup 
 to display a reference file inside the View and Markup window.
Click the hyperlink to display the reference file details on a form.
Hover on Show the detail form 
 to display the reference file type.
View the file type of the duplicate reference files. However, the file type of the missing reference file is not shown.
If the master file and reference file are attached to the same document, the software automatically creates a reference relationship, without searching for other instances of the reference file attached to different documents. In such
cases, the Reference Files Details window is not displayed.
If a master file references other files, the number of reference file relationships levels that are resolved is limited by the Nested reference file level setting defined in the system options. For more information, see Configure the
system options.
When attaching the master file, the software resolves and attaches the files with cyclic references when the Cyclic references allowed option in the system options is set to True. For more information, see Configure the system
options.
If the master file references files that do not exist in the database, then the missing reference files are displayed in the Reference Files Details window.
If you have administrative privileges, you can configure the display of missing and duplicate reference files in the Reference Files Details window. For more information, see Configure the display of missing and duplicate reference
files.
View reference relationships
To view the reference relationships for a document
1. Select a document.
2. Select Actions 
 > Relationships.
3. On the Relationships menu, select Version Relationships > Show Latest Reference Relationships.
To view the reference relationships for a file
1. Select a document.
2. Select Actions 
 > Relationships.
3. On the Relationships menu, select Show All Files.
4. Select a file, and select Actions 
 .
5. On the Relationships menu, select Show Referred by Files to view the files referenced by the master file.
To view other documents that the reference file is attached to:
1. On the reference file, select Actions 
 > Relationships.
2. On the Relationships menu, select Show Attached To.
View nested reference documents
To view the list of nested reference documents associated with a document
1. Select a document.
2. Click Actions 
 > Reference Documents.
3. On the Reference Documents menu, select Show Nested References.
The Show Nested References window displays the referenced documents along with nested reference files and their documents in a tree view.
You can view the file type of the nested reference files in the Show Nested References window.
If you are an administrator, you can configure the maximum number of levels of the nested reference documents to be displayed. For more information, see Set the user options.
In the Show Nested References window, for any document you can:
Click this
To do this
Display the documents referred to in a tree view
Actions 
Display the Action menu for the selected document
View and Markup 
Display the attached file inside the View and Markup window
Document Hyperlink
Display the document details on a form
View nested reference files
To view the files referred to along with their nested reference files:
1. Select a file for which you want to view the reference files
2. Select Actions 
 > Show Nested Reference Files.
The Nested Reference Files window displays the following:
Referenced files along with nested reference files in a tree view.
File type of the referenced files along with nested reference files
In the Nested References Files window, for any file you can:
Select this
To do this
Display the files referred to in a tree view
Actions 
Display the Action menu for the selected file
View and Markup 
View the file inside the View and Markup window
File Hyperlink
Display the file details on a form
Update reference relationships
To update the out-of-date reference files for a document with master file, you must:
1. Select a document.
2. Click Actions 
 > Reference Documents.
3. Select Update Reference Relationships.
4. Select one or more reference files that you may want to update.
a. For each reference file, select the reference file version in the Update Reference Relationship to column. This will update the relationship with the master document.
 You can choose from the out-of-date version, the latest released version, or the latest working version of the reference file.
b. Click UPDATE to update the reference relationships for the master document.
You can update out-of-date reference files for a file as follows:
a. Select a file.
b. Click Actions 
 > Update Reference Relationships.
c. Repeat step 4 as given above.
You can customize the display of reference files in the Update Reference Relationships window based on their reference relationship with the master document by using the Show All Items On Update References Dialog option in
Options 
. For more information, see Set the user options.
For more information about the columns in the Update Reference Relationships window, see the image and its description below:
Show Update Reference Relationships window
Column Name
Description
Name
Displays the name of the master document and a list of any reference files associated with it.
Parent Revision Name
Displays the name of the parent revision.
Update Reference Relationship To
Select the version of the reference file to which you want to create a relationship for the master document.
You can choose from the out-of-date version, the latest released version, or the latest working version of
the reference file.
Out of Date Revision
Displays the name, revision, and version number of the superseded version to which the master file is
currently related.
Latest Released Revision
Displays the name of the latest revision that was released for distribution.
Latest Working Revision
Displays the name of the latest revision that has been created but was not yet released for distribution.
Reference Control
Indicates the option selected for managing the relationship between the master file and this references file.
This field is display only.
Update referred by relationships
To update the out-of-date reference relationships for the master document and relate with the latest reference document, you must:
1. Select a document.
2. Click Actions 
 > Reference Documents.
3. On the Reference Documents menu, select Update Referred by Relationships.
4. In the Update ReferenceBy Relationships window, do the following:
a. Select one or more files attached to the referred master documents that you may want to update.
b. Click UPDATE to update the reference relationship of a master document with the latest reference document.
To update the out-of-date referred by relationships for the reference files, you must:
a. Select a file.
b. Click Actions 
 > Update Referred by Relationships.
c. Repeat step 4 as given above.
 For more information about the columns in the Update ReferenceBy Relationships window, see the image and its description below:
Show Update ReferenceBy Relationships window
Column Name
Description
Name
Displays the name of the master file referred by the reference file.
Parent Name
Displays the name of the master document to which the master file is attached.
Revision
Displays the current revision of the master document.
Revision status
Displays the current revision status of the master document.
Reference Control
Indicates the option selected for managing the relationship between the master file and this references file.
Working with objects
You can work and manage objects in your projects and plants using actions configured for basic functionality.
Create and manage objects
1. In the relevant feature set, select Create [object type].
 You can also create an object and relate it to the current object by selecting Actions 
 > Create [object type]. To create and relate a comment to an object, the command is Actions 
 > New Comment.
2. On the Create object form, select the property details from the different tabs available, and type the values required that apply to the new object.
3. In Select Files, add a file to the object if required, and select Upload.
If your system is configured with Engineering Number System (ENS), you can create multiple objects and the number is incremented for each new object created. You can review the ENS objects created in the Summary pane. For
more information, see ENS preview summary.
If you create an object that is attached to a workflow, it is attached asynchronously.
Each object displays its property details under multiple tabs, such as Main Details, Structure, Source, History, and so on.
In a wizard, you can query the required object type and, when you click Finish, this object type will be related to the main object type.
Copy an object
1. Select an object, and click Actions 
 > Copy.
2. On the Copy object form, select the required property details, and values displayed and give the copy a new name.
View the object details
Select the name of an object to open the object's Details page. Any related items are listed in separate tabs.
 You can also select an object in the Results table, and click Actions 
 > Details 
.
Update an object
1. Click Actions 
 > Update.
2. On the Update object form, select the properties and values to update. Other related items are listed in separate tabs, such as version and modification information in the Source tab.
When you rename an object, the unique identifier definition (UID) or unique key definition, as well as UIDs used in relationships, are updated, as necessary.
Delete an object
What's the difference between deleting and terminating an object?
When you delete an object or relationship, it is permanently removed from the database.
When you terminate an object or relationship, the object's status is changed to Terminated, but its history is preserved in the database.
Select Actions 
 > Delete.
Terminate an object
Select Actions 
 > Terminate.
Relate the objects in a wizard
In a wizard, you can query the objects that must be related to the main object and relate using the relationship maintenance type control. When you click FINISH, the main object is created and related to the queried objects.
Create a relationship between objects
You can create relationships between objects using the Relationship Builder, which is a found in the Actions menu. You can use it to see how objects can be related to each other and help you understand how data is connected in your
project and system. For example, you can select a new equipment design document and create a relationship to existing equipment tags or select a class definition and add related classifications.
Create and edit a relationship
1. Select objects in a results grid, and click Actions 
 > Relationship Builder.
2. In the Relationship Items section, add one or more objects to relate to the selected objects from the following tabs:
Pins - shows objects previously saved as pins in your Pinboard or newly created objects in New Items.
Existing - shows objects that are already related to the selected objects.
Search - search for objects in your system using specific search criteria and Filter options.
3. Select RELATE.
You can only see items that you have access to in the Existing tab or returned in the search results.
If any security rules are applied, only related objects that match the security rules are displayed in the Existing tab.
In a search, you can use the FILTER options to help you select the search criteria. The default behavior of the search is to use the Contains operator. You can use the search operator Is Equal To to return more targeted search
results. For more information on configuring the default search operator, see Update an API Entry Point display.
Terminate an existing relationship
1. Select the object from which you want to terminate a relationship, and select Actions 
 > Relationship Builder.
2. Click the Existing tab, and select the existing related item from the list.
3. Select TERMINATE.
Managing related items for an object relationship
You can configure and manage related items for a specified object relationship using the Manage Related Items functionality. For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Managing Models and Assets
A single physical item in a facility is represented by three related objects in the database:
Tag - The physical part as designed
Model - The requisitioned item, including a model number
Asset - The physical part located in the facility, with its own serial number
In HxGN SDx2, you can:
Create asset and model documents.
Manage and view relationships between the models, and assets and the properties of each, such as specifications, manufacturer serial number, maintenance procedures, and more. For more information, see Create and edit a
relationship.
Create an asset
1. In the Tags, Assets and Models feature set, select Create Asset.
2. On the New Asset page, select an asset classification from the tree view.
3. Provide other details on the remaining tabs.
 If ENS is set up, the number is incremented for each new asset when you select APPLY.
How can I manage sections access on an asset form?
If you are a System Administrator, you can configure sections access in the FDWAsset-Form which defines who can view the section on an asset form in which it appears. For more information, see Manage sections in a form and Manage
section access groups.
Create a model
1. In the Tags, Assets and Models feature set, select Create Model.
2. On the New Model page, select the model classification from the tree view.
3. Provide other details on the remaining tabs.
 If ENS is set up, the number is incremented for each new model when you select APPLY.
Visualizing your data
This section gives you an overview of all the ways you can view, print, markup, and navigate files.
To start:
Select a document containing one or more files, a tag, or a file, and select 
 View and Markup from the shortcut icon ribbon or Actions menu.
In this section:
Viewing and navigating your files
Marking up and comparing your files
Printing your files
Quick View
Using visual intelligence to analyze your data
Visualization troubleshooting
 The view markup and control now uses PDF technology powered by Apryse™ WebViewer™ for viewing and navigating your files.

The viewing, conversion, hot spotting, printing, and redlining of files secured with Microsoft Azure Information Protection (AIP), or Information Protection in general (MIP), is not supported. You can attach, store, and manage those
protected files with HxGN SDx2.
The viewer does not support Office 95 or earlier files. You must save such files in later Office versions before working with them.
Viewing and navigating your files
Viewing and navigating your drawings and models is easy with the Graphic View Control (GVC). The GVC delivers a number of features, which are listed below.
What file types are supported in the GVC view control?
The following file types are supported in this view control:

Streaming 3D Models
-

Key Features
View and navigate 3D models and 2D drawings
Measure distances of objects in a 3D model
Move through a 3D model using Fly Mode
Create snapshots of 3D models to mark up
View SmartFrame layers in a 2D drawing
View Smart Interop Objects in a 2D drawing
Things to Keep in Mind
A hotspot displays when you select a smart object in a model.
Objects in a model can be tagged and linked to the same object in a 2D drawing using visual intelligence.
You can select an object in a drawing or in a model and view a list of related properties.
GVC requires reference files to be in the Web Client. For more information, see Working with reference files and relationships.
View an active drawing
This method is only available for documents in the RESERVED state.
1. Update your query scope filter to allow you to view the data in the Consolidated design component. For more information, see Filter data by design component.
2. Search for the drawing you want to view. For details on finding objects, see Search for objects.
3. Select Actions 
 > View Active Drawing.
View tags in an active drawing
To view a tool drawing associated with a consolidated object:
1. Update your query scope filter to allow you to view the data in the Consolidated design component. For more information, see Filter data by design component.
2. Search for the tag you want to view in a drawing. For details on finding objects, see Search for objects.
3. Select Actions 
 > View Item in Active Drawing to view the drawing associated with the tab..
 If the tag appears in drawings from more than one authoring tool, you are asked to choose which tool drawing to open.
View a 3D model using a view work area
To stream 3D models using View Work Areas:
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select View Work Areas.
2. In the Results grid, select a View Work Area.
 HxGN SDx2 primary settings delivers an All Objects Default View Work Area to display a tag in a 3D model with the View Item in Model command. The All Objects Default View Work Area will display the full model
shared into HxGN SDx2.
3. Select Actions 
 > View and Markup.
 You may experience slower performance when opening a model for the first time, or if you are refreshing a model. This is due to required processes on the server and notifications may appear to that effect. A cached copy of
the model will be stored for faster subsequent views.
Navigating Hexagon 3D file formats
The following section details how to navigate the following 3D file formats:
 The following limitations exist:
The GVC is only compatible with 64-bit systems.
Regarding Android Versions 1.2 and later:
Cannot load and display models greater than 1.5 GB.
Touch screen navigation can be limited when medium to large, dense models are loaded.
Hexagon 3D File Formats:
3D WebGL Rendition (.binz)
Smart Review (.vue)
Smart 3D (.zvf)
In this section:
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using the menu bar
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using touch gestures
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using a keyboard, keypad, and mouse
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using navigation and selection commands
Measure distances in Hexagon 3D model file formats
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using touch gestures
How do I do things like double-tap, drag, or pinch?
When using a touch screen device, movements like double-tap, drag, and pinch are referred to as gestures.
Gesture
Description
Tap
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Double-tap
Rapidly touch the screen surface twice.
Drag
Move your fingertips over the touch surface without losing contact.
Pinch
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then bring them closer together.
Press and Hold
Touch the screen for an extended period.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them apart.
 You can zoom, pan, and rotate at the same time by combining the gestures for each.
How do I select more than one object in a Hexagon 3D file format?
You can enable or disable multi selection by:
Double-tap anywhere in the model if you are using a touch-enabled device.
Double-click anywhere in the model if you are using a computer.
 Alternatively, you can press Ctrl and click the objects in the model if you are using a computer.
After you enable multi selection, you can tap or click each object you want to select. The number of objects selected is displayed in the sidebar. Only actions applicable to all of the objects are displayed.
Navigation
Gesture
Return to Home View
Double-tap with two fingers to bring back the home view of the model.
-OR-
Press and hold the Compass button.
Enter Fly mode
Tap Fly Mode 
 to enter. Your cursor changes to a crosshair located at the center of your view to
indicate that you are in Fly mode.
Exit Fly mode
Press and hold one finger, and then lift it. Your normal cursor then displays and not the crosshair.
Move forward, left, or right
Press and hold two fingers, and then drag them to move up, down, left, or right.
Move backward
As you are moving around in Fly mode, lift one finger to move backward.
Move halfway to target point
Double-tap with one finger to move the view automatically halfway to the target point. Press and hold
the SHIFT key while tapping with one finger to move back out halfway from the target point.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them apart.
Pan
Drag one fingertip from side to side or up and down to pan the view.
Rotate
Drag one finger in any direction to rotate the model view. You can move in a clockwise or counter
clockwise direction to rotate the model about the center of the main view. If you select one object, the
point of rotation is the center of the selected object. If you select multiple objects, the point of rotation
is the center range of the selected objects.
Zoom in
Place your fingertips close together around the focus area, and then spread the fingertips apart.
Zoom out
Place your fingertips apart around the focus area, and then pinch or bring the fingertips close
together.
Select one object in the model
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Select multiple objects in the model
Press CTRL on a virtual keyboard and select the objects.
-OR-
Double-tap in an area outside of the model section to start multi-selection, and then select each
object. To end multi-selection, double-tap again in an area outside the model.
 If you do not have a physical keyboard, select CTRL on the device's virtual keyboard. Use your
device controls to open the keyboard.
What can I do with Fly mode?
Here are some tips for using Fly mode:
As you move through the model, the closer you get to your target, the slower the movement rate becomes. The further away from the target, the faster the movement rate.
To exit Fly mode, press F.
Walk through your model in the direction of the crosshair. Roll the middle mouse wheel forward and back, or press and hold the designated WASD key.
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using a keyboard, keypad, and mouse
There are many keyboard, keypad, and mouse options for navigating Hexagon 3D file formats. The following sections group the options by the type of navigation movement or action.
How do I select more than one object in a Hexagon 3D file format?
You can enable or disable multi selection by:
Double-tap anywhere in the model if you are using a touch-enabled device.
Double-click anywhere in the model if you are using a computer.
 Alternatively, you can press Ctrl and click the objects in the model if you are using a computer.
After you enable multi selection, you can tap or click each object you want to select. The number of objects selected is displayed in the sidebar. Only actions applicable to all of the objects are displayed.
General options
Navigation
Action
Fly mode
F
Fit all
V
Home view
H
Redo
CTRL + Y
Undo
CTRL + Z
Open or close the Settings panel on the Menu bar
P
Expand Menu bar
M
Exit Measure and clipping
ESC
Isolate
CTRL + I
Turn measurement on and off
N
Set home
CTRL + H
Zoom Area
Z
Look options
These options change what you see just as if you were standing in the model and turning your head.
Navigation
Action
Up
I
Down
K
Left
J
Right
L
Move options
Navigation
Action
Up
E
Down
C
Left
A
Left arrow 
Right
D
Right arrow 
Around (up, down, left, and right)
Middle mouse drag
In (or forward)
W
Up arrow 
Mouse wheel forward
In and left
W + A
Mouse wheel forward + A
In and right
W + D
Mouse wheel forward + D
Out (or backward)
S
Down arrow 
Out and left
S + A
Mouse wheel backward + A
Out and right
S + D
Mouse wheel backward + D
Press and hold the key for the direction to walk.
Click the middle mouse button to move halfway in to your target point. Use SHIFT + middle mouse click to move back out the distance of the eye point from the target. The perspective angle does not change.
Press and hold the middle mouse button to pan left or right. This allows you to move the model without changing the direction in which you are looking.
Rotate options
Navigation
Action
Left
*
Multiply * on keypad
Right
/
Divide key / on keypad
Rotate
Select and hold. The model rotates around the current center point of the view.
Rotate around a selected object (orbit rotate)
Select and drag an object to rotate the model around the selected object for a 360-degree
view. However, in Fly mode, you cannot rotate around objects.
If multiple objects are selected, the point of rotation is the center of the range of the
selected objects.
 Press W and then drag your mouse in Fly mode for enhanced control and higher precision turning.
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using the menu bar
The following options are displayed on the menu bar:
 Menu
Displays a vertical menu bar containing commands and settings. Select to open and close the menu bar. You can also open and close the menu bar by pressing M.
The following options are available when you select Menu 
:
 Isolate
Hides the entire model and displays only the selected objects. When objects are isolated, you can zoom in and out, rotate, or pan.
You can also press CTRL + I.
 Environment
A 3D model environment consists of selected lighting, background, and surface settings. You can use the default preset view, select from a list of preset environments, or modify an environment as required.
The options available include:
 Presets
Provides a list of delivered preset environments with specific lighting, background, and surface (ground plane) settings.
All edits made to preset environments are not persisted.
Because changes to presets are not persisted, you can reset any Lighting, Background, and Surface edits by selecting another preset environment and then re-selecting the preset environment.
 Lighting
Simulates variations in lighting to provide a more realistic view of the model. Lighting options can show a more naturalistic view of the physical structures represented by model objects.
Model Brightness - Specifies the level of brightness in the model. For darker object colors, select a low brightness (exposure) percentage. For brighter colors, select a high brightness percentage.
Ambient Exposure - Controls the intensity of the ambient light. Also, it controls lighting intensity when the Realistic Rendering visual setting is activated. This option is activated by default.
Direction Lights - Turns on the horizontal light direction in the model and displays options for casting shadows, setting the light orientation angles, and source lighting. The following actions are available while adjusting light direction.
Shadows
Turns on and off the ability to cast shadows.
Light Rays
Displays beams of light shining across the model to simulate the effect of light passing through dusty or foggy areas. Other lighting settings such as the Intensity, Shadows, Orientation, and Sunlight affect the light ray display.
Turning off Direction Lights automatically turns off Light Rays. If you have both Sun Light or Moon Light options enabled, the software uses the sun to cast the light rays.
Lens Flare
Simulates lighting behaviors based on the way light bounces inside the lens of a camera. Like the Light Ray option, Lens Flare is visible when facing the sun. For the moon or other low light settings, Lens Flare has minimal
impact on the display. To enable the Lens Flare option, turn on Direction Lighting under the Environment > Lighting settings.
1. Open your 3D model, and then select Menu 
 > Environment > Presets 
.
2. Select the Sunset environment. The default sun location for this preset is 12:00 noon.
3. Select the Lighting tab 
, and then make sure Direction Lights is enabled.
4. Turn on Lens Flare, and then move the view to face the sun making sure the model does not block the sun.
5. You can change the location of the sun and use settings such as Model Brightness and Ambient Exposure to further customize your display.
The direction of the sun and the model elevation are settings that define the basis of the Lens Flare effect.
When you enable Lens Flare, your camera view must be facing the primary light source (sun or moon).
Make sure the model is not blocking the light source.
If you have both the sun and the moon enabled in the Lighting settings, turn the view towards the sun to get the flar effect.
Change any additional Lighting settings such as the Model Brightness and Ambient Exposure to further customize the flare effect.
Orientation
Sets the Rotation and Elevation angles for the light sources.
 Show/Hide Light
Turns on and off the selected (Moon or Sun) light source.
 Elevation/Rotation Lock
Locks both light source settings so that the angle between the sources is maintained. When unlocked, you can move each light source independently to set the angle.
Rotation
Enables you to move a light source to the required angle. The rotation angle ranges from -180° to +180°. Use the slider or enter the required rotation value. Select the circle representing the light source and drag it to change the
position.
Elevation
Specifies the elevation position in setting the direction of the light source. The elevation angle range is from -90° to +90°. Use the slider or enter the required elevation value. Select the source light circle in the vertical toolbar to
set the elevation.
Properties
Defines the source light to edit (Sun Light or Moon Light).
Show Light - Turns on and off the selected (Moon or Sun) light. You can also select the Show/Hide icon 
.
Color - Defines the color of the selected light source.
Rotation - Enables you to move a light source to the required angle. The rotation angle ranges from -180° to +180°. Use the slider or enter the required rotation value.
Elevation - Specifies the elevation position in setting the direction of the light source. The elevation angle range is from -90° to +90°. Use the slider or enter the required elevation value.
Intensity - Determines the exposure of light on the model, which changes the intensity of the model colors. Use the slider or enter a percentage value.
 Background
Select either a solid Color, Gradient, or a SkyBox image background for the display.
 Surface
Enhances the model view by providing the effect of a ground, a floor, or other surface types for better depth and spacing perspectives of model objects. You can define a solid color for a surface or select a texture image.
Visible - Turns on and off the selected surface display.
Elevation - Specifies the height of the surface plane in the model. The elevation measurement units depend on the measurement units selected for the model.
Color - Defines the color. Use the RGB sliders or enter the hexadecimal color code.
 Select
Selects graphical objects and displays the following Floating Action Bar (FAB).
FAB Option
Description
Select FAB
Expands and collapses the Select control. When Fence Select is activated, the software suspends mouse and touch
navigation.
Select (Expand/Collapse)
Displays the Select control at the bottom of the view, and automatically activates Fence Select. The software
suspends mouse and touch navigation, and default selection.
Fence Select
Turns fence selection on and off. When Fence Select is activated, the software suspends mouse and touch
navigation. Use commands in the Select control to define fence selection options.
Inside Select
Selects objects that are entirely in the fence box. This command is activated by default when Fence Select 
 is
enabled.
Overlap Select
Selects all objects entirely inside the fence box and any objects that are outside but touching the fence.
Cancel Select
Cancels and removes the current fence selection.
 Undo
Sets the view to the previous one. Use Undo to move back to the previous positions. You can also Undo by pressing CTRL + Z.
 Redo
Reverses your last view position change. You can also Redo by pressing CTRL + Y.
 Refresh
Updates the view with the latest data. Select Refresh whenever data from the source has been modified to see the latest model view.
 The 
 Refresh command reduces viewing performance since this re-runs the workspace query that creates the graphic tree structure.
 This command is only available if an administrator has configured Force Refresh. For additional information, see Force Refresh to update graphics in a Smart 3D model.
 Settings
Provides various settings to control the display and shows system information. Use the Information and Diagnostics settings to troubleshoot display problems. You can open and close the Settings panel by pressing P.
The following options are available when you select Settings 
:
Visual
Projection Mode - Select either Orthographic or Perspective model view.
Anti-Aliasing - Reduces the jagged edges of object surfaces to provide a smoother finish.
Outline - Displays outlines on all model objects using the default outline color, which is white on dark backgrounds and black on light backgrounds.
Ambient Occlusion - Provides a more realistic lighting for objects by accentuating small surface details and adding soft shadows.
Transparency - Turns transparency on and off in the model. By default, transparency is turned on.
Optimize Performance - Automatically turns off all rendering effect settings such as Outline and Anti-aliasing during navigation to provide a smoother and faster location transition. For 3D model viewing, this option is activated by
default.
Fly-To - Provides a "fly-to" camera mode in which the camera moves from one view to another instead of an instant transition. When activated, this option applies to the Fit, Home, Undo, and Redo commands.
Realistic Rendering - Applies reflectivity and textures to objects in a model to improve the visual quality.
View Cube - Displays a six-faced view cube to change the view direction of your 3D model. To change the view, select a face or corner on the view cube, or select the More button to choose the view type and direction. A selected
view is not persisted in the model. When enabled, the view cube displays only in a 3D model. The More button does not display on devices with small screens such as tablets or mobile phones.
What can I do with View Cube?
1. Load your 3D model. The model opens using its pre-defined default view.
2. Under Settings, select Visual, and then enable the View Cube option.
The view cube displays in the graphic view.
3. Select a corner on the view cube for a perspective or isometric view, or select a cube face for an orthographic view.
-OR-
Select the More button beside the view cube to select the Projection Mode and the required Planar (Orthographic) or Isometric view.
 The More button does not display on devices with small screens such as tablets or mobile phones.
4. You can turn the option on or off at any time. A selected view is not persisted in the model.
Projection Mode options within View Cube:
Orthographic
Provides an orthographic views (front, top, bottom, and side) of the 3D model.
Perspective
Displays a 3D object on a 2D surface. Objects that are further away appear smaller, and closer objects are larger.
Display Axis - Turns the display axis on and off.
Navigator - Turns on and off the navigator display.
Continuous Highlight - Highlights graphics in the view when you hover your mouse over them. The highlight color is the color of the object. This option is not enabled by default.
Information
Provides product information such as version numbers and data sources for the current model.
System Scan
Displays information such as the estimated available RAM, the number of threads in use, worker threads in use, and the maximum number of download threads.
Diagnostics - Checks your network connection and performance.
Diagnostics Mode - Turns on and off a continuous check of your network connection.
Log - Displays the network connection. You can copy and delete the display.
WebGL - Provides information that can be used by customer support for troubleshooting purposes.
Aspects
Turns on and off the display of aspects. Aspects are parameters that represent additional information needed for placement, such as safety or maintenance clearance.
 The Aspects option displays only for models containing aspects, and the aspects that display are those defined in the model file. If the file does not contain the actual model geometry for the selected aspect, the aspect does not
display.
 Home
Resets the display to the default view.
You can also press H.
 Fit
Zooms in to one or more selected objects. If you do not select any objects, Fit places all visible objects in the view.
If you have multiple items selected, this fits to the center of the selected objects.
You can also press V.
 Clip
Allows you to hide objects or portions of a model to focus on a specific area. HxGN SDx2 supports clipping and inverse clipping. Inverse clipping hides objects inside a clipping volume and shows all objects outside the clipping volume.
 Measure
Turns measurement on (expanded control) and off (collapsed control). When expanded, use control commands to create point-to-point Distance measurements or three-point Angle measurements. The measurement text displays the Δ
(delta) notation for axis values ΔX, ΔY, and ΔZ to indicate differences along each axis and see the directional breakdown of distance measurements.
How can I measure distances in Hexagon 3D model file formats
1. Select Measure 
.
2. Select Settings 
 to choose the measurement unit. You can also change the measurement unit after placing a measurement.
3. Select Angle 
 for three-point measuring. By default, the Distance measurement is enabled.
4. Select the first object or location point on the model.
5. Select the second point to see the distance measurement, or select the next two points for the angle measurement.
6. You can pause and resume measurement by selecting Place Point 
. Pause to perform other operations such as changing your view and selecting other objects. When you resume, the measurement restarts from the next point you
place.
The distance measurement text area displays the D (total distance) value with the option to expand the area to see the full measurement set (D, DX, DY, and DZ values). The Expand 
 and Collapse 
 icons display beside the D value.
When you take measurements, you cannot interactively edit any control points. Turn off measurements to modify control points.
The following actions are available while taking measurements:
Expand/Collapse
Expands or collapses the measure control. When collapsed, measurement is turned off.
Start/Stop
Turns on and off placement of measure points. After you select the Distance or Angle measurement, select the first object or location of the measurement. For Distance measurement, select the next object or location. The software
displays the measurement between the two points. For Angle measurement, select the object or location for the next two points. The software displays the angle between the three points. Select Place Point again to stop measurement.
Repeat this to start measuring at a different location in the model.
Angle
Measures the angle between three points to show the orientation of objects relative to each other.
Place Point
Turns on and off placement of measure points. After you select the Distance or Angle measurement, select the first object or location of the measurement. For Distance measurement, select the next object or location. The software
displays the measurement between the two points. For Angle measurement, select the object or location for the next two points. The software displays the angle between the three points. Select Place Point again to stop measurement.
Repeat this to start measuring at a different location in the model.
Delete
Removes the last placed measurement.
To delete multiple measurements: Select Place Point to stop measurement. For each measurement to remove, select the measurement display, and then select Delete.
Show/Hide
Toggles measurement display on and off. When you turn off the display, measurement placement is also off.
Settings
Displays the following options:
Units of measure
Selects the required measurement unit to be used when placing measurements. The default unit is meters (m).
Snap points
Draws the precision points as glyph and line geometries when you hover over 3D graphics. This option is on by default. You can toggle this option on and off.
As you hover over the object geometries, the software displays a tooltip identifying the precision point geometry type. The example figure below shows the Edge tooltip indicating that the precision point is the edge of an object. Other
tooltips that can display include EndPoint for a corner and MidPoint when you hover over the midpoint of an object.
Exit
Select Measure in the menu bar to exit measurement and delete all current measurements.
 Fly
Represents a navigation mode in which you use the mouse and/or keyboard to move through the model. When you are in Fly mode, the cursor changes to a crosshair at the center of your view.
To exit Fly mode, press F.
Here are some tips for using Fly mode:
As you move through the model, the closer you get to your target, the slower the movement rate becomes. The further away from the target, the faster the movement rate.
To exit Fly mode, press F.
Walk through your model in the direction of the crosshair. Roll the middle mouse wheel forward and back, or press and hold the designated WASD key.
 Zoom Area
Enlarges the display in the view to a defined rectangular location.
Navigate Hexagon 3D file formats using navigation and selection commands
The navigation and selection commands allow you to browse and review its models.
The following options are displayed on the navigation control:
Option
Description
Move in and move out
Select the + and – to move you forward and backward from the fixed center point of the current view.
Compass
Select to face north.
Select and hold, and then drag to rotate the view around the center point.
2D View
Toggles the view between a top-down (2D) position (relative to the current center point) back to the previous 3D view. The blue fill indicates when
you are looking in a top-down view direction.
Walking View
Select the Walking View to rotate the view to a horizontal, forward-facing position. This locks the view to the current elevation.
What can I do with the Walking View?
Here are some tips for using the Walking View:
To start a walkthrough, select Walking View, and then drag your cursor to a specific object in the view. When the object highlights, release or stop the drag motion. The software updates the view and places you at the new location.
The blue fill indicates when Walking View is enabled.
To exit the command, select Walking View.
The elevation lock is enabled when you drag and drop the walking man icon.
Right-click to rotate the eyepoint around the center point of an object under the cursor.
Fly mode and the navigation IJKL keys allow you to look around and move the camera center point.
Navigation WASD keys move you through the model at the same elevation. If you select and fit to an object, the elevation changes to the elevation of that object.
Create a snapshot of a 3D model
1. Select the Snapshot Gallery - Snapshots panel to open the gallery if it is not already opened.
 If there are already snapshots of the model, the number of snapshots that you have access to is displayed on the collapsed panel.
2. Create a snapshot by selecting .
a. Enter the following details in the Create Markup Snapshot form, and select Finish when you are done.
i. Description: Type your own information for describing the snapshot.
ii. Is file markup locked: Check if you want to lock the markups created using this snapshot.
iii. Item owning group: Select an owning group for the snapshot and the markups created using this snapshot.
The snapshot appears as a card in the gallery panel.
3. Select the Markup Layers link on a snapshot card to switch to the markup view.
 If markup layers have already been added to the snapshot, the number of markup layers you have access to appears in the link.
What can I do with a snapshot?
The Snapshot Gallery panel displays all snapshots created using a Hexagon 3D model. These snapshots appear as cards below the toolbar.
Using the Snapshot Gallery, you can:
Filter listed snapshots by owner.
Create a new snapshot.
Update, terminate, or delete a snapshot using the Actions 
 menu.
Select the thumbnail to zoom to that area in the model view.
Select the Markup Layers link to view, modify, or add markup layers.
Select the drop-down arrow to view snapshot properties.
 If open, collapsing the Snapshot Gallery panel closes the Actions 
 menu.
1 Collapse panel
2 Filter snapshots
3 Create a snapshot
4 Title of snapshot
5 Actions menu
6 Thumbnail
7 Mark up snapshot
8 Snapshot properties
From a workspace definition, you can view any related markup snapshots by selecting Actions > Show Markup Snapshots.
For information on using markup tools, select Help 
 on the toolbar.
Navigating 2D file formats
The following section details how to navigate the following 2D file formats:
Non-Hexagon 2D File Formats:
AutoCAD Drawing (.dwg)
MicroStation Design File (.dgn)
Hexagon 2D File Formats:
SmartSketch (.igr and .sma)
Smart P&ID (.pid)
Smart Orthographic Drawings (.sha)
Smart Electrical (.spe)
Aspen Basic Engineering (.zyq)
 The following limitations exist:
The GVC is only compatible with 64-bit systems.
The GVC cannot load and display drawings greater than 200 MB.
In this section:
Navigate 2D file formats using the menu bar
Navigate 2D file formats using touch gestures
View SmartFrame layers in a 2D drawing
View Smart Interop Objects in a 2D drawing
Navigate 2D file formats using touch gestures
How do I do things like double-tap, drag, or pinch?
When using a touch screen device, movements like double-tap, drag, and pinch are referred to as gestures.
Gesture
Description
Tap
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Double-tap
Rapidly touch the screen surface twice.
Drag
Move your fingertips over the touch surface without losing contact.
Pinch
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then bring them closer together.
Press and Hold
Touch the screen for an extended period.
Spread
Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then move them apart.
 You can zoom, pan, and rotate at the same time by combining the gestures for each.
Navigation
Gesture
Pan
Drag one fingertip from side to side or up and down to pan the view.
Zoom In
Place your fingertips close together around the focus area, and then spread the fingertips apart.
Zoom Out
Place your fingertips apart around the focus area, and then pinch or bring the fingertips close together.
Select one object in the graphic
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Select multiple objects in the graphic
Press CTRL on a virtual keyboard and select the objects.
-OR-
Double-tap in an area outside of the model section to start multi-selection, and then select each object. To end multi-
selection, double-tap again in an area outside the model.
 If you do not have a physical keyboard, select CTRL on the device's virtual keyboard. Use your device controls
to open the keyboard.
Navigate 2D file formats using the menu bar
 To open and close the menu bar, tap Expand 
 or press M.
The following options are displayed on the menu bar.
Option
Description
Home
Resets the display to the default view.
You can also press H.
Fit
Zooms in to one or more selected objects. If you do not select any objects, Fit places all visible objects in the view.
If you have multiple items selected, this fits to the center of the selected objects.
You can also press V.
Markup
Toggle between viewer controls.
Zoom Area
Enlarges the display in the view to a defined rectangular location.
Undo
Sets the view to the previous one. Use Undo to move back to the previous positions. You can also Undo by pressing CTRL + Z.
Redo
Reverses your last view position change. You can also Redo by pressing CTRL + Y.
Layers
Displays all sheet and SmartFrame layers in to the current deliverable associated to the model. You can turn one or more sheet and SmartFrame
layers on and off to filter the display and see specific objects. When you turn sheet and SmartFrame layers off, you cannot see objects assigned
to the layers. If you turn off all SmartFrames associated to a sheet layer, the sheet layer is also turned off.
Settings
Provides various settings to control the display and shows system information. Use the Information and Diagnostics settings to troubleshoot
display problems.
The options available include:
Information - Provides product information such as version numbers and data sources for the current model.
Transparency - Turns transparency on and off in the model. By default, transparency is turned on.
Display Axis - Turns the display axis on and off.
FlyTo - Provides a "fly-to" camera mode in which the camera moves from one view to another instead of an instant transition. When
activated, this option applies to the Fit, Home, Undo, and Redo commands.
Visual:
Background - Select either a solid Color or Gradient background type for the display.
Continuous Highlight - Highlights graphics in the view when you hover your mouse over them. The highlight color is the color of the
object. This option is not enabled by default.
Optimize Performance - Automatically turns off all rendering effect settings such as Outline and Anti-aliasing during navigation to provide
a smoother and faster location transition. For 3D model viewing, this option is activated by default.
Outline - Displays outlines on all model objects using the default outline color, which is white on dark backgrounds and black on light
backgrounds.
Soft Shading - Provides soft proximity shadows along narrow openings and intersecting edges between objects in close proximity to each
other.
Anti-alias - Reduces the jagged edges of object surfaces to provide a smoother finish.
Ambient Occlusion - Provides a more realistic lighting for objects by accentuating small surface details and adding soft shadows.
Navigator - Turns on and off the navigator display.
Projection - Select either Orthographic or Perspective model view.
The following settings check your network connection and performance:
Diagnostics Mode - Turns on and off a continuous check of your network connection.
Log - Displays the network connection. You can copy and delete the display.
You can open and close the Settings panel by pressing P.
The following Floating Action Bar (FAB) is available at the bottom center of the viewer:
FAB Option
Description
Documents
Turns the sheet and layers display features on and off. You can see one sheet at a time.
Layers
Toggles the selected layer display list on and off. You can select the layers to view. Only objects assigned to the
selected layer display. Turns sheet layers on (expanded control) and off (collapsed control).
Sheets
Toggles the sheet display list on and off. The software displays sheets available for the current drawing. Select each
sheet to view.
View SmartFrame layers in a 2D drawing
1. Open the drawing. The Document control automatically displays below the drawing.
2. Tap Expand  > Layers 
 or tap Document  to expand the control, and then tap Layers 
. Layers available for the current sheet display in a vertical pane.
3. Select the layer to view. When you select a layer, the software displays objects assigned to that layer.
4. Clear the layer check box to turn the layer display off. All SmartFrames and objects assigned to that layer are hidden.
5. When you have selected the required layers, tap the Back button on the vertical pane, or tap Layers 
 again in the Document control to close the layer list.
View Smart Interop Objects in a 2D drawing
From a model view, you can view a Smart Interop Object in a 2D drawing.
1. Click a Smart Interop Object in a model view.
2. Click Menu 
 on the hotspot bubble, then View Item in Drawing on the Actions menu.
The 2D drawing displays, fitted to the object.
Navigating tags in split-screen mode
You can view a tag in both 2D and 3D files at the same time by selecting Show in Drawing or Show in Model on a tag's hotspot bubble. This split-screen navigation offers excellent contextual information surrounding a tagged object. This
reduces work time by providing large amounts of navigable data within a single view.
To start:
1. Search for a tag or document in your system.
For help, see Search for data and objects in your system.
2. Open the tag or document for viewing.
3. Select a tag, and select Show in Drawing 
 or Show in Model 
 on the hotspot bubble.
If the tag is found in more than one file, select a file from the Pick the document to display for [tag name] dialog.
Only file formats supported in the Graphic View Control (GVC) can be viewed during split-screen mode. PDF file formats are not supported in the GVC.
What file types are supported in the GVC view control?
The following file types are supported in this view control:
File format
Native application
File extension
Note
CAD
AutoCAD Drawing
.dwg
MicroStation Design File
.dgn
Hexagon 2D Drawings
SmartSketch
.igr
.sma
Smart P&ID
.pid
Smart Orthographic Drawings
.sha
Smart Electrical
.spe
Aspen Basic Engineering
.zyq
Hexagon 3D Models
3D WebGL Rendition
.binz
Smart Review
.vue
A 3D WebGL Rendition
(.binz) is required to
view this file format in
standalone
environments.
Smart 3D
.zvf
A 3D WebGL Rendition
(.binz) is required to
view this file format in
standalone
environments.
Streaming 3D Models
-
-
Streamed from the
Visualization Data
Service (VDS).
You cannot access the Snapshot Gallery nor the Visual Intelligence panel during split-screen mode.
4. Select Close to exit the split-screen mode and return to the original view.
Show in Drawing or Show in Model opens the tag in the right viewing panel, highlights the tag, and zooms into it.
Tag selection is linked between viewing panels for easier navigation.
To adjust the viewing panels:
Double-tap the left or right split bar arrows.
Drag the split bar to the left or right.
Double-tap the split bar to evenly space.
Marking up and comparing your files
Marking up and comparing your 2D files is easy. The View and Markup Control delivers a number of features, which are listed below.
What is the View and Markup Control?
The View and Markup Control is a third-party tool utilized for View and Markup of certain file formats, including markup renditions (PDF).
Here is what's new if you are upgrading to the new View and Markup Control from the Brava! HTML Viewer:
You must enable the view control. For help, see Make sure your settings are enabled.
The stamps and symbols file types have changed from XSP to SVG. For configuration help, see Configure stamps and symbols.
Markup layers are saved as .xfdf files and the file type UID is FT_xfdf.
What file types are supported in the view and markup control?
The following file types are supported in this view control:
File format
Native application
File extension
Image
JPEG File Interchange
.jpg
Microsoft Windows Bitmap
.bmp
Portable Network Graphics
.png
Tagged Image File
.tiff
.tif
Microsoft Office
Excel
.xls
Excel Open XML
.xlsx
Excel (Macros)
.xlsm
PowerPoint
.ppt
PowerPoint Open XML
.pptx
PowerPoint (Macros)
.pptm
Word
.doc
Word Open XML
.docx
Word (Macros)
.docm
Text
Adobe Portable Document Format
.pdf
Text
.txt
Key Features
Navigate pages of a file, zooming in or out
Change how the file is displayed in the viewer
Pan and select objects within the file
Search text in the file
Add comments as well as view and search existing comments
Markup the file using stamps, symbols, or other annotations
Lock markups to prevent edits
Consolidate markups to avoid redundancy during document review
Download the file and markups locally
Print the required pages of the file with markups and watermarks
Compare files side-by-side to view differences
Things to Keep in Mind
You cannot view or navigate hotspots in a 2D file format when viewing and marking up the file. To view or navigate hotspots, view the file in the GVC.
Any embedded JavaScript in the PDF file is disabled for security reasons.
Several users can view and markup the same file at the same time.
View and Markup user preferences can be modified to allow you to markup without viewing the files first, if possible. For more information, see Set the user options.

When using a touch screen device, movements like double-tap, drag, and pinch are referred to as gestures.
Gesture
Description
Tap
Briefly touch the screen surface with your fingertip.
Double-tap
Rapidly touch the screen surface twice.
Drag
Move your fingertips over the touch surface without losing contact.

Use two fingers to touch the screen surface, and then bring them closer together.
