related to Role3. User3 is a valid deputy because it has Role3, which manages Role1. User2 is not a valid deputy because it is not directly managed by Role3, nor is its Role1 managed by Role3.
MANAGEROLE
In this scenario, the software filters deputies based on those users who are directly managed by the same role. User1 is related to Role2 which manages, through a relationship, Role4. User2 is directly related to Role4. User2 is a valid
deputy because it is directly managed by the same role. User3 is not a valid deputy because it has no relationship to Role4.
ANY
In this scenario, the software filters deputies based on users regardless of role. All users are valid deputies.
Manage your workflow steps
Workflow steps can be performed in sequence or, when multiple steps can happen at the same time, in parallel.
When a workflow step requires action from you, it appears in the your To Do List. The To Do List provides a place for you to view all workflow steps that have been assigned to you and their statuses.
Workflow steps manage each stage of the process including validation, approval, distribution, and notification. Each workflow step has a target completion date that determines when overdue notifications are sent and workflows expire.
 By default, the duration of each workflow step is set to zero. This means it is expected that a step will be closed on the same day it is started. For information on where the step duration is configured, see Create a workflow step.
Understand how workflow steps behave
Acknowledge an information step
1. In the To Do List, select one or more steps, and select Actions 
 > Acknowledge.
2. Type any details in the Acknowledge box, and select OK.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
Approve a workflow step
1. In the To Do List, select one or more steps, and select Actions 
 > Approve.
2. Type any details in the Approve box.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
Approve or reject a transmittal
1. In the To Do List, select one or more transmittals, and either approve or reject them.
2. Type any details for the recipient of the next step.
 To complete the approve step, the To organization must be set to the same originating or collaborating organization on the document.
Cancel a workflow step
1. In the To Do List, select one or more steps, and select Actions 
 > Cancel.
2. Type any details in the Cancel box.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
Claim a workflow step
Steps can be set up so that one user can respond for all recipients or respond for all recipients that share the same role. If the step is set up in either way, you must claim the step before you can complete the action it requires.
1. In the To Do List, select a step, and select Actions 
 > Claim.
2. Type any details in the Claim box.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
Complete a workflow step
1. In the To Do List, select one or more steps, and select Actions 
 > Complete.
2. Type any details in the Complete box.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
3. Select Completed.
Reject a workflow step
1. In the To Do List, select one or more steps, and select Actions 
 > Reject.
2. Type any details in the Reject box.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
Indicate a workflow step cannot be completed
What happens if a workflow step cannot be completed?
If you indicate that a step cannot be completed, the step disappears from your To Do List, and the workflow moves through the defined rejection path, which can be another workflow or another step in the same workflow.
1. In the To Do List, select one or more steps, and select Actions 
 > Unable to Complete.
2. Type any details in the Unable to Complete box.
Comments are saved with the object and shared with the recipient of the next step.
Messages to the next step are saved with this step and shared with the recipients of the next step.
3. Select Unable to Complete.
Attach a workflow to an object
When a submittal, action, transmittal, or technical query is created, a workflow is automatically attached. However, for documents or folders, you must manually attach a workflow after creating them.
1. Select an object from the list, and select Actions 
 > Workflow > Attach Workflow.
2. In the Attach Workflow dialog, select a workflow from the list, and then select OK.
 Workflows are displayed based on the interfaces you selected when creating workflows.
3. When the attach process is complete, refresh all views to see the updated data for the objects and their related workflow.
Delete an attached workflow
You can delete a workflow from a selected workflow item using the Delete Workflow action.
Select a workflow object, and click Actions 
 > Delete Workflow.
After you delete an attached workflow from a workflow object, the following changes are made:
The relationship between the object and the workflow is removed and the workflow status of the object is cleared.
All the workflow history for the object is removed from the workflow.
Remaining workflow steps for the object are removed from each user’s To Do List.
Terminate an attached workflow
You can terminate an attached workflow for a specific workflow item using the Terminate Workflow action.
1. Select a workflow item, and click Actions 
 > Terminate Workflow.
2. Click Yes in the Terminate Workflow dialog, and type a reason for the workflow termination.
 This only terminates the relationship, it does not terminate the object or the workflow.
After you terminate an attached workflow, the following changes are made:
The relationship between the object and the workflow is terminated.
Workflow history records the termination and the point in the workflow where it happened.
All historical information regarding steps processed prior to termination are saved.
All uncompleted steps for the object in the workflow are terminated and removed from the To Do List for all users.
View a workflow details report for an object
A workflow details report provides information about each step of the workflow attached to the selected object. It helps in analyzing the workflow and making the necessary changes based on your requirements.
1. Select an object from the list, and then select Actions 
.
2. In the Actions menu, select Workflow > Workflow Details Report.
3. When the report is executed successfully, download it from Notifications 
. For more information, see View notifications of reports.
View the Workflow Overdue Steps Report for an object
A Workflow Overdue Steps Report provides information about overdue steps in the workflow attached to a selected object. It helps you track pending workflow actions and take the necessary steps to complete the workflow.
1. Search for the required object, and select Actions 
.
2. In the Actions menu, select Workflow > Workflow Overdue Steps Report.
When the report is run successfully, it is displayed in LISTVIEW format in a new window.
 You can also view a workflow overdue steps report from your To Do List. For more information about the To Do List, see Using the To Do List.
View the All Overdue Steps Report
An All Overdue Steps Report retrieves information from the system and displays overdue steps from all workflows attached to objects such as folders, documents, and so on. It helps you track overdue workflow steps and take the
necessary actions to complete the workflows.
1. In the Workflow Management feature set, select All Overdue Steps Report.
2. When the report is run successfully, download it from Notifications 
. For more information, see View notifications of reports.
 The report is displayed in XHTML format.
Correct PDF rendition generation failures
When renditions do not generate correctly, the system directs the workflow to a step to correct the failure. The submitter is notified and can contact a system administrator to determine the issue and fix it.
When the problem is fixed, the workflow progresses to the next step.
Reassign work to a different user
When you are unavailable or out of the office, you can reassign your own work to a different user. However, your business administrator can configure steps in a workflow so that they can also be reassigned to another user.
 Your logged on user details are also displayed in the reassign window so you can assign tasks back to yourself. Administrator users may also want to help the workflow by unblocking other users.
Reassign your tasks temporarily
You can reassign your workflow tasks to another user for a designated period of time.
When should tasks be reassigned and by whom?
If you are out of the office, you or a business administrator can temporarily reassign tasks to another user. During the dates you are away, any tasks assigned to you are automatically reassigned to your designated user. The task is
removed from your To Do List, and added to the To Do List of the designated user. Tasks the designated user does not complete can be manually reassigned back to you when you return.
When you cannot complete tasks, you can reassign them to other users who have similar roles.
1. Select Settings 
 > Task Reassignment 
.
2. Select the From Date and To Date during which your tasks will be assigned to another user.
3. In Assignee Filters, select the role criteria, Same Role, Managed by Role, Manage Role, or Any Role, to filter the list of users to those that can be assigned the tasks.
4. In Assignee Selection, search for and select the user to which you want to reassign the items using one of the following:
Select a Project, and select a user from the Assignee list in the same row. This user is assigned the workflow tasks only for that project. You can select different users for each project.
Select Assignee 
 options, and select a Common Assignee. A Common Assignee is a user that has a common role across a single project or multiple projects and can be assigned your tasks for projects based on that
common role.
5. Select FINISH.
 When you return to the office, you can run the My active workflow reassignments or My Document Review Assignments reports to see the current status of any tasks that were reassigned to your deputy. You can then ask your
deputy to manually reassign any uncompleted tasks back to yourself.
Reassign a workflow step
1. Select a workflow step, and click Actions 
 > Reassign.
2. Select a user from a selected role to reassign the workflow step to that designated user.
You can only reassign workflow steps to a single user, not multiple users.
You can filter both the roles and users to narrow the list to a specific user.
When reassigning a workflow step:
A consolidate step takes precedence over the review step or document receipt notification step.
A review step takes precedence over the document receipt notification step.
You cannot reassign any notification steps.
Using List Edit
After a search or query, the object data returned is displayed in an organized results table. The object properties appear under headings in each column in a set order, and the values for these properties appear in the table rows.
With the correct permissions, you can create, duplicate, and delete objects in the table rows, and edit their property values using List Edit.
How do I configure an object for List Edit?
The ability to use List Edit must be configured by the system administrator for each specific object and data type so you can edit the objects and their properties.
What controls the column order?
The columns you see and their order in which they appear are determined by the object's column set. Each type of object can have its own configured column set based on their classification that determines which properties display in the
results table and the order in which they appear.
 For more information about configuring column sets, see Configure column sets.
Using the To Do List
Your To Do List displays all of the workflow objects assigned to you or your role that are relevant to the selected plant query scope configurations, which have tasks that you need to action or complete.
Virtually all your interaction with workflows occurs in the To Do List, where you can easily view the workflow, view the related tasks, view the individual task details and attachments, and action or complete your tasks.
Click the To Do List 
 in the side bar to open the To do List: Overview menu. A count indicates the number of tasks that need actioning.
You can reassign workflow tasks to another user when you are out of the office. For more information, see Reassign work to a different user.
You can display the To Do List as a function tile in a feature set. For more information, see Relate a method to a feature set.
To Do List overview
The To Do List: Overview menu is where you can select the workflow tasks that you have been assigned, you can claim, or are overdue. You can filter the task types by selecting a task type filter from the list to display the available tasks
in the results table.
 You can also select a task type in the pie chart to display the available tasks.
What are the To Do List overview task type filters?
The To Do List: Overview menu displays tasks grouped by task type using the following filter options:
Select this
To display
All
Tasks that are available to you or your role.
OVERDUE
Tasks that are overdue and require your immediate action or completion.
My assigned/claimed tasks
Tasks previously claimed by you or explicitly assigned to you for action.
My non-claimed tasks
Tasks that you are eligible to action, but which you must explicitly claim first. These are tasks assigned to a role or
group for which only a subset of people need to take action.
[Object type]
Tasks that apply to a specific type of item, such as an Action, Document Revision, or Technical Query.
 Overdue tasks are highlighted with the Task is overdue 
 symbol.
Select Refresh 
 to ensure the To Do List: Overview menu displays the latest list of assigned, claimed, or overdue tasks.
Select Configurations 
 to reduce the number of tasks displayed to only those in a specific query configuration.
Results table
The results table displays all of your tasks selected by the task filter. You complete your tasks by selecting each task separately and processing the actions in order to complete the task including any workflow step checklists.
When you select a task in the results table, the To Do List task detail pane displays more information for each task under separate tabs, such as Task, [Task Type], and Workflow.
What information is displayed in the task details pane?
The To Do List task details pane displays the selected task's information under the following standard tabs:
Name
Description
Task
Displays the details of the specific workflow task from the selected query configuration.
[Task Type]
Displays the class definition [Task Type] as the name of the tab, such as Document Revision, or Change Request, and shows the details of the workflow
task item type, including any attached files.
Workflow
Displays a brief view of the workflow focusing on the active task, the process steps, and the next workflow tasks.
Checklist
Displays when a checklist has been configured for the workflow step. (Optional)
Checklists
A Checklist tab is displayed in the To Do List task details pane if a checklist is configured for the workflow step. Checklists can be mandatory (as indicated with a *) and must be completed before the workflow step can be signed off. They
can also be configured so the workflow step can still be signed off, even if the checklist is not complete.
Only the workflow tasks that are relevant to your selected query scope plant configurations are displayed in your To Do List.
If you select multiple tasks in the results table, only actions that are relevant to all of the selected tasks are available. The individual workflow details for each task is not displayed.
Tasks are displayed under separate configuration tabs for each query scope configuration. Selecting a different configuration tab automatically changes the create scope to that configuration.
You can sort the order of how tasks are displayed in the results grid. For more information, see Configure automatic sorting of column items.
For more information on Workflow tab, see Workflow viewer.
Workflow viewer
The workflow viewer displays your workflow tasks in a dynamic, organized, and systematic way. You can:
Show your task steps in the workflow for the selected object and see all of the next steps.
Quickly confirm the exact status and details of your workflow steps.
Action the workflow task to complete the required task steps.
See the number of recipients of the same task that are parallel to the one you are assigned.
Change the display options to view the workflow tasks exactly how you want.
Analyze the workflow path to see where your task is going to next.
Reposition and zoom steps using the mouse or touch screen controls.
Hide or display the process steps on the workflow path.
There are two ways to view your tasks in the workflow:
Workflow tab
Select the task in the results table, and click the Workflow tab to display your task in a brief view of the workflow.
View workflow
Select a task in the results table, and click Actions 
 > View Workflow to open the task in the View Workflow window.
Workflow viewer options
The following options are available on the tool bar in the Workflow tab and in the View Workflow window.
Icon
Name
Description
Legend
Lists the types of steps and paths by color or icon.
Filter
Provides options to change the display of the workflow steps.
Reset
Resets the workflow steps back to the original display state.
View Workflow
Opens the task in the full View Workflow pane.
Open in new tab
Opens the task in the full View Workflow pane in a new browser tab.
What are the Filter options?
The Filter 
 options allow you to change the display of the workflow steps.
Filter option
What it allows you to do
Brief View
Change the view to a brief view of only your task steps after the selected task, or expand all the parallel workflow paths and steps.
Landscape
Change the workflow orientation between upright and landscape.
Minimize Process
Hide or expose all process steps in the workflow.
The following options are available on the workflow steps and the process steps.
Icon
Name
Description
Details
Lists the workflow step details and status, such as target date, overdue date, and so on.
Parallel Tasks
Lists the action and any other roles that are assigned the same task to be actioned in parallel to you.
Expose process steps
Display the process steps between tasks.
Hide process steps
Hide the process steps between tasks.
Task details
You can see the details of your task, such as description, status, recipients and so on, by clicking Details 
 on the task workflow step. The information displayed also includes any message from the previous step, target date, or overdue
date, as well as other relevant task information, such as the workflow step definition. For example:
Parallel tasks
You can see the workflow step action required by you and other recipients by clicking Parallel Tasks 
 on the workflow step. The task window lists the action required for you to complete the task, it's description, and target date. It also
lists all the other users and their roles that are assigned the same task in parallel to you. For example:
Working with transmittals
There are three types of transmittals:
Incoming Transmittals – Transmittals created by the owner company to record the receipt of documents from the contractor. Documents are loaded into the system and then related to the transmittal.
Internal Transmittals – Transmittals initiated within the organization to send documents to internal users.
Outgoing Transmittals – Transmittals initiated within the owner company to send documents to another organization. Information can be shared with organizations that do not have a contract or access to the HxGN SDx2 system by
selecting the Offline option while creating the transmittal.
 Contractor and supplier organizations can send documents to another company using the submittal process. For more information, see Submittals.
 Your system administrator may modify the workflow so that the steps differ from those shown. The steps are still carried out using the procedures described below.
How is the transmittal due date calculated?
The value specified in the Suggested due date box is set as the workflow step response date. If no value is specified, then it is calculated as given below:
Online transmittals
The due date is calculated based on the Outgoing transmittal default response days value provided on the contract work package. For example, if the default response days is 10, the system adds 10 working days to the current
date and sets the result as the due date.
If the default response day property is empty, the response days value equals the current date.
Offline transmittals
If a contract work package is selected:
The due date is calculated based on the Outgoing transmittal default response days value provided on the contract work package. For example, if the default response days is 10, the system adds 10 working days to the current
date and sets the result as the due date.
If the default response day property is empty, the response days value equals the current date.
If a contract work package is not selected:
The response days value equals the current date.
Who receives the email in an offline transmittal?
After the transmittal is submitted, an email, along with a link to download the zip file, is sent to the email address available on the ToUser or the Organization. If both ToUser and Organization are selected, precedence is given to the
ToUser.
 If the email address is empty for the ToUser, a Wrong/Invalid email address step is sent to the To Do List of the sender so they can update the email address and resend the transmittal.
 The zip file contains the transmittal cover letter and any files attached to the document.
In an offline transmittal, does the link (to download the files) in the email expire?
Yes. To add a layer of security, the link expires after 15 working days by default. However, this may be modified by your administrator.
In an offline transmittal, what should I do if I have problems downloading the zip file?
 This could happen when the link provided in the email expires or the zip file does not exist.
Reply to the email, providing the details of the error message. You will then be emailed a new link, or the validity of the existing link will be extended. For more information, see Reshare a transmittal.
Create a transmittal
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | Create a transmittal <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/67c0e2aa023b7369fe80deb8>
1. In a communication feature set, select the type of transmittal you want to send.
 You can also create a transmittal from the Actions 
 menu of one or more signed-off documents.
2. Create your transmittal and provide the other information as required.
For an internal transmittal, you must set the Due Date.
For an online outgoing transmittal, you must select the contract.
For an incoming transmittal, the transmittal number is not automatically generated by the system. You can enter the number provided by the supplier or the vendor.
3. If you are creating an incoming transmittal, navigate to the next page, and attach the file or files by following the steps below:
a. In the New Files pane, select the LOCAL FILE SYSTEM tab.
b. Drag the necessary file or files, or select CHOOSE FILES to select the file.
 You can add new files to or delete existing files from an existing transmittal by using the Manage Files command. For more information, see Manage files.
4. Navigate to the next page, and add the related document or documents.
 At least one signed-off document in the Current state must be related to a transmittal.
5. If you are creating an outgoing transmittal:
a. If one or more documents do not have the collaborating organization identified, set the collaborating organization in the Collaborate column.
 The Collaborate column is visible only if you select a contract work package. If a document does not have a collaborating organization set, it is not defined to be shared with the transmittal recipient organization. This is
indicated by 
 in the Collaborate column. To complete the transmittal, you must set the collaboration by selecting the check box or, if the document is incorrectly included, remove the document from the transmittal.
b. Define the file type by selecting the PDF or Native check box on the selected transmittal documents. The default transmittal file type is set to PDF.
 To complete the transmittal, you must select at least one file type for each document or remove the document from the transmittal.
c. To include the signed files, select Signed check box on the selected transmittal documents.
 If a document has more than one file, the Signed check box is available for selection only if all the files have a corresponding signed file.
d. To include the QA review consolidated PDF renditions, select QA Review PDF check box on the selected transmittal documents.
 If a document does not have the QA review consolidated PDF renditions, the check box is disabled.
6. Select Finish.
For an outgoing (online), internal, or incoming transmittal, a a Transmittal Issued task is sent to the recipient's To Do List.
For an outgoing (offline) transmittal, an email is sent to the recipient.
The From CC users get a Transmittal issue notification step and the To CC users get a Transmittal receipt notification step.
 The submitter can see the details of an issue by selecting on the step in the To Do List. The submitter must address any issue before issuing the transmittal.
 You can also select Save as Draft to save the transmittal and send a Prepare content and assign recipients task to your To Do List to continue working on it later.
For an outgoing transmittal, you get a Prepare content and assign recipients task in your To Do List.
For an internal or an incoming transmittal, you get a Prepare Transmittal task in your To Do List.
Preview the transmittal letter
For a transmittal that has not yet been issued, you can review the transmittal letter before sending it.
Select the transmittal, followed by Actions 
 > Transmittal > Transmittal Letter Preview.
Prepare the transmittal
The following is applicable if you selected SAVE AS DRAFT while creating the transmittal.
1. In your To Do List, select
Prepare Transmittal task in the case of an incoming and internal transmittal.
Prepare content and add recipients task in the case of an outgoing transmittal.
2. Select Actions 
 > Transmittal > Update.
3. In the Update form, update the information as needed on the first page.
4. Navigate to the next page to add or remove the documents.
 You can also select Actions 
 > Relationship Builder to add or remove the documents.
5. Select UPDATE to save the changes.
6. To issue the transmittal, select Actions 
 > Completed. The system sends:
an email to the recipient if the Offline option is checked for an outgoing transmittal.
the transmittal to the recipient's To Do List in the case of an online transmittal.
Outgoing transmittals
The From CC users get a Transmittal issue notification step and the To CC users get a Transmittal receipt notification step. To acknowledge the step,
Select Actions 
 > Acknowledge.
The submitter gets an Update Missing Information step in the To Do List if a collaborating organization is not set on any of the attached documents.
For an offline outgoing transmittal, the submitter gets a Wrong/Invalid Email Address step in the To Do List if the From CC user or the To CC user does not have an email address in the system.
 The submitter can see the details of an issue by clicking on the step in the To Do List. The submitter must address any issue before issuing the transmittal.
Receive a transmittal
Online transmittals
1. In your To Do List, select the Transmittal Issued task.
 You can view the transmittal content by selecting Actions 
 > Transmittal > Export Transmittal Contents.
2. Select Actions 
 > Completed to accept the transmittal.
 You can reject the transmittal by selecting Actions 
 > Unable to Complete.
Offline transmittals
You will receive an email containing a link to download the transmittal zip file.
Acknowledge the transmittal
When a transmittal has been accepted by the receiver, or when an offline transmittal has been sent, it is shown as Completed in the system.
In your To Do List, select the Transmittal Completed task, followed by Actions 
 > Acknowledge.
Reshare a transmittal
 The Reshare Transmittal command is visible only for issued, offline transmittals.
On request, the administrator contacts the sender who then uses the Reshare Transmittal command available in the Actions 
 menu of the transmittal object. The administrator or the sender will then email a new link to the requester or
inform the requester to use the same link.
Cancel a transmittal
You can cancel a transmittal. If the transmittal has been issued or completed, the current workflow is terminated, and the transmittal is placed in a new cancelled workflow. If the transmittal has not been issued, the workflow is terminated,
but the details are saved for future reference.
 You can undo the cancel.
Learn more about transmittals
Transmittals are used to distribute a controlled set of documents in the system to a specific set of recipients. The term transmittal refers to the report used to record and track the distribution of the documents. The report details how many,
and what, documents are involved, the recipients, and the reason for their issue. Transmittals play an important part in tracking a project's progress, such as releasing contractual construction documents to contractors at the start of the
project, and they can be used for the distribution of documentation to meet milestone objectives and payments.
HxGN SDx2 supports transmittals with one sender organization and one recipient organization. A transmittal also has one sender and one recipient user or role. To inform other people about the transmittal on both the sender and the
receiver side, you can copy in additional users using the To CC and From CC user options.
What happens when a transmittal is issued?
When a transmittal is issued, the transmittal report along with any attached documents are available for recipients to download. Transmittals can contain contractually agreed upon documents that require implementation. Transmittals can
also be used to send documents for a documentation review.
In the case of offline transmittals, the recipients receive an email, along with a link to download the zip file.
In the case of online transmittals, the recipients receive transmittals in their To Do List.
What does the transmittal status mean?
The following life cycle statuses are specific to transmittals:
Review Status
Outgoing
Internal
Incoming
Visible To
Description
RESERVED
Creator in originating organization
Transmittal is pending issue.
ISSUED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Transmittal is issued to recipients.
REJECTED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Transmittal is rejected by recipient
organization.
COMPLETED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Transmittal action is complete and
finished.
CANCELLED
Originating and receiving
organizations
Transmittal is cancelled.
Viewing the History
HxGN SDx2 has powerful information management capability for the safe and efficient management of data held in a database. It accurately records the history of each object modification from its creation to the present time.
As the object history is preserved in the database, you can quickly view the history of attributes for a selected object or a document over their lifetime using the History.
Select the object in the results grid, and click Actions 
 > History.
 You can also select History 
 from the object or document shortcut hover menu.
Object history
For all objects, the History page opens showing the object's name, creation date, and number of active and historical attributes. Each attribute is listed in a grid view, as shown in the following example for tag CV-2021.
Document history
For all documents, the History page opens with the OVERVIEW tab, where you can view the document name, revision issue date, and number of revisions. The history of changes to the document's revisions, versions, and their attached
files are displayed in a train line display in issue date order, with the latest revision displayed on the right. The following example shows the overview for document 01-0000-ENG-000A with the latest Working revision 03A.
 You can scroll the train line display to the left to expand the timeline to see any previous revisions. A revision issue date is not displayed by Working revisions until it is signed off as a new Current revision. For more information, see
Document overview.
View attribute details
The DETAILS tab shows all the active and historical attributes for the selected object displayed in a grid view. The grid view lists all the object attributes in rows, and displays the changed attribute details under separate column headers,
such as Value, Valid From, Set By, and so on.
For example, the following image shows the details and timeline for tag CV-2021.
How is the attribute's status displayed in the grid view?
The following table shows the attribute's status in the grid view.
Icon
Attribute description
The attribute property is active in the database.
The attribute relationship is active in the database.
The attribute property has been terminated in the database.
The attribute relationship has been terminated in the database.
You can see the history of an object's attributes even if they were set to Terminated as the history of the object is preserved in the database. Objects that are Deleted are permanently removed from the database along with their
history.
You can export the attribute list in the grid view to Excel by clicking Export to Excel 
. The export respects the state of the attributes in the grid, so if the attributes have been grouped, Excel displays them as grouped.
View the object timeline
The Object Timeline displays all the changes made to the selected attribute over the lifetime of the object in an interactive timeline. You can select various timeline options, which allow you to view each attribute change, or set of changes
by date, by user, and so on.
Select an object attribute row in the grid view, an information box opens and points to where the attribute changed on the timeline.
How do you use the timeline options?
You can use the following time line options to change how the attributes display on the timeline:
Name
Button
What it displays on the timeline
Highlight single row
Highlights where the selected row attribute impacted the object history timeline. This is the default setting.
Highlight Set By
Highlights every time a user has impacted the selected attribute in the timeline.
Highlight Attribute
Highlights every time the selected attribute was edited in the timeline.
Zoom to fit
Resets the timeline back to fit all of the attributes into the display.
An information box displays a summary of the attribute change including the description, who actioned the change, and the change date.
You can zoom into the timeline to expand when the attribute was changed or to see a set of changes.
Grouping attributes by column header
You can see all instances of specific types of attribute grouped in a list by grouping the data together by a specific column header. This displays all the changes to attributes that are the same type grouped together in rows.
You simply select a column header type and drag the header name onto the indicated grouping area to group the objects by that type. You can also add or remove groupings or combine groupings to display the data you want to see.
For example, to group the attributes for a tag by Value, you drag the Value column header and drop it onto the grouping area, as shown by the following Tag CV-2021:
:
 When you first select the document DETAILS tab, the different document types are automatically displayed grouped by the document Level column header with the Document revision type selected by default. For more
information, see View document details.
Filter and sort the attributes
You can easily sort the attributes alphabetically in the grid view by clicking the column header to change the order. You can also use the Filter 
, and select the option on each column header to target the list of attributes.
 By combining filtering and sorting, you can narrow the list of attributes to a set you want to see
Document overview
When you select the History for a document, the OVERVIEW tab opens by default. Here you can view the history timeline of the document's revisions, versions, and files in a train line display starting from the latest Current or Working
revision on the right-hand side. Each previous revision iteration of the document since it was first issued displays to the left and includes a Revision Issue Date. This date is not displayed by a Working revision until it is signed off and
becomes the latest Current revision.
You scroll across the train line display to expand the timeline of the document to see all active current, working, and superseded revisions, along with their versions and attached files, as shown in the following example:
 The OVERVIEW display follows the configured document life cycle, which displays the revision state using an icon. For more information on the document life cycle, see Documentation life cycle.
By selecting Latest versions only, you view the latest active document revisions in issue date order, along with their versions and attached files. The following example shows revisions for 01A, 02A, and the latest working revision,
03A.
 Working revisions do not display an issue date until sign off.
By selecting Removed Items, you also display any inactive terminated revisions, their versions and attached files. For example,in the following image you can see the previously terminated revision of 03A, as highlighted in red. The
original 03A revision was terminated and replaced by revising 02A to a new Working revision 04A.
 The Total Revisions number includes any terminated revisions that have been removed.
View document details
When you select the document DETAILS tab, the master, revision, and version document types are automatically displayed grouped by the Level column header with the Document revision type expanded by default.
You can quickly change the document type to another by expanding the level, such as the Document master or Document version. You can also remove the grouping by column header or add more groupings to find and display the data
you want to see. For more information, see Grouping attributes by column header.
The following example shows the document attribute changes grouped by the Level column header, as shown by the expanded Document revision type.
Viewing attached files
When a document revision has attached files, the number of files is displayed under the version number as an attachment. When you select the version number, the attached files are displayed as thumbnails in a grid view under the train
line display.
For example, the following document revision 01-0000-ENG-000A has four attached file for revision 03A version 1 as shown by 
.
 Click on a thumbnail and the attachment file opens in View and Markup.
What are the thumbnail options?
The following options can be accessed from the document thumbnails:
Icon
Description
Displays the selected file in View and Markup.
Displays the image information for the selected thumbnail file.
Loading documents and data using Data Take-On
Data Take-On is typically used to load items in bulk. When a large amount of data needs to be loaded, a load file can be used to speed up the process. Using a Data Take-On job is an alternative for the interactive create or update process.
It uses the underlying Data Take-On feature capabilities to import, validate, and load data.
You can run a Data Take-On job for the different types of objects based on their classification.
As Data Take-On runs the job through the defined process, the job's progress and summary are displayed for each stage.
The Data Take-On job is automatically placed on to the DTO Loader workflow, and the object is imported into a staging area, where it is validated and loaded into the database, using Data Take-On. You can view your jobs and monitor their
progress in the My Data Take-On jobs page.
On successful completion of your Data Take-On job, you can view the associated data and documents in the respective feature sets within the domain specified in the job definition. For more information, see Load Configurations tab in
Create a job definition. You must ensure that your role has the required permissions to view the data in the respective feature sets.
If you are a business administrator, system administrator, document controller, or project engineer, you can use the load methods to load existing data such as documents and tags to your system.
 Any user mapped to the System Administrator or Business Administrator role can access the Data Take-On jobs functionality.
For more information about Data Take-On, see What is Data Take-On?
For more information about configuring Data Take-On, see How do I configure Data Take-On?
Run a Data Take-On job
You can run a Data Take-On job for different types of objects based on their classification. The Data Take-On job is automatically attached to the DTO Loader workflow and imported into the staging area, validated, and loaded into the
database using Data Take-On.
What are the pre-requisites for running a Data Take-On job?
The following parameters must be kept under consideration while running a Data Take-On job. However, these parameters will change regularly as we add more features and continuously enhance our solution. We recommend you to
revisit the documentation frequently to keep track of changes to these parameters.
The maximum number of rows in your comma separated values (CSV) file must not exceed 50,000, in case of documents and tags. The application notifies you if the number of rows in your CSV file exceeds this limit.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document or all the documents that can be created as part of a Data Take-On job is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 4GB.
The maximum number of files that can be attached as a zip file to documents must not exceed 10,000. Even when you are uploading multiple zip files, the total number of files in all the zip files must not exceed 10,000.
Data Take-On can run a single job at a time.
Before you start running any Data Take-On jobs, ensure that the client application is registered for your tenant. Otherwise, the jobs may not move to the next stage after Stage processing. Contact your system administrator for more
information. Also, see Configure a client application.
You must also ensure that any PBS items such as Area, Unit, and so on, listed in your CSV file, also exist in the domain specified in the job definition. For more information, see Load Configurations tab in Create a job definition.
For running a Data Take-On job, you must be having a valid organization and security code assigned to you by your administrator.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load <Object>.
 The following methods are delivered in the Data Take-On feature set:
Load Tags
Load Documents
2. Type a description for the Data Take-On job.
3. Select the Suppress ENS check box if you want the ENS to be suppressed, and objects to be named according to the values in the CSV file.
 The Suppress ENS check box is enabled by your administrator.
What is the purpose of the Suppress ENS check box?
The purpose of this flag is to allow data to be loaded respecting the property values given in the load file rather than applying the ENS definition that is configured for the class definition of this object. This would typically be used to
load historical data with specified properties, for example, name, rather than having ENS define the property. When data is created interactively or through the Import Data feature without this setting, the configured ENS is still
applied to the object.
4. Select Step 2, and upload the input file.
 The input file formatting is validated after successful upload, before the Data Take-On job is created. Delete the input file if any formatting errors are displayed, and upload it again after rectifying the errors.
Monitor the Data Take-On job progress
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Show My Data Take-On Jobs.
2. On the My Data Take-On Jobs page, select a Data Take-On job, and select Actions 
.
 You can monitor the progress of the Data Take-On jobs you created in the current scope, or you can select one of the following options:
Select this
To do this
Approve
Approve and trigger the next workflow step of the selected Data Take-On job.
Terminate
Terminate the selected Data Take-On job, along with any attached files.
Delete
Delete the selected Data Take-On job, along with any attached files.
Download Report
Download the validation report for the selected Data Take-On job.
View Summary
View the summary of the selected Data Take-On job.
The Data Take-On job remains in the database even after the job is successfully processed. You can retain the job for historical purposes or delete it using the Delete option in the Actions 
 menu.
To avoid unexpected results, do not try to re-attach a workflow to a completed job using the Attach and Edit Workflow or the Attach Workflow options in the Actions 
 menu.
The Review Validation Report step is in the delivered workflow, DTO Loader. The Review Validation Report step is related to the SDVApprove step definition, which ensures that the Approve or Sign-Off step is not available in
the To Do List for any Data Take-On jobs that have validation errors.
View job progress and statistics
You can view the progress and summary of a job in Data Take-On as it progresses through each stage in the selected workflow.
View job progress
When a job is created, it is displayed in the Show My Data Take-On Jobs tile in the Data Take-On feature set, and automatically begins in the selected workflow.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Show My Data Take-On Jobs.
2. On the My Data Take-On Jobs page, select , next to Actions 
.
3. Select Refresh 
 multiple times to see the change of status at every stage.
The information provided at each stage is as follows:
Stage - Displays the name of the uploaded file, the import definition used, and the percentage of the staging process completed.
Validation - Displays the process of the imported data through the validation rules as a percentage complete.
Load - Displays the progress of the transfer and the loading of the data into the database.
View job summary
The Summary page displays overview information about the job. This summary information includes the current status of the job and statistics about timings, data objects imported, total number of records in the input files, relationships,
properties, rules, objects deleted, and so forth.
The information provided in each column is as follows:
Status - Current status of the workflow.
Import - Current status of the stage step and statistics.
Validation - Current status of the validation step and statistics.
Load - Current status of the load step and object statistics.
 The number of items listed as imported or loaded in the Summary page may differ from the totals you expect to see.
Job summary stage and load counts
Stage counts
These are determined on import as follows:
One object on multiple lines in one file is counted only once where the values for the properties are the same. However, if the same data is included in two different input files, it will be counted twice. This rule is in place to support the
tracking of the data back to its source (file and line number) for error reporting.
Blank cells for a property will still create the property but define no value for it.
Blank cells for an object are ignored, assuming it is not mandatory. The object is not generated, the relationship is not created, and nothing is added to the counts. If the object is mandatory and has blank cells, the object is not
created, and an error is recorded in the validation report.
The action defined on an item has no effect on whether the item is included in the import count, as everything is imported. This is unlike the load count, which is selectively based on the defined action.
Constants are counted like a property on each applicable object, as per the mapping. For example, if a constant is defined for each tag and 1000 tags are created, the count will include 1000 properties for adding the constants,
unless multiple lines reference the same tag.
Load counts
These are determined on loading as follows:
Information sent to the landing tables is dependent on the load map. If an object is not configured to be loaded, it will not be sent.
The load filter prevents objects with errors of a certain severity or greater from being loaded.
The action set for an item determines whether it is included in the loading and the load count.
No Action - The objects are not sent during loading and they are not counted.
Check Exists - The object is not loaded, but the relationship is sent. The object is counted.
Delete and Terminate - The object UID is sent, but not the properties. The object is counted.
Compare Value and Check Staging File Exists - The object's properties are not sent and the object is not counted.
Relationships with a load action are sent to the database, unless:
An object on one end of the relationship fails a Check Exists check.
An object on one end of the relationship does not pass the load filter.
 If either of these issues exist, the relationship is not sent, and the object is not counted.
Objects, properties, and relationships are included in the count if they are processed, even if no update was made to the item.
Extra items may be created that are not included in the count. For example, additional relationships and other objects created due to claiming, and a load of a Primary Classification relationship will cause HxGN SDx2 to fill in all
relevant SPFClassificationLevel relationships.
 If the job is stuck at any stage of the job progress, the Recovery Scheduler retries the job at regular polling intervals of 30 minutes.
Review validation errors
After the validation stage is completed, a validation report is available for download. The information in the Microsoft Excel report allows you to take any corrective actions.
1. On the My Data Take-On Jobs page, select a Data Take-On job, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Validation Report link available below the VALIDATION progress bar.
The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
You can also select Actions 
 > Download Report and download the validation report.
What does the validation report contain?
When a validation report is generated by HxGN SDx2, you can download it as a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet. The report includes details about the job and validation summary information, as illustrated in the following image:
By default, there are two severity levels for issues.
Error - Displayed as red in the graph.
Warning - Displayed as yellow in the graph.
The validation summary sheet provides information regarding the number of warnings and errors for each type of object. Additional sheets in the validation report provide more detail on these objects through either clicking the hyperlink,
such as EqpClass, or by selecting the Worksheet tab.
Object types
The following details are also available in separate tabs for each of the object types in the Excel file:
Name
Description
Name
The name of the object that caused the error.
UID
The unique identifier of the object that caused the error, used to identify potential issues with the mapping.
Column name
The column name in the import mapping CSV file that created the object.
Action
The action for the object, property, or relationship as identified in the import map.
Message
The default failure message, as defined on the validation rule that failed. Auto-generated rules have hard-coded default messages that may be changed
by editing the rule.
Severity
The severity level of the rule that failed, such as Warning, Error, or a custom severity.
Propagated From
Indicates that this object is in error because another object or property had an error that was propagated to this item.
Line Number
The line in the CSV file that created the object. Data Take-On reports the first instance of the object in the file only. If the same object is mentioned many
times, it is reported only once. If the same object appears in two files, it is reported only once.
File Name
The name of the file that contains the data.
Rule Name
The name of the rule that failed.
Value
The value of the property if this is a property rule, or the UID of the object if this is an object rule.
Error Type
The type of error encountered, as defined in Possible error types.
Property or RelDef name in Data Take-On
If the rule is based on a property or relationship, this field displays the property or relationship definition name as it appears in Data Take-On.
Additional Info
Any additional information relevant to a rule. Some rules can provide extra information that is not overridden by the user message, such as concurrent
engineering rules reporting the other configurations in which the item has been found.
Possible error types
The following are the possible error types that may occur:
Name
Description
Native Object Error
An error that happened to that object.
Property Error
An error that happened to a property.
Propagated Property Error
An error that was propagated from a property to an object.
Propagated Object Error
An error that was propagated from a related object.
.
Review load stage errors
The load stage transfers the data to the database landing tables before loading the data into your database. The Summary page displays the Load stage status and the number of objects, properties, and relationships that have been
processed. When the transfer is complete, the tab displays a green check mark.
When the data transfer to the database landing tables is complete, the load process runs a set of rules for the incoming data. If the data passes these checks, the load process continues.
Errors
If the database checks result in errors, the loading process is stopped, and the load workflow step fails. The system creates a load report that includes a list of the errors and quantities that you can review.
Warnings
If the database validation rules generate warnings only, the load process continues. The system creates a load report that includes a list of warnings and the quantities that you can review.
After any errors in the loaded data have been corrected, you can retry the load process. For more information, see Retry a failed load process.
A load report is available for download only if the Data Take-On job fails in the second stage of the load process. The information in the Microsoft Excel report allows you to take any corrective actions.
1. On the My Data Take-On Jobs page, select a Data Take-On job, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Load Report link available below the LOAD progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
What does Data Take-On check for when loading the data into the database?
The following database checks are run on the data transferred to the database landing tables.
Name
Description
Severity
Cardinality Check
Relationships are checked to see if the cardinality of the relationship definition has been breached. If
that occurs, the load is blocked.
Error
Exclusively Claimed
If the object being updated exists outside the user's selected configuration and it is claimable, but has
been exclusively claimed to a different sub configuration, the load is blocked.
Error
File Object Internal System Property Values
Changes to the following internal system property values on ISPFFile are ignored:
SPFLocalDirectory
SPFLocalHostName
SPFLocalFileName
SPFRemoteFileName
These values are all set automatically so do not need to be mapped.
Warning
File Property Action Check
Only one Check Staging File Exists property action is allowed per object. If there is more than one, an
error is raised and the load is blocked.
Error
Future Issue Dates
Future issue dates are not allowed. If any are found, the load is blocked. Dates are validated against the
UTC time zone.
Error
Master Document Updates
If the load includes a master document with a revision and the master exists outside of the user's
selected configuration, the system assumes that the user is revising the document to the configuration
and does not want the previous ‘Updating item from lower configuration’ rule to block the load. If this
occurs, a warning message is produced, but the load is not blocked. Subsequent updates to the master
are ignored.
Warning
Object Identification
If the object being loaded is identified by a UID, the database's class definition it is mapped to is
checked to see if it has a UID definition. Also, if you are loading an object using a unique key, the
database's class definition is checked to see if it has a unique key definition.
Error
Revision Code Check
If a revision is using a non-custom revision scheme and the order of the issue dates does not match the
order of the revision codes, the load is blocked.
Error
Revision Issue Date
If there are two revisions for the same master with the same issue date, the load is blocked. This check
applies to working (null) issue dates as well.
Error
Updating item from lower configuration
If the object being updated exists outside the user's selected configuration and the object is not
claimable, the load is blocked. If the object is claimable, the load claims it when updating the object in
the load.
Error
Retry a failed load process
If an error occurs during the Load step, such as when a database timeout error occurs, the job fails. You can retry the loading by completing the failed step related to this job from the To Do List. Data Take-On attempts to resume the load
process from the last good state, re-loading data, if necessary.
For more information about configuring Data Take-On, see How do I configure Data Take-On?
For more information about running Data Take-On jobs, see Loading documents and data using Data Take-On.
.
Data Take-On troubleshooting
The following topics will help you troubleshoot any issues encountered while using Data Take-On.
Import
The following topics provide troubleshooting for issues that may arise during the import process.
Import mapping: Mapped related object property is not saved
Error message
Only column headers are saved. Mappings could not be saved because the parent mapping for the primary or related object is not saved. Ensure the parent mapping is saved.
Problem
During import mapping, the mapping for the mapped related object property is not saved.
This might occur if the parent of the mapped related object property is saved after the mapped related object property, or a staging relationship definition with the same name already exists in the system.
Solution
Save the parent of the mapped related object property first, then save the mapped related object property.
Replace the prefix in the Staging Relationship Definition box with a different value.
Validation
The following topics provide troubleshooting for issues that may arise during the validation process.
Invalid value for enum level on property
Error message
Value is invalid for enum level on target property
Problem
The above error is observed in the validation report after validation stage.
Cause
The target property scoped by the EnumListLevelType is mapped to an enum value. However, either the parent EnumListType is not part of the import mapping or it does not contain the enum type value.
Solution
You must ensure that the parent EnumListType is part of the import mapping and that it contains the correct enum type value. For more information, see Validation rule description.
Job Processing - CSV files
The following topics provide troubleshooting for issues that may arise when jobs are processed in Data Take-On Job Management.
Error when file object is loaded before the object to which it is attached
Error message
Object reference not set to the instance of an object
Problem
When loading files, an error occurs when you attempt to load a file object before you load the object to which it is attached, such as when you set the load object weight to load a design file before the design document that is related to the
file.
Cause
This issue occurs because the file exists in the database before the revision to which it should be attached.
Solution
You must set the load object weighting to load a file object before loading the file to which it is attached. For example, you must set the load object weight so that the design document will be loaded before the design file.
 You can adjust the load object weight by selecting Data Take-On > Load Definitions > Actions 
 > Create Load Object Mappings > Details > Object Weighting. For more information on setting the load object weightings, see
Define load object mappings.
Object weights have not been configured correctly
Error message
File CSV Validation Failed [one or more load object mappings have zero object weight]
Problem
Job cannot be processed in Data Take-On.
Solution
Ensure that the object weights are set correctly for load object mappings and that none of the weights is set to zero.
 You can adjust the load object weight by selecting Data Take-On > Load Definitions > Actions 
 > Create Load Object Mappings > Details > Object Weighting. For more information on setting the load object weightings, see
Define load object mappings.
Job is not created with error: Object successfully created, but failed to be attached to the workflow
Problem
You may get an error when trying to create a Data Take-On job in the Data Take-On feature set.
Object successfully created, but failed to be attached to the workflow
Cause
This error may occur in the following cases:
In the CSV file, there are rows with columns that do not match the column header.
The number of columns in the CSV file do not match with the import mapping columns created in Data Take-On.
Solution
You must ensure that the data in the CSV file matches the data in the specified import definition by comparing the CSV file and the import mapping columns in the import definition. You must correct the data in the CSV file and create a
new Data Take-On job with the corrected CSV file. For more information on import mapping, see Define import mapping.
Load fails with error: The request limit for the database is 2000/3200 and has been reached
Error message
The request limit for the database is 2000/3200 and has been reached
Problem
Unable to load data into the database.
Cause
The database resources (worker processes) are insufficient.
Solution
Contact your system administrator.
Load fails at stage 1: Transferring data
Problem
Loading process may fail while transferring data with the following error message:
User must be related to organization to continue with registration
Cause
If no tool signature is available for the tool and the configuration into which the data is being loaded, Data Take-On creates a tool signature and then runs the Data Take-On job. However, if the user running a Data Take-On job is not related
to any organization, the job fails with this error message.
Solution
The user running the job must be updated with the correct organization by the administrator.
Load fails with error: Transferring data failed
Error message
Transferring data failed
Problem
Loading process may fail during the transferring data stage with the above error.
Solution
You can get additional information by viewing the exact error message in the View Summary page from the Actions 
 menu of the selected job in Show My Data Take-On Jobs. For more information, see Viewing job progress and
statistics.
Load fails at stage 3: Loading data to database
Problem
Loading process may fail during the third stage of loading data to the database with the following error message:
Unique key or query def was not unique
Cause
This error occurs when the object you are trying to create already exists in the database.
Solution
Remove the duplicate object from your system.
Load fails at stage 3: Data is partially loaded into the database
Problem
The loading process fails during the third stage of loading data to the database. For example, you load 1000 tags, and the process stops after loading 500 tags.
Solution
Run a new Data Take-On job using the same CSV file. The 500 tags that were loaded successfully in the previous job will be updated and the remaining 500 tags will now be created.
Integrating with design tools
Sharing data
 You can share data only to projects in the active state.
 Currently, the share feature supports only Smart 3D, Smart Instrumentation, Smart Electrical, and Smart P&ID sharing.
Authoring tools, once registered with HxGN SDx2, can share object data or documents to be stored in the database. In fact, one of the primary purposes of HxGN SDx2 is to be a common repository where you can gather information about
objects from multiple tools and view the discrepancies between data from one tool to another
Once the administrator has created the registration relationship and has set up the tool's data domain, the rest of the sharing is performed from the authoring tool itself. Most of the tasks for performed inside HxGN SDx2 once the project is
underway is done by administrators, who can view the progress of the data as it enters the database and merges with data about the same objects from different authoring tools.
For more information about what sharing is and what kinds of sharing are supported, see Sharing documents and drawings.
For more information about the tasks of the administrator overseeing the compilation of data from the various authoring tools, see Monitoring the progress of loaded data.
Continuous sharing
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | Sharing vs. Continuous Sharing Overview <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/65f1c91d29a46ded5b7bfec4>
 Currently, the share feature supports only Smart 3D, Smart Instrumentation, Smart Electrical, and Smart P&ID sharing.
When you share data from a design tool or using continuous share, all the data and the graphics are sent to HxGN SDx2 and stored in the database.
Data is transferred to HxGN SDx2 through web APIs in the JSON format, rather than the XML format.
The design tools share data in JSON format. The sharing process places the JSON file in the database and loads the data from the JSON files to the HxGN SDx2 project. After the document is shared, users of that software or other design
tools can synchronize with the data from the JSON file.
Graphics files are stored in the database and related to the shared data in the JSON file. Once the graphics data is in the database, you can view the object in HxGN SDx2, with all the applicable data stored for the object.
Sharing documents and drawings
Sharing a document for design review
You can share data only to projects in the active state.
Be sure the Integration scheduler is started before sharing data or documents from an authoring tool.
When you share a document for design review using continuous sharing, you create either a graphical document or a non-graphical document in the HxGN SDx2 database.
For graphical documents, the viewable shared file is related to the graphical document object, along with the data file and the tombstone file. Once shared, the authoring tool continuously checks for any new graphical documents, and
automatically replaces and updates the viewable shared file.
For non-graphical documents, the shared report file, in its native format, is attached to the non-graphical document objects, along with any other files sent by the authoring tool.
All files shared with HxGN SDx2 and related to the graphical and non-graphical documents are stored in the database, and the information in them is available for other authoring tools to consume.
Sharing a revisable document
Alternatively, you can share a revisable document, which shares the entire document along with the tags associated with it.
When you first share a revisable document, a three-level complex document object is created in the HxGN SDx2 database. This three-level document object includes a Document Master, Document Revision, and Document Version. On
subsequent occurrences of sharing the document with HxGN SDx2, only new revisions or versions are created. The software relates revisions to the master document. Changes in the document status of a related revision update the
status of the subsequently shared versions and revisions of the document.
When you share a revisable document, the authoring tool sends four files to the HxGN SDx2 database:
An associated data file that contains graphical representation objects and relationships to tags. Graphical representations, when loaded, are used for viewing and navigation between the graphics and the tags.
A metadata file that contains the information necessary to create the complex document objects in the database.
A tombstone file that contains information about tags in the document that existed but have been removed.
A visual representation of the document that you can view without the authoring tool. For many Hexagon applications, this is the application's native format. The viewable file can also be a Microsoft Excel® workbook or another
viewable file type, such as PDF or DOC. You can review the visual representation of the document, which is attached to the document revision, using SmartPlant Markup Plus or Smart Review. You can also mark up the visual
representation of the document using SmartPlant Markup Plus.
HxGN SDx2 creates relationships between all four of those files and the document version being created with the share operation. The data, metadata, and tombstone files are also related to the graphical, or native file.
If you are sharing from any authoring tool, the major and minor revision lists of the selected document or documents appear only if you have not revised the document using the HxGN SDx2 revision or authoring tool revision.
The Comment box allows you to enter information about the selected shareable documents.
All the owning groups available in HxGN SDx2 are displayed for sharing.
A document that has been previously signed-off can be re-shared into HxGN SDx2. You must revise this document to share it again.
When multiple documents are selected for sharing, only property values provided by all the selected documents appear in the grid. Changing a value in the grid changes that value for all the selected documents.
Create a revisable document from Smart P&ID or Smart Instrumentation
Documents are shared from Smart P&ID and Smart Instrumentation using the continuous sharing feature only. To view those documents in HxGN SDx2 for internal review or external issue, you must create a revision of the document in
HxGN SDx2.
 When the design tool sends the document to HxGN SDx2 using continuous sharing for the first time, HxGN SDx2 creates the document in the database in the Reserved state, but does not create a version or revision of it.
The following procedure describes the steps that you must perform in HxGN SDx2 after the continuous share of a document or drawing from the design tool to create a document revision for viewing, issuing, or sending for review.
 You must have either an Engineer or Document Controller role to do this.
1. From the Query and create scope dialog box, select either P&ID or Instrumentation in the Integration Data Filter section. For more information about setting the integration data filter, see Filter data by design component.
2. Select Design Tool Integration > Integration Documents Only, and find the document you want to review.
 You can also find your document by selecting Documents > Smart Active Documents when your Integration Data Filter is set to P&ID or Instrumentation or by selecting Documents > All Documents when your
Integration Data Filter is set to Consolidated.
3. Select Actions 
 > Create document version.
If you have never done this process to create a first version of this continuously shared document before, HxGN SDx2 creates a first revision, using appropriate revision schema for your organization or the document type, and a
first working version.
If the document has been shared before and the last revision was signed off in HxGN SDx2, you are prompted to create a new revision using the Revise dialog box. For more information about creating a revision, see Revise a
document.
If the document has been shared before and the last version is still in the Working state, HxGN SDx2 creates a new working version, increasing the version number by one.
Once you have a working version, you can issue it internally or externally for review or sign off.
Share a revisable document or drawing
If you are sharing a drawing with the first revision, refer to Share a new revisable document or drawing.
Unlike continuous sharing for design review, sharing a revisable drawing is initiated using the Share command from the design tool.
You can only share documents or drawings that have a security code that matches the security codes assigned to your user in HxGN SDx2. The security code for the document appears as a read-only value.
1. From within the design tool, select Design Tool Integration > Share.
 This command is available only if you have registered the active project. Refer to Register from a design tool for more information about registering your design tool.
2. Review the information displayed about the selected document or documents, and provide any missing information.
3. Select OK to complete the sharing procedure.
Share a new revisable document or drawing
The following steps illustrate the workflow for sharing a document that needs an initial revision.
 Unlike continuous sharing for design review, sharing a revisable document is initiated using the Share command from the design tool.
1. Select Design Tool Integration > Share. For more information, see Share a revisable document or drawing.
2. Choose documents to share from the Share dialog box, and select OK.
3. On the New Revision Required dialog box, select the new documents that you want to revise.
4. Select Yes to revise the new document.
5. Select a revision scheme with which to revise the new document on the Revise dialog box. For more information on revising documents, see Revising a document.
6. Choose a security code to be applied to the new document or drawing. The options available are limited to the security codes you have access to.
Only users with access to the same security code as the document are able to see it in HxGN SDx2 or revise it again later.
Security codes are assigned to users per configuration in HxGN SDx2. For more information see, Assign security codes to a user.
7. Select OK.
8. Select OK on the Share dialog box to complete the sharing process.
Share signed-off or superseded documents or drawings
The following steps illustrate the workflow for sharing a signed-off document.
Unlike continuous sharing for design review, sharing a revisable document is initiated using the Share command from the authoring tool.
You can only share documents or drawings that have a security code that matches the security codes assigned to your user in HxGN SDx2. The security code for the document appears as a read-only value.
1. Select Design Tool Integration > Share. For more information, see Share a revisable document or drawing.
2. Select documents to share from the Share dialog box, and select OK.
3. On the New Revision Required dialog box, select the signed-off or superseded documents that you want to revise.
4. Select Yes to revise the signed-off document.
5. After the Revise dialog box appears, select a revision scheme with which to revise the signed-off document. For more information on revising documents, see Revising a document.
6. Select OK.
7. Select OK on the Share dialog box to complete the sharing process.
Revisions and Versions of Shared Documents and Drawings
 Unlike continuous sharing for design review, sharing a revisable drawing is initiated using the Share command from the design tool.
The first time you share a revisable document, the software creates a new document master and the first revision in HxGN SDx2. A revision (major) is an officially recognized change to a document. A version (minor) is an intermediate
update that you have shared. Revisions can be shared for distributing the data, or they can go through the approval process, depending on your needs. Each revision of a document can have multiple versions.
After the first share of a revisable document, the software creates new versions and revisions. Subsequent revisions are related to the master document and stored as revisions.
Some tools provide the ability to manage revision information and display it in the title block. When you enter the revision information (revision number, description, and so forth) in the tool, that information is shared with the document and
is used to define the revision. If the tool does not provide this, you can select this information from the available list during the sharing process.
Changes in the document status of a related revision change the status of the master document.
When you share data from a design tool, you may not be able to view all the properties that you shared in HxGN SDx2.
Shareable documents from Smart 3D
Document type
Shared output
Graphic report format
Notes
Orthographic drawings
Data JSON file with 2D representations and links to 3D
model data
2D graphic file (.sha)
Isometric drawings
Data JSON file with 2D representations and links to 3D
model data
2D graphic file (.sha)
Additional files such as
.pcf, .pod, neutral files
(.b), and reports (.txt) are
shared along with the
.sha file.
Reports
Data JSON file with 2D representations and links to 3D
model data
Excel spreadsheet file
Loading shared documents and drawings
 The first shared revision of a document creates a Facility Data Warehouse (FDW) document master in the database. Each FDW document has a mandatory relationship to SPFPrimaryClassification. Complete the mapping
between Common Schema and the HxGN SDx2 schema to identify this classification.
When you share a graphical document, you can check the load status of your graphical document, and, if the system encounters an error loading the document, you can retry the share.
Tell me more about loading shared documents and drawings
