After you share a document, either a revisable document or a document for design review, HxGN SDx2 processes the tool data and the tombstone files from the design tool, using the Scheduler Service.
The SDxiHubPublishDocLoadSchedulerTask polls periodically for the files that need to be processed, and then processes them to load the graphical file, and the relationships between the document and the shared files, to the
database.
 HxGN SDx2 uses the Business Admin user to poll for and load documents with the SDxiHubPublishDocLoadSchedulerTask. This user must have Business Administrator role permissions at Configuration Top in the inherited
mode or at the level of the specific plant or project these documents were shared to for them to be processed.
In HxGN SDx2, it is the administrator's job monitor the loading of documents and drawings shared from the authoring tool and take the necessary steps to resolve issues that arise during that process. You can find more information about
that in the Monitoring the progress of loaded data section.
Sharing with file mode
The sharing feature sends objects and their data to HxGN SDx2. If you want to review the drawing before the changes are updated in HxGN SDx2, you can share using File Mode. The files shared using File Mode are saved in the
location specified in the Folder to save XML files box.
 Before sharing, you must register the project and connect through File Mode.
1. In the design tool, select Integration > Share.
 You need to share with File Mode enabled.
2. Select one or more documents in the Select Documents dialog box.
3. In the Share dialog box, review the information displayed about the selected document or documents, and provide any missing information required.
 You can change the name of the data file and meta-data file, if necessary.
4. Select OK.
 If the OK button is unavailable, verify that the path listed in the Folder to save XML files box is set to a valid location that you have read/write permission to.
View the Inconsistency Report for a consolidated object
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | View the Inconsistency Report for a Consolidated Object <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6610953052213f8e81dafdd0>
When you share data, the objects are placed or updated in the consolidated design component. Objects that are consolidated can have inconsistencies between the existing information in the consolidated design component and the new
information from your most recent share. You can view inconsistencies in the Inconsistency Report for object.
1. In the Tags, Assets and Models feature set, select Inconsistent object to search for the relevant objects.
2. In the Results grid, select an object.
3. Select Actions 
 > View Data Inconsistencies.
 Inconsistent values are highlighted. If an inconsistent value is a relationship to another object, the inconsistent objects are listed by their names, separated by commas when multiple related objects are present.
Tell me more about the Inconsistency Report
Every design component for which a tool is expected to contribute information to a consolidated object has a column in the Inconsistency Report for object screen. HxGN SDx2 expects information for objects that have a class definition
that is related to a design component. Only non-authoring design components that have a tool signature registered with HxGN SDx2 have visible columns.
 The Integration Engine uses the CorrelationID to identify the tool objects to determine inconsistencies.
 You can change the sorting of the results list on the Inconsistency Report for object screen by selecting a column heading.
View the Tag Existence Dashboard
The Tag Existence Dashboard displays all of the tags in the current Create scope and whether they exist in the different design components.
The dashboard is available as a home screen dashboard. When displaying the home screen dashboards, navigate to the Tag Existence Dashboard using the controls at the bottom of the page. For more information on using dashboards,
see Using an InsightView dashboard.
 The dashboard is available to users with the Project Engineer, Discipline Engineer, or Business Administrator role.
What does the Tag Existence Dashboard look like?
The numbered sections of the dashboard are described below.
1. Design Components: Use this section to select the design components to include in the dashboard.
2. Overall Tag Existence: This displays the percentage of tags, in the current Create scope, that exist (or are not expected to exist) in the selected design components.
3. Tag Existence by Class: For each tag class, this displays the percentage of tags, in the current Create scope, that exist (or are not expected to exist) in the selected design components.
 Select a section of the chart to filter the results grid to show only the tags in the selected section.
4. Results grid: This displays each tag, its class, and whether or not it exists in each design component.
Select Actions 
 to display the Actions menu for the selected tags, or for the specific tag if none are selected.
Use  to filter the tags based on class and whether or not the tag exists in each design component.
Use 
 to remove all filters from the results grid.
The following icons are used in the results grid:
Icon
Description
The tag exists in this design component.
The tag is missing from this design component, and is expected to exist.
The tag is missing from this design component, but is not expected to exist.
 This tag is included in the percentage of tags that exist in the Overall Tag Existence and Tag Existence by Class charts.
Select a section of the Tag Existence by Class chart to filter the results grid to display only the tags that are in the selected section.
Select Actions 
 to display the Actions menu for the tag or selected tags.
 If there are no design components available for selection, the design components have been limited by your administrator using the Review Tag Existence method, and the design components available are not correctly
configured.
Revising documents
You can edit an existing document and create revisions of the document with major and minor revision value sets. The revision value depends on the selected revision scheme. When revising a document, you can change the major and
minor revision values on the document.
You can revise your document in HxGN SDx2 and the revised values are saved to the tool database without having to share the document.
You can revise a document using any of the previous revisions available from the last shared revision.
For example, if you revise a new document using the revision scheme RevAlpha (A, B, C, D…) and revision C, then HxGN SDx2 reserves revision number C for the document. If you are revising the same document with RevAlpha, you
can use any of the previous revision numbers, such as A or B. But, if you share the document to HxGN SDx2 with revision number C, you can only use revision numbers D and above. The following table contains the available revision
numbers based on the document revision state in HxGN SDx2:
Document revision state
Next available revision number
Working (C)
C and up
Current (C)
D and up
A tool can revise documents without sharing them only if the tool's SmartPlant adapter indicates that the tool supports HxGN SDx2 revisions.
You can revise your document in HxGN SDx2 and the revised values are saved to the tool database without having to share the document.
You can revise a document into a project only. Revising a document into a plant is not currently supported. For more information, see Revising a document.
Currently, documents can be revised to only one project at a time.
Revising a document
You can revise only if you have done the following:
Registered to the active project.
Implemented the HxGN SDx2 revision functionality for the design tool.
Currently, you can revise a document into only one project at a time.
1. From the design tool, select Design Tool Integration > Revise.
 If you are using a new document or a document that does not yet have a defined revision scheme, select the revision scheme you want to use from the Revision Scheme Name list.
2. In the Revise in Tool section, select the next available major and minor revision values.
 The current revisions of the tool are not available in the Major and Minor lists. You can select the current revisions of the tool only if they are not active.
3. Select OK.
Any revision scheme that was previously assigned to a document can be changed even if the document has been shared. For example, if the original revision scheme for a document was set as Rev01A, you can change the
revision scheme to Rev1A at any time.
A progress bar displays the progress of the revise operation.
Operate and maintain a plant
HxGN SDx2 allows you to capture, organize, and link large volumes of facility information and provide web-based access to everyone who needs it.
Create and manage changes to the plant
HxGN SDx2 provides two automated workflows to execute changes to your plant.
Change request workflow allows you to execute simple changes to your plant within your company or execute and collaborate on large projects with contractors.
Temporary change request workflow allows you to create temporary change requests, where the change is reverted to the original state once an expiration date is reached.
What does the change request status mean?
Status
Description
Open
The change request has been created.
In Progress
The change request has been prepared, and has not yet been approved.
Approved
The change request has been approved, but not yet carried out.
Completed
The closure approval step has been completed and any conflicts have been resolved.
Rejected
The change request has been rejected.
Change request workflow
 In the change request workflow, the following steps are implemented at the plant level
Prepare the change request
Evaluate feasibility
Co-ordinate the change request
Review proposed solution
The change request workflow provides a simple process for tracking, reviewing, and coordinating change requests. Your administrator might change the change request workflow to meet your company requirements.
Temporary change request workflow
A temporary change request is used to manage a change that has an expiration date. The change request is created and progresses through a workflow where the change is carried out. When the expiration date is approaching, the
request is sent into the Review temporary change workflow where the change is either reverted back to its original state or extended until a later date.
 The change request is sent to the Review temporary change workflow a number of days before the expiration date is reached. The number of days is set by your system administrator as described in Schedule a task to start a
review temporary change workflow.
Initial temporary change request workflow
 Click a box in the diagram to go to the associated procedure.
Review temporary change workflow
 Click a box in the diagram to go to the associated procedure.
What do you want to do?
Create a change request
Prepare the change request
Analyze the impact of the requested change
Evaluate the change request
Coordinate the change request
Review and approve or reject the change
Correct a change request failure
Complete the change request
Evaluate whether a temporary change is still required
Approve a temporary change extension
View the workflow of change request
Search for change requests
Create a change request
1. In the Change Management feature set, select Create Change Request.
2. Type the required information on all tabs and select Next.
3. Attach any related files from your computer to the change request.
4. Select:
Prepare Change to send the change request to the Prepare Content step. This step allows you to update the change request with related documents and tags, and to mark up the changes you are requesting. For more
information, see Prepare the change request.
Finish to create a To Do List task for the Evaluate feasibility step to be carried out. For more information, see Evaluate the change request.
Create a change request within a markup layer
For all the file types associated with a document, you can create a change request from within a markup layer. It is a two-step process:
You place a specific image, called a change request pin, on the file, and enter the details of the change request.
The Web Client creates the change request, which is displayed in your To Do list for you to Prepare the change request. The Web Client also attaches a changemark to the change request pin, so that you can see the change request
information on the View and Markup with Change Request page.
1. Search for the required document.
2. From the document's Actions menu, select View and Markup with Change Request.
 This option is available only for the current signed-off version of the document.
3. If you are viewing a Hexagon 2D drawing with a markup rendition (PDF) generated, select the Markup tab.
4. Select Markup 
, and select:
New markup to add the change request pin in a new markup layer
Open for edit to add the change request pin to an existing markup layer
5. From the expanded toolbar, select Image.
6. Select Image 
, and then select Image Options 
 to choose the image your organization uses for change request pins.
Even if several images are available, only one can be used as a change request pin. Change requests are only created when you choose the image that your organization uses for change request pins.
 The default image is GreenActionPin 
. However, your administrator might have changed it using the SDACreateClassObjAndRelFromMarkup client API.
7. Select anywhere in the file to place the change request pin.
 To move or resize the change request pin, select 
, and select the change request pin.
8. Create the change request as described in Create a change request.
9. Select Markup 
, and select Save Markup.
 When a change request is completed any related change request pins are automatically deleted.
Prepare the change request
1. Select the Change Request in your To Do List 
, and then select Actions 
 > Prepare change.
 The Prepare Change page is automatically displayed when you select Prepare Change when creating a change request.
The Prepare Change page provides a single location to update your change request, such as:
Adding/Updating details
Marking up the changes that you are requesting
Attaching drawings, files, or other documents
Relating documents and tags
2. Carry out the required tasks as described below and select Complete to send the change request to the Evaluate feasibility step. For more information, see Evaluate the change request
 You can also select Save as Draft to save the change request so that you can continue working on it later.
You can do the following in the same page when you proceed to further steps:
Review any rejected change actions
Initiate an internal change action
Edit or add details
Select Update to add or change details of the change request.
Relate documents and tags
1. Select Manage Documents or Manage Tags to display the documents or tags in the Results window.
2. Select the documents or tags to include.
You can also search for documents or tags in the search bar.
You can use the Filter by options to return more targeted search results.
3. Select RELATE.
The related documents and tags are displayed in their respective DOCUMENT VERSIONS and TAGS tabs. You can use these tabs to relate additional documents and tags:
a. In the relevant tab, search for required documents or tags using the search bar.
b. Select the documents or tags in the list of results, and select RELATE.
You can also use FILTER options when searching for documents.
Attach files
Select Manage related files to attach files from your computer to the change request.
View potential conflicts and impacts
Select Potential Conflicts or Potential Impacts to Analyze the impact of the requested change.
Analyze the impact of the requested change
The Change Impact Analysis page allows you to do the following:
Add and remove documents or tags from the action.
Review conflicts with other actions.
Send technical queries about documents or tags in your or another person's change action.
 The items in the Potential Impacts and Potential Conflicts lists can be filtered so that you can easily find the required items.
Add or remove documents or tags
When you select a tag or document, additional tags and documents that could be impacted by the change are displayed and can be easily added to the change request.
1. Select the tag or document in the list.
2. In the Potential Impacts list, select the tags or documents to add, and select Apply to relate them to your change request.
If the item is already related, you can remove it from the change request. Select Delete 
 next to the tag or document to remove it.
 Expand 
 any document to find out more about its status and issue purpose.
Review conflicts
1. Select the tag or document related to the change request.
2. In the Potential Conflicts list, review the items that could conflict with your requested change.
 Only items with the status of In Progress or Issued are displayed.
3. Select the item to view the information and responses for the change request.
4. Select Impact Analysis from the item's 
 Edit menu to view its impact analysis information.
Evaluate the change request
Why should I evaluate the feasibility of a change request?
Once a change request is created, the feasibility of the request can be evaluated. If one or more users can carry out the task, claim the step first and then evaluate it. You can review the impact analysis and identify the potential conflicts of
the submitted change request.
1. Select Evaluate feasibility in your To Do List, and then select Evaluate Change on the Actions menu.
Select Manage Documents or Manage Tags to relate a document or tag to the change request. For more information, see Relate documents and tags to the change request given below.
Use Potential Impacts and Potential Conflicts to Analyze the impact of the requested change.
2. Select:
Approve to complete the evaluation step and send it to the next step.
 If this is a temporary change request, a merged PDF rendition of the change request is created, including markups.
Rework to return the change to the Prepare Change Request step for the originator to make additional changes.
The rejection reason is displayed on the Prepare Change page so that the originator can see why it was rejected.
When a change request is sent for rework at the Evaluate feasibility step, a new revision of the change request is created.
Reject Change to reject the change request and send it to the Completion Notice step. For more information, see Rejecting a change request.
3. If you selected Reject Change or Rework, enter the reason.
After the Evaluate feasibility step,
A merged PDF rendition of the change request is created, including markups and attached files. This PDF rendition is available for the change request even after it is closed.
Once the change request is approved, the change request workflow implements the process step "Review Document Version" which automatically creates a new revision for the document related and navigates to the next
step.
Relate documents and tags to the change request
1. Select Manage Documents or Manage Tags to display the documents or tags in the Results window.
2. Select the documents or tags to include.
You can also search for documents or tags in the search bar.
You can use the Filter by options to return more targeted search results.
3. Select RELATE.
The related documents and tags are displayed in their respective DOCUMENT VERSIONS and TAGS tabs. You can use these tabs to relate additional documents and tags:
a. In the relevant tab, search for required documents or tags using the search bar.
b. Select the documents or tags in the list of results, and select RELATE.
You can also use FILTER options when searching for documents.
Coordinate the change request
Set an action or contract to the documents related to your change request. A warning message is indicated if a document is not set to either an action or a contract.
You must have the MoC Owner role assigned to coordinate the change.
1. Select Coordinate change implementation in your To Do List, and then select Coordinate Implementation on the Actions menu.
2. Use Potential Impacts and Potential Conflicts to Analyze the impact of the requested change.
3. Select Complete when the requested changes are implemented.
To review the proposed solutions, log on as a user with the MoC Approver role to Review and approve or reject the change to prepare it for the next workflow step.
Review and approve or reject the change
1. Select the review or approval task from your To Do List, and then select the appropriate step on the Actions menu.
2. If required when reviewing, relate any documents and tags to the change request.
How can I relate documents and tags when reviewing the change request?
 When reviewing the change request, you can relate it to the documents and tags as shown below:
Plant scope:
You can view and relate only signed off documents.
1. Select Manage Documents or Manage Tags to display the documents or tags in the Results window.
2. Select the documents or tags to include.
You can also search for documents or tags in the search bar.
You can use the Filter by options to return more targeted search results.
3. Select RELATE.
The related documents and tags are displayed in their respective DOCUMENT VERSIONS and TAGS tabs. You can use these tabs to relate additional documents and tags:
a. In the relevant tab, search for required documents or tags using the search bar.
b. Select the documents or tags in the list of results, and select RELATE.
You can also use FILTER options when searching for documents.
3. Select:
Approve to complete the workflow step and send it to the next step.
What happens when I approve the workflow step?
After you review and approve the changes, the workflow step is completed and navigated to either of the following process steps:
SignOff Change request: The process step is automatically implemented to signoff the related documents and send to the next step.
Completion notice: You are required to implement the process step to complete the change request workflow. For more information, see Complete the change request.
Rework to return the change to a previous step.
 When a change request is sent for rework at the Review Proposed Solution step, a new revision of the change request is created.
Reject Change to reject the change request and send it to the Completion Notice step. For more information, see Rejecting a change request.
4. If you selected Reject Change or Rework, enter the reason.
 The reason entered for the rework is displayed on the Coordinate Implementation page so that the change coordinator can see why it was sent for reworking.
Rejecting a change request
When you reject a change request, enter a reason for the rejection. It is then sent to the Completion Notice step, and the details of the change request are updated with the rejection reason, rejection date, and the name of the user
who rejected the change.
 If an attached document has already been revised, the latest revisions of the document are terminated.
Correct a change request failure
 You must have the MoC Owner role to correct the failures.
1. Select the Correct Failure step in your To Do List, and then select Correct Failure on the Actions menu.
2. Select Manage Related Information in the Correct Failures page to relate documents, tags, and other objects as required.
3. Select Complete if the failure is corrected, or Reject to close the change request. The change request then progresses to the next step.
Complete the change request
Select Completion notice in your To Do List, and select Completed on the Actions menu.
 When a change request is completed any related change request pins are automatically deleted.
Evaluate whether a temporary change is still required
1. Select Evaluate Change in your To Do List, and then select Evaluate Change on the Actions menu.
2. Select:
Extend if the change is still required and should be extended.
 If you select to extend the change, you must enter a reason for the extension.
Close if the change should be reverted to its original state.
Approve a temporary change extension
1. Select Approve extension in your To Do List, and then select Review Change on the Actions menu.
2. Select Update.
3. In the Expiration date field, select the new date to revert the changes, and then select Update.
4. Select Approve to approve the extension.
 Rework sends the change back to the Evaluate change step.
View the workflow of change request
1. In the Change Management feature set, select Change Requests.
2. Select View workflow on the Actions menu of the change request. In the View Workflow page, you can view the progress of the workflow.
Search for change requests
Use the search options in the Change Management feature set to find change requests.
View the Plant Life Cycle Changes dashboard
 You can view this dashboard if you are assigned to any of the following roles: Engineer, MoC Coordinator, MoC Approver, Planner, Business Administrator, System Administrator
The Plant Life Cycle Changes dashboard displays all the change requests in the plant scope.
What does the Plant Life Cycle Changes dashboard look like?
Open the dashboard
1. Select Dashboards 
 to display the home screen dashboards.
 If home screen dashboards are set as your home page, select Home 
 to display the dashboards you can access. For more information, see Using an InsightView dashboard.
2. Navigate to the Plant Life Cycle Changes dashboard using the controls at the bottom of the page.
The following table summarizes the widgets on the dashboard and the information they display. This information can be used to coordinate the plant life cycle changes.
Widget
Purpose
TODO LIST
Displays a summary of your To Do List
OPEN CHANGE REQUEST BY PRIORITY
Displays pie chart for both normal and temporary change requests by priority such as Low/Medium/High
PRIORITY OPEN CHANGE REQUESTS COUNT
Displays both normal and temporary change requests count by status such as Open, In Progress, Approved and High Priority
(III - High)
PENDING CHANGE REQUESTS
Displays both normal and temporary change requests count which are not yet approved, whose status is Open and In Progress
ALL CHANGE REQUESTS
Displays bar Chart for both normal and temporary change requests by status and priority.
OPEN TEMPORARY CHANGE REQUESTS COUNT
Displays count of temporary change requests whose status is set to Open
Create and manage data books
A data book is a collection of structured documents that contain sections or chapters with other documents. You can create various types of data books such as vendor data books, fabrication records, systems data books, and so on.
Why do you need data books?
As a document controller, you maintain a lot of maintenance records, fabrication records, vendor records, and so on. A data book helps you maintain records as a collection of structured documents.
Create a data book
1. In the Data Books feature set, select Create Data Book.
2. Select a data book type, followed by Next.
 When assigning classification to the object, you can use the Classification search bar to search and filter for the appropriate classifications based on their Name and Description.
3. Use the Item owner(s) list to specify one or more data book owner(s). If the owner is not assigned, the current user (who creates the data book) will be set as the data book owner by default.
 Only the data book owner(s) can update, manage, and terminate a data book. They are also the only user who can generate the consolidated PDF for a data book.
4. Complete the remaining information, and select Finish.
Update a data book
Search for the data book, and then select Actions 
 > Update Data Book.
Terminate a data book
Search for the data book, and then select Actions 
 > Terminate. Objects are successfully terminated.
Manage data books
You can create and order chapters in your data book.
In the Data Books feature set, select the data book, and then select Actions 
 > Manage Data Book.
 Only the data book owner can manage a data book. The data book owner is one or more user(s) selected from the Item owner(s) list for the data book or the user who created the data book if an owner was not selected.
Edit the description
Select 
 > Edit 
 to edit the description of the data book or its chapters.
Create a chapter
You can create two types of data book chapters:
Dynamic - Displays the latest version of the attached document.
Static - Displays a specific version of the attached document.
To create a data book chapter:
1. Select 
 to add a new chapter.
2. Type the required information, and select the Chapter Type from the list.
You can view the description of the data book chapters and sub-chapters from the data book's tree view, provided if the description exists for the chapters or sub-chapters.
You can expand or collapse the data book structure at chapter and data book level.
 The data book chapter(s) and sub-chapter(s) which has content are indicated by the icon 
. An empty data book chapter or sub-chapter is indicated by the icon 
.
Reorder chapters
Select Reorder 
 and drag the data book chapters to where you want to see them in your data book.
You can also reorder data book chapters from the Data Book Chapters feature.
When a consolidated PDF is generated, the chapters are included in the selected order.
Relate objects to the data book
1. Select 
 to relate objects to the selected data book or chapter.
2. In the Relationship Builder page, select the Search tab, and search for the objects to relate, such as documents, assets, models, or tags.
3. Select the required objects, followed by Relate.
 The Search feature only displays objects that can be related to the selected data book or chapter type.
 The objects related to the data book and its chapters can be filtered so that you can easily find the related items.
The data book is related to its chapters and their sub-chapters.
For any data book chapter, you can perform the following actions on the related documents and tags:
To sign off one or more documents, select corresponding document(s), and click Actions 
 > Sign Off.
To edit tags and their property values, select corresponding tag(s), and click Actions 
 > List Editing > List Edit.
Copy structure
1. Select a data book and select Actions 
 > Copy Structure.
2. Type the required information and select Copy.
Select Pins 
 > New Items to view the copied data book.
Documents and tags related to the data book are not copied.
The copied chapters are in the same order as in the original data book.
Reorder chapters of your original data book prior to copy for the required order of the data book.
Copy content
You can copy the content along with the related documents, tags, and models to your data book.
1. Select a data book and select Actions 
 > Copy Content.
2. Type the required information and select Copy.
Select Pins 
 > New Items to view the copied data book.
The copied data book consists of all existing content from the original data book.
Reorder chapters of your original data book before you copy, for the required order of the data book.
Generate a consolidated PDF for a data book
You can combine all the PDFs of documents and files attached to a data book and its chapters into a single consolidated PDF.
 Only the data book owner can generate a consolidated PDF. The data book owner is one or more user(s) selected from the Item owner(s) list for the data book or the user who created the data book if an owner was not selected.
 The consolidated PDF generation of a data book is faster if you generate each individual PDF first for every file attached to the documents or data book chapters.
How are documents attached to a data book organized?
When you generate the PDF of a data book, the documents and files attached to the data book chapters are organized according to the order arranged while managing the data book.
1. Select the data book, and select Actions 
 > Generate Merged Rendition.
2. Select View Merged Rendition.
 You can configure the bookmarks for a merged rendition of a data book. Based on the defined settings, the bookmarks are displayed in the PDF generated from the data book. For more information, see Configure bookmarks for
the PDF generated from the data books.
Execute a project
Multi-organization collaboration is a key component in facilitating on time delivery of documentation throughout the design, construction, and handover phases of the project.
HxGN SDx2 provides a set of preconfigured collaboration work processes to manage document planning and receipt, technical queries, and action tracking.
Load the submittal documents/tags and perform document reviews
Submittals are the process by which the documents and tags are loaded into the HxGN SDx2 system. The loaded documents build the project Master Document Register (MDR). Relations between documents and tags can be loaded to
support easy navigation from tag to document and vice versa. The submittals are run online by contractors from sources outside of the trusted domain and HxGN SDx2 handles the transfer of the information to a secure location, scans the
files for viruses, validates the content, and loads it into the database. The submittal's content may include metadata and files, and an intermediate step can be configured for checking the malicious content. Where contractors are not
allowed online access to HxGN SDx2, the submittals are run on behalf of the contractor by the global document controllers.
As the content is loaded into the system, it requires a review by one or more experts to ensure that it complies with the project specifications and standards. As the deliverables are submitted into the system, they progress through the
quality assurance (QA) workflow which manages their review with markup and comment consolidation for return to the contractor. During this process, the reviewers can raise Review Actions that require monitoring to completion. The
Review Actions follow an independent lifecycle and workflow from that of the document, as they may impact many things and have a unique schedule for closing.
The review process is managed by the document review matrix.
Submittals
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Introduction to Submittals
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6200c5a59a28a802d9cde2ac/attempts/636130e2a143b1823df23e97?preferredLang=en>
A submittal is used in the collaboration process for contractors to send documents and the related files, tags and tag to document relationships, to the owning company's system. The submittal is a control file that contains all the details of
the submitted objects, including tags and any document file attachments. Each submittal has a defined classification that determines which workflow path the submittal process will follow.
When a submittal file is uploaded, the attached files are transferred to the database where the files can be scanned for viruses before being loaded into Data Take-On. Data Take-On validates the quality of the incoming data in the supplied
files before they are loaded into the system.
There are three types of submittals:
Master Document Registry (MDR) - Initiated by the contractor to send data in a master document register to the owner company.
MDR planning submittal - Creates and populates the MDR with a document delivery schedule. The document delivery schedule consists of reserved documents with associated delivery milestones for a given issue purpose,
on a planned date.
The planning submittal must have the document names, title, planned issue purpose, and date. For each submittal, a new revision of the MDR document must be supplied in the first row with a new revision and issue date.
 While processing the MDR planning submittal, if one or more issue purposes in the attached file are not related to the current plant or project, the validation rules stop the processing, and the user must relate the
issue purpose to the current plant or project to process the submittal.
MDR deliverable submittal - Issues documents with their associated files for the specified issue purpose. These documents should be identified on the MDR as planned documents.
The MDR document may be listed in the first row of the .csv file in all the MDR submittals. It must have a document number, revision, issue date, and file. Having an issue purpose is optional. For each submittal, a new
revision must be supplied with the revision and issue date.
The deliverable submittal requires each document to have a revision, issue date, file, and issue purpose. For each submittal, a new revision must be supplied with the revision and issue date, or the same revision must be
supplied with a different issue purpose.
Master Tag Registry (MTR) - Initiated by the contractor to send data in a master tag register to the owner company.
The MTR submittal process allows you to load two types of CSV file formats. The basic format contains the tag data and must have the tag number, tag classification, document number, revision, and type. The inverted format
contains the tag properties and must have the tag name.
Tag Document Relationships (TDR) - Initiated by the contractor to send data on tag document relationships to the owner company.
The tag document must have a tag number, tag classification, document number, and revision.
Planning submittal
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Planning Submittal <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6200ee74eeabc9545e556c08/attempts/6368a2f81bcd94d67375f212?
preferredLang=en>
MDR planning submittal can only work with Project configurations.
You must be a Contractor Document Control or Global Document Control to create the MDR planning submittal.
You can load the planned revision of a document before any data is loaded into the company's system, and relate it to plant equipment that is to be loaded into a document revision later. You can use the planned revision to compare the
data with the actual revision.
When you load the planned deliverables, the planned revision of the document has a status of RESERVED. When the actual deliverable is loaded into the system, the planned deliverables' status automatically changes to
RESERVEDSUPERSEDED. However, if the actual revisions are deleted for any reason, the planned revision status is changed back to RESERVED.
Steps
Description
MDR planning submittal
The MDR planning submittal templates are stored on Smart Community and available for download.
Create the MDR cover sheet
This file is stored on the MDR document and typically is a report from the contractor's system.
Populate the CSV file
Populate the CSV file with the deliverables and issue plan dates. The MDR document is usually listed as the first row of the file, though this is optional.
Create the planning submittal
The submittal is created, and the documents and plan milestones are loaded into the system.
Review submittal progress
A progress screen is used to view the upload, validation, and creation of the deliverables.
Correct validation/export stage errors
Any errors reported by the validation and export stages are addressed prior to re-submission.
Review deliverable submission
The MDR and the deliverable milestones of planned deliverables can be reviewed in the system.
What can be done on a planning submittal?
 While processing the MDR planning submittal, if one or more issue purposes in the attached file are not related to the current plant or project, the validation rules stop the processing, and the user must relate the issue
purpose to the current plant or project to process the submittal.
Create the first revision of the MDR with new attached planned revisions.
The first row usually contains the MDR document, though this is optional.
The MDR document name, title, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and area must be supplied.
The MDR document classification must be set to MDR.
The MDR filename is optional though the submittal create process will still request one.
The subsequent rows are repeated for each planned issue purpose of a planned document.
The planned revisions require a name, title, revision of ‘--', classification, area, and a document issue date though the issue date is ignored. The issue purpose and the planned date of issue at that purpose also need to be
supplied.
All other information is optional.
Update the current revision of the MDR to update the issue plan of the reserved revisions or add new ones.
The first row usually contains the MDR document.
The MDR document name, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and area must be supplied.
The issue date of the MDR is ignored if the name and revision match the current revision as this must not be changed on an issued revision.
The MDR document classification must be set to MDR.
The MDR title can be updated.
The MDR filename must be left blank.
Create a new revision of the MDR document to update the issue plan of the reserved revisions or add new ones.
The MDR revision must be later in the revision sequence than the current revision and the issue date must be later than or equal to that of the current revision. A time stamp is automatically added to the issue date.
The MDR filename is optional though the submittal create process will still request one.
Update the planning information of planned revisions.
This can be done by updating the current MDR revision or creating a new one as above.
The subsequent rows can contain planned revisions.
The planned revisions require a name, classification and area that matches that of the existing planned revision, and an issue date though the issue date is ignored. The revision should still be ‘--‘. The issue purpose and
planned date of issue at that purpose also need to be supplied.
The title can be updated.
All other information is optional.
Update the planning information of existing deliverables.
This can be done by updating the current MDR revision or creating a new one as above.
The subsequent rows can contain planned revisions as above or existing deliverables or a combination. The existing deliverables could be recently submitted documents in the Current state or documents that have been revised
from the plant to the project as part of a change in the Working state.
The existing deliverable revisions require a name, revision, classification and area that matches that of the latest revision of that deliverable, and an issue date though the issue date is ignored. The issue purpose and the
planned date of issue at that purpose also need to be supplied.
The title can be updated.
All other information is optional.
 When updating planning information, you do not need to send the complete MDR planning submittal. For example, if you planned more than a few documents and only want to update certain information of one document, the
updated planning submittal can contain that single document alone.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for a planning submittal
Planning submittals are used to create documents with a reserved status on the Master Document Register with the planned dates for the deliverables at each issue purpose.
Pre-requisites
All submittals require the following information created in advance. This information is used when processing the submittal. The submittal will create or update the MDR and all related documents.
All issue purposes, classifications, and disciplines referenced in the submittal must be related to the current project or its parent plant. If you relate an issue purpose, a classification, or a discipline at the plant level to a project, it
disables its inheritance from the plant.
A contract must exist between the contracting organization submitting the deliverables and the organization hosting the HxGN SDx2 system.
The contract must have a related review matrix that defines the consolidator for the document review work process.
This mapping is used to submit the planned deliverables. One row must contain the MDR document which is usually the first row. Two examples of content are shown below, one for the MDR row and one for the planned deliverables.
All the columns listed below must be included in the CSV file.

Deliverable submittal
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Deliverable Submittal <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6200f339be7ad12e1d368f90>
A deliverable submittal contains documents that are ready to be delivered to the company. The submittal uploads all files to a "safe" area, creates new revisions of the documents, and attaches the files.
The delivered revisions are routed using a document review workflow which automatically assigns documents to the reviewers and a consolidator.
MDR deliverable submittal
An MDR deliverable submittal issues documents with their associated files for the specified issue purpose. These documents should be identified on the MDR as planned documents.
You can use this to submit the documents and route them using a document review workflow.
Steps
Description
Populate the MDR CSV file
Populate the CSV file with the deliverables and their associated filenames. The MDR document is listed as the first row of the file, though this is optional.
Each deliverable and its corresponding filename should be entered in separate rows.
If a single deliverable has multiple associated files, each file must be listed in a separate row. The deliverable column should contain the same value
for all rows related to that deliverable, while the filename column will contain the unique filename for each file.
Create the deliverable submittal
The submittal is created and the documents are routed for review. Optionally the submittal is first reviewed by the document controllers to review the
metadata information of documents. The document controllers then approve or reject the submittal.
Review the submittal progress
A progress screen is used to view the upload, validation, and creation of the deliverables.
Correct validation/export stage errors
Any errors reported by the validation or export stages are addressed prior to re-submission.
Review submitted documents
The MDR and the deliverables can be reviewed in the system. You can also search for some of the submitted tags and review their attribution and
relationships. You can query by date to get the full list.
Review the document metadata
If the Initial Submittal Review flag is enabled on the project, the submittal process pushes a review step to the document controller for reviewing the
metadata information of documents. At this stage, the documents cannot be found in the system using the search capabilities.
You cannot submit a historical revision if the Initial Submittal Review flag is enabled. However, the historical revisions can still be loaded using the
DTO loader workflow.
If a document is submitted with an incorrect issue purpose and subsequently rejected during the initial submittal review process, the contractor can
resubmit the document with the corrected issue purpose. Upon resubmission, the document revision will be updated to reflect the corrected issue
purpose.
What can be done on a deliverable submittal?
Create the first revision of the MDR with attached deliverable documents.
The first row usually contains the MDR document, though this is optional.
The Action, Document Number, Revision, Issue Date (dd-mmm-yyyy), Document Title, Classification, and Area must be supplied.
The MDR document classification must be set to MDR.
The MDR filename is optional though the submittal create process will still request one.
The subsequent rows contain the deliverable documents.
The Action, Document Number, Revision, Issue Date (dd-mmm-yyyy), Document Title, Classification, and Area must be supplied.
The filename is defined to link the correct updated file to the document.
The issue purpose is optional but is required if the planning submittals and the review plans are being used to manage the review process. This is also required for any form of progress measurement or reporting.
All other information is optional.
Update the current revision of the MDR to update the deliverable revisions or add new ones.
The first row usually contains the MDR document, though this is optional.
MDR document number, revision, issue date (dd-mmm-yyyy), and file name must be supplied.
The issue date of the MDR is ignored if the name and revision match the current revision as this must not be changed on an issued revision.
The MDR title can be updated.
The MDR filename must be left blank.
New deliverable documents or new revisions of the existing documents can be added.
Create a new revision of the MDR document to submit new or updated deliverable documents.
The MDR revision must be later in the revision sequence than the current revision. The issue date must be later than or equal to that of the current revision.
A time stamp (the current time) is automatically added to the issue date. The combination of the date and time of the next revision should be later in time than the previous revision.
New deliverable documents or new revisions of existing documents can be added.
Submit new revisions of the existing deliverables.
The submitted deliverable revisions require a name, revision, classification and area that matches that of the current revision of that deliverable, and an issue date though the issue date is ignored.
The MDR revision must be later in the revision sequence than the current revision. The issue date must be later than or equal to that of the current revision.
A time stamp (the current time) is automatically added to the issue date. The combination of the date and time of the next revision should be later in time than the previous revision.
The title can be updated.
The document filename is usually supplied.
The issue purpose is optional but is required if the planning submittals and the review plans are being used to manage the review process.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for deliverable submittal
Deliverable submittals are used to issue documents and their associated files to the company. The MDR is used to identify which documents are issued by the contractor for a review.
Pre-requisites
All submittals require the following information created in advance. This information is used when processing the submittal. The submittal will create or update the MDR and all related documents.
All issue purposes, classifications, and disciplines referenced in the submittal must be related to the current project or its parent plant. If you relate an issue purpose, a classification, or a discipline at the plant level to a project, it
disables its inheritance from the plant.
A contract work package must exist between the contracting organization submitting the deliverables and the organization hosting the HxGN SDx2 system.
The contract work package must have a related review matrix that defines the consolidator for the document review work process.
This mapping is used to submit the deliverable documents along with the files. One row might contain the MDR document, which is usually the first row. Two examples of content are shown below, one for the MDR row and one for the
deliverables.
All the columns listed below must be included in the CSV file.

MTR/TDR submittal
An MTR submittal is initiated by the contractor to send data in a master tag register to the owner company. A TDR submittal is initiated by the contractor to send data on tag document relationships to the owner company. You can use the
MTR/TDR submittal to load the tags and create the document to tag relationships.
Steps
Description
Download the tag MTR/TDR template
The tag MTR/TDR templates are stored on Smart Community and available for download.
Populate the MTR/TDR CSV files
For MTR csv files:
Populate the two CSV files; one for the tag list and one for its attribution.
For TDR csv files:
Populate the CSV file to build the relationships between the documents and tags.
Create the MTR/TDR submittal
For MTR submittal, the submittal is created, and the tags loaded into HxGN SDx2.
For TDR submittal, the submittal is created and the documents to tag relationships are created.
Review submittal progress
A progress screen is used to view the upload, validation, and creation of the deliverables by the one who submits the submittal.
Correct validation/export stage errors
Review the validation report and address any reported errors before resubmitting the files.
What can be done on an MTR submittal?
The MTR submittal process allows you to create, update, and delete tags with classification and properties.
Depending on the requirements of the contract work package, HxGN SDx2 provides two options for managing tag numbering.
If the owning company wants to maintain control of the tag numbering, tag numbers are created and allocated to the contractor to use when submitting the MTR.
If the tag numbers are provided by the contractor as part of their MTR submittal, the tags can be created in the system and related to the contractor organization. This prevents other contractors from using the same tag numbers.
What information must be supplied?
The MTR submittal process allows you to load two types of CSV file formats. The basic format contains the tag data and must have the tag number, type, area, and unit. The classification should be specified as MTR to create an MTR
submittal. The inverted format contains the tag properties and must have the tag name.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for MTR submittal
MTR submittals are used to load tags and their properties into the system.
This mapping is used to submit the tags and their base data.

What can be done on a TDR submittal?
The TDR submittal process allows you to relate tags to the delivered revisions to indicate the actual deliverable.
What information must be supplied?
The tag document must have a tag number, tag classification, document number, and revision. The tag classification is required as tags with the same name, but different classifications can exist in HxGN SDx2.
Learn more about the preconfigured mappings for TDR submittal
TDR submittals are used to relate tags to their reserved or deliverable documents.
This mapping is used to relate tags and their deliverable documents.

The tags are related to a specific revision of a document.
How do submittals work?
Create a submittal
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | How to Create a Submittal <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/65adb800166be4fedd290c46>
1. Select the Submittals feature set, and then select Create MDR Submittal.
2. Provide other details as needed.
a. Details
1. Enter Title to the submittal.
 Provide a description for the submittal.
2. Select From organization name from the list.
 It is a default value for most users, set by your global document control user on behalf of your organization.
3. Select the Contract to relate the documents, and verify if related objects can be submitted.
 To organization is automatically defined by your choice of contract.
4. Select Item owning group, the department or team within the organization primarily responsible for the document.
3. Upload the CSV control file that contains all the appropriate submittal details, such as planned or reserved deliverables.
 The CSV file formatting is validated after successful upload, before the submittal job is created. Delete the CSV file if any formatting errors are displayed, and upload it again after rectifying the errors.
4. Upload supporting files to attach to the documents specified in the CSV control file.
 To avoid system generation of the submittal name, always select the Suppress ENS check box. This check box is enabled by your administrator.
Monitor the submittal progress
1. In the Submittals feature set, select Show My Submittals.
2. Select  on My Submittals page, next to Actions 
.
3. Select Refresh 
 multiple times to see the change of status at every stage.
 You can select the following options from the Actions 
 menu:
Select this
To do this
Approve
Approve and trigger the next workflow step of the selected submittal.
Delete
Delete the selected submittal, along with any attached files.
View Summary
View the summary of the selected submittal.
Review validation errors
After the validation stage is completed, a validation report is available for download. The information in the Microsoft Excel report allows you to take any corrective actions.
1. On the My Submittals page, select a submittal, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Validation Report link available below the VALIDATION progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
Review export stage errors
An export report is available for download only if the submittal fails in the second stage of the export process. The information in the Microsoft Excel report allows you to take any corrective actions.
1. On the My Submittals page, select a submittal, and select Refresh 
.
2. Select the Export Report link available below the EXPORT progress bar.
 The report is saved to the default download location on your computer.
 If any submittal jobs are awaiting approval by a user later than the value specified by DaysBeforeCleanUp setting, a file not found error may occur during the export process. This is because the files have been removed from the
SubmissionsShareDirectory.
Search for planned deliverables
What does a deliverable milestone include?
Deliverable milestones help track the progress of documents or deliverable items, and their issue purpose, that a contractor submits. A deliverable milestone includes the following information:
Issue purpose - Specifies a single reason for sending a document.
Planned issue date - Specifies the date on which the actual revision of a deliverable item for a specific issue purpose is scheduled to be sent by the contractor.
Receipt date - Specifies the date on which the document review workflow starts for a specific issue purpose.
Issue date - Specifies the revision issue date for the deliverable item for a specific issue purpose.
Issued revisions - Specifies all the revisions of a deliverable item.
Review Status - Specifies if the revision of a deliverable item is in review or completed.
Reviewed revision - Specifies the latest reviewed revision of a deliverable item.
Date returned - Specifies the date on which the document sent for a review was returned.
Return code - Specifies the current state of the review set by the consolidator for the deliverable item for a specific issue purpose.
When is a deliverable milestone created?
When a deliverable is created as part of a Master Document Register submittal, the software creates a progress deliverable milestone for the deliverable item. The deliverable milestone stores the planned and receipt dates of the
deliverables for the issue purposes along with the review completion information, such as review status, date returned, and return code.
You can search for planned documents that have been submitted and determine when they should become available to you based on the planned dates and issue purpose.
Every deliverable item can be issued for multiple issue purposes. Examples of issue purposes include Issued for review (IFR) or Issued for construction (IFC). Each issue purpose has its own milestone, but only one milestone is created for
a deliverable item for a given issue purpose. In addition, planned dates and actual dates are stored on the deliverable milestone. This allows you to track the progress of contractors by seeing when items are planned and when the items
were actually delivered.
1. In the Documents feature set, select the type of documents to find; for example, Engineering Documents.
2. Select Actions 
 on a deliverable item and then select Show Deliverable Milestone.
The deliverable milestones track the planned date for a given issue purpose and the delivery against that milestone including the revisions issued at that issue purpose.
Upload a document
If you want to submit a document without using a CSV file, then:
1. In the Submittals feature set, select Upload Document.
2. On the Upload Document form, select the document using the search functionality.
The search result filters the document based on the entry point given in parameter 2 of UploadDocument client API. For more information on the client API, see UploadDocument client API.
On selecting the document, the system automatically populates the existing revision scheme and suggests the next major and minor revision.
How do revision schemes work?
Unlike other entities in HxGN SDx2, revision schemes are not tied to specific configurations (like project or plant levels). This means they can be used across multiple levels and projects. When searching for a revision scheme,
the system follows a hierarchical approach. It starts at the current level (for example, project) and works its way up through the configuration tree. If a revision scheme is found at any level, the search stops, and that scheme is
made available. If no revision scheme is found at any lower level, the system searches at the Configuration Top level, which is the highest level in the configuration hierarchy and sets the available revision scheme.
For example: Relating Revision Schemes to Projects
Revision Scheme ONE is associated with Plant A.
Revision Scheme TWO (and possibly others) is associated with Project_1407.
In this scenario:
Revision Scheme ONE will be available for selection in Plant A.
Revision Scheme ONE will not be available for selection in Project_1407 due to the association with Revision Scheme TWO.
In essence, revision schemes provide a flexible way to manage different versions of data across various levels of your project hierarchy.
3. Select one or more issue purposes.
4. In New Files, drag the files or select the file system where you want to access the files to attach to the new version of the document.
5. Select Apply to upload the document into the system and stay on the form to upload another document or select Finish to upload the document and exit the form.
 The new revision is routed to the QA Review Verification workflow, which verifies if the document revision has been reviewed at the same revision and issue purpose, and if not reviewed, routes the revision to the QA Review
workflow.
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
Reviews
The review process is managed using the document review matrix. The review matrix routes the documents to reviewers as they move in based on the document type, discipline, and so on.
Manage document review matrices
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Document Review Matrices <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/656ef4b24a64fe8264ccafc1>
Document review matrices are used to set up recipient lists for reviews in the technical query workflow. The recipients are selected based on the properties or relationships of the object in the workflow. Each recipient on the matrix has a
Reason for Receipt (RFR), which indicates why the recipient is included.
A document review matrix is a table with recipients and document attributes such as discipline, issue purpose, security code, and so on. Each cell in the grid is marked with the reason for receipt for each recipient and might also include the
format in which they will be receiving the document.
What do you want to do?
1. Create a document review matrix.
2. Populate the document review matrix.
3. Select the matrix to be used when creating a project. For more information, see Configure a document review matrix.
Create a document review matrix
1. In the Workflow Management feature set, select Create Document Matrix.
2. In the New Create Document Matrix page, type the required information, and select Finish.
A matrix is created with the default column set provided in the class definition. For more information, see Configure a document review matrix.
To view the matrix, select Workflow Management feature set > Document Matrices.
Populate the document review matrix
To assign recipients and specify properties:
1. In the Workflow Management feature set, select Document matrices.
2. Select the matrix and select Actions > Assign Recipients.
3. Select Add Row 
 and specify a value for the property cells in each row.
The attribute columns for the matrix are defined by the system administrator according to project requirements. Typical attributes include Area, Type, Discipline, and Issue Purpose.
Select 
 next to the column item to add or remove columns.
You can use the asterisk (*) wildcard character to associate a row with all the values of an attribute.
Selecting a property or relationship as a filter, and entering a wildcard (*) will only consider the objects which have the attribute (property or relationship) instantiated.
The submittal process is intended to be used by the external organizations, such as vendors, suppliers, and so on, to submit new document revisions. If certain documents or documents with particular issue purposes, do not
require a review, the matrix can be configured to route the documents around the engineering review. However, the documents are signed off and the deliverable milestones (document planning information) are updated.
Toggle review mode 
 - The review mode is turned on by default when you add a row. When you turn it off 
,
any existing recipients in that row are removed (see screenshot below).
the documents that satisfy any of the attribute values are not sent for reviews.
The following relationship-related properties allow multiple value selection: Area, Unit, Discipline, and Issue Purpose. The selection of the values in Area and Unit are mutually dependent. For example, if you select a specific
area, the units related to that area are filtered and available for selection.
The following enumerated list properties allow single value selection: Category, Type, Sub Type, and Security Code. The selection of the values in Category, Type, and Sub Type are mutually dependent. For example, you have
to select a specific document category to select a type related to that category.
4. To add the recipients, select 
 next to the Reason for Receipt.
Select the recipients cell to activate the edit mode, and to add 
, remove, or highlight the recipients.
To remove the recipients from the document review matrix, you need to select 
 next to the user, and do the following:
Select Remove to remove the user from the active row.
Select Remove All to remove the user from all the rows of the matrix.
To highlight a user in all the rows of the matrix, you need to select 
 next to the user, and select Highlight. To turn off the highlight, you need to select 
 next to the user, and select Unhighlight. In the user interface:
You can highlight only one user at any given time.
Highlight is turned off when you refresh the view, save or undo any changes.
The default values of the recipients are R (reviewer), I (information), and CON (consolidator).
Reason for receipts related to the SDXDocumentReviewMatrix class definition will be displayed in the system. For more information on how to configure the reason for receipts, see Configure a document review matrix.
 Each user appears in a chip-based interface (users separated by comma is the default view), and you can view the user information by selecting on the chip.
What do the buttons on the Assign Recipients page do?
Select
To do
Add Row
Add an empty row at the beginning of the matrix.
Delete Row
Delete the selected row.
Duplicate Row
Create a copy of the selected row right below the selected row. Select a row to enable this button.
SAVE
Save any changes.
Undo All
Discard any changes.
Compare the users in the matrix
You can compare the recipients within the matrix to know where the recipients are in all rows. This helps you to check if the recipients are correctly assigned and also fill any missing gaps.
1. In the Workflow Management feature set, select Document matrices.
2. Select the matrix, and select Actions > Assign Recipients.
3. Select 
 next to Recipients. Select 
 to switch to the normal view.
4. Select the users you want to compare.
You can select the users available in the current view only.
You can select a maximum of three users.
The user selection is saved for the current view.
The selected users are displayed in a column view, along with empty rows if any.
You can hide the empty rows by selecting 
, available next to 
.
You can perform all the basic matrix operations in this view, such as copy the assignments of one user to another, add a user, remove a user, add a row, duplicate a row, and so on.
When you add or remove any of the selected users, the view modifies accordingly.
You can also add or remove a user not in this view.
You can undo any unsaved changes.
Copy user assignments within the matrix
1. In the Workflow Management feature set, select Document matrices.
2. Select the matrix and select Actions > Assign Recipients.
3. Select 
 next to the source user, and select Copy Assignments.
4. Select 
 next to the target user, and select Append Assignments. The target user is added to the corresponding cells in the matrix where the source user is present, and highlighted.
For example, let's assume User B has review assignment for drawings of unit 100 and information only assignment for datasheets of unit 200. You can copy User B’s assignments to User A using the Copy Assignments and Append
Assignments options. User A then has the same review settings as User B along with the ones that were already defined.
Filter column headings in a matrix
You can use the Filter option to restrict the number of displayed rows in the matrix. You can filter by multiple columns.
1. In the Workflow Management feature set, select Document matrices.
2. Select the matrix and select Actions > Assign Recipients.
3. Select 
 next to the column header you want to filter, and select Filter 
 to see the filtering options.
4. Type the criteria.
 Select And to combine conditions. Select Or to specify that one condition be met.
The applied column filters will be cleared when you add or duplicate a row.
The currently selected row will be cleared when you apply a filter.
When you close the Assign Recipients window, the filters are not preserved.
With the filter turned on, if you edit the property values and save the changes, the system reloads the matrix rows based on the filter criteria.
Show and hide column headings
1. Select 
 next to the column header, and select Columns 
 to see the column headings available for display.
2. Select a column heading to show it in the matrix.
3. Clear the column heading to hide it in the matrix.
When you add or remove a column, all the hidden columns will be visible.
You can undo the current changes by selecting RESET. However, you cannot undo the changes after selecting APPLY.
When you close the Assign Recipients window, the hidden columns are not preserved.
Copy the document review matrix
You can copy a document review matrix that is created in the same configuration as well as another configuration.
If you are copying a matrix which is created outside of the active create scope, you must select Copy Across Plant/Project in the Copy Matrix form.
By default, configuration items from the active scope are listed in the Copy Matrix form. Your administrator can modify this to list the configuration items based on the configuration states.
You can query a document review matrix created in the same configuration or higher configurations.
Copy the structure
1. Select a matrix and select Actions 
 > Copy Matrix Structure.
2. Edit the information and select Copy.
Select Pins 
 > New Items to view the copied matrix.
The copied matrix consists of all existing columns from the original matrix.
The filter criteria and assigned users related to the original matrix are not copied.
Copy the content
1. Select a matrix and select Actions 
 > Copy Matrix Content.
2. Edit the information and select Copy.
Select Pins 
 > New Items to view the copied matrix.
The copied matrix consists of all the columns, including the filter criteria and assigned users, from the original matrix.
Review delivered documents
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Introduction to QA Review Workflow <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6405ef91eac62d273e9a2eff> .
When a document is submitted to the system, you are assigned a review task or a consolidation task based on the document review matrix. Select the task in your To Do List to review the document. If you cannot review the document,
you can reassign the task to another user or waive the task.
 Your system administrator may modify the workflow so that the steps differ from those shown. The steps are still carried out using the procedures described below.
What is a Quality Assurance (QA) Review?
The Quality Assurance (QA) Review allows your company technical experts, engineers, and stakeholders to review documentation deliverables and other documentation during a project. The aim of the review process is to ensure that the
correct information is delivered to the right people so they can efficiently perform Quality Assurance Reviews. The review results can then be efficiently communicated with the suppliers or contractors.
During a project, the suppliers or contractors deliver documents regularly at various stages, with different revision numbers, and issue purposes. Some documents are related to the design aspects of the project so are more critical than
others, so the review is used to ensure that the most critical documentation receives the appropriate level of scrutiny.
When document revisions enter the system, they are evaluated to determine if a quality assurance review should be initiated. If so, then a review is triggered and notifications are sent to each of the reviewers.
Each reviewer can make markups, add review comments, add general comments, reassign the review task to another person, or waive a review. Review comments are observations made during the reviews that require additional follow
up. The review comments are attached to a workflow to ensure that they receive the right level of follow up. Each review functions independently, so they can be performed concurrently without the visibility of other reviews. However,
reviewers may see the comments and markups of all other completed reviews to help minimize duplication and promote discussion.
The Consolidator opens the documents and creates a consolidated layer that contains all approved comments to the contractor document. The Consolidator can also create a new consolidated marked up file of the marked up document. A
consolidator can know the progress of all the reviews from the QA Document Review Consolidation page. Reviews that are in progress are shown as In Review and those not started are shown as Review not started against the
corresponding reviewer.
What is the difference between primary and reference documents?
You can distinguish between the different documents that appear in a communication review or response:
Primary documents are the main subject of a review or response and require action, comment, or query.
Reference documents are included for reference or provide additional information to support a review or response, action, comment, or query.
What happens when a revised document is attached through the submittal workflow?
The QA Review Verification workflow handles the document revision attached through the submittal workflow and checks if the document revision has been reviewed at that issue purpose.
1. If the document revision has been reviewed at the same revision and issue purpose, the system sets the return code on the document to Acknowledged and exits the workflow.
If the document revision has not been reviewed at the same revision and issue purpose, the system directs this workflow to the next step.
2. The system checks if the issue purposes on the document are related to the current project.
If yes, the system directs this workflow to the QA Review workflow.
If no, the system does not allow the document revision to be submitted.
Perform QA review
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx®, HxGN SDx2 | Review Documents and Drawings <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/647843f927e10045fe3cd382> .
1. In your To Do List, select a document review task, and select Actions 
 > Perform QA Review.
 Select WAIVE to decline the document review. You cannot waive a partly completed review.
2. Add markups and actions on the document.
3. On the Reviews tab, add the review comments.
 On the toolbar, select CREATE INTERNAL TECHNICAL QUERY to request more information from an internal resource.
4. Select COMPLETE. The review is marked as complete and is removed from your To Do List.
Main document review steps
The main stages of a document review are:
Name
Description
Assignment
A document is assigned to a reviewer based on the document review matrix. The documents are added to the QA Review workflow, and notifications are
sent to the reviewers. A due date is calculated, and a review task appears in the To Do List of each reviewer.
Review
The reviewer carries out the document review, and can add markups to the review. The reviewer can also create an outgoing action or an internal
technical query within the review activity. When finished, the reviewer completes the task.
Consolidate
The consolidator collates all the completed document reviews and any related actions. The consolidator reviews all the comments and markup layers for
the document, and selects all applicable markup layers for consolidation. The consolidator can also reject the actions from the original document
reviewers. The consolidator then sets the return code and completes the task.
Issue
After the consolidation task is complete, the actions are initiated to the contractor. These actions appear in the contractor's To Do List and follow an
independent life cycle to track their acceptance and closure. The rejected actions are returned to the submitter.
Get to know the QA Document Review user interface
1
The details of the review, where you can add your review and markups, as well as select individual tabs to add and
view actions or comments.
2
Creates an internal technical query in the responder's organization to gather more information.
3
The document summary includes the name, description, and files, and displays the responders and contributors
selected for the review.
4
Select COMPLETE to finish your review. Select WAIVE to choose to ignore the review.
Markup layers with reviews in draft or waive status are not visible to other reviewers or the consolidator.
The system automatically selects all the markup layers of completed reviews for rendition generation. However,
if the same revision is submitted for a review with a new issue purpose, the system automatically selects the
markup layers created for the current review and not the layers of previous reviews. A consolidator can manage
this selection. For more information on how to configure this setting, refer to Navigate client API.
5
The supporting information, where you can attach files and manage any related items.
6
