Tag ColumnSets sheet
Work through the starter pack worksheets
You can work through the sheets in the SDx2 Starter Pack to develop the information HxGN SDx2 requires for your basic configuration.
Work through the following sheets in order:
1. Plants sheet
2. Areas sheet
3. Units sheet
4. Document Classifications sheet
5. Document Classification Mapping sheet
6. Disciplines sheet
7. Material Classifications sheet
8. Tag Classifications sheet
9. Tag Classifications Mapping sheet
10. Tag ColumnSets sheet
When you complete the required information for your basic configuration, you can Generate load files.
Plants sheet
In the Plants sheet, you can name and describe your plants and set their initial status. This is the beginning step for creating your PBS.
Each row creates a different plant in your HxGN SDx2 tenant.
 We have provided SDx Setup and Demo as example plants for you. You can replace or delete these examples.
Plants listed on the Plants sheet become available for associating to Areas in the next step.
Plant Name - The name of your plant. The plant name becomes a selectable entry on the Areas sheet. This field is mandatory.
 Do not use special characters at all in any part of a plant name or PBS, such as area or unit. These include .<,>^=+:?|/';{}[]~`!%*()&$@#. However there is an exception to this, you may use double apostrophe (") at the
beginning of a name to indicate that it starts with an apostrophe (').
The plant name is case-sensitive and cannot exceed 52 characters if Smart Instrumentation is involved in integration.
The plant name must be unique within the current design component. This limitation is imposed by Smart Instrumentation.
We recommend that the Smart Instrumentation design component schema name and design component name match the plant name.
We recommend that the plant name be the same name across all authoring tools.
You can use digits and spaces within the body of the plant name, for example Plant 1. However, spaces are treated the same as underscores; so, if you name a new plant with a name that already exists, but use a space or an
underscore to differentiate, an error message will state it is already in existence. For example, Plant 1 and Plant_1 are considered the same.
Description - The description of your plant. An optional field.
Status - Active or Created can be the status of your plant. Typically, you choose Active. This field is mandatory.
The Plant is the highest level of the PBS. The Plant can be referred to as the Config for which capital projects can be executed. The basic configuration supports three levels of PBS:
1. Plant
2. Area
3. Unit
 HxGN SDx2 does not support a secondary PBS.
Areas sheet
In the Areas sheet, you can name and describe areas for your plants. Each row creates a different area in your HxGN SDx2 tenant.
 We have provided SDx Setup and Demo as example plants with areas for you. You can replace or delete these examples.
Areas listed on the Areas sheet become available for associating to Units in the next step.
1. Begin in the second row of the sheet by selecting a plant in the Plant column.
2. On the next row below your selected plant, enter a name for your first area in the Name column. For example, if you select a plant on row 2, you can enter the first area within that plant on row 3.
 Do not use special characters at all in any part of a plant name or PBS, such as area or unit. These include .<,>^=+:?|/';{}[]~`!%*()&$@#. However there is an exception to this, you may use double apostrophe (") at the
beginning of a name to indicate that it starts with an apostrophe (').
 To display an area or unit name that begins with an apostrophe, enter the name with a double quotation mark ("), not a single apostrophe ('). For example:
To display '7000, enter "7000 in the Area's Name column.
To display '65, enter "65 in the Unit's Name column.
3. In the same row, enter a description for the area in the Description column.
4. Repeat this process for your remaining areas.
5. After creating the areas, select the rows containing areas that are in the same plant, and in the Data tab, select Group.
 If you want to add a new area to an existing plant, insert a row among the existing areas and add the new area. This keeps the grouping unchanged.
The area is the second level of the PBS:
Plant - The plant that the areas in the following rows are related to. This is an optional field. You cannot create an area without a plant that contains it.
Name - The name for the area. You cannot create an area without a name.
Description - The description for the area. This is an optional field.
Units sheet
In the Units sheet, you can name and describe units for your areas.
Each row creates a different unit in your HxGN SDx2 tenant.
 We have provided SDx Setup and Demo as example plants with areas and units for you. You can replace or delete these examples.
1. Begin in the second row of the sheet by selecting a plant in the Plant column.
2. On the next row below your selected plant, select an area that you created for that plant in the Areas sheet. For example, if you select a plant on row 2, you can select an area for that plant on row 3.
 All areas are available for you to select at this stage, so you must select an area that you have associated with the plant on the Areas sheet.
3. In the same row, enter a name for the unit in the Unit column.
 Do not use special characters at all in any part of a plant name or PBS, such as area or unit. These include .<,>^=+:?|/';{}[]~`!%*()&$@#. However there is an exception to this, you may use double apostrophe (") at the
beginning of a name to indicate that it starts with an apostrophe (').
 To display an area or unit name that begins with an apostrophe, enter the name with a double quotation mark ("), not a single apostrophe ('). For example:
To display '7000, enter "7000 in the Area's Name column.
To display '65, enter "65 in the Unit's Name column.
4. In the same row, enter the following:
Unique code for the unit in the UnitCode column.
Description for the unit in the Description column.
5. Repeat this process for your remaining units.
6. After creating the units, select the rows containing units that are in the same area, and in the Data tab, select Group.
 If you want to add a new unit to an existing plant, insert a row among the existing units and add the new unit. This keeps the grouping unchanged.
The unit is the third level of the PBS, and it is the lowest level of the PBS that you can create using the SDx2 Starter Pack. After preparing your tenant, you can create additional levels of the PBS interactively.
Plant - The plant that the areas and units in the following rows are related to. This is an optional field. You cannot create an area without a plant that contains it, and you cannot create a unit without an area that contains it.
Area - The area of the plant that contains the unit on the same row. Areas without a name and units within areas without a name do not function. All areas must have names.
Unit - The unit within the area on the same row. Units without a name and units without an area do not function. All units must have names.
UnitCode - The unit code is an unique identifier for the unit within the area. This is a mandatory field for creating an unit.
Description - The description for the unit. This is an optional field.
Document Classifications sheet
Many customers have a predefined hierarchy to classify their documents.
 The hierarchy provided in the Document Classifications sheet is an example. You can replace the example with your own hierarchy, or you can load a subset of the sample based on your specific needs.
We recommend that you customize the document classifications to meet your business needs.
What are classifications?
In HxGN SDx2, you can manage how documents, assets, and tags are displayed in your system using classifications. Classifications work like a file folder system, and you can manage that file folder system using document classifications
for documents, material classifications for assets, and tag classifications for tag types.
We deliver a set of example document types and standardized tag types as part of the Common Schema. By creating classifications for your documents or tags, you can make our Common Schema objects match your business
terminology for your documents or tags.
In the SDx2 Starter Pack, the Document Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for documents, the Material Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for assets, and the Tag
Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for tag types. In the Document Classification Mapping sheet, you can map our Common Schema document types to your document classifications. In the Tag
Classifications Mapping sheet, you can map your tag classifications to our Common Schema tag types.
Each row in the Document Classifications sheet creates a level on the document classification hierarchy for your tenant.
1. Enter a single document classification node for your hierarchy on a row in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, or Level 4 column.
You can enter information in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, or Level 4 columns, but you cannot enter information in multiple columns on the same row.
Document classification nodes that are contained by other nodes in your hierarchy must be in a row below those nodes.
For example, if your Level 1 document classification on row 2 is Design Documents, you cannot have information in the Level 2, Level 3, or Level 4 columns on the same row because row 2 is creating the Design Documents level 1
document classification.
2. Enter a description for the document classification node in the Description column.
 You can map the document classifications that you enter in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, or Level 4 columns to Common Schema document types in the Document Classification Mapping sheet. The values you enter in
the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, or Level 4 columns must match exactly the values that you put in the Document Classification column of the Document Classification Mapping sheet.
Level 1 - The highest or top level of the document classification hierarchy.
Level 2 - The second level of the document classification hierarchy.
Level 3 - The third level of the document classification hierarchy.
Level 4 - The fourth or lowest level of the document classification hierarchy.
 The SDx2 Starter Pack can create up to four levels in the document classification hierarchy.
Description - The description for the level of the document classification hierarchy created on the same row. The description is optional.
Document Classification Mapping sheet
The Document Classification Mapping sheet contains the Common Schema document types.
 We provide a sample in which a subset of Common Schema document types is mapped to the example document classification in the Document Classifications sheet.
In the HxGN SDx2 system, the document classification mapping connects the Common Schema document types to your document classifications and determines the classification of the resulting Facility Data Warehouse (FDW)
document. When you load a document from an integrated design tool, a FDW document is created. For more information about FDW documents, see Loading shared documents and drawings and Sharing documents and drawings.
To use document management and control for documents loaded through integrated design tools, any classifications that you create in the Document Classifications sheet must be mapped in the Document Classification Mapping
sheet for your tenant to associate your classification to the Common Schema document type that the integrated tool supports.
You must map the document classifications to the Common Schema document types if you use design tools for documents and integrate them with HxGN SDx2. A document created by the design tool generates a FDW document. To
successfully integrate design tools, you must map the Common Schema document types that you share from your design tools.
To map your classifications to the Common Schema document types, you can add your classifications from the Document Classifications sheet and create unique identifiers for them.
1. In the Document Classifications sheet, copy your document classification from the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, or Level 4 column.
2. In the Document Classification Mapping sheet, paste your document classification in the Document Classification column in the same row as the Common Schema document type - the EnumEnum column - that you are
mapping it to.
3. In the same row in the Document Classification UID column, enter your document classification in unique identifier formatting. Unique identifier formatting must begin with SDC_, use _ (underscore) instead of spaces, and use
character reference entities for any characters that are reserved in XML. For more information about XML reserved characters, see the XML Standard documentation <https://www.w3.org/TR/xml/> . The unique identifier must be
unique to your classification. You can reuse the unique identifier in any row that uses the same classification. For example, the sample in the spreadsheet maps two different Common Schema document types to 3D Model, and both
use the same unique identifier, SDC_3D_Model.
The Common Schema document types enable more specialized functionality in HxGN SDx2 forms.
EnumEnum UID - The unique identifier of the Common Schema document type. Do not change the information in this column.
EnumEnum - The EnumEnum of the Common Schema document type. Do not change the information in this column.
Document Classification - The name of the document classification. The name of the document classification must match the name you created for your document classification on the Document Classifications sheet. For more
information, see Document Classifications sheet.
Document Classification UID - The unique identifier of the document classification. The values in this column must begin with SDC_, use _ (underscore) instead of spaces, and use HTML character reference entities instead of
characters that are reserved in OData protocol. For more information about OData protocol reserved characters, see the OData ABNF <https://www.odata.org/documentation/> . You must enter a value for a document classification
UID to use the corresponding Common Schema document type in your HxGN SDx2 tenant.
 A document classification UID is required for the Common Schema document type to be available in your tenant.
Mandatory - Indicates that the Common Schema document types in this row must be mapped if you are using Data Take-On.
 You can change the classification of Model, SAT Model, P&ID, and Plan drawing, but these four Common Schema document types must be mapped to your classifications.
 If you are using the SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm to set up multiple tenants with different classifications, such as a major company working across language barriers or different governmental requirements, you can use the
Mandatory column to signal to other administrators who are using the same SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm that certain mappings are expected by the parent organization or central office.
Disciplines sheet
Disciplines are specializations, such as a knowledge discipline or a set of work practices, that HxGN SDx2 can use to give users access to different functionalities and different tasks. We provide some example disciplines in the SDx2
Starter Pack. You can decide what disciplines are in your tenant. You can add disciplines or remove disciplines.
For example, you can have a user who is an environmental and civil engineer who has different job duties from an industrial systems engineer. By creating disciplines for environmental engineering, civil engineering, and industrial systems
engineering, you can set functions and tasks specific to those two different engineers even though they both share the Engineer role in HxGN SDx2.
In the Disciplines sheet, each row creates a new discipline.
Enter a name for a discipline in the Name column, and enter the description for that discipline in the same row in the Description column.
Disciplines are available as a dropdown selection for several forms within HxGN SDx2, such as during document reviews.
Name - The name of the discipline. This appears in several interactive fields in your final tenant. We recommend that you use an abbreviation or acronym for the discipline name to improve usability.
Description - The description of the discipline. The description is optional.
 You can add or edit disciplines interactively after you configure your HxGN SDx2 tenant. For more information, see Create a discipline and View and update disciplines.
Material Classifications sheet
Many customers have a predefined hierarchy to classify their assets.
 The hierarchy provided in the Material Classifications sheet is an example. You can replace the example with your own hierarchy, or you can load a subset of the sample based on your specific needs.
We recommend that you customize the material classifications to meet your business needs.
What are classifications?
In HxGN SDx2, you can manage how documents, assets, and tags are displayed in your system using classifications. Classifications work like a file folder system, and you can manage that file folder system using document classifications
for documents, material classifications for assets, and tag classifications for tag types.
We deliver a set of example document types and standardized tag types as part of the Common Schema. By creating classifications for your documents or tags, you can make our Common Schema objects match your business
terminology for your documents or tags.
In the SDx2 Starter Pack, the Document Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for documents, the Material Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for assets, and the Tag
Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for tag types. In the Document Classification Mapping sheet, you can map our Common Schema document types to your document classifications. In the Tag
Classifications Mapping sheet, you can map your tag classifications to our Common Schema tag types.
Each row in the Material Classifications sheet creates a level on the asset classification hierarchy for your tenant.
1. Enter a single node for your asset classification hierarchy on a row in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 4, or Level 5 column.
You can enter information in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 4, or Level 5 columns, but you cannot enter information in multiple columns on the same row.
Asset classification nodes that are contained by other nodes in your hierarchy must be in a row below those nodes.
2. On the same row, enter a description for the asset classification node in the Description column.
Level 1 - The highest or top level of the asset classification hierarchy.
Level 2 - The second level of the asset classification hierarchy.
Level 3 - The third level of the asset classification hierarchy.
Level 4 - The fourth level of the asset classification hierarchy.
Level 5 - The lowest or bottom level of the asset classification hierarchy.
Description - The description for the node in the asset classification hierarchy. The description is optional.
Tag Classifications sheet
In the Tag Classifications sheet, you can create tag classifications to register tags within a hierarchy, and next, in the Tag Classifications Mapping sheet, you can map your classifications to the Common Schema tag type hierarchy. In
this way, you can tailor the Common Schema tag type hierarchy to your business purposes.
 The hierarchy provided in the Tag Classifications sheet is an example. You can replace the sample with your own hierarchy, or you can load a subset of the sample based on your specific needs.
What are classifications?
In HxGN SDx2, you can manage how documents, assets, and tags are displayed in your system using classifications. Classifications work like a file folder system, and you can manage that file folder system using document classifications
for documents, material classifications for assets, and tag classifications for tag types.
We deliver a set of example document types and standardized tag types as part of the Common Schema. By creating classifications for your documents or tags, you can make our Common Schema objects match your business
terminology for your documents or tags.
In the SDx2 Starter Pack, the Document Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for documents, the Material Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for assets, and the Tag
Classifications sheet manages what classifications are available for tag types. In the Document Classification Mapping sheet, you can map our Common Schema document types to your document classifications. In the Tag
Classifications Mapping sheet, you can map your tag classifications to our Common Schema tag types.
1. In the Tag Classifications sheet, enter a classification on a row in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 4, or Level 5 column to create a tag classification.
 Any rows you add must be below the higher level to which they belong in your classification hierarchy. For example, in the sample classification, Flow instrument, a third level item in the hierarchy on row 4, belongs in the
Non-specific instrument classification, a second level item in the hierarchy on row 3, because the Flow instrument row is below the Non-specific instrument row and because no other second level item is between them.
2. Optionally, you can enter a description for your tag classification on the same row.
3. Enter the next classification on a new row.
 Any changes that you make to the Tag Classifications sheet must be duplicated in the Tag Classifications Mapping sheet.
Level 1 - The highest or top level of your tag classifications hierarchy.
Level 2 - The second level of your tag classifications hierarchy.
Level 3 - The third level of your tag classifications hierarchy.
Level 4 - The fourth level of your tag classifications hierarchy.
Level 5 - The lowest or bottom level of your tag classifications hierarchy.
Description - The description of your tag classification.
Tag Classifications Mapping sheet
If you want to use your own tag classification hierarchy and take advantage of the integration capabilities that HxGN SDx2 provides, you must map your tag classification hierarchy to the Common Schema hierarchy.
The data provided in the Mapping column in the Tag Classifications Mapping sheet is an example for you. You can replace the sample mappings with your own classifications.
The Integration Engine follows your classification hierarchy by using the most specific mapped enum in the tag type hierarchy to set the primary classification. The Integration Engine then sets the classification properties based on the
classification hierarchy above that primary classification. For more information about the Integration Engine, see Understand the Integration Engine. For more information about the tag type hierarchy, see Maintaining tag types in a
hierarchy.
We recommend that you only map tag classifications that you expect to frequently upload data for, and to minimize your setup time, we recommend avoiding mapping tag classifications at a granular, low-level scale, such as level 3, level 4,
or level 5 unless you are using integrated design tools. Forms carry more specific information if you more precisely map your tag classifications, which means that your forms require less preparation after your digital twin is set up. If you
are not using integrated design tools, granular, low-level tag classification mapping may be less valuable than a high-level mapping for your tag classification, such as to level 1 or level 2.
Any changes that you make to the Tag Classifications sheet must be cross-checked in the Tag Classifications Mapping sheet.
You must have mapped values for the following Enum Level 1 items:
PipeRun
Equipment
PipingComponent
Instrument
If you are using integrated design tools, you must have mapped values for Enum Level 1 items.
If more detailed mapping is required later, you can map the additional levels as needed.
You can map the delivered Common Schema tag types to your tag classifications.
1. In the Mapping column, select your tag classification to map it to the Common Schema tag type on the same row.
 The values available in the Mapping column are from your tag classifications in the Tag Classifications sheet.
Enum Level 1 - The highest or top level of the tag type hierarchy. In HxGN SDx2, this is the TagClass level of the tag type hierarchy. Do not change values in this column. This information is provided for reference.
Enum Level 2 - The second level of the tag type hierarchy. In HxGN SDx2, this corresponds to the SpecificType1 level of the tag type hierarchy. Do not change values in this column. This information is provided for reference.
Enum Level 3 - The third level of the tag type hierarchy. In HxGN SDx2, this corresponds to the SpecificType2 level of the tag type hierarchy. Do not change values in this column. This information is provided for reference.
Enum Level 4 - The fourth level of the tag type hierarchy. In HxGN SDx2, this corresponds to the SpecificType3 level of the tag type hierarchy. Do not change values in this column. This information is provided for reference.
Enum Level 5 - The lowest or bottom level of the tag type hierarchy. In HxGN SDx2, this corresponds to the SpecificType4 level of the tag type hierarchy. Do not change values in this column. This information is provided for
reference.
Description - The description of the tag. Do not change values in this column.
Enum Value - The unique identifier of the tag. Do not change any values in this column.
Mandatory - Indicates that the Common Schema document types in this row must be mapped.
You can change the classification of mandatory tag types, but they must be mapped.
If you are using SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm to set up multiple tenants with different classifications, such as a major company working across language barriers or different governmental requirements, you can use the Mandatory
column to signal to other administrators who are using the same SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm that certain mappings are expected by the parent organization or central office.
Mapping - The tag classification that is mapped to the Common Schema.
Tag ColumnSets sheet
In the Tag ColumnSets sheet, you can relate your tag classifications from the Tag Classifications sheet to Column Items.
HxGN SDx2 has dynamic forms that load properties specific to the type of tag you are working on.
The dynamic forms use tag column sets to populate fields. A column set is a relationship between the tag classification and a column item. Column items are properties of tag types that you can associate with your tag classifications. By
relating your tag classifications to the column items, HxGN SDx2 can load properties specific to those tag classifications in the dynamic sections of forms.
 The data provided in the Tag ColumnSets sheet is an example for you. To use tag column sets for your classifications, you must change the data in this sheet.
To relate your classifications to column items:
1. In the Tag Classifications sheet, copy the information in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 4, or Level 5 columns.
2. In the Tag ColumnSets sheet, paste your tag classifications in the Tag Classifications column. You can paste the same value in multiple rows to associate it with multiple column items.
You can associate each classification with multiple column items by adding another row with the same tag classification.
You can modify the sample data to match your tag classifications so that the column items in the Column Item column are available in your tenant.
 The values in the Tag Classifications column must match exactly the values you provided in the Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, Level 4, or Level 5 columns of the Tag Classification sheet.
 Your tenant requires the load files for tag column sets to have column items loaded in your tenant and to have your tag classifications related to column items. We recommend updating the sample data to match your tag
classifications so that you have the column items available in your tenant.
Tag Classification - The tag classification that is associated with the column item on the same row.
Column Item - The name of a column item.
Generate load files
After you complete the worksheets in the SDx2 Starter Pack, you can generate your load files using the Info sheet.
In the Info sheet, select the Generate Load Files button.
 If you have a complicated plant or classification hierarchies, generating load files may take time.
The load files are created in the SiteSetup folder where your SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm file is located. Each time you generate load files, a timestamped folder is created with those load files.
You need these files in the next step when you Load the tenant setup information.
Load the tenant setup information
Document classifications, tag classifications, material classifications, disciplines, tag column sets, and revision schemes are initially created for the entire tenant.
 Any load file that contains non-English characters must be saved in the UTF-8 character encoding before it can be processed.
1. Set your scope to Configuration Top by making sure the Query and Create scopes are Not Set, and set your Integration Data Filters to Consolidated. For more information, see Change the query scope.
2. In the Business Administration feature set, select Import Data.
3. In the Import tab, either drag the following files to the tab, or browse for the files on your local computer to add them.
 The files must be loaded in the following order.
a. Disciplines\Disciplines.xml
b. Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData\1Level1DocEnums(SDX).xml
c. Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData\2Level2DocEnums(SDX).xml
d. Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData\3Level3DocEnums(SDX).xml
e. Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData\4Level4DocEnums(SDX).xml
f. Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData\5DocClassifications(SDX).xml
g. Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData\6DocumentClassifications_Mapping.xml
h. Classifications\Tag Classifications\ImportData\1Tag Classifications.xml
i. Classifications\Tag Classifications\ImportData\2Tag Classifications Mapping.xml
j. Classifications\TagColumnSets\1Tag_ColumnSets.xml
k. Classifications\TagColumnSets\2Tag_ColumnItems.xml
l. Classifications\TagColumnSets\3ColumnSetsAndColumnItemsRels1.xml
m. Classifications\TagColumnSets\4ColumnSetsAndColumnItemsRels2.xml
n. Classifications\Material Classifications\ImportData\Material_Classifications.xml
You can use 
 to drag the files into the order they are listed here.
For a complicated tenant, your XML files can exceed the maximum size permitted for Import Data. If you encounter this issue, separate your XML files into groups and load them in the order listed here.
4. Select Import.
 If a file fails to load, you can view the error messages returned as described in View load errors. By default, after a failure, the system will continue to import the next file.
The History tab shows the recently imported files.
How do I view the import history?
You view the files that have been imported successfully in the History tab.
Files that were imported in the last 30 days are listed. The files are grouped by date, and the user that carried out the import.
Set a revision scheme
Optionally, you can set a revision scheme or create a new revision scheme at the tenant level before loading the plant setup information. The revision scheme is the naming scheme for document revisions.
For more information about loading the plant setup information, see Load the plant setup information.
 You can do this step later if you want different plants to have different revision schemes.
To set an existing revision scheme for a specific plant, see Update a revision scheme.
To create a new revision scheme, see Create a new revision scheme.
You can terminate delivered revision schemes that you do not want to use on your tenant. For more information, see Terminate a revision scheme.
Load the plant setup information
You can load the PBS files that you generated using SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm.
1. Set your scope to Configuration Top by making sure the Query and Create scopes are Not Set, and set your Integration Data Filters to Consolidated. For more information, see Change the query scope.
2. In the Business Administration feature set, select Import Data.
3. In the Import tab, either drag the following files to the tab, or browse for the files on your local computer to add them.
 The files must be loaded in the following order.
a. PBSObjects\1SPFPlants.xml
b. PBSObjects\2PBSPlants.xml
c. PBSObjects\3PBSAreas.xml
d. PBSObjects\4PBSUnits.xml
 You can use 
 to drag the files into the order they are listed here.
4. Select Import.
After the files are successfully loaded, new PBS objects are created.
Manage the plant configuration details
Document classifications, tag classifications, template classifications, disciplines, and issue purposes are initially created for the entire tenant. This means that you can have the same values across all plants and projects allowing you to
search for specific classifications across the entire tenant. For each plant, you can then select the specific values required.
1. Search for the plant, and select Actions 
 > Manage Plant Details.
2. Select the required document, tag, and template classifications.
 By default, all of the classifications created for the tenant are available for the plant. Selecting classifications means that only the selected items are available for the plant.
3. Select the required disciplines and issue purposes for the plant.
4. Select Update to save the changes.
For more information on template document classifications, see Configure template documents.
For more information about issue purposes, see Configure issue purposes.
Set plant user details
If you are setting up integrated design tools while preparing your HxGN SDx2 digital twin, you can configure user access after creating the projects. For more information about creating projects, see Set up the projects.
If you are not using integrated design tools or are not immediately registering your design tools, you can manage user access and configure security for your plants:
Configure user access
Configure security
Configuring disciplines for a user
Set up the projects
A project is required for registering integrated design tools. You can set up projects interactively in HxGN SDx2. For more information about project level configurations, see Prepare the projects.
To set up your first project and register your design tools:
1. Create a project.
2. Relate the disciplines, issue purposes, and document and tag classifications to the project. See Manage the project configuration details.
3. Configure a document review matrix.
4. Configure user access.
5. Configure security.
6. Configuring disciplines for a user.
7. Register your design tools.
Prepare your digital twin interactively
You can configure HxGN SDx2 to meet your day-to-day business needs as long as you adhere to the rules and guidelines. These guidelines:
Provide rapid and efficient implementation.
Maintain preconfigured work processes and industry standards.
Before registering a design tool or starting to share data and documents, you must set up the system based on the incoming data that you expect.
If this is your first HxGN SDx2 tenant, using the SDx2 Starter Pack may be easier. For more information, see Use the starter pack to prepare your digital twin.
The following diagram is an overview showing required configuration and steps to extend HxGN SDx2.
Tenant setup overview
The load files for the disciplines, classifications, and tag column sets are created using the SDx2 Starter Pack. For more information, see Load data into your system.
The load files can then be imported into the HxGN SDx2 database. For more information, see Import a load file.

Configure revision schemes
The revision attributes identifies how documents are named following revisions. A set of pre-configured revision schemes is delivered, but you can also create your own revision scheme.
Create a new revision scheme
The revision attributes include the major and minor revisions, which are combined to create a sequence. The revision scheme defines a valid sequence for these attributes. A set of pre-configured revision schemes is delivered, but you can
also create your own revision scheme.
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Create Revision Scheme.
2. Type a Name and Description for the scheme.
3. Select a Major Sequence Type.
4. Complete the remaining boxes as required.
Property
Description
Custom Revision
This revision scheme can be customized by the user.
 No validation is performed. This box is display-only for queries.
Major sequence
The major sequence value and the first part of the revision scheme. This part normally changes when a document undergoes a
major change.
This box should be left empty/blank for numeric sequence types.
Major sequence type
The major revision sequence type. Options include sequence, repeating sequence, or numeric.
Major sequence minimum length
Major sequence minimum length (only applicable if Major sequence type is set to numeric).
Major sequence pad character
The pad character for the major sequence revision code (only applicable if Major sequence type is set to numeric).
Minor sequence
The minor sequence value and optional second part of the revision scheme, which normally changes when a document undergoes
an intermediate change and release.
This box should be left empty or blank for numeric sequence types or if you do not require a minor revision scheme.
Minor sequence type
The minor revision sequence type. Options include sequence, repeating sequence, or numeric.
Minor sequence minimum length
Minor sequence minimum length (only applicable if Major sequence type is set to numeric).
Minor sequence pad character
The pad character for the minor sequence revision code (only applicable if Minor sequence type is set to numeric).
Config level
The configuration level to which the revision scheme is applicable (not used in HxGN SDx2).
Take next major revision on merge
Set this option if a new major revision is to be created when merging a document up from a project to a plant. If the revision details
of the project revision are to be retained on merge, then leave this blank.
Classification
Provides revision schemes for a specific document classification or type. This is done by relating the revision scheme to a node of
the classification tree. Revisions schemes are inherited down the classification tree.
Configuration Item
Provides revision schemes for different plants or projects. For example, you might need different revision schemes in PlantA and
PlantB.
Interface Definitions
Provides revision schemes for specific document classes. For example, you might need different revision schemes for internal
documents, such as templates and shared documents.
This strategy is controlled by relating the revision schemes to a suitable document interface. Once related, the revision scheme is
valid only for classes that realize that interface. Additionally, these classes are not able to use any other schemes that are not
related to one of its realized interfaces.
How do revision schemes work?
Unlike other entities in HxGN SDx2, revision schemes are not tied to specific configurations (like project or plant levels). This means they can be used across multiple levels and projects. When searching for a revision scheme, the system
follows a hierarchical approach. It starts at the current level (for example, project) and works its way up through the configuration tree. If a revision scheme is found at any level, the search stops, and that scheme is made available. If no
revision scheme is found at any lower level, the system searches at the Configuration Top level, which is the highest level in the configuration hierarchy and sets the available revision scheme.
For example: Relating Revision Schemes to Projects
Revision Scheme ONE is associated with Plant A.
Revision Scheme TWO (and possibly others) is associated with Project_1407.
In this scenario:
Revision Scheme ONE will be available for selection in Plant A.
Revision Scheme ONE will not be available for selection in Project_1407 due to the association with Revision Scheme TWO.
In essence, revision schemes provide a flexible way to manage different versions of data across various levels of your project hierarchy.
Update a revision scheme
You can update a revision scheme to assign it to a plant in your PBS or change portions of the revision sequence.
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Revision Schemes.
2. In the Results grid, select a revision scheme.
3. Select Actions 
 > Update.
4. Enter information as needed. For more information about the Revision scheme details, see Create a new revision scheme.
Terminate a revision scheme
You can terminate a revision scheme to hide it from users in your tenant.
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Revision Schemes.
2. In the Results grid, select a revision scheme.
3. Select Actions 
 > Terminate.
Configure issue purposes
Each time a document is created, it can be assigned a specific issue purpose. This designates why the document is being issued, such as a draft for review or design, and so on.
Create an issue purpose
1. Select the Business Administration feature set, and select Create Issue Purpose.
 The issue purpose is always created under Configuration Top, independent of the currently selected scope.
2. On the New Issue Purpose page, provide the information in the Main details and Issue Purpose details sections.
 In the Sequence box, type a value to determine the order of appearance. The lower the number, the higher is the order of appearance.
 To relate new issue purposes to a plant or project, see Manage the plant configuration details and Manage the project configuration details.
Configure companies and organizations
Users are grouped together into organizations. Relationships with organizations control access to documents, data, communication items, and printers. Internal organizations can be specific departments or offices within your company.
External organizations can be external companies, suppliers, or manufacturers.
Transmittals, actions, and technical queries are used to manage communication and document distribution between internal and external organizations.
In a project, the default security rules control access so that users from external organizations only see documentation and data originating in their organization. However, an external organization user can see the originating organization's
documentation and data, if the external organization is identified as a collaborating organization by the originating organization user. See Configure security rules.
 Users should only be assigned roles from their own organization to avoid issues with the workflow.
Create an organization
1. Select the Business Administration feature set, and select Create Organization.
2. Enter the Name for the organization.
3. In the Organization role details area, select the type of organization including whether it is internal or external.
4. Complete the Organization details as required, and select Finish.
How do I assign an organization to multiple documents?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple documents:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the documents to the organization.
How do I assign an organization to multiple tags?
To relate a collaborating organization to multiple tags:
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations.
2. Select the organization in the list, and then select Actions 
 > Bulk Assign Items.
3. Use the relationship maintenance type control in the update form to relate the tags to the organization.
Load data into your system
A load file generator, SDx2_Starter Pack.xlsm, is available on Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . This file generates all the XML files necessary for setting up your system. For information on how to
download sample files from Smart Community, see Find sample data on Smart Community.
 Make sure that you save the downloaded file in a local folder. Otherwise, the macros will not work properly.
 You must have the System Administrator or Business Administrator role to load these files.
1. Modify the delivered sample data in the file as necessary to meet your requirements.
 Some values in the Excel® file are mandatory and must not be modified or removed. For more information, see Work through the SDx2 Starter Pack worksheets.
2. In the Info tab, select the Generate Load Files button.
 The load files are generated in XML format and saved in the SiteSetup folder, located in the same path where the Microsoft Excel file is available.
What files are generated by the load file generator?
When you generate the load files from this file, the following files will be generated:
Folders
Files
Classifications\Document Classifications\ImportData
1Level1DocEnums(SDX).xml
2Level2DocEnums(SDX).xml
3Level3DocEnums(SDX).xml
4Level4DocEnums(SDX).xml
5DocClassifications(SDX).xml
6DocClassifications_Mapping.xml
Load the files in the order they are listed here.
 For information on mapping document classifications, see System setup mappings.
Classifications\Material Classifications\ImportData
Material_Classifications.xml
Classifications\Tag Classifications\ImportData
1Tag Classifications.xml
2Tag Classifications Mapping.xml
Load the files in the order they are listed here.
 For information on mapping tag classifications, see System setup mappings.
Classifications\TagColumnSets\ImportData
1Tag_ColumnSets.xml
2Tag_ColumnItems.xml
3ColumnSetsAndColumnItemsRels1.xml
4ColumnSetsAndColumnItemsRels2.xml
Load the files in the order they are listed here.
 Tag column sets display column items in dynamic forms based on the classification
in the consolidation design component. For more information about column sets, see
Configure column sets.
Disciplines
Disciplines.xml
PBS Objects
1SPFPlants.xml
2PBSPlants.xml
3PBSAreas.xml
4PBSUnits.xml
Load the files in the order they are listed here.
 For more information on using these files, see Create the PBS using Import Data.
 Load these files into the database based on your system requirements.
3. Set your Create Scope to Scope Not Set.
4. Import the files into the database using the Import Data option. For more information, see Import a load file.
 If necessary, select 
 and drag the files into the order they are listed in the table.
5. When the files are successfully loaded, verify that all the files are loaded into the system.
 Document classifications, tag classifications, tag column sets, material classifications, and disciplines are created for the entire tenant. For each plant, you can select the specific values required. For more information, see Manage
the plant configuration details.
Review the classifications supported in your system
Use the following steps to ensure the necessary load files were processed and the classifications needed are defined in your HxGN SDx2 environment.
 The System Administrator role is required to perform these steps.
From the Business Administration feature set, select Classifications.
You must have the FDWTagClassification and SDADocumentClassification objects to share data from design tools to HxGN SDx2.
Prepare the plant
The following processes guide you through setting up your plant structure and your user access:
Plant breakdown structure (PBS)
Configure companies and organizations
Manage the plant configuration details
Configure user access
Assign roles to a user in a configuration
Assign security codes to a user
Configuring disciplines for a user
Configure security
Configure the Plant Breakdown Structure (PBS)
Engineering data is organized using a PBS. For example, a PBS for organizing plant data might be plant, area, unit. The PBS hierarchy is defined in the schema.
Create the PBS interactively
The Plant Breakdown Structure feature set can be used to interactively create the Hexagon default PBS hierarchy.
If you are creating a plant for the first time, see Create the plant.
If you are managing an existing PBS, see Manage the Hexagon default PBS interactively.
 For preparing your digital twin, you can use load files to set up your PBS. For more information about using load files, see Use the SDx2 Starter Pack to create your digital twin.
Create the plant
You can create the plant in the Plant Breakdown Structure feature set. For information on managing your PBS after initial setup, see Manage the Hexagon default PBS interactively.
You must have the Business Administrator role to create the PBS.
This method can only be used to create the Hexagon default PBS hierarchy.
1. Set your Create Scope to Not Set.
2. Set your Integration Data Filters to Consolidated to create the plant.
 To view Integration Data Filters, select Settings 
 > Scope.
3. In the Plant Breakdown Structure feature set, select Create Plant.
4. Enter a name for the plant. When creating a plant, ensure that you follow the guidelines in Plant Naming Guidelines.
5. Set the status to Active.
 Accelerated 3D Graphics Enabled is for a future enhancement.
 You can select Manage Config Details to choose the required classifications, disciplines, and issue purposes for this plant. For more information, see Manage the plant configuration details.
Manage the Hexagon default PBS interactively
The PBS can be set interactively in the Manage PBS System Hierarchy dashboard.
You must have the Business Administrator role to create the PBS.
This procedure can only be used to create the Hexagon default PBS hierarchy.
You must have Integration Data Filters set to PBSManager to manage the PBS.
 To view Integration Data Filters, select Settings 
 > Scope.
1. If you are creating an area or unit, check that your Create Scope is set to the plant you are managing.
2. In the Plant Breakdown Structure feature set, select Manage PBS System Hierarchy.
3. In the left pane, select  next to the Plant, Area, or Unit to update or terminate that object or to create an object under it.
 When creating a plant, ensure you follow the guidelines in Plant Naming Guidelines.
4. Complete any other required fields.
Plant naming guidelines
Use the following guidelines when naming your plant, or top-level hierarchy items, which will be used in an integrated environment. These limitations are imposed by the authoring tools and the database software. For these guidelines,
"plant" is used as an example of a top-level hierarchy item.
Do not use special characters at all in any part of a plant name or PBS, such as area or unit. These include .<,>^=+:?|/';{}[]~`!%*()&$@#. However there is an exception to this, you may use double apostrophe (") at the beginning of a
name to indicate that it starts with an apostrophe (').
A warning message that the characters are invalid is displayed if you use any special characters.
The plant name is case-sensitive and cannot exceed 52 characters if Smart Instrumentation is involved in integration.
The plant name must be unique within the current design component. This limitation is imposed by Smart Instrumentation.
We recommend that the Smart Instrumentation design component schema name and design component name match the plant name.
We recommend that the plant name be the same name across all authoring tools.
You can use digits and spaces within the body of the plant name, for example Plant 1. However, spaces are treated the same as underscores; so, if you name a new plant with a name that already exists, but use a space or an
underscore to differentiate, an error message will state it is already in existence. For example, Plant 1 and Plant_1 are considered the same.
Configure user access
Access control configures what is exposed to each user in the feature sets and actions menus of objects, and what objects are returned from any search or relationship expansion. The user roles consist of sets of access groups that grant
access to commands and relationship expansions grouped together by functional operation. The filtering of data items returned to the user is governed by security rules defining which access groups can see data items based on different
conditions.
Existing security rules can be modified. For example, to filter documents based on a new custom property.
Method, entry point, and relationship access can be removed or modified. For example, to remove a search option.
Access groups should not be removed from existing roles.
New custom access groups can be added to existing roles. For example, to expose new data structure management.
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
What are the rules for changing the access control?
Security rules
Security rules provide conditional access to data items by access group. These rules can be freely edited, for example, to change the conditional filter or the access group for the rule.
Security codes are assigned to users for each plant or project in which they work. The rules match the security codes to those on documents and other objects to filter the search results. The security codes provided with the database
can be freely changed.
Command and relationship access
Access to methods, entry points, and relationships is granted by access groups and can be removed or replaced.
Each role has a role-specific access group to allow commands to be specifically exposed to each role.
A common change is to remove a method from a general access group that is on many roles and add it to one or more role-specific access groups to limit its availability.
The conditions used on the methods cannot be changed.
You can create new access groups to expose new methods, entry points, and relationships.
You can edit the relationship access group configuration for the info map and so on.
Roles
Existing roles can be extended with custom access groups, but existing access groups cannot be removed.
New roles can be created with any mix of existing or new access groups.
Update a user
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx, HxGN SDx2 | Update a user <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/6399da041ea0ebd7e4612da4>
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the User tab, find the required user, and select 
 > UPDATE.
3. You can update any of the following information:
Description
Organization that must be set to assign a security code
E-mail address that is used for all e-mail notification to that user
The preferred notification mechanism
 To disable a user's account, select Disable User.
If a user's account is disabled, a business administrator must reassign items owned by the user to other roles or users. Items that can be transferred to another user include workflow steps, actions, and technical queries. For
information on how to reassign items, see Reassign work to a different user.
The notification mechanisms available are:
E-mail - The user receives e-mails
Notify - The user receives a notification in the Notifications list.
E-mail and notify
These notifications are sent out by activities such as completion of batch reports and user subscription processing. For example, the user may wish to be informed when a given document is approved.
Manage user role assignments
Role assignments link the user to a role in a given configuration, such as an Engineer in Plant A. For more information about setting up security for your users, see Configure security.
Role assignments are managed in the User Management page.
Select
To display
Configuration
The list of configurations on the left and a matrix of users against roles. When you select a configuration, you can see the users assigned to
roles within that configuration.
Role
The list of roles on the left and a matrix of users against configurations. When you select a role, you can see which users are assigned to which
configurations for that role.
User
The list of users on the left and a matrix of roles against configurations. When you select a user, you can see the role assignments for that user.
You need to have the Business Administrator or System Administrator role to manage user role assignments.
You can create new roles using the create role functionality, see Create a role.
To ensure a user role is accessible, verify that the role's classification aligns with the user's organizational status. Conversely, ensure that the user's organizational status corresponds to the role assigned to them. For instance, roles
can be classified as internal or external. Internal roles should be assigned to users within the owner organization, such as Document Control. External roles should only be assigned to external users, such as Contractor Document
Control.
What do the buttons on the User Management page do?
Button
Select this to
Select one check box at a time.
Select all check boxes in a row when any check box in the row is selected or deselected.
Select all check boxes in a column when any check box in the column is selected or deselected. It also changes all the security codes in a column when
any security code changes are made.
Enable Inherit mode.
Enable Copy mode. For more information on copying role assignments, see Copy role assignments to another user.
Show the configuration top level.
Order the list with new items at the top.
Assign security codes to a user
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx, HxGN SDx2 | Create a security code <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/63ebad2aca38a591ec8c23b4>
Security codes are used to restrict user access to documents and data using configured security rules, see Configure security rules. HxGN SDx2 delivers preconfigured security codes that can be changed if required, see Create a security
code. For a user to find documents and data in a project, they must be assigned matching security codes for that project.
You can assign one or more security codes to an existing user within a specific configuration. You must have a Business Administrator or System Administrator role to access this option.
In order to assign security codes, your role must be configured with at least one domain on its role-to-access group relationships. For more information, see Manage the access groups for a role.
An organization must be set for the user to assign a security code.
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the Configuration tab, select the required configuration.
Use the Filter to search for the configuration. The filter options 
 change how the filter works.
To show the configuration top level, at the top of the Configuration tab, select  > Show Configuration Top.
Use Promote Newest Items 
 to bring new users to the top of the list.
3. Either:
For each user, select the required security codes in the Security Codes column, or
Select 
 and, for any user, select the security codes to apply to all users on the page.
 You can filter the User column to display the required users only. If the Persist toggle is enabled in the filter, the users remain filtered when you select a different configuration. You can remove disabled users from the list of
users displayed by selecting 
 > Hide disabled users.
 Roles can also be assigned to users in this Configuration tab, see Assign roles to a user in a configuration.
Assign roles to a user in a configuration
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here:
HxGN SDx, HxGN SDx2 | Assigning roles <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/64188e460b2c96fda1521c69>
HxGN SDx, HxGN SDx2 | Assigning roles to new users on a project <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/641892e40b2c96fda1521dbc>
 In order to manage role assignments, your role must be configured with at least one domain on its role-to-access group relationships. For more information, see Manage the access groups for a role.
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the Configuration tab, select the required plant or project.
Use the Filter to search for a configuration. The filter options 
 change how the filter works.
To show the configuration top level, at the top of the Configuration tab, select  > Show Configuration Top.
Use Promote Newest Items 
 to bring new users to the top of the list.
3. Enable Inherit Mode 
 if you want to select all the items under a plant or project when you assign a user to a role.
For example, if you select Plant A and Plant A has two projects, when this option is enabled, the same role assignments will be made for the user for the two projects as well as for Plant A.
4. For each user, select the roles that you want to assign to the user for the selected plant or project.
 You can filter the User column to display the required users only. If the Persist toggle is enabled in the filter, the users remain filtered when you select a different configuration. You can remove disabled users from the list of
users displayed by clicking 
> Hide disabled users.
 Unchecking a role removes it from the user.
How do I select all check boxes in a column or row?
When managing role assignments in the User Management page, you can change the behavior of the check boxes using the buttons at the top of the page.
Select this
To select or deselect
One check box at a time.
All check boxes in a row when any check box in the row is selected or deselected.
All check boxes in a column when any check box in the column is selected or deselected.
 Selecting this button will also change all the security codes in a column when any security code changes are made.
 If Inherit Mode 
 is enabled, it is applied to all selected check boxes in the row or column.
What happens if a user is removed from a role?
If a user is removed from an assigned role in an existing workflow, the user is informed that all their claimed steps are terminated from the system, and the tasks are removed from their To Do List. The steps are automatically reassigned
so that they can be claimed by other users with that role assignment.
However, if a user is disabled or terminated, the business administrator must reassign all the user's claimed tasks so that the workflow steps can continue on the workflow path.
 Any completed or previously reassigned user workflow steps are not terminated and continue on the workflow path.
Manage the organization for users
You must have a Business Administrator or System Administrator role to access this option.
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the Configuration or the Role tab, select the required configuration or role.
 Use Promote Newest Items 
 to bring new users to the top of the list.
3. Either:
For each user, select or change the organization in the Organization column, or
Select 
 and, for any user, select or change the organization to apply to all users on the page.
 You can filter the User column to display the required users only. If the Persist toggle is enabled in the filter, the users remain filtered when you select a different configuration. You can remove disabled users from the list of
users displayed by selecting 
 > Hide disabled users.
Get an overview of users assigned to a role
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the Role tab, select a role to view which users are assigned that role in each configuration.
Use the Filter to search for a specific role. The filter options 
 change how the filter works, and the drop-down menu 
 selects the items the search includes.
You can select 
 to filter the users displayed. You can remove disabled users from the list of users displayed by selecting 
 > Hide disabled users.
 indicates that the user is also assigned the role for any plants and projects below that configuration.
You can also create new roles using the create role functionality, see Create a role.
Role management
To manage the role assignments a user can make, there is a relationship set between specific roles. For example, a user can assign other users to roles that are managed by their role. So a user with a Project Manager role may assign
other users to the Engineer role. However, the user cannot assign users to the Project Manager role.
You can use the User Management functionality to view or edit the roles being managed by another role and to view or edit the roles that manage other roles.
1. In the User Management page, select the role you want to update in the ROLE tab.
2. Select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In Related Items, select the MANAGE ROLE tab or the MANAGED BY ROLE tab.
a. Search and edit the roles that this role can manage in the MANAGE ROLE list.
b. Search and edit the roles that can manage this role in the MANAGED BY ROLE list.
 When you update a role, be sure it is linked to the roles that can manage it; otherwise, it will not be possible to assign users to the role.
 You can create new roles using the create role functionality, see Create a role.
Copy user access
User role assignments and security codes can be copied between users.
Once you have created new users, you may want to copy role assignments from an existing user to the new users. This can be done for a specified role or configuration. For more information, see Copy role assignments to another user.
When you have created new configurations, you can copy the assignments for all the users in an existing configuration to the same users in the new configuration. For more information, see Copy user access between configurations.
 Any existing role assignments and security codes are overwritten by the copy process.
Copy role assignments to another user
 In order to manage role assignments, your role must be configured with at least one domain on its role-to-access group relationships. For more information, see Manage the access groups for a role.
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the Configuration tab, select the required plant or project.
You can also select a role in the Role tab to copy assignments between users.
Security codes are also copied in the Configuration tab. They are not copied in the Roles tab.
To show the configuration top level, at the top of the Configuration tab, select  > Show Configuration Top. You can only copy assignments for this level if it is shown.
3. Select Copy Mode 
 at the top of the list of users.
 A banner is displayed telling you to select a user to copy from. When a user is selected, the banner shows the selected user.
4. Select Source for the user from which you want to copy assignments.
 You can filter the User column to display the required users only. If the Persist toggle is enabled in the filter, the users remain filtered when you select a different configuration. You can remove disabled users from the list of
users displayed by selecting 
 > Hide disabled users.
5. In the Target column, select the users to copy the assignments to.
Use Promote Newest Items 
 to bring new users to the top of the list.
Select the check box at the top of the Target column to select all the users on the current page.
6. Select Save to save your changes.
 You must save your changes before selecting a new source user or moving to a different tab.
Copy user access between configurations
 In order to manage role assignments, your role must be configured with at least one domain on its role-to-access group relationships. For more information, see Manage the access groups for a role.
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select User Management.
2. In the Configuration tab, find the plant or project to copy user access from, and select 
 > COPY ACCESS.
3. In the Copy Access window, select the plants and projects to copy the user access to. A summary of your selections is shown in the right of the window.
 Use the Filter to search for the configuration. The filter options 
 change how the filter works.
 You can only copy to plants and projects that have a status of Active or Created.
4. Select Finish to copy the role assignments and security codes to the selected configurations.
 Only role assignments that you manage are copied to the target configurations. Role assignments that are in the target configuration but not present in the source configuration are removed.
Configuring disciplines for a user
By default, a user can create documents and other items associated with any of the disciplines available in the active configuration. The following options are available:
To limit the disciplines for a user, they must be related to the user.
The default behavior is that when no disciplines are configured on the user in the active configuration, the user will not have access to any of the disciplines. To change this behavior, you must replace the
DISCIPLINESFORUSERINCREATECONFIG_OR_ALL environment variable with the DISCIPLINESFORUSERINCREATECONFIG variable in the PropComparisons property for the following edge definitions:
EDG_SDADocMasterDisciplineCreateConfig
EDG_SDAProjectComsDisciplinesCreateConfig
EDG_SDAWorkPackDisciplinesCreateConfig
EDG_FDWTagDisciplineCreateConfig
EDG_FDWAssetDisciplineCreateConfig
 For a user to query the documents and other items associated with any of the disciplines available in the active configuration, the ALLDISCIPLINESFORQUERYCONFIG environment variable is available for use. You can configure
the above-mentioned edge definitions on the display items to achieve the following:
Return all the disciplines for the current query configuration.
If no disciplines are found for the current query configuration, the environment variable returns all the disciplines present in the next higher configuration level.
If no disciplines are found for the current query configuration, including any of the higher configuration levels, the environment variable returns nothing.
Create a discipline
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx, HxGN SDx2 | Create a discipline <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/634e97702d473d4781f29ca0>
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Create Discipline.
2. On the New Discipline page, provide the information in the Main details section.
The discipline is always created under Configuration Top, independent of the currently selected scope.
To relate new disciplines to a plant or project, see Manage the plant configuration details and Manage the project configuration details.
For information on configuring disciplines for a user, see Configuring disciplines for a user.
View and update disciplines
To view
Select Disciplines in the Business Administration feature set.
To update
Search for your discipline, and select Actions 
 > Update.
To terminate
Search for your discipline, and select Actions 
 > Terminate.
Configure security
What are the rules for changing the roles, access groups, and security rules?
The following rules must be followed when changing roles, access groups, and security rules.
Roles
Existing roles can be extended with custom access groups. However, you must not remove the relationship between the existing access groups and existing roles. An exception is that you cannot modify or delete the access groups
related to the following relationship definitions SDxModuleRestrictedRole, SPFManagesRoles, SPFRoleRoleCategory.
New roles can be created with any mix of existing or new access groups.
Access Groups
Do not remove the delivered access groups.
Delivered access groups can be related to any delivered items such as properties, methods, interfaces, and relationship definitions along with custom relationship definitions.
Existing access groups can be removed or replaced on delivered methods, entry points, and relationships.
Do not remove existing access groups from delivered roles.
Domains and Domain Groups
Delivered domain groups cannot be modified or deleted. However, there is an exception where custom domains can be added to the delivered and custom domain groups.
Security Rules
Security rules provide conditional access to data items by access group and can be freely edited.
 Security rules can have a major impact on system performance and the delivered security rules have been configured to improve system performance by moving some relationships to object properties. Any customized rules
will not benefit from these improvements so don't create complex rules that lead to queries with a large number of ORs or JOINs.
Which security features determine what can be seen or done in the system?
What you can see or do is determined by which objects you need to see and which actions you need to perform to do your job.
Your administrator can use many different components of the security model to control what you can do or see in the system.
For more information on the security model, see Security model overview.
Security model feature
What it does
Role and role assignment
Determines your level of access to data and functionality in a specific plant or project
configuration. You can belong to more than one role per configuration. Roles are
associated with related access groups, domains, and owning groups.
Access group
Determines what actions you can perform in the system and which functional
components you can access in your system. Users are related to roles, which are
related to access groups.
Security rule
Limits the types of data that you can query in the system. Your associated access
groups are used to determine which security rules are relevant.
Security code
Sets different security levels for users and data in the system. Security codes are
used by security rules, which determine your access to documents and data items
that can be secured by these codes. You may be assigned different sets of security
codes in different plants or projects.
Owning group
Sets up ownership of data, typically by department or discipline, as well as controls
your access to an object or parts of an object based on its ownership. Objects can be
owned by a user or by an owning group. Owning groups are associated to roles. The
default owning group is engineering to which everyone has access.
Configuration
You can be assigned different roles in different plants and projects.
You can create and manipulate data in a single plant or project. This is also known as
the create scope. Objects created in projects are not visible from parallel projects or
its parent plant.
You can query across multiple plants and projects. This is also known as the query
scope.
Security is configured to ensure users see only the items to which they should have access. This access differs for internal and external organizations. Two access groups are used to define these rules:
SDACompanyFilter – This is used on internal company roles.
SDAContractorFilter – This is used on external contractor roles.
The central security rules are configured to access documents and comments. The SDACompanyFilter is used on rules that allow the users to see the documents from any organization as long as their security level permits it. The
SDAContractorFilter is used on rules that allow the users to see the documents from their organization as long as their security level permits it.
The relationship between methods and access group conditions govern who can edit what and when.
The majority of roles have a relationship to the FDWDocEditByOrg access group. This is used in most of the conditions on updating methods; for example:
It is only possible to edit documents originating from their organization.
The global document control role also has the SDADocEditANYOrg access group.
The conditions on the updating methods also include a test of: OR Instr(Env.ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERINCREATECONFIG, 'SDADocEditANYOrg')>0.
This means that if the user is in a role that is related to the SDADocEditANYOrg access group, they can edit documents from other organizations.
The document, tag, work package, and communication forms have access control configured by access group with sections configured for different roles.
The majority of roles have a relationship to the SDAByMyOrgOnlyFormAccess access group that is related to sections in which the originating organization display items are read-only. This access control ensures that the
users can create items only from their own organization.
The global document control role also has a relationship to the SDAProjComsEditANYOrg access group.
This gives access to editable display items for originating organizations on documents and tags.
This also gives access to editable display items for the From User on project communications. However, the From Organization is still read-only, because the role cannot create project communication items that come
from other organizations. The only exception is an internal transmittal, which can be used to record the receipt of documentation when submittals are not used.
Markup access is governed in a different way. The LimitViewMarkup method has conditional access so the users can see only markup layers that are created by users of their organization.
Security model overview
The security model is a flexible mechanism to control role-based user access to data and the operations that can be performed on that data.
The security model is comprised of:
Users
Roles, domains, and access groups
Configurations
Role assignments and owning groups
Security rules
Security codes
A user is assigned a role in a configuration for example, to work as a Designer in Project1. Roles are related to access groups, which control access to methods (commands), relationships, and security rules. Any number of methods or
relationships can be grouped together on an access group. A role can provide functionality through any number of access groups, and the same user can play different roles depending on the configuration.
Users and objects, like documents, can both be related to specific security codes to limit access, and security rules can be used to further restrict access and are related through the access group.
The security model controls user access to:
Application modules
Menus and toolbars
Shortcut menu commands
Relationship manipulation and navigation
Data segregation based on user and tool ownership
Conditional data access supported by query
Access group configuration
Access groups control user access to actions, menu commands, feature sets, functions, relationships, and navigation. They are created to represent different levels of functional access by organizing methods, relationships, and
permissions so that they can be associated with specific roles.
Creating access groups is one of the primary tasks when you configure security. Roles are related to access groups, which in turn are related to methods, interfaces, and view definitions. This provides the roles access to the different
components of the system. Therefore, assigning users to a specific role controls what the user can and cannot do in the system.
 For more information on assigning users to roles, see Manage user role assignments.
Create access groups
 You need to have the System Administrator role to create new access groups and manage their roles.
