1. In the System Administration feature set, select Create Access Group.
2. Enter the name and description for the access group.
3. Expand the Access group details section, and select the applicable Configuration statuses, such as Active, Approved, Completed, and so on.
 Before users can access any documents or data, a new access group needs to be assigned roles. For more information, see Manage roles for an access group.
Most access groups should be restricted to active configurations. This means that method actions and commands for the access group are only available for objects in a project when the project is in the Active state.
Access groups controlling management functions may use different statuses. For example, the Merge command needs to be available on objects in a completed project.
After an access group has been created, the next task is to configure the command and relationship access.
Manage the access groups for a role
Roles are related to a set of access groups with domains and owning groups optionally configured on these relationships.
1. Search for a role, and select Actions 
 > Manage Access Groups.
2. Add or remove the access groups as required.
3. Select the owning groups or domains to configure for the relationship.
Why would I want to restrict method access by object ownership?
The access granted by any access group in a role can be restricted to a set of owning groups by identifying them on the role or access group relationship. This enables the same access group to be used in different roles on different sets of
data identified by their owning group.
The most typical use of this is to restrict access to documents and data by their department or discipline by using the owning groups to represent a department or discipline.
The domains accessible for query are all the domains configured on the role-to-access group relationships for the user and all their dependent domains.
At least one domain must be configured on the role to access group relationships, otherwise, the user cannot access any domain information.
You can restrict method access based on the ownership of an object, select the owning groups that you want to restrict the access to in the Owning Group column.
For more information, see Manage roles for an access group and Use the manage related items dialog.
You can also create new roles for an access group, see Create a role.
Manage roles for an access group
Roles are related to a set of access groups with domains and owning groups optionally configured on these relationships, so your users can access data on your system. You can assign different roles to each access group.
1. Search for the access group, and select Actions 
 > Manage Roles.
2. Search and locate the roles to assign to the access group in the left Library pane.
3. Select Relate selected items 
 to relate the selected role to the access group.
 You can also drag a role to the right Related Items pane to relate it to the access group.
At least one domain must be configured on the role or access group relationships; otherwise, the user cannot access any domain information.
You can restrict the access of a role to methods using owning groups. For more information. see Restrict access to methods using owning groups.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Manage methods for an access group
Methods are exposed as function tiles in a feature set, actions, action menus, toolbars, and search options. Methods are displayed to users through their relationships with specific roles. When an access group is associated with a role, the
methods that the access group exposes are available to all the users assigned to that role.
The relationships between methods and access groups can be configured using Manage Methods command, which allows you to add methods to an access group and edit the relationship properties.
Search for an access group, and select Actions 
 > Manage Methods.
You can also use the Manage Access Groups action from the method.
You can restrict access to methods using owning groups, see Restrict access to methods in an access group and Further ways to restrict access to methods.
For more information on creating, updating, and managing methods, see Configure methods.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Restrict access to methods in an access group
The access granted by any access group can be restricted to a set of owning groups by identifying them on the access group relationship. This enables the same access group to be used in different roles on data identified by their owning
group. In this way, you can restrict access to methods based on the ownership.
You can restrict method access based on the ownership of an object so that the user must be either the owner of the object or related to the owning group found on the access group. When an owning group is set on a role, all the method
functionality granted by that access group can be restricted to only the objects within that owning group. Using this approach, the role of a user can grant them permission to update objects that belong to one owning group, but not update
the objects that belong to other owning groups.
 To restrict access to methods using owning groups, see Restrict access to methods using owning groups.
At least one domain must be configured on the role or access group relationships; otherwise, the user cannot access any domain information.
For more information on managing owning groups, see Configure owning groups.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Further ways to restrict access to methods
The following describes what options can be used for access restrictions and how to configure them.
Restricting method access by design component
You can restrict access to methods based on the Integration Data Filter (IDF) selected for the method to create a relationship with an integration design component. This ensures that the method is only visible to the end user based on their
selected IDF when set in their scopes selection.
Restricting update access to configuration-controlled objects
A client API configured to update will not expose its actions methods on configuration-controlled objects unless the user sets the create configuration scope to the existing configuration of the object. For example, a document created in a
project cannot be updated unless the create configuration scope is set to that project.
Restricting method access by conditions
A method can be related to a condition that must be satisfied before the method can run. A condition can be configured on the method, and this condition must be satisfied when users in the access group try to run the method. For
example, the document update method may be available only to engineers on CURRENT documents, but an administrator may be condition that allows them to revise a SUPERSEDED document as well.
Restricting method access by object ownership
You can restrict method access based on the ownership of an object so that the user must be either the owner of the object or related to the owning group found on the access group. To do this, when the access group is related to a
method, set the option required in the Filter by Owner Or Owning Group column.
When an owning group is set on a role, all the functionality granted by that access group can be restricted to only the objects within that owning group. The methods on the access group are not automatically restricted in this way, the Filter
by Owner or Owning Group property needs to also be set on the method related access group.
When a method action menu is evaluated, the system first checks if the owning group filter flag is set on the method related access group. Then if set, it also checks if the role or access group relationship has an owning group. If the
selected object has an owning group, it must be one of those on the role – access group relationship for the method to be available to the user.
Restricting method access to object workflow recipients
You can restrict method access to only workflow action recipients if the object is in workflow. To do this, when the access group is related to a method, set the Restrict to workflow recipients property.
When set, this restricts access to the method when the object is in a workflow. When the object is in a workflow, the method is only available to users that are recipients of an action for the current workflow step. Information-only recipients
do not get the method even if they are recipients of the current step.
Manage relationship definitions for an access group
You can update a relationship from a relationship definition to an access group when the role of the user has an access group related to the relationship definition.
Search for an access group, and select Actions 
 > RelDef Access.
When a relationship is being created between two objects, the creation in each direction is controlled separately.
The system relates the object at end 1 onto the object at end 2 based on the interfaces on the object compared with the interfaces at end 1 and end 2 of the relationship definition.
For more information on relationship definitions, see Relationship and edge definitions.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Manage access groups for an API Entry Point
You can relate access groups to an API Entry Point for the access group to see a menu items or functions.
1. Go to the API Entry Point in a results grid, and select Actions 
 > Update.
2. In the Required Access Groups tab, add criteria or wildcards to the search bar to find the access group, and select Search 
.
3. In the Access Groups results list, choose the access group from the available list, and select Add.
Users must have an access group that allows you to update API Entry Points.
If you remove the relationships between the API Entry Point and its access groups, the API Entry Point becomes available to all users.
To terminate an access group from the API Entry Point, in the Required Access Groups tab, choose the access group from the list, and select Terminate.
You can also use the Manage Related Items dialog to relate access groups to an API Entry Point. For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Managing roles
Roles govern the user access to commands and data. Users can perform different roles in different configurations (different plants or projects).
Roles determine the user’s level of access in the system (based on related message access groups).
Roles determine the domains accessed (queried) by a user.
Roles determine the access to different data, based on owning groups configured on the role.
Roles determine the access to different data, based on domains configured on the role.
Role assignments link the user to a role in a given configuration. For more information, see Manage user role assignments.
To configure or create new roles, you need to login as a system administrator.
For more information on creating roles, see Create a role.
For more information about the roles delivered with HxGN SDx2, see Delivered roles.
Create a role
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Create Role.
2. In the New Role dialog, enter the name and description for the new role.
3. In Role details, you can select a role category from the Role categories list. The new role can then access data and information based on the role category it is associated with.
What does the role category do?
By setting the role category, you can ensure the system filters roles based on the selected category to secure access to data and documents to only the roles associated with the selected role category.
Two role categories are delivered with the software. When you create a new role, you can associate the role with one of categories as follows:
Projects-Internal - If a new role is associated to this category, the role is defined as an internal role and can be used for internal project communications.
Projects-External - If a new role is associated to this category, the role is defined as an external role and can be used for external project communications.
4. In Related Items section:
a. Select the MANAGE ROLE tab to search for and select the roles in the list that this new role can manage, such as Contractor Discipline Engineer.
b. Select the MANAGED BY ROLE tab to search for and select the roles in the list that can manage this new role, such as Business Administrator.
 When you create a new role, be sure to link it to the roles that can manage it in the MANAGE BY ROLES tab; otherwise, it will not be possible to assign users to this new role. For more information on managing
user role assignments, see Manage user role assignments.
 New roles must be related to access groups before any users can access documents and data in a project. For more information, see Manage the access groups for a role.
Delivered roles
While you can create your own custom roles in HxGN SDx2, it includes several default roles designed to cover many of the basic tasks of your users. A description of each delivered role is provided below. For a detailed look at what tasks
can be performed by user in each role, refer to the HxGN SDx2 Roles Matrix <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> .
System Administrator - An IT professional with a strong technical background, responsible to configure, maintain, and support the overall system infrastructure. They ensure the proper setup and functionality of software applications and
system components across the environment.
Business Administrator - An administrator with expertise in configuring and maintaining business-related aspects of the system. This includes the setup and management of projects, contracts, disciplines, users, Work Breakdown
Structures (WBS), Plant Breakdown Structures (PBS), and other organizational components. Their responsibilities ensure that business operations are accurately represented and supported within the system.
Contractor Discipline Engineer - An engineer responsible for preparing documents, tags, and other technical content for submission. This information is provided to Document Control for upload into the system. These engineers do not
engage directly with the owner operator unless guided by a project engineer. They respond to project communications addressed specifically to them. Users in this role work for the contractor or another external organization and contribute
discipline-specific expertise to the project.
Contractor Document Control - A documentation specialist responsible for creating, submitting, and tracking project documents sent to the owner-operator. They are generally part of a contractor or external organization, ensuring
accurate and timely flow of project documentation. This role includes generating transmittals and submittals, uploading content, and assembling comprehensive information packages.
Contractor Project Engineer - A project engineer responsible for creating technical queries and reviewing project documentation for contractual compliance. They play a key role in ensuring that engineering deliverables meet project
requirements and contractual obligations. Users with this role work for the contractor or another external organization and often serve as a liaison between discipline engineers and the owner-operator.
 The contractor roles have limited access to HxGN SDx2. If a user is assigned a contractor role in addition to a non-contractor role, some features are hidden and unavailable to them even if another one of their roles has the
necessary permissions to access the features. This is because the limited access permissions for the contractor role override the permissions of the other roles.
Discipline Engineer - An engineer responsible for creating project documents and communications, as well as reviewing incoming documentation relevant to their area of expertise. They contribute to the technical quality and accuracy of
engineering deliverables and support project coordination within their discipline.
Document Control - A user responsible for creating and managing documents, monitoring their progress, and expediting them as needed. This role ensures that documentation flows efficiently throughout the project lifecycle. Additionally,
they can act as a surrogate to perform actions on behalf of other users, such as creating project communications or submissions when delegated.
Global Document Control - A user responsible for creating and managing documents across all organizations involved in the project. They monitor document progress, expedite submissions as needed, and ensure timely communication.
This role may also act as a delegate to perform tasks on behalf of other users, such as initiating project communications or submissions. Additionally, they are responsible for creating document matrices to support submittal review and
tracking processes.
Project Engineer - A user with a strong engineering background, combined with project management expertise to successfully drive technical projects. A project engineer participates in project reviews, creates new projects, and manages
project communications. They are responsible for monitoring compliance with contractual requirements and ensuring the overall quality of project deliverables. As the default consolidator for reviews, they integrate feedback and ensure all
relevant information is accurately reflected in project documentation.
Data Capture Administrator - An administrator with a background in information systems, data management, or a related technical field, with expertise in configuring and maintaining data-driven applications. They are responsible for
configuring and managing the Data Capture functionality within the system. This role ensures that data collection processes are properly set up, maintained, and aligned with project or organizational requirements, enabling accurate and
efficient data management.
Data Capture Reviewer - A user with a strong background in data management and quality assurance with strong attention to detail and analytical skills. They initiate Data Capture jobs and review their progress, including any revisions
and data updates. This role ensures the accuracy and completeness of data collected during the process, facilitating effective data management and project tracking.
MoC Owner - A user with an engineering background or equivalent technical expertise responsible for reviewing change requests and approving their implementation. This role ensures that all changes are assessed, documented, and
executed in alignment with operational and safety standards. In some cases, users with extensive plant operations or safety management experience may also fulfill this role, provided they demonstrate a strong understanding of change
control processes.
MoC Approver - A user with a strong background in project management, risk assessment, and organizational processes with experience in operations or IT, to effectively evaluate and manage changes that could impact systems,
processes, or services.
Data Model Viewer - A user with a background in information management, who can view the Common Schema within HxGN SDx2. This role enables users to analyze data structures, relationships, and schema definitions to support
system integration, traceability, and consistent data interpretation across applications.
Viewer - A common role that provides read-only access to project information, dashboards, and reports. This role is primarily for users who need to analyze or reference data without making any changes to it.
Engineer - A user working within operations, inspections, maintenance, and reliability teams. They perform navigation tasks, as well as view and manage project documents and data relevant to their discipline and responsibilities. This role
commonly supports daily tasks by accessing and reviewing information critical to their operational responsibilities.
Planner - A user with a background in engineering or operations management who ensures effective execution of inspection and maintenance work packages. This role is essential to optimizing resource allocation, reducing downtime, and
aligning maintenance tasks with operational schedules.
SDxConnectors Administrator - An engineer with expertise in systems integration and configuration, responsible for setting up and managing HxGN SDx2 connectors, which integrate third-party applications with HxGN SDx2. Their goal
is to ensure seamless data exchange, adding value by enhancing project visibility and decision-making across integrated systems.
SDxConnectors Viewer - A user with basic operational knowledge who can view data from third-party systems integrated with HxGN SDx2. This role is focused on accessing and analyzing external data without making changes to it.
Manage the access groups for a role
Roles are related to a set of access groups with domains and owning groups optionally configured on these relationships.
1. Search for a role, and select Actions 
 > Manage Access Groups.
2. Add or remove the access groups as required.
3. Select the owning groups or domains to configure for the relationship.
Why would I want to restrict method access by object ownership?
The access granted by any access group in a role can be restricted to a set of owning groups by identifying them on the role or access group relationship. This enables the same access group to be used in different roles on different sets of
data identified by their owning group.
The most typical use of this is to restrict access to documents and data by their department or discipline by using the owning groups to represent a department or discipline.
The domains accessible for query are all the domains configured on the role-to-access group relationships for the user and all their dependent domains.
At least one domain must be configured on the role to access group relationships, otherwise, the user cannot access any domain information.
You can restrict method access based on the ownership of an object, select the owning groups that you want to restrict the access to in the Owning Group column.
For more information, see Manage roles for an access group and Use the manage related items dialog.
You can also create new roles for an access group, see Create a role.
Role assignments
To manage the role assignments a user can make, there is a relationship set between specific roles. For example, a user can assign other users to roles that are managed by their role. So a user with a Project Manager role may assign
other users to the Engineer role. However, the user cannot assign other users to their Project Manager role.
You can use the User Management functionality to view or edit the roles being managed by another role and to view or edit the roles that manage other roles.
1. Go to the User Management page, and select the role you want to update in the ROLE tab.
2. Select Actions 
 > Update.
 You can also search for a role, and select Actions 
 > Update. You can update the role settings the same as found in Create a role.
 When you update a role, be sure it is linked to the roles that can manage it; otherwise, it will not be possible to assign users to the role.
For more information on managing user roles, see Manage user role assignments.
For more information about the roles delivered with HxGN SDx2, see Delivered roles.
Configure owning groups
Owning groups are set up by the System Administrator role to govern the ownership of data in a system. They can be configured to restrict access to different sets of data in the same access group. Owning groups are associated with roles
to separate user access to different types of objects or parts of an object based on their ownership, such as by department or by discipline.
The owning groups available to a user are those configured for the user's role. Role assignments are used to link the user to a specific role in a given configuration, such as an ENGINEER in Plant A.
Objects can be owned by an owning group or by a specific user role related to an owning group.
The default owning group is ENGINEER to which everyone has access. Other owning groups are also configured by default, such as VIEW ONLY to restrict access to only view documents or reports.
The Engineer role is related to a number of access groups and the ENGINEER owning group, which restricts access to objects only owned by the ENGINEER owning group.
Role assignments can be configured as described in Manage user role assignments.
Create an owning group
1. Select System Administration feature set > Create Owning Group.
2. Type a name and description for the new owning group, and select FINISH.
Restrict access to methods using owning groups
When you relate a role to an access group, you can limit that relationship based on the owning group of an object. In other words, you can grant permission for a role to access the methods to view and manage objects in a specific access
group only when the objects belong to a specific owning group.
Using this approach, a role may grant users permission to update objects that belong to one owning group, but not allow the update of objects that belong to another owning group. The most typical use of this is to restrict access to
documents by specifying an owning group to represent a specific department or discipline.
The access granted by any access group can be restricted to a set of owning groups by identifying them on the access group relationship. This enables the same access group to be used in different roles on data identified by their owning
group. In this way, you can restrict access to methods based on the ownership.
1. Search for an access group, and select Actions 
 > Manage Methods.
2. Select the method that you want to restrict the access to, and select the owner or owning group filter in the Filter by Owner Or Owning Group column.
For more information on managing owning groups, see Configure owning groups.
At least one domain must be configured on the role/access group relationships; otherwise, the user cannot access any domain information.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Configure security rules
Security rules can be used to limit the objects that users can access. You restrict the data by creating a security rule that links one or more class definitions to one or more access groups with a condition. When used with owning groups,
the security rule further segregates user access to the different classes of objects.
Security rules on a class definition
Rules can be configured on a class definition. The SPFSecurityRule class definition, and the SPFClassDefSecurityRule and SPFSecurityRuleAccessGroup relationship definitions are used to configure the security rules.
When a security rule is configured on a class definition, the security rules that are applied during a relationship expansion are controlled by the SPFSecurityRuleExecution12 and SPFSecurityRuleExecution21 flags. These are property
definitions that are exposed on the ISPFRelDefExt interface definition. For example, <ISPFRelDefExt SPFSecurityRuleExecution12 = True>.
When the SPFSecurityRuleExecution12 is set to True, the security rules are applied to End2 when expanding from End1.
 If the SPFSecurityRuleExecution12 is set to True and no relationship exists, the software applies the security rules based on the instantiated interface definition. This uses the security rule associated with the first
class definition encountered that has a security rule.
When the SPFSecurityRuleExecution21 is set to True, the security rules are applied to End1 when expanding from End2.
If either the SPFSecurityRuleExecution12 or the SPFSecurityRuleExecution21 are set to False, then the security rules are not applied to the relationship expansion.
If an entry point on an interface definition, any security rules configured on class definitions that realize the same interface definition are applied. The rules are also applied on any graph definitions on a class definition that is realized
by an interface definition.
Security rules on the end object of a relationship definition
Security rules can also be configured directly on objects, but only when the security rules are accessed through a specific relationship or edge definition. This scenario for security rules is useful where the end interface of the relationship
definition is realized by multiple class definitions.
Relationships – direction 12 – Applies the configured security rule directly to the End2 object without evaluating the security rule applied to the End2 object’s class definition. For example, SPFRelDefSecurityRule12.
Relationships – direction 21 – Applies the configured security rule directly to the End1 object without evaluating the security rule applied to the End1 object’s class definition. For example, SPFRelDefSecurityRule21.
 When defining relationship definitions directly on the security rule and the SPFSecurityRuleExecution12 or SPFSecurityRuleExecution21 flags are set to False, the security rule is not evaluated on relationship expansions.
Edges – Applies the security rule to all the objects returned by the edge definition. For example, SPFEdgeDefSecurityRule.
Create a security rule
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx, HxGN SDx2 | Create a security rule <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/63f53552867220f8e1ee7946>
You can group multiple class definitions and access groups together into a security rule and restrict their access based on a condition.
 You need to have the System Administrator role to create security rules.
 Security rules can have a major impact on system performance. Do not create complex rules that lead to queries with multiple ORs or JOINs.
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Create Security Rule.
2. In the Create Security Rule page, type the name and description in the Main details section.
3. In the Security rule definition box, type the syntax to describe the condition that the object must meet for the methods related to the access group to appear. Example: obj-
>SPFCXmtlExternalCompany_12.Name=ENV.USERORGANIZATIONNAME.
The security rule syntax is the same as that used to create conditions, but security rules do not support keywords, such as ‘NOT’, 'OBJ1', 'OBJ2', or function keywords, such as ‘ISSET’, or ‘ISSETB’. For more information, see
Condition syntax.
The keyword 'INSTR' is supported so you can calculate a value on a property string (Obj.Name) to part of a substring (XYZ). For example, INSTR(Obj.Name, 'XYZ').
If a security rule expression contains interface definitions then a comparator value should be 0 and should not contain ‘>' or '=’. When ‘>' or '=’ is used and set to '0', this means the security rule expression can or cannot
contain the interface. For example, INSTR(obj.Interfaces, 'ISPFSubscribableItem') = 0.
If a security rule expression left value is different from the object expression then the comparator value should be ‘0’. For example, INSTR(Env.SecurityCodesForUserInQueryConfig, Obj.SecurityCodeName) > 0.
4. Find and select one or more class definitions from the Class definitions list to associate with the security rule.
5. Find and select one or more access groups from the Access groups list to associate with the security rule.
6. Find and select one or more edge definitions to expand and apply the security rule.
7. Find and select one or more relationship definitions to expand from End1 to End2 and apply the security rule.
8. Find and select one or more relationship definitions to expand from End2 to End1 and apply the security rule.
For example, the following security rule definition has a relationship definition expansion:
Obj->SDAItemSecurityCode_12.Name = Env.SecurityCodesForUserInQueryConfig Or obj.objdefuid = ‘SDAArea’
This security rule definition ensures that the results either match the security code or are in an SDAArea, which may not have a security code.
View and update security rules
To view
Select Security Rules in the System Administration feature set.
To update
1. Search for your security rule in the System Administration feature set.
2. Select Actions 
> Update.
Configure security codes
Security codes are used to restrict user access to documents and data using configured security rules. Three security codes are delivered - unclassified, restricted, and proprietary. You can change these codes, or create new codes, if
needed.
Create a security code
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/63ebad2aca38a591ec8c23b4> , HxGN SDx2
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/634e97702d473d4781f29ca0> | Create a security code <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/63ebad2aca38a591ec8c23b4>
You must be a user with the System Administrator or Business Administrator role to create security codes.
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Create Security Code.
2. On the New Security Code page, provide the required information.
A security code is always created under Configuration Top, independent of the currently selected scope.
Once security codes have been created, they need to be assigned to a user. For more information, see Assign security codes to a user.
View and update security codes
To view
Select Security Codes in the System Administration feature set.
To update
1. Search for your security code in the System Administration feature set.
2. Select Actions 
 > Update.
Prepare the projects
You can use the Business Administration feature set to set up projects, including contracts, organizations, template documents, and more. To set up a project:
Create a project
Manage the project configuration details
Define work areas
Register your design tools
Create a project
1. Select the Business Administration feature set and select Create Project.
2. In the Name box, type a name for the project or the lower-level configuration.
3. In the Status box, select a Status (Active or Created).
4. For a new sub-project, select the project beneath which you want to create the sub-project.
5. Either:
Select Manage Config Details to create the project, and select the required classifications, disciplines, and issue purposes for the project. For more information, see Manage the project configuration details.
Select Finish to create the project without selecting further configurations.
Do not use special characters at all in any part of a project name. These include .<,>^=+:?|/';{}[]~`!%*()&$@#.
A warning message that the characters are invalid is displayed if you use any special characters.
Manage the project configuration details
Document classifications, tag classifications, disciplines, and issue purposes are initially created for the entire tenant. This means that you can have the same values across all plants and projects allowing you to search for specific
classifications across the entire tenant. For each project, you can then select the specific values required.
1. Search for the project, and select Actions 
 > Manage Project Details.
2. Select the required document and tag classifications.
 By default, all of the classifications created for the plant are available for the project. Selecting classifications means that only the selected items are available for the project.
3. Select the required disciplines and issue purposes for the project.
4. Select Update to save the changes.
Complete a project
As a Business Administrator, you can set the status of an executed project to Completed. To update the project status to Completed, search for the project, and select Actions 
 > Set Project Status to Completed.
 Once the project status is set to Completed, you can view the project data but are not allowed to not make any changes to it. However, if you have logged in using a System Administrator role or a custom role with admin
privileges, you are allowed to edit the project and its data.
 You can revert the status of a completed project by selecting Actions 
 > Set Project Status to Active.
Cancel a project
As a Business Administrator, you can set the status of a project to Cancelled. To update the project status to Cancelled, search for the project, and select Actions 
 > Set Project Status to Cancelled.
 Once the project status is set to Cancelled, you can view the project data but are not allowed to not make any changes to it.
Configure a document review matrix
Document review matrices are used to set up lists of recipients based on properties or relationships of the object in the workflow. Each recipient on the matrix has a Reason For Receipt (RFR), which indicates why the recipient is included.
A document review matrix is a table with recipients and matrix categories. The matrix categories can be based on any property or relationship on the object going down the workflow, such as area, document type, issue purpose, discipline,
and so on. Each cell in the table is marked with the reason for receipt for each recipient.
Configure a document matrix
1. Model the matrix class.
Ensure the matrix class realizes an interface that implies ISPFDistributionMatrix.
2. Model the row class.
3. Define a relationship between the two primary interfaces of the matrix and matrix row classes as a specialization of SPFDistributionMatrixRow.
4. Model the row mapping.
a. Create an interface for the mapping properties. Make sure this interface implies the ISPFDistributionMapRow interface and is realized by the matrix row class.
b. Create the mapping properties - these will appear as columns in the matrix table. In the default delivered matrix, Area and Unit are relationship-related properties which should be configured manually in the column set using the
SPFColumnItemDependentItem relationship definition. The other columns like Category, Type, and Sub Type are enumerated list properties which are handled automatically by the system.
c. Map these properties to the properties and/or relationships on the object that will be going down the workflow.
d. Configure the column set with column items pointing to matrix properties and relate this column set to the SPFClassDefDisplayColumnSet class definition. When you create or copy a matrix, this column set is related to the
instance of the matrix using the SPFDistributionMatrixColumnset relationship definition.
If the column set is not related, when the user creates a matrix instance, the column sets are generated dynamically and related to the matrix instance.
Multiple cardinality relationships can be used to map a workflow item to matrix rows. If the object in the workflow is related to two or more items by a row mapping relationship, then all the related rows will be selected when the matrix
is evaluated, and users on each selected matrix row will be included as step recipients.
The column items do not support the following data types: UoM, DateTime, YMD, and Double.
If one or more matrices are related to a workflow step, when it starts, the system tests the step matrix condition against the object in the workflow to determine which one to use. If one matches, the mapping properties for the matrix row are
determined as above. The values for the matrix look up are then retrieved from the object by traversing from the matrix mapping properties to the related object properties and/or relationship. These values are used to identify the matrix row
to use.
Matrix management
Matrices are created like any other object.
Create a new matrix
For information on how to create a matrix, see Create a document review matrix.
Assign matrix recipients
The Assign Recipients form is used to specify the users or roles included in the selected distribution matrix, as well as the action required from each user or role. For more information, see Populate the document review matrix.
The following table lists the reason for receipt codes to be used in the matrix based on the action required from each recipient.
Reason for receipt
Code
Recipient
Reviewer
R
Reviews the document or drawing.
Consolidator
CON
Reviews and consolidate the reviews.
Information only reviewer
I
Is informed about the review process but no action is required.
You can allow submitted documents to bypass the workflow by turning off the toggle review mode in the matrices that were created based on the ISDXDocumentReviewMatrix interface definition.
The reason for receipts can be configured using the SDXDocumentReviewMatrix class definition and SPFDistributionMatrixRestrictedReasonForReceipt relationship definition.
By default, the add recipients search dialog box uses the entry point SAEP_InternalLoginUsersEntryPoint as the default filter to populate the values. Using the filters available, you can further refine the search results. For more
information on how to configure these filters, refer Parameter 3 and Parameter 4 in AssignRecipients client API.
To associate the matrix to a contract
For a new contract, select the matrix to generate reviews in the Matrix to generate reviews box in the New Contract Work Package page.
For an existing contract, update the contract by selecting the matrix to generate reviews in the Matrix to generate reviews box, in the Update Contract Work Package page.
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
Configure roles and users for HxGN SDx2
The HxGN SDx2 schema and configuration files deliver roles that allow you to configure internal organizations and external contractors, as well as users, to participate in project communications activities. You can configure your company
or project specific roles.
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
For more information on configuring roles and users, see Manage user role assignments.
For more information about configuring security, see Configure security.
Define work areas
 You must create a work area and work package in your HxGN SDx2 project before you can register a design tool for integration.
Work areas are classified objects that are used to filter the information that is continuously synchronized between HxGN SDx2 and other Hexagon products like Smart 3D, Smart Electrical, and Smart P&ID. Because they are classified
objects, a tree is used to separate and distinguish between the different types of filters.
Internal Work Area
Used by internal users (users associated with an internal organization, like an Owner-Operator) to define what is synchronized with the 3D model. These filters are used internally to make changes to the project.
External Work Area
Used to limit what can be synchronized by external users (users associated with an external organization, like an EPC) to populate their 3D models. If this filter is used, the tags returned by list or graphical queries are items that are
referenced by the work area.
 A work area can be related to only one organization. More than one work area can be related to the same organization.
Create a named space
To filter the data synchronized between HxGN SDx2 and Smart 3D according to the named space that data is associated with, create the named spaces in HxGN SDx2 and use them in a Smart 3D work area. For more information about
creating filters, see Create a work area.
1. Select Design Tool Integration > Create 3D Named Space.
2. Type a name and description for the named space.
3. In the Volume Criteria box, provide the coordinates that define the named space: E1, E2, N1, N2, EL1, EL2.
Create a work area
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | Work Area and the PBS Hierarchy <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/660c518a3215142e6728a470>
You must have a work area and related work package in your HxGN SDx2 project before you can register a design tool for integration.
This feature is available only to a user with a Business Administrator role.
After you have created your plant and project in HxGN SDx2, set your Create scope to the project to create a work area to use when registering your design tool.
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Create Work Area.
2. From the classification tree, select the type of work area to create. For example, select Brownfield > Internal for any existing projects within the organization.
3. Navigate to the next page, and in the MAIN DETAILS tab, provide a name and description for the new work area.
 Although you are not required to associate a work package with your new work area, you can relate one or more if they already exist. Provide search criteria or use a wildcard (*), and select 
 next to the Work Package box
to see a list of existing work packages that you can choose to relate to the work area.
4. Select the organization, work package, and design component that you want to associate with this work area.
 In a greenfield work area, you can limit instruction generation for the Integration Engine by selecting Use for Handover to mark the work area. For more information, see Manage instruction generation using
SDxiHubGenerateInstructionsSchedulerTask.
5. In the ADVANCED INFORMATION tab, define the volume criteria by entering the information needed.
6. Select FINISH.
To update the description or organization in a work area, select Actions > Update for the relevant object.
To terminate a work area, select Actions > Terminate for the relevant object.
Create a view work area
A view work area allows you to view specific work areas.
 This feature is available only to a user with a Business Administrator role.
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Create View Work Area.
2. In the MAIN DETAILS tab, provide a name and description for the new view work area.
3. In the ADVANCED INFORMATION tab, define the volume criteria by entering the information needed.
To update the description or volume criteria information in a view work area, select Actions 
 > Update for the relevant object.
To terminate a view work area, select Actions 
 > Terminate for the relevant object.
Create a work package from work area
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | Create a Work Area and Work Package <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/66314b0c26bbbd968b4933b1>
You must have a work area and related work package in your HxGN SDx2 project before you can register a design tool for integration.
This feature is available to a user with a Business Administrator role.
On creation, the system relates the work package to the selected work area.
The work package is created with the Status set to Open. The Status is updated to Active when the workflow completes.
To create a work package:
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Work Areas.
2. For any selected work area, select Actions 
 > Create Work Package.
3. In the Create Work Package dialog, provide the required information.
4. Select FINISH.
To update a work package, select Actions 
 Update for the relevant object.
You can also create a work package from the Work Packages feature set. For more information, see Create an engineering work package.
Register your design tools
Each integrated design tool can have slight differences in the way it registers with HxGN SDx2. You can find more detailed information in the Help documentation dedicated to each integrated design tool.
To register a design tool:
1. Create a work area.
2. Create a work package from work area
3. Register your integrated design tools
For general information about registering design tools, see:
Registering projects
Register from a design tool
Learn more about registering
Register your integrated design tools
The following sections have shortened step-by-step instructions for registering integrated design tools and links to Help for each design tool.
 You must create a work area and work package in your HxGN SDx2 project before you can register a design tool for integration.
Preparing your generic user to register authoring tools
Your tenant arrives with a generic user to be used to register projects, GenericM2M. This user is automatically loaded a relationship to a default organization. However, before you can register with that user, you must either assign it to
another organization with the appropriate roles, or you must assign the appropriate designations to the default organization using the following steps.
1. In the Business Administration feature set, select Organizations, and find the Hexagonali organization.
2. Select Actions 
 > Update.
3. For the Organization role details, select the types of organizations that are able to create work areas within the project you are registering, such as Internal, External, Contractor, and so forth.
4. In the System Administration feature set, select Clear Cache.
If the organization associated with this generic user has been designated as a specific type, you can then register only with work areas that are of the same designation. For example, if you designate this organization as internal,
attempts to register with an authoring tool only allow the selection of an internal work area, and no external work areas are available for registering to.
You can select more than one option on the Update dialog box. So, you can register with more than one type of work area.
Intergraph Smart 3D
You can find complete documentation at Smart 3D Help <https://docs.hexagonali.com/p/S3DV> .
The following considerations are required to take advantage of Smart 3D integration with HxGN SDx2:
1. Register with HxGN SDx2 so that you can share data from your Smart 3D model with HxGN SDx2.
2. Install the Intergraph Smart 3D Integration Service.
3. Install SmartPlant.NET.
4. Install SmartPlant.NETCore.
5. Start the Integration Service.
6. Configure Integration Service settings.
Although you can start the service at any time after installation, the service does not share data until integration settings are configured. This allows you to configure settings for one or more models in the same or different site
databases.
During the synchronize to HxGN SDx2 process, you can use the commands available in Smart 3D Project Management to monitor the status of the synchronization operation for completeness and for errors and resynchronize
objects to HxGN SDx2 if needed.
Intergraph Smart Electrical
You can find complete documentation at Smart Electrical Help <https://docs.hexagonali.com/p/SEL> .
1. Ensure the Intergraph Smart Electrical Share Service is installed.
2. Install Intergraph SmartPlant.NET.
3. Install the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler.
4. Configure the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler.
5. Configure the Server tab.
6. Start the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler Service.
7. Register Smart Electrical with HxGN SDx2.
Prerequisites for registering with HxGN SDx2.
8. Share data and documents with HxGN SDx2.
Document Types for Sharing with HxGN SDx2.
9. Share documents from Intergraph Smart Electrical to HxGN SDx2.
Find Documents to Publish/Share from Intergraph Smart Electrical.
Intergraph Smart Instrumentation
You can find complete documentation at Smart Instrumentation Help <https://docs.hexagonali.com/p/SI> .
1. Ensure the Smart Instrumentation Share Service is installed.
2. Review the Smart Instrumentation Share Service and Intergraph Schematic Scheduler Service.
3. Install the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler.
4. Install Intergraph SmartPlant.NET.
5. Configure the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler.
6. Start the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler Service.
7. Register Smart Instrumentation with HxGN SDx2.
Intergraph Smart P&ID
You can find complete documentation at Smart P&ID Help <https://docs.hexagonali.com/p/SPID> .
1. Review the Smart P&ID Shared Service and Intergraph Schematic Scheduler Service.
2. Ensure Smart P&ID Share Service is installed.
3. Install the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler.
4. Install Intergraph SmartPlant.NET.
5. Configure the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler.
6. Configure the Server tab.
7. Start the Intergraph Schematic Scheduler Service.
8. Register Smart P&ID with HxGN SDx2.
9. Share data and documents with HxGN SDx2.
Integrating Third-Party Tools with HxGN SDx2
You can integrate third-party tools; however, the process is involved and can require knowledgeable web developers or aid from Hexagon Services.
For more information about integrating a third-party tool, see Integrating Third-Party Tools with HxGN SDx2.
Registering projects
Before you can share data between design tools and organizations using HxGN SDx2, you must register the plant in the design tool with a project in HxGN SDx2. The relationship you create during this registration includes a tool signature
or design component, which tracks where the data is coming from when shared. Each design tool can register to HxGN SDx2 and receive a unique tool signature. A new set of tables is created in the database as part of registration for the
design component that the tool supports. This allows HxGN SDx2 to segregate data from different sources.
You can register projects only when they are in the active state.
Each design tool can be registered to an HxGN SDx2 project only once.
Tables created during registration depend on the design component property. If the design component property is populated, design component tables are created with the design component property value as the table name prefix.
Your tenant arrives with a generic user automatically loaded with association to your organization and the necessary privileges to be used to register projects.
What tables are created during registration?
A design tool is related to a design component using the DesignToolComponents relationship.
If the design component related to the design tool has the DesignComponentDomain property instantiated, tables for the tool's data are created in the HxGN SDx2 database during tool registration. The prefix of the
DesignComponentDomain property value is used to name the tables.
If the DesignComponentDomain property is blank or not instantiated, the tables are named with the prefix of the tool signature value. See the following examples.
Name of the tool
Value of the DesignComponentDomain property
Value of the tool signature
Tables created
Smart 3D
S3D
'AAAC'
S3DObj
S3DObjIF
S3DOBJPR
S3DOBJREI
S3DREL
For more information about creating, viewing, or reusing registrations, see the following topics:
Register from a design tool
Learn more about registering
Register from a design tool
Before you can begin sharing data with HxGN SDx2, you must first authenticate and then register your design tool plant with a project in HxGN SDx2.
The authentication of any tool is completed using one of the authentication servers. Every request (share, register, and so on) is authenticated before it is completed.
During the registration process, you choose what work area to use when sharing data from this design tool. Work areas must be created in HxGN SDx2 before you can register the design tool. For more information, see Create a work
area.
Okta authentication server with IWA (Integrated Windows Authentication) is not supported.
To register a new design tool, your administrator must create a new client in the Authentication Server and enter all the details like Client ID, Client Secret, and so on.
You may register a tool to a work area only once.
For more information about why you must register your design tool plant with a project in HxGN SDx2, see Registering plants.
For more information about the information that appears in the Registration window, see Learn more about registering.
Create a new registration
 A user with client credentials and client name with the appropriate roles for registering a plant must be available in HxGN SDx2 for you to register the plant.
1. From the design tool, select Design Tool Integration > Register with SDx.
2. In the Authentication dialog box, type the SDx Server URL, and provide values for the Client ID and Client Secret in the Authorization Code with Proof Key for Code Exchange section.
 The Client ID and Client Secret fields in the Client Credentials section are unavailable. The values for these fields are retrieved by the system but not displayed on this dialog box.
Tell me more about the Authentication dialog box
You can get the details to enter in the Authentication dialog box from your administrator.
To know more about registration, see Registering plants.
 The default URI used for the PKCE client for any of the authentication servers must be http://localhost:62810/.
1. In the SDx Server URL, enter the URL of the HxGN SDx2 site you want to register with.
2. In the Authentication code with Proof Key for Code Exchange section, enter the Client ID and the Client Secret in the respective boxes.
The Client ID identifies the Smart Client.
The Client Secret authenticates the Smart Client and allows access to Smart API.
The value in Authentication Code with Proof Key for Code Exchange redirects you to the applicable authentication server logon page.
When you are using Okta with PKCE for authentication, the Client Secret box is optional.
You can file a support case for assistance with configuring client credentials and setting a new secret so that your HxGN SDx2 tenants stay in line with your organization's policy.
The value in Client Credentials authenticates the client and redirects you to the register page. These fields are unavailable as the system finds the values automatically.
3. In the Login page, provide the requested information.
 In the Register dialog box, the Tool ID box automatically populates with the tool you are registering.
4. Add further information about the tool you are creating this registration for in the Tool Instance Description box.
 A new registration relationship and unique tool signature are created for each unique tool description provided, allowing you to register the same design tool to one project multiple times for different organizations or disciplines.
5. Provide the following information about the HxGN SDx2 environment you want to register with.
SDx Sever URL
Provide the URL to the HxGN SDx2 instance you want to register with.
SDx Plant
Displays the name of the plant that contains the project you want to share data with. This field is populated by the
software when you select a work area.
SDx Project
Displays the name of the project where you want to send shared data. This field is populated by the software when
you select a work area.
Select a Work Area
Choose the work area that includes the data you want to share with the project.
 Users associated with an internal organization can choose one of the defined work areas. Users associated
with an external organization must choose an external filter associated with their organization. For more
information, see Create a work area.
SDx Web Client URL
Provide the URL of the HxGN SDx2 instance.
6. Select OK.
Modify the details of an existing registration
 If you want to make any changes to the registration details, you can register a tool to HxGN SDx2 again.
1. In the Authentication dialog box:
If you are using a host workstation, you can edit the SDx Server URL.
 You cannot edit the boxes in the Register dialog box.
2. Select Ok in the Register dialog box to modify the registration details.
Learn more about registering
Using the Register Tool window, you can do the following:
Create a new registration relationship to a project in HxGN SDx2.
View information about existing registration relationships that exist for the same project and same design tool, if you have the appropriate security role, such as Business Administrator.
 Authorization server uses the AuthorizationCodewithProofKey (PKCE) method to authorize each request.
How do I extend my system?
You can extend your system by changing the data model to provide the type of data you need. You can also extend the user interface to create, find, and view the type of objects and data you need. The changes are options that must be
made within the defined guidelines described in the following sections.
Configuration rules
Several governance rules must be followed when configuring HxGN SDx2. These are in place to ensure upgrades run successfully, new features can be easily implemented, and performance remains optimum. While some objects such as
workflow master templates are protected during the upgrade process, most other objects are not. As a result, any configuration changes made to delivered objects may be lost.
 Configuring items to contain numerous relationships can have a negative impact on system health and performance. Few examples of where many relationships can impact performance are:
Security rules
Column sets for entry points
Column sets for the To Do List
Display items on forms
Before deploying configuration changes to a production system they should always be tested with your typical data sets not only for accuracy but also for performance impact.
To help ensure successful upgrades, take advantage of performance upgrades, and make it easier for Support to assist you with issues, keep the following rules and suggestions in mind when configuring your system.
General objects
Avoid deleting or terminating any objects provided with the software, including column items, display items, methods, widgets, feature sets, entry points, and so forth. In most cases, you can hide these objects from users by editing
relationships or access control.
You can disable existing entries in enumerated lists but do not delete or terminate them.
Do not delete or terminate any delivered properties, interfaces, or relationship definitions. In most cases, you can limit exposure to these items by configuring the user interface.
Do not remove display items from form sections or column items from column sets. Instead, create and implement new forms or column sets without the items you don't want to include.
Roles, access groups, and security configuration rules
The following rules must be followed when changing roles, access groups, and security rules.
Roles
Existing roles can be extended with custom access groups. However, you must not remove the relationship between the existing access groups and existing roles. An exception is that you cannot modify or delete the access groups
related to the following relationship definitions SDxModuleRestrictedRole, SPFManagesRoles, SPFRoleRoleCategory.
New roles can be created with any mix of existing or new access groups.
Access Groups
Do not remove the delivered access groups.
Delivered access groups can be related to any delivered items such as properties, methods, interfaces, and relationship definitions along with custom relationship definitions.
Existing access groups can be removed or replaced on delivered methods, entry points, and relationships.
Do not remove existing access groups from delivered roles.
Domains and Domain Groups
Delivered domain groups cannot be modified or deleted. However, there is an exception where custom domains can be added to the delivered and custom domain groups.
Security Rules
Security rules provide conditional access to data items by access group and can be freely edited.
 Security rules can have a major impact on system performance and the delivered security rules have been configured to improve system performance by moving some relationships to object properties. Any customized rules
will not benefit from these improvements so don't create complex rules that lead to queries with a large number of ORs or JOINs.
User interface configuration rules
The following rules must be followed when changing the user interface.
Feature Sets
Delivered feature sets, including their existing methods and API entry points, must not be changed. You must also not change the display text, content order, groupings, or icon.
Feature sets can be reordered on the home page, and you can add custom API entry points.
Custom feature sets can be created and populated with methods and API entry points, as well as integrated with third-party applications using Web extensibility.
Methods and API entry points must not be disconnected from feature sets. These can only be removed from the feature set by removing user access to the command.
Delivered and custom methods can be added to a custom feature set.
The method conditions cannot be modified as the condition may be used in many places. Instead, it should be copied and replaced as needed.
You can modify the methods associated with the SPFStepClassMethod, SPFClassDefDefaultMethod, and SPFInterfaceDefMethod relationship definition.
You cannot modify the methods associated with the SPFClientAPIMethods relationship definition.
Custom commands and API entry points can be added to a feature set.
Delivered reports can be added to a feature set.
You can terminate relationships between feature sets and reports.
API Entry Points
API entry points filtering must not be changed.
Custom API entry points can be created, updated, and have indexing enabled.
Existing API entry points cannot be updated, deleted, or terminated, but can have indexing enabled.
All document or tag API entry points have indexing enabled by default, which cannot be cancelled.
Existing API entry points can be added to groupings and custom feature sets.
Column sets can be added to entry points by terminating the existing relationship and creating a new one.
API Entry Point Groups
Delivered entry point groups cannot be deleted.
Delivered relationships cannot be deleted.
Relationships can be created to a new SchemaItem entry point group.
Forms
Sections can be added to, or removed from any form tab.
Sections can be reordered in a form.
Custom access groups can be added to the delivered sections.
Custom forms can be created. They can be used to replace delivered forms by terminating the existing relationship between the delivered form and the class definition, and creating a new relationship for the new form.
Existing display items can be added to new sections.
Default values on relationship display items can be altered on specific display items. For example, to set the default security code.
Existing form purpose relationships cannot be modified.
Existing form purposes cannot be modified for the existing sections.
Column Sets
Existing column sets must not be altered, including the content and order of the columns, because it could change the behavior of the system. Column sets must be copied and modified in order to be managed.
Many column sets contain items that are not exposed in the list views but instead dynamically build display items on tabs on the forms, so care is required.
Dynamic display items from column items are an efficient way to extend forms.
Do not delete or terminate any existing column set or column items.
Do not terminate the following relationship definitions related to the column sets: SPFColumnSetColumnItemConfig, SPFColumnSetObjClass, SPFColumnSetColumnItem.
You may relate column sets to the entry points,
You may add or remove the column sets from the following relationship definitions: SPFPinnedUser, SPFDisplayItemFilterColumnSet, SPFRelDefEnd1ColumnSet and SPFRelDefEnd2ColumnSet.
Delivered column items can be related to new column sets.
Display Items
You may copy the display items related to the following relationship definitions: SPFDisplayItemDependentItem, SPFDisplayItemSecondaryDependentItem and SPFDisplayItemFilterColumnset.
Workflow process remodeling rules
Two versions of each workflow template are delivered.
A master workflow template with the prefix MSR that cannot be used in the system and is supplied only for reference.
An editable copy of the workflow template with the prefix HEX that customers can use and edit in their system.
The following rules must be followed when changing the HEX version of the template:
Do not modify any existing step definitions, step classes, step statuses, or their related conditions.
You can copy the step definition, its step class, and related condition.
You can add, remove, and reorder steps in the workflow template.
You can provide your own prefix to the workflow template.
You cannot modify any existing notification definition.
Notification definitions can be copied.
 Workflows are configured with a set of steps that represent your business process. Certain steps in the process are critical to the operation of that work process. These critical steps are indicated in the work process and
should only be altered within the scope described at each step.
 For details on how to configure workflow steps, see Manage workflow templates.
Data model configuration rules
The Common Schema must be allowed to evolve because the scope of what the schema defines will change over time. However, objects and the properties and relationships between them have been modeled to support HxGN SDx2
work processes and to meet industry-standard data exchange.
 Unless explicitly stated otherwise, all rules defined here apply to both the meta-schema and the Common Schema.
For more information about how schema evolution rules allow for forward and backward compatibility with authoring tools, see Common Schema evolution rules.
Use these links to find more information about rules for creating and modifying specific schema objects:
Rule enforcement
Every schema object is stamped with the official revision and version for which it was created. Objects that predate the latest released version must adhere to the rules defined here. These rules are supported by the delivery and setup
procedures.
Creation rules
Keep the following rules in mind when making changes that affect object or relationship creation.
You may create new objects of any type, but the relationships between new objects and existing objects must not violate a revision rule.
You may create new relationships between existing objects, but the new relationship must not violate a revision rule.
Deletion rule
An object (including the relationships of the object) that supports ISchemaObj cannot be deleted if its revision or version predates the current software revision or version.
Revision rule
If a schema object type is not included in this section, any type of revision to that object is allowed.
Common Schema evolution rules
The schema evolution rules support four software schema configurations. In the following four examples, Tool 1 and Tool 2 represent applications written to integrate with the Common Schema. The schema version number has two parts
(X.X), where the first number is the schema as it was released by Hexagon. The second part is optional and indicates support for customized changes made by the user.
The schema version for a tool indicates the version of the Common Schema for which the tool adapter was written. Including the asterisk (*) indicates that the metadata used by that tool has been updated to the latest set of user-specified
customizations and delivered by the Common Schema. Because all four configuration examples include the asterisk, you can conclude that the metadata used by the adapter has been updated to work with the latest Common Schema.
Configuration 1 is the simplest case. All tools and the Common Schema are written for the same version of the schema, and there should be no issues.
Configuration 1: All tools (adapters) and the Common Schema on the same version
Configuration 2 shows that a new version of the Common Schema was loaded. However, each tool is running with its adapter written to work for the previous version. In this configuration, the new Common Schema cannot include any
changes that would prevent the old tool adapters from working with the new Common Schema.
Configuration 2: Tools are on an older version of the schema than the Common Schema
Configuration 3 shows the new versions of Tool 1 and Tool2 were loaded. These tools have adapters that were written for a version of the Common Schema that is newer than what is currently loaded. The tool adapters must support
backward compatibility, and they have less functionality because of the older, loaded version of the Common Schema.
Configuration 3: Tools are on a newer version of the schema than the Common Schema
Configuration 4 is the worst-case scenario. Some of the tool adapters are working with a schema version that is older than the loaded schema, and some of the tools are working with a newer schema version than the loaded schema.
Configuration 4: Tools are on both older and newer versions of the schema than the Common Schema
Schema modification rules help you avoid breaking existing tool adapter software (as in configuration 2 and in part of configuration 4). Schema evolution rules also allow newer versions of tool adapters to work with older versions of the
SmartPlant schema (as in configuration 3 and the other part of configuration 4).
When you decide to modify - or extend - the schema, consider the impact any modifications could have.
If you make a conforming change (for example, you add a new, optional property definition), what happens?
If you make a non-conforming change (for example, you add a mandatory interface definition), what happens?
How do you resynchronize your extended schema with a new schema from Hexagon?
Do schema files and data files have to be forward-compatible and backward-compatible in order to be useful?
Changes that might break existing publish or retrieval logic are not allowed, because those changes would break configurations 2 and 4. However, you can create explicit rules for the adapter to follow to avoid these problems.
Tool adapters must be written to work with old versions of the Common Schema. Additions made to the Common Schema after initial release must not be hard-coded into a tool adapter unless the coding allows the adapter to continue
working with older schema versions that do not have the additions (for example, using If statements). Likewise, any changes made to the map file after initial release must be delivered as a differential set of instructions rather than as a
new map file. The version of the Common Schema then drives the loading of these differential instructions.
Class definitions
For existing class definitions, the following rules apply to both the schema and the meta-schema.
Don't alter existing object definitions, including UID and Name property, because it could change the behavior of the system.
 You may modify the Unique Key and Unique ID.
Don't change the Description and Display Name of a class definition.
You may change the relationships of a custom class definition.
You may create or remove custom relationships for both delivered and custom class definitions.
Don't modify the Reason for Receipt class definition, as it is used for new workflow steps.
Don't change the component schema associated with the class definition.
Don't change the model definition associated with the class definition.
Don't change the class factory definition associated with the class definition.
You may extend an existing (delivered and custom) object class definitions by realizing new custom interfaces for property or relationship extensions, or additional optional existing interfaces. This includes new optional interfaces on
an object, such as an organization. However, there are few exceptions such as:
Don't create Required relationship for both custom and delivered class definitions.
Class definitions delivered with the HxGN SDx2 may not realize the ISPFClaimableItem and ISPFClaimedItem interface definitions.
Both delivered and custom class definitions may realize delivered and custom interface definitions.
 Don't create required realize relationship for both custom and delivered class definitions.
Don't change an optional interface definition to be required.
Don't change a required interface definition to be optional.
New object definitions require a custom primary interface.
New object classes may realize any existing core interfaces, as long as functionality is not impacted.
You may create new sharing relationships between shared class definitions and shared object definitions.
Don't delete existing sharing relationships between shared class definitions and shared object definitions.
Interface definitions
Although the COM rules state that interfaces are immutable, the rules for existing interface definitions in the Common Schema are not so rigid.
Don't change the UID property.
Don't change the Name property.
Don't change the IsRequired property for delivered objects.
You may change the Description, SpecializationValue, InterfaceIcon, and PropertyCategory properties of an interface definition.
You may remove the relationship definition for the interface definition SPFInterfaceDefMethod.
A Custom interface definition can be realized by one or more class definitions. However, it may not be added as a Required Rel to class definition.
A Custom interface definition may have Exposes relationships with class definitions or Implies relationships with other interfaces.
You may add optional property definitions to an interface definition.
You may relate a method or a column set to an interface definition.
Don't delete existing property definitions exposed by delivered interface definitions.
Don't change required property definitions to optional definitions.
Don't change optional property definitions to required definitions.
Don't delete or terminate delivered Exposes or Implies relationships. Other relationships may be terminated.
You may add new optional Implies or Exposes relationships.
Don't change required Implies relationships to optional Implies relationships.
Don't change optional Implies relationships to required Implies relationships.
An example of an interface definition and its properties
Property definitions
You can create new custom properties and expose them on a new custom interface realized on the object class.
 We recommend to limit the number of property definitions to 250 on an interface definition.
Existing property definitions must adhere to these rules.
Don't change the UID property.
Don't change the Name property.
You may change the Description and DisplayName properties of a property definition.
Although they are not currently used, you may change the TypeData and Default properties of a property definition.
Don't change a required ScopedBy relationship to an optional one.
Don't change an optional ScopedBy relationship to a required one.
An example of a property definition and its relationships
Enumerated lists
Existing enumerated lists must adhere to these rules.
Don't change the UID property.
Don't change the IsOpenList property.
You may change the Name property, because it is not tied to the UID.
You may change the Description property.
You may change the IsTypeList property (not currently used).
You may change the IsOrdered property; however, this rule is likely to be restricted later.
You may give the EnumNumber property if one has not already been defined.
Don't change the value assigned to the EnumNumber property if it was previously defined.
You may add existing Contains relationships.
Don't delete existing BaseEnumListForEnumLevel relationships.
You may create new BaseEnumListForEnumLevel relationships.
An example of an enumerated list and its properties
Enumerated entries
The following rules apply when you edit enumerated values in enumerated lists:
Do not change core picklists as they are critical for the system to function. This includes Revision States (CURRENT, WORKING, SUPERSEDED, and so on) and Transmittal Issue States (INITIATED, REJECTED, ISSUED, and
so on).
You may add to engineering, or data-related, picklists (for example, Fluid codes and Nominal diameter) as required. You can disable existing entries but do not delete or terminate them.
Don't change the UID property for an existing enumerated entry.
