You may change the Name property (that is, the short description) of an existing enumerated entry.
You may change the Description property of an existing enumerated entry.
You may provide a value for an undefined EnumNumber property.
You may update EnumEnumber properties that are associated with the classified items.
Don't change the value for an EnumNumber property if it was previously defined.
An example of an enumerated entry and its properties
ENS definitions
Existing ENS definitions must adhere to these revision rules.
Don't update the existing (custom and delivered) ENS definitions.
You may copy the ENS definitions and their relationships.
Don't update the existing (custom and delivered) ENS item definitions.
You may copy the ENS item definitions and their relationships.
Unit of measure lists
Existing unit of measure (UoM) lists must adhere to these rules.
Don't change the UID property.
You may change the Name property, because it is not tied to the UID.
You may change the Description property.
You may change the DimUnits property.
You may change the UoMListType property.
You may give an undefined EnumNumber property a value. Once the value is assigned, it may not be updated or deleted.
Don't change the EnumNumber property if it was previously defined.
Don't delete existing contains relationships.
You may create new contains relationships.
Don't delete UoMConditions relationships.
Don't change default SI unit of measure for the UoM list.
Example of a unit of measure list and its properties
Unit of measure
Don't change the UID property of an existing unit of measure.
You may change the Name property (that is, the short description) can be changed.
You may change the Description property.
You may change the A (ACny) and B (BCny) conversion factors. However, you should only do so if there is an error in the value or if greater precision is required.
Relationship and edge definitions
Existing relationship definitions must adhere to these revision rules.
When adding new or changing existing edge or relationship definitions, consider the following:
While you may add new relationships between existing objects, it should only be done rarely and with caution. Adding a new relationship could change an existing object's use in the system.
As an example, you may add a new relationship to an organization.
Don’t define mandatory cardinalities to existing interfaces. Issues can occur if objects of those instances already exist.
Don't alter existing relationships, including cardinality, because it could change the behavior of the system.
 Don't alter the SPFFileTypeFileType relationships controlling the alternate rendition unless following specific documented instructions.
Only change existing relationships and edges to:
Hide or expose them in the Web Client and in the Info Map.
Configure the security rule used on expansion to match any object security rule changes (relationships only). This may impact performance.
Additionally, existing relationship definitions must adhere to these revision rules.
Don't change the UID property.
Don't change the Name property.
Don’t change the names of the Role on either end of the relationship definition.
Don’t change the localities of reference.
Don’t change the minimum or maximum cardinalities.
You may add custom relationship definitions, for example, SPFDisplayItemRelDef,RelfDefColumnSet.
You may add custom domains to the SPFDomainDependencies tree.
You may add custom classdefinitions to the existing component schema which creates componentization relationships between them.
Don't delete Componentization relationship between the classdefinition and component schema.
Don't change the interface definition at either end of a relationship.
You can define an undefined SpecializationOf property.
Don't change a defined SpecializationOf property.
An example of a relationship definition
Configure the schema
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | What is Schema? <https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/65e777c375096d28809e3780>
A schema comprises a number of components that work together to define your data, including:
Class definition - A named description of a set of objects that play similar roles and share some of the same properties and relationships.
For example, an object that is an instance of an organization may play one or more of the following roles: internal organization, external organization, supplier, manufacturer, or contractor.
Interface definition - A collection of properties available to an object that, as a set, represent the role an object plays. Interface definitions also represent the ends of relationship definitions.
Each role is represented by a different interface definition that exposes properties and supports relationships specific to that role.
Relationship definition - A definition of how objects can be related to each other.
For example, a relationship definition describes the relationship that can exist between an organization and a contract. The relationship definition can include information such as how many organizations each contract can be related
to and what happens to an organization when a contract is deleted.
Property definition - Attributes that describe objects. Properties are related to a class definition through an interface definition.
For more information about rule and restrictions for creating and editing the data model, see Data model configuration rules.
Common Schema configuration
 eLearning is available for this topic. Check it out here: HxGN SDx2 | Understanding schema: Class definitions, interface definitions, and property definitions
<https://universityhexagonali.360learning.com/course/play/660dad48e2dc3543e199ad84>
The objects in your system, such as the class definitions and interface definitions, have been modeled in the Common Schema to support your work processes as well as to meet industry-standards for data exchange.
You can search for and review the following objects in your system:
Class Definitions
Interface Definition
Property Definitions
Relationship Definitions
Enumerated List Types
Enums
Enumerated List Level Types
Edge Definitions
Graph Definitions
View Definitions
What are the rules for configuring the data model?
The Common Schema must be allowed to evolve because the scope of what the schema defines will change over time. However, objects and the properties and relationships between them have been modeled to support HxGN SDx2
work processes and to meet industry-standard data exchange.
 Unless explicitly stated otherwise, all rules defined here apply to both the meta-schema and the Common Schema.
For more information about how schema evolution rules allow for forward and backward compatibility with authoring tools, see Common Schema evolution rules.
Use these links to find more information about rules for creating and modifying specific schema objects:
Rule enforcement
Every schema object is stamped with the official revision and version for which it was created. Objects that predate the latest released version must adhere to the rules defined here. These rules are supported by the delivery and setup
procedures.
Creation rules
Keep the following rules in mind when making changes that affect object or relationship creation.
You may create new objects of any type, but the relationships between new objects and existing objects must not violate a revision rule.
You may create new relationships between existing objects, but the new relationship must not violate a revision rule.
Deletion rule
An object (including the relationships of the object) that supports ISchemaObj cannot be deleted if its revision or version predates the current software revision or version.
Revision rule
If a schema object type is not included in this section, any type of revision to that object is allowed.
Only the System Administrator role can be used to set up properties and property management in the HxGN SDx2 environment.
The objects in your system, such as the class definitions and interface definitions, are modeled to support your work processes and meet industry-standards for data exchange. However, system administrators can make changes to
the model by adding new properties and managing pick lists, and so on. For more information, see Common Schema data model changes.
Smart Administration only returns schema objects which are in an approved state in HxGN SDx2.
Create a property definition
You can create a new property definition only from custom interface definitions that have the Modified by Customer property. Properties have a type that is configured by a Scoped by relationship in the Common Schema. Properties are
most commonly scoped by a string and integer.
1. Select a custom interface definition, and click Actions 
 > Create Property Definition.
2. In the New Property Definition dialog box, enter the details, such as name, description, and display name.
3. In the Scoped by property type box, search and select the property type that scopes the property definition.
 You can select from standard property types like string, integer, Boolean, and so on, or you can set a predefined list of values for the property as an enumerated list type that is scoped by the property by default. For more
information, see Enumerated lists.
4. Enter other options as applicable.
Properties are created in the Draft state by default and are not released to the integration process until they have been reviewed using the Common Schema change request process and set to the Approved state.
Methods are available only for custom interfaces with the SDxByCust property.
Enumerated lists
Enumerated lists, or picklists, contain all the enumerated entries configured for a specific property definition. The enumerated picklist is displayed as an Enum List Type property and contains a defined list of enum entries that can be
selected when the list is used to define a display item on a form.
 You can set the predefined list of values for a property definition that you can select in a form by setting the Scoped by property to the Enum List Type. For more information, see Create a property definition.
Create an enum entry
You can modify picklist entries in the Enum List Type by creating new EnumEntries for EnumLists that have the Product Source property or Blank for CustomLists.
Select an Enum List Type, and click Actions 
 > Create Enum Entry.
Enum entries are created in the Draft state by default and are not released to the integration process until they have been reviewed in the Common Schema change request process and set to the Approved state.
If the parent Enum List Type for a new Enum entry is an out-of-the-box Enum List Type with a status set to null, its status is set to Draft and it is related to the change request.
You cannot create two Enum entries in one list with the same name, description, display name, or Enum number due to validation that occurs when Enum entries are created, updated, copied, and related to a list.
When you create Enum entries that are an extension of a default Enum list, the new Enum entry numbers are allocated to start at 10,000 or greater. This avoids conflicts with any future default Enum entries delivered through
updates.
If you try to change any Enum list that has the SDxSource property set to SIOCore, a validation error informs you that the new Enum entry numbers should be set to 10,000 or greater.
Disabling an enum entry
You can disable any enums from being exposed in List Edit or on Create and Update forms by updating the enum and setting the Is Disabled property.
Create forms suppress the display of all disabled enums from menu lists, even if the default enum is set to a disabled value.
Details forms allow the display of values containing disabled enums so that data loaded with these values can still be reviewed.
The Loader ignores the enum Is Disabled property when loading objects and data, so any disabled enums are also loaded into the database.
Enumerated hierarchies
Enumerated lists in a property definition can be combined to form an enumeration hierarchy defined as an Enumerated List Level property. These can be used to describe all the enumerations at a given level in an enumeration hierarchy.
When enumerated lists are combined in an Enumerated List Level, they create child enumerated lists that can be referenced by other enumerated lists.
An Enum List Type can contain Enum entries or other Enum List Types, which contain entries for the Enum List Type above them in the hierarchy. Enum List Items can then be extended to the Enum List Level. When a property definition
has an associated enumerated list level type, it effectively allows pick lists to be defined that are dependent on the selection of other entries.
Maintaining tag types in a hierarchy
 Only users with the System Administrator or Business Administrator roles have access to create schema objects, such as interface definitions or property definitions.
You can maintain the tag types in a tree view hierarchy, which allows you to add, update, and delete enum list items or enum entries for specific tag types in a displayed tree view. This functionality provides a mechanism in the schema
component to dynamically generate the realized relationship definitions for the class definitions. These list items are displayed in the enum and classification tree list, and includes the mapping between them. You can select the point in the
tree structure where a new list item or enum entry can be added and you are presented with a form to add the tag type information.
You can navigate the hierarchy of a tag type to expose the class definitions and interface definitions, as well as add custom interface definitions at specified levels. The tag class definition is a base class definition and realizes interfaces for
all related tag classes below in the hierarchy. When you add a new custom interface, all inherited class definitions automatically realize the same interface.
You can update existing tag type enum list items, but all schema item changes must follow the standard schema approval workflow and must be related to a change request for subsequent approval.
You can disable an enum list type in the tree view but only when it has no active child notes.
The Data Model Viewer role only has access to view schema objects.
Tag base class example
When objects are shared from a design tool to HxGN SDx2, the object results in a consolidated object of one of the following high-level class definitions:
Tag
Port
Document
In HxGN SDx2, typing property values are used to specify the class that will be used by design tools that are integrated. For example, for tags, these properties are TagType, TagClass, and SpecificType1 through SpecificType5.
This type of organization, using properties exposed by interfaces rather than different class definitions for all types of shared objects, allows design tools to share objects at any level of the hierarchy. The object shared by the design tool is
created at the most specific class definition possible, based on their information about the object. Once the object is consolidated, it is stored in the HxGN SDx2 database as the most generic class definition possible, in other words, as a
Tag, Port, or Document. This organization allows different design tools to consolidate information for the same objects, even when they view it at different levels of the Tag Type Hierarchy.
For example, if one design tool knows and shares an object as a temperature instrument, but another design tool knows that the object is a thermocouple when it shares the instrument, HxGN SDx2 will create both objects as tags, but still
be able to consolidate the data from the two design tools.
Example of determining the specialization of a shared object
Consider a rotary pump shared from a tool. In the design HxGN SDx2 database, the rotary pump is created as an instance of a RotaryPump. Once this class definition is selected, the object is assigned a Tag Type from the following list:
Data
Item
Network
Point
Run
Spatial item
The rotary pump falls under the Tag Type of Item. The selected Tag Type determines what tag classes are available. This list contains a number of different kind of tags that are part of the plant or ship, such as equipment, duct
components, instruments, pipes, piping components, supports, and so forth. In our hierarchy, pumps belong to the Equipment tag class.
Once a tag class is assigned, the options available for SpecificType1 are defined. In the case of our shared item, which belongs to the Equipment tag class, the possible values for SpecificType1 include the following:
Black box system
Civil equipment
Electrical equipment
Generator
HVAC equipment
Instrument equipment
Non-specific equipment
Process equipment
Wiring equipment
Our rotary pump is an example of Process equipment, which is set for SpecificType1. This selection determines what options are available for SpecificType2 to further define our object. Process equipment can be divided into different
subtypes, which are available as options for the SpecificType2 property. These options include values fluid transfer equipment, heat transfer equipment, mixing equipment, process vessels, solid transfer equipment, and so forth.
Rotary pumps are Fluid Transfer Equipment, which is set for the SpecificType2 property and determines that the following values are available for SpecificType3:
Blower
Compressor
Fan
Pump
Once SpecificType3 is set to Pump, the values for SpecificType4 are defined. These include several kinds of pumps, such as Centrifugal pump, Gear pump, Metering pump, Reciprocating pump, Rotary pump, and so forth.
The shared object has the SpecificType4 property value set to Rotary pump so that it is fully categorized in the HxGN SDx2 database. Using this information, we can build the following table. Each bold entry in the table indicates a class
definition that our rotary pump can be an instance of, from the least specific on the left to the most specific on the right. In addition to the values shown in this table, the rotary pump can also be an instance of the Tag class definition, the
most generic class definition available.
TagType
TagClass
SpecificType1
SpecificType2
SpecificType3
SpecificType4
Item
Instrument
Equipment
HVAC Equipment
Process Equipment
Fluid Transfer Equipment
Blower
Pump
Centrifugal pump
Gear pump
Rotary pump
Though this example ends at the SpecificType4 property, there is a SpecificType5 available to classify an object further, if the selected SpecificType4 object can be divided into further subtypes.
Some objects will not need this many levels for classification. Some types of objects may be fully classified using fewer SpecificType# properties.
For example, cable trays are defined at the TagClass level, but do not need to a SpecificType1 to classify them further.
For another example, shared tubing has the following classification:
TagType - Item
TagClass - Pipe
SpecificType1 - Tubing
The items in bold in the table above have both an interface (with properties) and a class definition associated with them.
All class definitions realize at least one interface definition. However, some class definitions that are associated to an interface definition are still displayed in the tag type hierarchy.
Configure the enum list types hierarchy
 Only users with the System Administrator role can update or delete newly created custom enum list items.
You configure the enum list types using the Tag Types functionality, in which you navigate and expand the enum list items in a tree view hierarchy. From a selected enum list item, you can also add new enum list items and create new
enum entries, as well as create interface definitions or property definitions from the primary class definition.
In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Tag Types.
The following example shows the hierarchy for the Black box system enum list type. When you select an enum list item at a specified level, you can view the related interface and class definitions for that enum list item in the CLASS
DEFINITIONS and INTERFACE DEFINITIONS tabs.
Newly created objects have a draft status and can be displayed by selecting the Show draft items option before being related to a change request for subsequent approval as part of Common Schema Change Request workflow.
New enum list items created at a specified level are automatically inherited by child enum list items below in the tree view hierarchy.
The update or delete option is not available for enum items delivered with the software or system solution by default.
Create a new enum list item in the hierarchy
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select TagTypes.
2. In the Tag Types dialog box, expand the hierarchy to the location where you want to add a new enum list item.
For example, Tag Types > Item > ArchitecturalElelment > Architectural building.
3. Select options 
 for the selected list item, such as Architectural building, and click Create List Item (Enum List Type).
4. Complete the details in the create form for the new list item, and click FINISH.
 The new draft list item appears indented to the selected list item, as shown by Building Height. New draft list items are highlighted with a blue tick until they are attached to a change request.
Update an enum list item
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Tag Types.
2. In the Tag Types dialog box, expand the hierarchy to locate the enum list item you want to update.
3. Select options 
 for the selected list item, and click Update.
4. Edit the list item's details in the form as required, and click UPDATE.
You can disable an enum entry from display in the hierarchy by selecting the Is Disabled option in the Update form.
You can select Delete to remove a selected draft enum list item. Only draft schema objects can be deleted.
Update an enum entry
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Tag Types.
2. In the Tag Types dialog box, expand the hierarchy to locate the enum entry you want to edit, and click Update.
3. Edit the enum entry's details in the form as required, and click UPDATE.
You can disable an enum entry in the enum list hierarchy by selecting the Is Disabled option in the update form, as shown by Brick Size.
You can select Delete to remove a selected draft enum entry. Only draft schema objects can be deleted.
Create an interface definition for an enum list item
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Tag Types.
2. In the Tag Types dialog box, expand the tag type hierarchy to locate the list item you want to add a new interface definition to.
For example, expand TagTypes > Item > Equipment > Black box system.
3. In the main window, select the list item options 
, and click CREATE INTERFACE DEFINITION.
4. Complete the create form details for the new interface definition, and click FINISH.
The new draft interface definition is displayed highlighted by the blue tick in the INTERFACE DEFINITIONS list for the selected enum type.
I
 You can edit the interface definition by selecting it, and clicking options 
. Delete is only allowed if the interface definition is in Draft status.
Create a property definition for an interface definition
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Tag Types.
2. In the Tag Types dialog box, expand the tag type hierarchy to locate the enum list item to expose the interface definition you want to add a new property definition to.
For example, expand TagTypes > Item > Architectural Element.
3. In the INTERFACE DEFINITIONS tab, select the interface definition, and click options 
 > Create Property Definition.
4. Complete the create form details for the new property definition, and click FINISH.
5. Click + to expand the interface definition details to view the new property definition draft highlighted by a blue tick.
 You can edit the property definition by selecting it, and clicking options 
. Delete is only allowed if the schema object is in Draft status.
Attach new items to a change request
After creating a new list item, an interface definition, a property definition, or an enum entry, you can attach the objects to a change request for review and subsequent approval as part of Common Schema Change Request workflow.
1. Click the Show draft items option to display all the created list items highlighted by a blue tick that are not related to a change request.
2. Select a change request from the Attach To Change Request list.
3. Select REVIEW AND APPLY to review the draft items in the Summary dialog.
4. Click APPLY to attach the draft items to the selected change request for subsequent review and approval.
You can select FINISH to attach the items to the change request without displaying the Summary dialog.
Once a draft item is related to a change request, it is no longer highlighted using the Show draft items option.
Add a new change request to the list
1. In the Configure Enum Types dialog, click 
 Create Common Schema Change Request.
2. In the Create Common Schema Change Request form, add details for the change request, and click Finish.
Common Schema data model changes
You manage changes to the Common Schema data model using the delivered Common Schema change management process. This process allows you to request, implement, and approve schema object changes, such as new properties
or pick lists. You can track the status of the change request at each stage or return the change to a previous state for further work after review. When all the changes are approved, the Common Schema is regenerated for use by
integration.
The following workflow shows the basic steps specific to the Common Schema change management process.
 The Common Schema is only released to integration when all schema objects are in the Approved state.
What do you want to do?
Create Common Schema change request
Prepare Common Schema change request
Create Common Schema objects
Review Common Schema change request
Rework the Common Schema change request
View the Common Schema change request objects
Report on customized schema objects
Create Common Schema change request
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Create Common Schema Change Request.
2. In New Common Schema Change Request, add details for the change request, and click Finish.
Prepare Common Schema change request
1. In your To Do List, select Common Schema Change Request.
2. Select Prepare Common Schema CR workflow step, and click Actions 
 > Prepare Common Schema Change Request.
3. Click options , and select Manage Related Information.
4. In the Relationship Builder > Pins > New Items, select the schema objects required for the change request, and click Relate.
For example, relate the enum list types, enums, interface definitions, and property definitions.
5. In Prepare Schema Change Request, click Complete.
Create Common Schema objects
You can create the Common Schema objects you want in your change request from the Data Model Administration feature set.
For more information on creating the schema objects, see Create a property definition and Maintaining tag types in a hierarchy
All object changes to the Common Schema are set to Draft status until they are Approved.
New interface definitions are related to the relevant data flows for the tools selected on the change request.
Review Common Schema change request
1. In To Do List, select the Common Schema Change Requests.
2. Select the Common Schema change request from the list, and click Actions 
 > Review Common Schema CR.
3. In Review Implementation, review the change request details and Common Schema objects, and select Approve.
The Common Schema is only released to integration when all schema objects are set to the Approved state.
You can delete any draft Common Schema objects using the Terminate action.
Rework the Common Schema change request
You can ask for modification on a Common Schema change request in the review step. Reworking an existing change request can help reduce potential mistakes and avoid creating other change requests.
1. Select the Common Schema change request, and click Actions 
 > Review Common Schema CR.
2. In Review Implementation, review the change request details and Common Schema objects, and select Rework.
3. In the Rework Common Schema Change Request dialog, enter the reason for the rework, and click OK.
 When you select to rework the change request, it is returned to the Prepare Common Schema change request step.
View the Common Schema change request objects
You can view the Common Schema change request objects in the Data Model Administration feature set.
Select Common Schema Change Requests, locate the completed change request, and click Info Map > Change Request scoped items to view the schema objects.
Report on customized schema objects
You can report on the schema objects in the System Administration feature set by running the existing Class Definitions report. This report allows you to easily identify customized schema objects and offers a general overview of any
modified schema objects in the system.
You can run the Class Definitions report using the (Interface) Modified by customer operator to return interface objects modified in the system selected by the EnumListType value.
If you do not add any criteria, the report returns all class definitions in the system by default.
The report is specific to the database and can only be accessed by the System Administration user belonging to the Admin owning group.
Configure Integration Schema End Point for HxGN SDx2 tenants
Smart Administration uses the Schema End Point in HxGN SDx2 as the source for Common Schema objects and extensions by default, when mappings are created for the design tools in your system. Smart Administration communicates
between the integrated tools using the schema objects that are all in the same format. Smart Administration reads the following objects:
Class Definitions
Interface Definitions
Property Definitions
Lists
List Items
UoM Lists
UoM List Items
Dataflows
Extend the existing stand-alone schema
You can extend your data model to:
Create objects or views of existing objects, by creating custom class and interface definitions
Store custom properties for objects, by creating custom property definitions
Create new relationships between objects, by creating custom relationship definitions.
To extend the schema, export the existing model, modify it as required, and then import the extended model.
Understand the Integration Engine
The Integration Engine is a collection of services that accepts and processes data shared from authoring tools. These services do the following things to manage design data in SDx2:
Collect objects with new data, as well as related objects and relationships.
Modify the data as necessary with calculations.
Create correlation IDs to link the objects with the same object as shared by other tools.
Consolidate or merge the new data with other data from this or other tools.
Compare the results to create instructions for authoring tools to correct or synchronize the data across the tools.
You can find more information about these services that make up the Integration Engine here:
Cumulate
Calculate
Correlate
Consolidate
Compare
Cumulate
The first step of the Integration Engine is cumulate. This step involves the gathering of objects and relationships that are processed in the other steps.
A scheduler task, SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask, triggers the Integration Engine by sending the changeset objects to the cumulate service.
The cumulate service of the Integration Engine groups the objects based on the GroupRelEdges and RelPathToGroup properties of the class definition. These group objects are sent to the correlate service through the message bus.
Processing tool data using SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask
HxGN SDx2 receives tool data through batch requests, and HxGN SDx2 creates changeset objects from those batch requests. When changeset objects are in a design component and configuration that are set for
SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask, it triggers the Integration Engine. For more information about changeset objects, see Tell me more about changeset objects.
You can update the configurations and design components that SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask begins consolidation for:
 You must have the Business Administrator or System Administrator role enabled to query or update SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask.
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Scheduled Tasks.
2. Select SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the Auto integrate scheduler task details section,
Design component domains - a list of design component UIDs separated by commas without spaces.
Configurations - a list of configuration UIDs separated by commas without spaces.
Maximum reintegration attempt count - the maximum number of times the group object should be retried by SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask for integration.
 If you do not select any configurations or design components, nothing is submitted.
Only the objects that have configurations and design components selected in the Auto integrate scheduler task details section are submitted for processing.
You can use asterisk (*) in the applicable box as a wildcard to select all configurations or design components.
Tell me more about changeset objects
The changeset objects are created with the Purpose property value set to ForHandover. SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask queries for ChangeSets with this value, then converts them to ChangeSets with the Purpose property
value set to ForIntegration and sends them to the cumulate service at regular intervals.
Below are the different types of ChangeSets that are created.
Changeset Name and UID prefix
Description
AIT
Changeset created for retrying shared groups and changesets.
RTEG
ChangeSet created for existing group objects in the system and
being processed currently. This ChangeSet is either picked up
by the SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask in handover
mode or sent directly to the cumulate service.
RTFT
ChangeSet created with the list of tool OBIDs which failed in the
cumulate service.
IHC
ChangeSet created during the conversion of changesets from
ForHandover to ForIntegration.
If the same tool objects are sent for processing again, the cumulate service checks whether the objects are modified. If they are not modified, the ChangeSet is ignored. If they are modified and an existing group object has the Processing
status, RTEG ChangeSet are created.
 When the same tool objects are sent for processing again and are modified while an existing group object is present, the RTEG ChangeSet is not created in the following situations:
If the existing group object has the RetryConsolidate or FailedConsolidate status
If the existing group object has an exception that can be retried
If the existing group object has stayed in the same state for more than one hour.
In these cases, the ChangeSet is submitted immediately.
Changesets
The cumulate process depends on the concept of changesets, which is the identification of the set of objects and relationships required for the other pieces of the Integration Engine:
To perform the calculations associated with the data flow.
To determine correlation values for the objects tied to the ChangeSet.
For the precedence rules to use for consolidation.
Calculate
The second step of the Integration Engine is calculate. In this step, various calculations are performed on the data objects fetched in the cumulate step. The calculated data objects are then ready for correlation in the third step.
At this time, HxGN SDx2 supports the naming rule calculation. For more information, see Configure naming rules for objects. Additional calculations will be available in future updates.
The default naming rule sets the name of an incoming object as the ControlName, but when the incoming object has no name, the UID of the object becomes the ControlName.
Correlate
The third step in the Integration Engine is correlate. In this step, the objects that have been accumulated and had calculations performed to augment them are correlated prior to being consolidated.
The correlate service generates a unique ID by applying certain rules, called correlation rules, to the objects. This unique ID is called the CorrelationID and is used, in combination with the type hierarchy of the object, to create and update
the consolidated objects in the consolidate design component.
Correlation service communication
The correlate service consumes messages sent from the previous services, cumulate and calculate. The messages contain the group object OBID. This correlate service processes all the object OBIDs that are present in the group object.
Correlation process
The correlation values that apply to the objects are determined by a set of rules that define how to identify the correlation values. These rules are described in the Correlation rules section.
Correlation rules
 Sets of correlation rules in the Common Schema define the correlation IDs. Each set of rules consists of a correlation rule definition that:
Contains an If criteria that must be met for the set of correlation rules to apply to a given object.
Contains an Else statement that is used to determine the correlation value if none of the correlation rules for the correlation rule definition evaluate to True. The Else statement should evaluate to a string. Frequently, the Else
statement is simply an empty string.
Includes an ordered set of one or more correlation rules (related using CorrelationRuleDefRule relationships), each of which contains:
An If criteria that contains a format definition that, when the criteria is met, is used to determine the correlation ID.
Correlation rule definitions are defined on the object type, specifically, the tag type or the port type. A single rule definition will be related to one or more correlation rules. For correlation rules to be applied, the condition on the rule definition
must be met.
Each correlation rule contains an If criteria and correlation value. When an object begins the correlate process, it is evaluated to determine if the If criteria on the rule is satisfied. If that criteria is met, that correlation value is applied. This
generates the correlation ID. Rules are processed one at a time, depending on the order value, until the correlation ID is generated. If none of the ordered If criteria are met, the Else statement is used to determine the correlation ID.
 If you have the System Administator role, you can view and manage correlation rules from the Design Tool Integration feature set. For more information, see View correlation rules.
Correlation rules and object types (TypeCorrelationRuleDef)
Each object type has a type hierarchy that describes the type of object. For tag objects, the hierarchy is established using properties in ITag (TagType, TagClass, SpecificType1, SpecificType2, SpecificType3, SpecificType4,
SpecificType5). For port objects, the hierarchy is defined inside IPort (PortClass, PortType1, PortType2). Other object types also have hierarchies.
Any of the types in these type hierarchies can be tied to one or more correlation rule sets (in other words, to the correlation rule definition for the set of correlation rules) using the TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships. This is an ordered
relationship definition, so if a given type is tied to more that one correlation rule definition, the order dictates which correlation rule definition should be checked first.
When evaluating a given object, the following logic determines its correlation ID:
Starting with its most specific type, check for TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships for that type.
If one or more TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships are found, get the correlation rule definition, and see if this object satisfies the If clause. If so, then:
Process the rules, which are ordered, until a rule if found for which this object evaluates to True. When this rule is encountered, its format (Correlate value) definition is used to format the correlation ID string.
If none of the rules evaluate to True, use the Else definition for the correlation rule definition to define the correlation ID. Frequently, the Else format will simply produce an empty string, which means that this object will not be
consolidated.
If the If clause for a correlation rule definition evaluates to False, proceed to the next correlation rule definition as defined by the TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships, and repeat the logic in the previous bullet.
If all the correlation rule definitions’ If clauses resolve to False, or if there are no TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships for the current type, proceed to the next higher level in the type hierarchy, and repeat this process.
To illustrate this, consider the following type hierarchy. In this hierarchy, each of the nodes has been numbered, and nodes with TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships have been circled.
For this example, nodes 2, 5, 9 and 12 have TypeCorrelationRuleDef relationships. Therefore, for an object of each type, correlation rule definitions are used to come up with correlation IDs:
No correlation rule definition - correlation ID is empty string.
Use correlation rule definition for node 2.
No correlation rule definition - correlation ID is empty string.
No correlation rule definition - correlation ID is empty string.
Use correlation rule definition for node 5.
Use correlation rule definition for node 2.
Use correlation rule definition for node 2.
Use correlation rule definition for node 2.
Use correlation rule definition for node 9.
No correlation rule definition - correlation ID is empty string.
Use correlation rule definition for node 2.
Use correlation rule definition for node 12. If the If clause evaluates to False, use correlation rule definition for node 2.
Types of correlation rule definitions
Four types of correlation rule definitions exist to relate type hierarchies to one or more correlation rule sets.
DefaultCorrelationRuledef - This type of rule definition is present on the top-most level of the tag or port type hierarchy. It applies to all types in the hierarchy (including the tags with only the Tag Class or Port Class set), except for
the types where either the Non-CorrelationRuledef or SkipCorrelationRuledef are related. In this case, the Correlation ID contains the Tag Class and Control Name. Duplicate checks are performed on the complete hierarchy and
the TypeHierarchy is stamped on a different property on the identity register
Non-CorrelationRuledef - This type of rule definition is related to a type when the entire hierarchy under that specific type is not correlated. It does not contain any rules.
SkipCorrelationRuledef - This type of rule definition is related to a type when that specific type is not correlated, while the types above and below it in the hierarchy are. It does not contain any rules.
SpecificCorrelationRuledef - This type of rule definition is related to types where the Correlation ID that is generated is different from the default correlation rule definition. For example, this type of rule definition is used if the Parent
Correlation value needs to be included in the Correlation ID.
Here are some examples of the types for which the specific correlation rules are provided by default.
Tag Class - Support, InternalStream, ProcessStream, Stair, Ladder, Cabling, Instrument
Specific type 1 - ValveOperator, Door, CableMarker, InstrumentValve, NonSpecificInstrument
Correlation and consolidation for objects with and without graphics
Extra considerations must be taken for objects with and without related graphics.
Tags with Graphics
Consolidate tags are created even when an exception is expected after evaluating the DefaultCorrelationRuleDef or SpecificCorrelationRuleDef. For types related to the SkipCorrelationRuleDef or Non-CorrelationRuleDef, the
identity register entries are created without a Correlation ID and exception to create the consolidate objects.
Tags without graphics
Consolidate tags are not created if there is an exception after evaluating the DefaultCorrelationRuleDef or SpecificCorrelationRuleDef. For types related to the SkipCorrelationRuleDef or Non-CorrelationRuleDef, no identity register
entries nor consolidate objects are created.
Rules and examples of IDR record values in different scenarios
These example correlation RuleDefs are used in the tag identity register entries and consolidation examples that follow.

Correlation ID - RegExReplace(Obj.TagClass)/GetEnumNumber(Obj.TagClass)/Obj.ControlName

Correlation ID - GetCorrelationID(Obj-

SpecificCorrelationRuleDef for Motor
If Clause - IsSetB(ControlName) AND IsOtherEndObjectExistingB(Obj.EquipmentServiceToEquipment_12) AND
HasUniqueProperty(Obj.ControlName,EquipmentServiceToEquipment_12)
Correlation ID - RegExReplace(Obj.TagClass)/GetEnumNumber(Obj.TagClass)/Obj-
>EquipmentServiceToEquipment_12.ControlName/Obj.ControlName

Empty string or undefined correlation IDs
If no correlation rule definition applies to an object based on its type, or if the evaluation of the correlation rules results in an empty string for the object, the object will not be consolidated during the consolidate process, since it doesn’t have
a unique correlation ID.
Objects in SharedGroup that are correlated
The topic Cumulate referred to the ChangeSet container and showed how the type definitions are tied to SharedGroup definitions. For the purposes of correlation, only those objects that tie to the SharedGroup definition for the container
are correlated. All other objects within the SharedGroup container, as well as all objects within the extended container, are not correlated.
The rationale behind this approach is that the SharedGroup definition identifies that set of objects that is needed to evaluate the correlation rules, as well as the precedence rules, for all objects tied to that SharedGroup definition. Objects
within the SharedGroup container that are not tied to the SharedGroup definition may or may not have all the objects in the SharedGroup container that are needed for their correlation and consolidation rules. Therefore, they are not
correlated or consolidated (or compared).
If clause for correlation rule definition and If clause for each correlation rule
The If clause for the correlation rule definition, and the If clause for each correlation rule for a correlation rule definition, are language clauses that evaluate to a Boolean value (either True or False). These clauses are described using the
same language that was used throughout the software for specifying criteria. However, several special functions have been added to support the requirements for correlation:
GetCountOfRelatedDesignComponents function
GetEnumNumber function
HasUniqueProperty function
IsSetB function
RegexMatchb function
RegExReplace function
For more information about syntax that can be used in correlation rules, see Common syntax.
Handling of Identity Register Objects for objects with duplicate names
Objects which have the same control name are duplicate objects.
The Identity register exception message uses the HasUniqueProperty function, so the Identity register exception message can contain the OBIDs of the duplicate objects. For more information about the HasUniqueProperty
function, see HasUniqueProperty function.
To resolve the duplicate objects, the data needs to be shared again.
Persistence of correlation information
The correlation values that are determined based on the correlation rules are persisted in the ISIOObject interface.
ISIOObject interface definition
The ISIOObject interface definition is used to maintain the correlation information for the Common Schema objects. The CorrelationID is the correlation value for the object (determined based on the correlation rules) and the macro value
(also based on the correlation rules).
Configure correlation rules and correlation definitions
 When you load or change naming, correlation, or precedence rules in the system using Import Data, you must clear the cache so that the new or changed rules are applied before processing incoming data. Select Clear Cache
from the System Administration feature set.
View correlation rules
You can view the correlation rules that the Integration Engine uses.
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Manage Correlation Rules.
2. In the Rule Definition tab, use the tree to select the Type of object from the tag, port, and breakdown hierarchy.
 Alternatively, you can start typing the name of the object type in the Filter field at the top of the box until you find the type of object you are looking for and then select it.
3. In the Rule Definition box, select a rule definition, and select Next.
The Summary tab displays the correlation rules for the type and rule definition that you selected.
Consolidate
The fourth step in the Integration Engine is consolidate. In this step, the objects that have been accumulated, had calculations performed to augment them, and have been correlated are consolidated (used to update the Consolidate
design component).
The consolidate service consolidates tool objects, based on the correlation IDs generated by the correlate service. Objects without a correlation ID are not consolidated unless they have graphics or drawings.
Since the correlation ID uniquely identifies each object, this value is combined with the type hierarchy of the object to locate the existing consolidated object, if it exists, for each of the objects being consolidated. If the consolidated object
doesn’t exist, the object being consolidated and the appropriate relationships are copied into the consolidate design component. If the consolidated object does exist, precedence rules will govern how the consolidated object and
relationships are updated based on the object being processed.
The interfaces and relationships to be consolidated for a tool object depend on the data flows related to the corresponding interface definitions and relationship definitions. If a related data flow indicates a different source design component
than target design component for interfaces or relationships, the interfaces or relationships are consolidated. Also, group inclusion of the relationship definition indicates which end object the relationship should be consolidated with.
 The Integration Engine follows your classification hierarchy by using the most specific mapped enum in the tag type hierarchy to set the primary classification. The Integration Engine then sets the classification properties based on
the classification hierarchy above that primary classification. For more information about setting up your tag classifications, see Tag Classifications sheet. For more information about the tag type hierarchy, see Maintaining tag types in a
hierarchy.
Configure precedence rules
HxGN SDx2 automates the consolidation of information about an object by choosing the source that takes precedence in the event of an inconsistency. The precedence rules determine the precedence of information entering the
consolidated design component. For example, both Smart P&ID and Smart Electrical can share information about electrical components in a plant, but the information from Smart Electrical should take precedence in the consolidated
design component over Smart P&ID information because information from Smart Electrical is more mature than information about the same electrical components coming from Smart P&ID.
Precedence rules apply in sequence, so the first rule that fits incoming data applies and sets the precedence value of the incoming information about an object. For more information about calculating precedence rules, see Review
precedence rules.
All default Hexagon precedence rules apply to any object type. You can create custom precedence rules for specific object types using a load file. For more information about creating custom precedence rules, Create a precedence
rule.
All precedence rules are created at Configuration Top and apply across the entire tenant to all plants and projects. You cannot link precedence rules to a specific configuration.
You must have the System Administrator or Business Administrator role to view and manage precedence rules.
Review precedence rules
Precedence rules control whether incoming property values for a correlated object replace the values that already exist for that object in the consolidated design component.
Properties
Every property in the consolidated design component, in addition to having a property value, has a current precedence value. When the precedence rules are applied to a property on an incoming object, they result in an integer
precedence value for that property on the object. The precedence value resulting from the analysis of the rules is compared with the current precedence value for the property in the consolidated design component and, if the incoming
precedence value is greater than or equal to the current precedence value, the incoming property value is used to update the data in the consolidated design component.
Precedence rules
The precedence rules are an ordered collection of rules. The Manage Precedence Rules page displays the following information about the precedence rules. For more information about creating a precedence rule, see Create a
precedence rule.
Order - The location in the sequential order in which the rules are evaluated to see if they apply to the data.
Name - The name of the precedence rule. Hexagon precedence rule names follow the syntax of PrecedenceCategory.DesignComponent.ObjectType as a naming convention.
Description - A description of what the precedence rule applies to.
Precedence Category - A property exposed on each interface definition that defines the precedence category for all property definitions and relationships tied to that interface definition. If the precedence category is Any, the rule
applies to all precedence categories.
Design Component - The design component to which the precedence rule applies. If the design component is All, the rule applies to data shared from all design components.
Object Type - The Name of the node of the object type in one of the type hierarchies. If the type is Any, the rule applies to all object types.
Criteria - Conditional criteria that must resolve to True for the rule to apply.
Precedence Value - The integer value that specifies the precedence for the property of an object. Values greater than or equal to take precedence.
Precedence rules are processed in sequential order, so the first rule that applies to the incoming object property sets the precedence value. A precedence rule applies to an incoming property if the property comes from the design
component, matches the object type, and meets the criteria.
 A precedence rule with a lower precedence value earlier in the sequence than one with a higher precedence value can result in the incoming property being given a lower precedence value than you intended.
For more information, see Relationships and group inclusion.
Precedence rule Order
The precedence rules are applied in sequence. The Order value establishes the place of a precedence rule in that sequential order. The first rule that has its requirements met sets the precedence value.
If no rules apply, the precedence value is 0.
Precedence rule Name
The HxGN SDx2 precedence rule Name follows the syntax of PrecedenceCategory.DesignComponent.ObjectType as a naming convention. We recommend that you use similarly clear naming conventions for any custom precedence rules
that you create.
Precedence rule Precedence Category
The Precedence Category is a property exposed on each interface definition that defines the precedence category for all property definitions and relationships that are tied to that interface definition. A precedence category for an interface
definition can be overridden by specifying a PrecedenceCategory for a specific property definition. If a PrecedenceCategory is not set for an interface definition, it can be set for a class definition.
HxGN SDx2 has a sequence for how it finds the Precedence Category:
1. The property definition.
2. The interface definition.
3. The class definition.
Tell me more about the PrecedenceCategory sequence
The Integration Engine begins with the precedence category that affects the lowest level: The property definition.
The PrecedenceCategory on the property definition overrides the precedence category of the interface definition and class definition. No PrecedenceCategory is set by default on property definitions.
The PrecedenceCategory on the interface definition is the primary way that PrecedenceCategory is set.
The PrecedenceCategory on the class definition applies if the interface definition has either no PrecedenceCategory or the PrecedenceCategory of Any. This typically pertains to the case of a generic interface, such as
IConstructionInfo, that applies to many Common Schema object types. In this case, the object types often have different pieces of information that make sense for that generic interface to expose. For example, the construction
information for a vessel differs from the construction information for an instrument. So, the generic interface definition inherits its PrecedenceCategory from the class definition.
You can use a custom interface definition to expose custom properties for tracking business-specific information about objects while maintaining the PrecedenceCategory for properties exposed by your custom interface definition.
 We recommend that you limit using precedence overrides on properties. Using PrecedenceCategory on property definitions as an override makes it difficult to help you troubleshoot problems with the Integration Engine on
your tenant.
Precedence rule Design Component
The Design Component identifies the design component for the incoming data that the rule applies to. Each precedence rule is tied to one design component and applies only to that specific design component. If the design component for
a rule is defined as All, the rule applies to all design components.
Because objects are processed based on the design component that shared them, the processing begins by filtering out all rules that don’t apply to the design component for the objects being processed.
Precedence rule Object Type
The Object Type is the Name for a node in one of the various object type hierarchies, such as the Tag Types hierarchy or Port Classes hierarchy. If the Object Type specified in the rule is Any, then the rule applies to all types. When the
Object Type set on the precedence rule matches any of the types in the type hierarchy of the shared data, it evaluates to True.
Since the precedence rules are applied in sequence, the behavior of a type of object regarding precedence rules is different than that for correlation rules. Whereas the correlation rules are based on processing going from the most specific
type to the most generic type, the precedence rules are based strictly on the ordering of the rules.
For example, you have a capacitor shared from Intergraph Smart® Electrical Design Software. The Tag Type hierarchy for a capacitor is Item > Equipment > Electrical equipment > Electric load > Capacitor. You have a precedence
rule ordered in position 11 that applies to Electrical equipment object types and another precedence rule ordered in position 24 that applies to Capacitor object types. Both precedence rules apply to All design components and Electrical
precedence category. Both precedence rules could apply to the incoming capacitor because the object type hierarchy of the incoming capacitor includes both Electrical equipment and Capacitor. The precedence rule in position 11 of the
sequence sets the precedence value for the incoming capacitor because 11 is higher than 24 in the sequence of precedence rules.
 The Integration Engine follows your classification hierarchy by using the most specific mapped enum in the tag type hierarchy to set the primary classification. The Integration Engine then sets the classification properties based on
the classification hierarchy above that primary classification. For more information about setting up your tag classifications, see Tag Classifications sheet. For more information about the tag type hierarchy, see Maintaining tag types in a
hierarchy.
Precedence rule Criteria
The Criteria defined for a precedence rule must evaluate to True for that rule to apply. You can set the Criteria to TRUE to have it always evaluate as True. The Criteria accepts the following criteria functions:
AllAreSetB, AnyAreSetB, NoneAreSetB, and AreSet functions
EndNamedRecord and EndNamedRecordB functions
HasUniqueProperty function
IsContextUnique function
IsOtherEndObjectExistingB function
IsRelatedTo and IsRelatedToB functions
IsSetB function
RegexMatchb function
RegExReplace function
For more information, see Criteria functions.
As delivered, the Criteria are set to TRUE because the precedence rules are designed to align with the Hexagon standard design process. You can use custom criteria to make the Integration Engine follow your business rules or
engineering design rules.
Precedence rule Precedence Value
Precedence rules apply the Precedence Value to incoming objects and relationships. The Precedence Value is an integer value that applies if the type, design component, property category, and criteria apply to the object. The
precedence value must be an integer greater than zero. There is no maximum number for the precedence level.
If the precedence value for an incoming property is greater than or equal to the precedence value of the property in the consolidated design component, the incoming property value replaces the property value in the consolidated design
component.
In the consolidated design component, every property for every non-relationship object has a precedence value, and every relationship has a precedence value for its ObjectPrecedenceValue property that is exposed by IObject.
Likewise, every incoming property on non-relationship objects needs a precedence value, and every incoming relationship needs a precedence value for its ObjectPrecedenceValue property that is exposed by IObject.
Relationships and group inclusion
The precedence value for a relationship is stored on the ObjectPrecedenceValue property of the relationship. The GroupInclusion value determines ownership for the relationship, and you can find the GroupInclusion value defined on
the relationship definition. The interface definition for the end specified by the GroupInclusion value defines the precedence category for all relationships for that relationship definition. For more information, see Group inclusion.
For example, the relationship definition OwnsNozzles has a GroupInclusion value of IncludeRelWithObj2. For the relationship definition OwnsNozzles, end 2 is the interface INozzleRole. INozzleRole has the Mechanical_Precedence
precedence category, so the relationship definition OwnsNozzles has Mechanical_Precedence as well. Because relationships inherit their precedence category from their relationship definition based on GroupInclusion, relationships
defined by OwnsNozzles share the same precedence category as INozzleRole, which is mechanical precedence.
Manage precedence rules
You can set the sequence of precedence rules for information coming to HxGN SDx2 from design components.
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Manage Precedence Rules.
2. Drag a rule up or down the list to change its Order in the sequence.
To move a rule to a position on a different page, you can edit the Order number directly in the Results grid.
To see details for a precedence rule, select a precedence rule, and select View Precedence Rule.
 You cannot reorder the sequence in the Results grid while a Filter is applied to a column in the Manage Precedence Rules screen.
Create a precedence rule
You can create a precedence rule by creating an XML load file with all the necessary information about the precedence rule and importing it.
1. Create an XML load file.
2. Use Container as the root element, and write your precedence rule.
How do I write a precedence rule load file?
You can write a precedence rule XML load file using the PrecedenceRule element. The PrecedenceRule element consists of three child elements: IObject, IPrecedenceRule, and ISchemaObj.
IObject has the attributes UID, Name, and Description.
UID is used in several relationships. We recommend entering an IObject UID that clearly defines the purpose of the precedence rule.
Name appears in HxGN SDx2 when you retrieve the object.
Description appears in HxGN SDx2 when you retrieve the object.
IPrecedenceRule has the attributes SequenceNo, ObjectType, DesignComp, PropertyCategory, Criteria, and PrecedenceValue.
SequenceNo is an integer for the order number of the rule in the sequence of precedence rules.
 When using an XML load file, we recommend loading a single precedence rule at a time or loading an uninterrupted sequence of precedence rule SequenceNo values. When you load a precedence rule, the
SequenceNo of your precedence rule increments the SequenceNo values that are equal to or greater than your loaded file by one.
ObjectType is Any or the Name of the node for the object type in the Tag Types, Port Classes, or Breakdown Types hierarchy.
How do I find the IObject Name of an object type?
To find the IObject Name of an object type:
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Breakdown Types, Port Classes, or Tag Types.
2. Navigate the hierarchy to the object type, and select it.
3. Go to the Class definitions tab.
4. The IObject Name appears in the Name column for the relevant class definition.
DesignComp is All_Component or the IObject UID of the DesignComponent.
How do I find the IObject UID of a DesignComponent
You can find the IObject UID of the DesignComponent using Export Data.
1. In the Design Tools Integration feature set, select Design Components.
2. Select Actions 
 > Export Data.
PropertyCategory is Any or the IObject UID of the Precedence Category that the precedence rule applies to. You can view the precedence categories in the Data Model Administration feature set by selecting
Enumerated List Type > PrecedenceCategories.
How do I find the IObject UID of a Precedence Category
You can find the IObject UID of a Precedence Category using Export Data.
1. In the Data Model Administration feature set, select Enumerated List Type.
2. In the Results grid, select PrecedenceCategories.
3. In the Picklist Values list, select the name of the PrecedenceCategory.
4. Select Actions 
 > Export Data.
Criteria is True.
PrecedenceValue is an integer.
ISchemaObject is an empty element.
Precedence rule sample XML
<PrecedenceRule>
<IObject UID="3D.3D_Component.Any" Name="3D.3D.Any" Description="Rule for precedence category 3D, design component 3D, object type Any" />
<IPrecedenceRule SequenceNo="1" ObjectType="Any" DesignComp="3D_Component" PropertyCategory="3D" Criteria="TRUE" PrecedenceValue="50" />
<ISchemaObj />
</PrecedenceRule>
3. Import your XML load file. For more information about importing the load file, see Import a load file.
Consolidated object and relationship termination
Objects and relationships in the consolidated design component are terminated only when they are terminated in all tool domains.
Special functions added for precedence criteria
Some of the functions described in the Criteria functions section are more likely to be used in the precedence rule definitions, as part of the criteria. In particular, the variations on the AreSet function are more likely to be part of the
precedence rules than the consolidation rules. You can find more information about the AreSet functions here.
Compare
The fifth, and final, step in the server processing is compare. In this step the objects that have been accumulated, had calculations performed to augment them, and have been correlated and consolidated are used to determine differences
to which the various design components or tools need to react.
Communication with the compare service
The compare service processes all the objects in the group object. Using the consolidated UIDs of the objects in the group, it identifies the corresponding objects in all the design components, and compares the specific object present in all
other design components with the consolidated object, taking certain actions based on the instructions set. The actions can include update, delete, or insert.
After comparison, if an interested design component does not contain the object, it creates inconsistency objects with specific instructions within that design component. Later, upon receiving the object, the inconsistent object is terminated
if there are no differences when compared with the consolidated object. However, if there are differences, the instructions are updated to reflect the change. The compare service also compares the state of an object in other design
components with its consolidated object, and updates the instructions for other tool objects. Based on that updated instruction, the object is updated or terminated in other tool design components.
Comparison process
Objects that came out of the correlation process with a correlation ID and are consolidated during the consolidation process are also used for the comparison process. All other objects gathered during the cumulate phase are ignored.
For each object that is consolidated, one of four things happened during the consolidation process:
The object was added to the consolidated design component.
The object was deleted from, or terminated in, the consolidated design component.
One or more properties were modified for the consolidated object.
No changes were made to the consolidated object.
New consolidated object
For a new consolidated object, a check determines, based on the class of the new object, which design components include objects of that class. For each design component other than the source for the new object, an inconsistency
object is created, indicating that the design component needs to add that object or mark the inconsistency object to be ignored.
Deleted consolidated object
If a consolidated object is deleted, then for each tool object tied to that consolidated object, an inconsistency object is created indicating that the design component needs to delete that object.
Updated consolidated object
If one or more properties on a consolidated object were updated during consolidation, then for each object tied to that consolidated object, including the one being processed, a comparison is performed. If any common property values are
different, an inconsistency object is created indicating that the design component that shared those values needs to update them based on the consolidated object.
The comparison should only include properties on interfaces tied to the design component for the object being compared.
No changes made to the consolidated object
Even if no changes were made to the consolidated object during the consolidation process, the consolidated object is still compared with the object used for the consolidation to determine if there are any differences.
Inconsistency objects during the comparison processing
During the comparison process, three things can happen regarding inconsistency objects:
They can be created.
They can be deleted.
They can be unchanged.
Inconsistency objects are created in all cases where an inconsistency object is detected and no current inconsistency object already exists for that object for that design component.
Inconsistency objects are deleted if they existed prior to the comparison and:
The result of the comparison is that there are no differences between the common properties of the consolidated object and the tool domain object.
The current inconsistency object is for creating an object, and the new exception is for deleting that object.
Inconsistency objects are unchanged if they existed prior to the comparison and the comparison does not result in them being deleted.
Inconsistency object status
Every inconsistency object is given a status of Requiring action when it is created. The exception to this occurs if a tool deletes an object but that deletion fails to terminate the consolidated object. In this case, the inconsistency object (for
creating the object) should have a status of Intentionally deleted.
A tool may choose to intentionally ignore the inconsistency object rather than change its data to match the inconsistency object. In this case, the tool should set the status for the inconsistency object to Intentionally ignored.
Handling Intentionally ignored inconsistency objects
Inconsistency objects that are marked as Intentionally ignored are not modified during comparison, except in the following cases:
The inconsistency object is to be deleted (in other words, the difference no longer exists). Intentionally ignored inconsistency objects are not kept alive if the comparison process would normally delete them.
The inconsistency object is for an update and one or more property values for the object have changed because of the actions of a different design component. In this case the status reverts to Requiring action.
Handling relationships during comparison
During comparison, relationships are handled similarly to consolidation. Based on the group inclusion, one end of the relationship definition owns each relationship. The creation or deletion of a relationship is treated as a property change
to the object that owns the relationship.
Every relationship definition is tied to one or more data flows, which tie the relationship definition indirectly to one or more design components. Based on these indirect relationships between relationship definitions and design components,
the comparison process determines whether the comparison of the consolidated object with a tool design component object should include relationships. If the comparison should include the relationships and there is a difference in the
relationships, this is treated as a difference in the object at the owning end (the end with which the relationship is included) of the relationships. In this case, an update instruction for the inconsistency object is created, unless an update or
create instruction for the inconsistency object already exists.
Where the maximum cardinality is one at one end, or perhaps both ends, of a relationship definition, the group inclusion is set to include the relationship with that object. Although, in rare cases, the object at one end is included with the
other object, which also includes the relationship. Therefore, the relationship is treated as if it were a property of that object.
Where the maximum cardinality at both ends of a relationship definition is many, in other words if it is a many-to-many relationship definition, the group inclusion specifies which end of the relationship definition owns the relationships.
Because many objects can be at the other end of the relationship definition, there is no guarantee that a tool provides all the relationships. The comparison logic for the many-to-many case can only compare a subset of the relationships:
Those relationships that are included with the current object are compared with the relationships for the consolidated object, and an inconsistency object is created if:
The current object contains a relationship not found for the consolidated object.
The consolidated object contains a relationship not found for the current object, and that relationship is sourced from the design component of the object being compared with the consolidated object.
Manage instruction generation using SDxiHubGenerateInstructionsSchedulerTask
When SDxiHubGenerateInstructionsSchedulerTask finds eligible SharedGroup objects, it schedules them for the compare process in the Integration Engine.
During a project handover, you can disable this scheduler task to prevent the compare process and pause instructions to integrated design tools so that the compare process occurs only once during the final consolidation of data. When
the project handover share is complete, the scheduler task must be enabled again so that the compare process can run a final inconsistency check on the consolidated data.
 You must have the Business Administrator or System Administrator role to query or update SDxiHubGenerateInstructionsSchedulerTask. The Integration Data Filter must be set to Consolidated.
1. In the Design Tools Integration feature set, select Scheduled Tasks.
2. Select SDxiHubGenerateInstructionsSchedulerTask, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the Generate inconsistency instructions scheduler task details section, enter the following:
Design component domains - a list of design component UIDs separated by commas without spaces that the scheduler task applies to.
Configurations - a list of project UIDs separated by commas without spaces that the scheduler task applies to.
 You can use asterisk (*) in the applicable box as a wildcard to select all configurations or design components.
Manage the AutoCommit microservice
For objects that are in the authoring design component, the AutoCommit microservice relays instructions from the compare service to HxGN SDx2 to maintain consistency between the consolidated object and the same object represented
in the authoring design component.
You can manage the AutoCommit microservice options interactively:
1. In the Design Tool Integration feature set, select Design Components.
2. In the Results grid, select a design component.
3. Select Actions 
 > Update.
Allow Auto Commit Changes - Enables the AutoCommit microservice.
Allow Auto Create - Allows the AutoCommit microservice to process create instructions.
Allow Auto Update - Allows the AutoCommit microservice to process update instructions.
Allow Auto Delete - Allows the AutoCommit microservice to process delete instructions.
Allow Commit Existing Instructions - Allows the AutoCommit microservice to process previously created create, update, or delete instructions if the corresponding Auto Commit Option is enabled. This is an asynchronous
process.
The IAutoCommitOptions interface works only on authoring design components. You can identify an authoring design component by the Is Authoring Design Component? setting on the Details page for the design component.
The AutoCommit microservice requires that you enable Allow Auto Create, Allow Auto Update, or Allow Auto Delete to run.
Options for the AutoCommit microservice are exposed on the IAutoCommitOptions interface for each authoring design component. You can use an XML load file to manage these options. For more information about the
IAutoCommitOptions interface, see IAutoCommitOptions.
Reference information for the Integration Engine
Criteria functions
Criteria functions are used in several modifiable areas for the Integration Engine:
If clauses for the correlation rule definition. For more information, see If clause for correlation rule definition and If clause for each correlation rule.
If clauses for each correlation rule for a correlation rule definition. For more information, see If clause for correlation rule definition and If clause for each correlation rule.
Criteria for naming rules. For more information, see Configure naming rules for objects.
Criteria for precedence rules. For more information see, Configure precedence rules.
 Criteria functions are not recognized in every situation. See the documentation for each Integration Engine use case to find functions usable in that scenario.
Criteria functions are language clauses that evaluate to a Boolean value (either True or False) or perform a regular expression. These functions use the Common syntax. For more information about regular expressions, see Microsoft's
