The default search operator is Contains, which means the criteria entered in the search bar is contained in the results for each returned object. For example, if you type ENG as the criteria, any objects that contain ENG are returned.
You can change the search operator so that 'Is equal to' Operator is selected by default for an Entry Point. This search operator helps improve search performance by targeting the results to return an exact match to the search criteria.
You can change the default search operator as follows:
1. Search for the Entry Point you want to edit, and select Actions 
 > Update.
2. In the Main Details, select the Use Equal To Operator By Default option so that the 'Is equal to' Operator is always selected in the search bar menu.
If you do not set the Use Equal To Operator By Default option, you can manually select the 'Is equal to' Operator in the search menu each time you run a search.
The delivered search operator default is Contains, but a search returns everything that contains the search criteria.
You must be a system administrator to update an API Entry Point display. For more information, see Update an API Entry Point display.
Edit the display name of a search menu item
You can change the name of the search menu item by editing the display name in the Display as box for an existing API Entry Point.
1. Find the API Entry Point you want to edit, and select Actions 
 > Update.
2. Edit the display name of the search menu item in the Display as box.
Configure the default search filter column set items
When you use FILTER on the Search menu, you can change the default column set items that are displayed as the search filters so you can control the specific set of search results returned for each Entry Point.
1. In HxGN SDx2, locate the Entry Point, and select Show Column Set.
2. Select the column set, and select Actions 
 > Manage Column Items.
3. In the Web Search Default column item, set each property attribute to True that you want to use as a default filter for that entry point. Any search then includes the selected property attributes in the Search menu criteria by default.
 You can choose up to a maximum number of five (5) column set properties from the list. This helps improve your search performance by restricting the number of properties you can select. If you select more than five (5), a
message warns you that too many filters are selected. If you still go ahead and run the search with six or more filters selected, peek search is disabled, and the search just uses Name as the default or the search filter option
configured at the top.
4. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select the Regenerate Metadata function to refresh the metadata.
Only string based properties can be used as the filter defaults, such as Classification or Category.
If no column set attributes are set as the search filters, Name is used by the Search menu by default.
If there is just one column set attribute set, it is automatically selected in the Search menu as the default.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Exclude search filter column set items
When you use FILTER on the Search menu, the full list of column set items are displayed for the selected API Entry Point. However, you can control each Entry Point object from displaying every column set item by excluding selected
attributes from the Search menu.
1. Locate the Entry Point, and select Show Column Set.
2. On the column set, select Actions 
 > Manage Column Items.
3. In the Web Client Filter Behavior column item, set the Exclude from filter property for each attribute you do not want to see in the Search menu when you use FILTER.
4. Go to the System Administration feature set, select the Regenerate Metadata function to refresh the metadata.
If you exclude an attribute that is set as a default Search menu item, the exclusion takes precedence.
If you exclude every attribute, Name is automatically included by default in the filter menu.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Configure the results grid sort order
To configure automatic sorting of objects in the results grid by finding the column set related to the object type you want to configure for automatic sorting, such as an API Entry Point.
When you locate the column set, you can configure the automatic sorting using the Manage Column Items dialog box, which allows you to configure the sort order of column items.
The two options used for configuring automatic sorting are:
Sort Sequence - determines the priority order of the column items to sort on.
Sort Order - determines whether to sort by ascending, descending, or none.
For example, you can start to sort the order of the column items for a specific column set by setting the Sort Sequence for the Name column item to 1 and the Sort Order to Ascending.
 For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Configure Actions menu items
You can display a method as a shortcut command on the Actions menu by relating it to an applicable client API. For a complete list of all the supported client APIs, see Client APIs.
Methods should also be related to an applicable interface definition and access group.
Newly created shortcut icons are saved to an azure storage account, which uses content containers to store icons for each tenant.
Icons are attached to a class definition so they can be related to their methods. For more information, see Configure a UI icon.
Display a method icon on the Actions menu
You can give prominence to method functionality using shortcut icons displayed when you open the Actions menu or displayed in a ribbon when you hover over any point of an object row in a results grid.
1. In HxGN SDx2, find the method, and select Update.
2. On the method update form, locate the Shortcut toolbar details section.
3. In the Shortcut icon box, enter the name of the icon to use as the method shortcut icon.
 Search for the name of the icon to use in System Administration > Icons. Do not use the filename.
4. In the Shortcut Display Text box, enter the text to be displayed.
5. In the Shortcut Sequence box, enter the number for the icon to appear in a sequence in the menu ordered from low to high. If the lowest number is one, it is the first icon to be displayed in the menu.
6. In the Shortcut Tooltip box, enter the text for the tooltip.
For more information on using action shortcuts, see Use the Actions menu to manage objects.
HxGN SDx2 uses an azure storage account that has content containers to store the icons for each tenant.
For more information on configuring custom UI icons for use with methods, see Configure a UI icon.
To display method UI icons in a feature set, see Create a feature set.
Configure conditions
Conditions control the availability of methods displayed in the search menu, actions menu, or as a function tile feature set. They can also control access to specific types of object or functionality, as well as check the validity to create
relationships between two objects. On some occasions a condition can also be made on a relationship itself.
There are two basic condition types:
Object conditions - these operate on a single object type.
Relationship conditions - these operate on two objects that participate in a relationship.
You can search for an existing condition in the search bar, or you can select the System Administration feature set, and select Conditions.
 You can also find a condition by choosing the related method, and selecting Actions 
 > Relationships > Show Condition.
The condition examples displayed are just examples. You must have a good working knowledge of the metadata used in your system to construct conditions.
After a condition is created, you can add it to a method by selecting the condition in the Condition list in the Main Details tab. For more information, see Update a method.
If an existing condition in a method is updated, it is automatically applied to the method.
Create a condition
Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Create Condition.
 For information on how to create a condition using the applicable syntax, see Condition syntax.
Update a condition
Search for a condition, and select Actions 
 > Update.
 For information on how to update the syntax in a condition, see Condition syntax.
Condition syntax
The following examples show how you can create a condition using various types of syntax and expression.
Common syntax
The following syntax criteria can be used to create a condition.
Primary, property, and related objects
Primary objects
The following syntax can be used to reference a primary object in a condition.
Syntax
Description
Obj.
For the main object
Obj->
For the object defined on the relationship object
Obj1.
For the object 1 as defined on the relationship definition
Obj2.
For the object 2 as defined on the relationship definition
Env
For the environment object
SPFOptions
For accessing the system options object
Property objects
You can access properties on an object by referencing the property separated from the primary object using a delimiter, such as Obj.Name.
Obj.Name = 'Test'
And to create a condition that checks that the document revision is in the WORKING state:
Obj->SPFFileComposition_12->SPFRevisionVersions_21.SPFRevState = ’WORKING’
Related objects
Related objects can be referenced by a relationship definition or edge definition qualifier added to the primary object
Relationship definition - to reference an object by a relationship definition, add the _12 or _21 direction qualifier suffix to the object name. For example:
To reference the item owner by a relationship definition use.
Obj->SDxItemOwner_12
Or to reference a property on a related object.
Obj->SPFRevisionVersions_21.SPFRevState
Edge definition - to reference an object by an edge definition, add the EDG_ direction qualifier prefixed to the edge definition.
Obj->EDG_LatestVersion references the latest version of a document by an edge definition.
Only one relationship can be navigated from each of the main objects.
You can reference an object's configuration using the keyword ItemConfig. For example:
An object's plant or project is stored as a property and not as a relationship, so you would use, Obj->ItemConfig.Property.
 You can also use ItemConfig with Obj1 and Obj2 objects. For example, Obj1.ItemConfig.Property.
Comparison operators
Simple comparisons
A condition is made up of two expressions and a comparison operator. The following comparison operators are supported:
Comparison Operator
Description
=
Equal to
!= or <>
Not equal to
<
Greater than
>
Less than
<=
Greater than or equal to
>=
Less than or equal to
like (use * for wildcards)
Similar to
Boolean comparison operators
The following Boolean operators are supported in comparisons:
Boolean Operator
Description
And
Performs a comparison containing values so that both conditions results in True
Or
Performs a comparison where the results are True if any one of the conditions is true
Any
Performs a comparison that is True for any of the values in the condition
All
Performs a comparison that is True only if it is true for all the values in the condition
( )
Parenthesis to group conditions or control the order in which they are used
For example, All(Obj.SDxPrgActive) = True or Any(Obj.SDxPrgActive) = False.
ANY qualifier
The ANY qualifier results in the selection of the best value for the to-many property based on the rest of the constraint.
Example
Result
Any(Obj->A.B)<4
True if any value for multi-valued property A.B has a value less than 4.
Any(Obj->A.B) = 4
True if any value for multi-valued property A.B has a value of 4.
Any(Obj->A.B) = Any(Obj->C.D)
True if any value for multi-valued property A.B matches any value for multi-valued property C.D.
ALL qualifier
The ALL qualifier is basically the opposite of the ANY qualifier in that it selects the worst value for satisfying the constraint.
Example
Result
All(obj->A.B )< 4
True if the largest value for A.B is less than 4.
All(obj->A.B) = 4
True if any value for A.B has a value of 4.
Combining ANY and ALL
You can combine the ANY and ALL qaulifiers to provide the best and worst value used in combination.
Example
Result
Any(obj->A.B) < All(obj->C.D)
True if the smallest value for A. B is less than the smallest value for C.D.
All(obj->A.B) < Any(obj->C.D)
True if the largest value for A.B is smaller than the largest value for C.D.
Any(obj->A.B) = All(obj->C.D)
True if there is a value for A.B that matches all values for C.D.
All(obj->A.B) = All(obj->C.D)
True if every value for A.B is the same as every value for C.D
Any(obj->A.B)<Any(obj->C.D)
True if the smallest value for A.B is less than the value for C.D.
All(obj->A.B) = All(obj->C.D)
True if every value for A.B is the same as every value for C.D
The Any and All qualifiers are only allowed at the start of mathematical expressions since the inclusion of more than one Any or ALL becomes confusing as to the intent.
All is normally only used on the right side for non-equality constraints because All can only be satisfied if every value is the same. Using Max and Min methods for math operations are preferable to Any and All qualifiers as they are
only allowed at the start. For example, (Max(obj->A.B) * Min(obj->C.D)) / (Max(obj->E.F) > 1000.
String comparison
String comparison
Instead of using the comparison operators, the following syntax can be used to test the existence of one sub string in another.
INST(MainString,SubString)
Logical expressions that evaluate to a string can be used for either string.
INSTR(Obj.Interfaces,'ISPFTransmittal')
Between
Used to evaluate whether an expression is found between two values.
List objects
Listing object interfaces
This syntax returns a list in a comma separated string of the interface names instantiated by an object. For example:
Obj.Interfaces
Listing objects in a list
You use IN in a condition to perform the validation of all elements in a list and return those that meet the condition as True. You can test that the string values are in a list by using the syntax, Obj->StringTest IN ('String1','String2','String3').
For example, the following syntax checks for selected file types in a list.
Obj->SPFFileFileType_12.UID IN ('FT_DocFile','FT_ImageFile','FT_SDxFile')
Count the number of objects
Use the following syntax to return the number of specified objects.
Count(Obj->RelDef_12)
You can use the same count syntax to check if an object has only one owner.
Count(Obj->SPFItemOwner_12) = 1
And use the count syntax to check the number of referenced files.
IsReferenceFile = Count(obj->SPFReferenceFile_21) <> 0
Or check that a document has files
HasFiles = Count(Obj->SPFFileComposition_21) > 0
Function condition expressions
The following functions can be used when creating condition expressions:
Standard functions
The following are standard functions:
Function Name
Description
Example
ABS
Specifies the absolute value of a number.
ABS(Obj.Value1) > 3
AVG
Specifies the average value for a returned set of values.
AVG(Obj.Value1, Obj.Value2) < 5
COUNT
Counts the number of objects in a set.
COUNT(Obj->TagArea) > 0
INSTR
Searches for a string in the values returned for the attribute.
INSTR(Obj.Name, "Admin User") > 0
INSTRB
Searches for a string in the values returned for the attribute. Returns TRUE if
the substring exists in the string and FALSE if it does not.
INSTRB(Obj.Name, "Admin User")
ISSET
Tests whether the property value has been defined or set. Returns an integer
value (1 if the property has been set).
ISSET(Obj.Name)>0
ISSETB
Tests whether the property value has been defined or set. Returns TRUE if
the value has been set and FALSE if it has not.
ISSETB(Obj.Name)
LEFT
Specifies characters on the left side of a string.
LEFT(Obj.Name) = "ABC"
LEN
Counts the number of characters in a string.
LEN(Obj.Name) > 5
LIKE
Returns TRUE if the object contains the value or the value is a substring of
the object.
Env.USERORGANIZATIONNAMEWITHDELIMITER
LIKE(Obj.SPFCollaboratingOrganizations)
LOWER
Converts the contents of the returned string to lowercase characters, and
compares the results to the specified string.
LOWER(Obj.Name) = "graph"
MAX
Specifies the highest of the returned values.
MAX(Obj.Value1, Obj.Value2) > 5
MID
Returns all characters from a given position in a string to the end of the
string.
MID("Intergraph", 6) would return a value of "graph".
MIN
Specifies a minimum value for returned values.
MIN(Obj.Value1, Obj.Value2) < 3
RIGHT
Specifies characters on the right side of a string.
RIGHT(Obj.Name) = "XYZ"
ROUND
Rounds the returned number to the nearest integer.
ROUND(Obj.Value1) >= 3
SUM
Evaluates the sum of all returned values.
SUM(Obj.Value1, Obj.Value2) < 5
TRUNC
Specifies the integer portion of a number.
TRUNC(Obj.Value1) = 3
UPPER
Converts the contents of the returned string to uppercase characters, and
compares the results to the specified string.
UPPER(Obj.Name) = "ADMIN"
Date and time functions
The following are date and time functions:
Function Name
Description
Example
DateAdd
Adds a time (in seconds) to a date and returns a new date.
You can create a condition to display the Update method
on the object for 1800 seconds after the object is created.
For example:
Now<DateAdd(Obj.CreationDate,1800)
DateDiff
Returns the difference between two dates in seconds.
You can create a condition to display the Update method
on the object after a specific duration of 1200 seconds
after the object is created. For example:
DateDiff(Now, Obj.CreationDate) > 1
Year
Returns the year for a date (evaluated using regional settings).
If the date and time is 12.01pm on March 18, 2024, a
functions returns Year = 2024
Year_GMT
Returns the year for a date based on Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).
Year_GMT = 2020
Month
Returns the month (between 1 and 12) using regional settings.
Month = 3
Month_GMT
Returns the month based on the international time.
Month_GMT = 3
Day
Returns the day (between 1 and 31) using regional settings.
Day = 18
Day_GMT
Returns the day based on Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).
Day_GMT = 18
Hour
Returns the hour (between 0 and 23) using regional settings.
Hour = 13
Hour_GMT
Returns the hour based on Greenwich Mean Time (GMT)..
Hour_GMT = 19
Minute
Returns the minute (between 0 and 59) using regional settings.
Minute = 54
Minute_GMT
Returns the minute based on Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).
Minute_GMT = 54
Second
Returns the second.
Second = 12
Weekday
Returns the weekday (Sunday = 1, Saturday = 7) using regional settings.
Weekday = 4 (Wednesday = 4)
UoM functions
You can use a UoM function to insert a unit of measure value into a function. The UoM function has two string arguments (the second string value can come from a user variable). The first argument identifies the unit of measure list, and
the second argument is the actual UoM value. For example:
This condition satisfies that each object has a length less the .04 m (40 cm).
UoM('LengthUoM','40 cm') > obj.length
This condition satisfies that each object whose length*width is greater than .2 m^2 (the SI UoM).
Obj.length*obj.width > UoM(AreaUoM,'200 cm^2')
 When conditions involve units of measure, the unit of measure values are converted to SI values for purposes of analyzing the condition.
Condition constants
The following constants are supported when creating conditions.
Control character constants
Control characters are special characters that are used when there is no way to enter them into a constraint other than by using the constant. For example, no mechanism exists to add a carriage return to a constraint without using the CR
control character.
Control Character
Description
Back
Backspace
CR
Carriage return
CRLF
Carriage return followed by line feed
FormFeed
Form feed
LF
Line feed
NewLine
New line
NullChar
Null character
Tab
Tab character
VerticalTab
Vertical tab character
 Control characters are treated as strings within the conditions language and can therefore be used wherever a string is valid.
Days of the week constants
The following system constants are supported.
CurDir - The current active system directory. This value is treated as a string.
Now - The system date and time. This value is determined when the condition is evaluated, not at the time it is defined. This value is treated as a date/time value.
System constants
The following system constants are supported.
CurDir - The current active system directory. This value is treated as a string.
Now - The system date and time. This value is determined when the condition is evaluated, not at the time it is defined. This value is treated as a date/time value.
UoM constants
The following system constant is supported.
PI - The mathematical constant Pi.
 UoM constants are treated as SI values.
Environment variable support
It is also possible to access the environment object that contains properties about the current user's session, such as the user name, access groups, and so on.
Environment object properties
The following environment object properties can be used with conditions:
Object Property
Description
ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERIN CREATECONFIG
This returns a list of access groups associated with the current create configuration/user (comma
separated string).
ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERIN QUERYCONFIG
This returns a list of access groups associated with the current query configuration/user (comma
separated string).
CALLEDFROM
As above.
CALLEDFROMDIALOG
Returns WebClient based on a session parameter.
CLASSDEFOBJ
This returns the ClassDef of an object. For example: obj.CLASSDEFOBJ.Name.
CONFIG1, CONFIG2, CONFIG3,
Name of the create configuration at the specified level.
CONFIGCREATE
Current create configuration string.
CONFIGLEVEL
Current create configuration level, such as 0, 1, 2, and so forth.
CREATECONFIGLEVEL1OBJ, CREATECONFIGLEVEL 2OBJ,
Exposes the create configuration object at the specified level to support access to additional
properties on this object.
CREATECONFIGOBJ
Exposes the current create configuration object to support access to additional properties on this
object. For example: ENV.CREATECONFIGOBJ.Property.
CREATECONFIGPARENTOBJ
Exposes the configuration above the current create configuration object to support access to
additional properties on this object. This provides access to the plant properties from the project
configuration. For example: ENV.CREATECONFIGPARENTOBJ.Property.
CREATECONFIGSTRING
Current create configuration string.
CREATECONFIGUID
Current create configuration UID.
CURRCONFIGLEVELNAME
Same as QUERYCONFIGSTRINGS.
CURRENTDATE
The current date format 2024/01/30.
CURRENTTIME
The current time format 2024/01/30-10:38:12:991.
CURRENTYMD
The current date in year/month/day format. For example: ENV.CURRENTYMD >=
obj.SPFStepTargetDate.
EFFECTIVITYDATE
The effective date is set to point in time you want to view data.
HOSTNAME
Current HxGN SDx2 server machine name.
OWNINGGROUPSFORCREATE CONFIG
All valid owning groups at this configuration level.
OWNINGGROUPSFORQUERY CONFIG
All valid owning groups at this configuration level.
OWNINGGROUPSFORUSERIN CREATECONFIG
All valid owning groups for this user at the current create configuration level.
OWNINGGROUPSFORUSERIN QUERYCONFIG
All valid owning groups for this user at the current query configuration level.
QUERYCONFIGLEVEL
Current query configuration level 0,1,2, and so forth.
QUERYCONFIGUIDS
Comma separated list of all current query configuration UIDs.
QUERYCONFIGSTRINGS
Comma separated list of all current query configuration names.
QUERYCONFIG1, QUERYCONFIG2,
Name of the query configuration at the specified level.
USER
Exposes the currently logged in ISPFLoginUser object to support access to additional properties on
this object.
USERDEFAULTCREATECONFIG
Current user default create configuration.
USERNAME
Current user's login user name.
USERORGANIZATIONNAME
Current user company name.
USERSACTUALNAME
Name of the current user.
USERUSERGROUPS
Comma separated list of user groups that the current user belongs to.
USERUSERTYPE
Returns Full, E-mail, or Paper based on whether the current user class has the active domain of
ISPFLoginUser, ISPFEmailUser, or ISPFPaperUser.
Condition validation
All conditions are validated that they will work correctly when any new create or update action is submitted to the application server. This helps improve performance of the system as conditions do not need to checked by the server that
they work just before being run by a method.
Common condition errors
The following are examples of common condition errors and show the correction that has been applied so they can be used. The specific correction for each is shown in bold type.
Missing a valid end
Incorrect
Not INSTR(Obj->EDG_StepWorkflowItem.Interfaces, 'ISPFTransmittal')
Correct
Not INSTR(Obj->EDG_StepWorkflowItem.Interfaces, 'ISPFTransmittal')>0
Missing Any and closing parentheses
Incorrect
(Obj->SPFFileFileType_12.UID IN ('FT_dwg','FT_dgn','FT_dxf','FT_pdf')
Correct
Any(Obj->SPFFileFileType_12.UID) IN ('FT_dwg','FT_dgn','FT_dxf','FT_pdf')
Missing relationship definition direction
Incorrect
Env.CREATECONFIGOBJ->SPFConfigurationConfigurationStatus.Name = 'Active'
Correct
Env.CREATECONFIGOBJ->SPFConfigurationConfigurationStatus_12.Name = 'Active'
Missing Any, parenthesis, and valid end
Incorrect
INSTR(obj->Realizes_12.Name, ''ISPFSubscribableItem'')
Correct
INSTR(ANY(obj->Realizes_12.Name), ''ISPFSubscribableItem'') > 0
Missing Any and parenthesis
Incorrect
Obj->EDG_LatestVersion.SPFIsDocVersionCheckedOut = False and Obj->EDG_LatestVersion.SPFIsDocVersionSuperseded = False and Obj.SPFRevState = 'WORKING' and (Env.CREATECONFIGUID = Obj.Config or len(Obj.Config) =
0)
Correct
Any(Obj->EDG_LatestVersion.SPFIsDocVersionCheckedOut) = False and Any(Obj->EDG_LatestVersion.SPFIsDocVersionSuperseded) = False and Obj.SPFRevState = 'WORKING' and (Env.CREATECONFIGUID = Obj.Config or
len(Obj.Config) = 0)
Configure methods
Methods are used to create custom functionality, which can be exposed to your users as actions and commands. The method functionality created can be displayed as a function tile in a feature set, as an action command on the Actions
menu, or as an icon on the shortcut ribbon.
The behavior of each method you create or update is driven by the client API that you select as you configure the method. Each client API represents a specific piece of code that can be invoked to perform the required functionality, such
as attaching an object a workflow. The method arguments defined on the API are dynamic and set on a tab along with some additional properties.
You create and update methods using the delivered Create Method and Update Method functionality, which displays the various configuration items and properties that you configure to set up a new method. The properties are defined by
the client API selected.
 You can select an Integration Data Filter in each method form to restrict access to methods based on the selected design component. This ensures only the relevant methods are visible. For more information, see Further ways to
restrict access to methods.
Create a method
 Before creating a new method, always check to see if a suitable method already exists in the system with the applicable properties and user access requirements.
You create methods using the Create Method wizard, which displays the method details in a ribbon of tabs that you individually use to configure and add the relevant details for the execution of the new method, such as adding properties,
interfaces, and access groups.
In the System Administration feature set, select the Create Method function tile to go to the Create Method wizard.
 You can select PREVIOUS to go back to review a previous tab or just select a tab to review and amend the properties as required.
Example create method
The following example shows how you use the Create Method wizard to set up a new method by configuring the objects associated with each tab. Each step in this scenario builds a method named AttachWorkflow, which can be used to
attach objects to a workflow:
1. Add details
Add the method details you require to create a method using the following ribbon of tabs. For example, as shown for the AttachWorkflow method:
1. In the Details tab, enter the details in the Name and Description boxes in the Main details section for the method.
2. In the Display as box, enter how the the method is to be displayed in the GUI and Actions menu.
3. In Method details section, select how you want the method results to be displayed in Web page box, such as in a context panel.
4. In Condition box, select the condition to run against objects of a given class definition to filter them.
5. In the Client API box, select the client API that provides the functionality you want to have behind the method. For example, AttachWorkflow client API.
6. In Related items section, select an Integration Data Filter (IDF) to create a relationship for the method with a design component.
 This ensures that the method is only visible to the end user based on the selected design component.
7. Select Suppress in Web Client if you want to stop the method being used.
8. Type the name of the sub-menu in the Cascade name box if you want the method to appear on a sub-menu on the shortcut menu.
9. Select Suppress Open in New Tab in Web Client if you want to stop the method opening in a new tab.
 There are other optional properties you can set, such as the Shortcut toolbar details. These control how the method is displayed, such as an Icon, Display text, Sequence, or Tooltip.
2. Add properties
Select the properties required for the method that are displayed. In this example, the properties displayed are from the AttachWorkflow client API.
 The list of properties available varies according to the client API selected.
3. Add interfaces
Set up the interface access for the method by selecting the required interface definitions from the list. For example, as shown for the AttachWorkflow method.
Select Filter 
 to refine the list to a specific set of interfaces. For example, by entering flow as the criteria.
Select Show selected items 
 so only the selected interface definitions are displayed, as shown.
4. Add feature sets
Select the feature sets from the available list to set up the access to a set in which you want the AttachWorkflow method to be displayed.
Use Filter Items 
 to refine the list to items you require.
Select Show selected items 
 to display only the selected items.
5. Add access control
Set up the access groups from the available list that will have access to the method.
Enter the criteria to refine the list of access groups to a defined set, and select Filter 
 as displayed by entering FDW.
Select Show selected items 
 to only display the access groups that have been selected, as displayed for the AttachWorkflow method.
6. Summary
The Summary displays all the selected configuration items you have selected for the method, as shown for the AttachWorkflow method.
 You can go back and select each tab to review the properties selected for the method, and amend the selections as required.
 You can test the method and edit it until you get to the exact result you require.
The arguments and properties you can update on the method are dynamic and can vary for each method as they are based on the specific client API you first select in the Add Details tab.
For more information on the updating a method, see Update a method.
Configure Revise method to inherit existing document relationships to the new document revision
Whenever revising a document, to automatically inherit and assign existing document relationships to its revision, you are required to update the RevisionRevise method parameter Relationship UID(s) to copy with the UID(s) of the
relationship definitions of the document where each value should be comma-separated. For example, the following parameter input FDWDocRevAsset,FDWDocFDWTag tells the software to copy and assign the existing document to asset
and document to tag relationships to the new document revision.
 The RevisionRevise method is assigned to the Revise client API. For more details about its parameters, see Revise client API.
Update a method
You can update an existing method using the Update Method action, which allows you to modify and edit the saved method properties. The update wizard displays the various configuration items in the same ribbon of tabs previously
displayed when the method was created.
You can select each of the Update Method tabs to edit and modify the various selected properties in exactly the same way as used to create the method, such as in setting up user access with access groups in Access Control.
1. Go to a method in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > Update Method.
2. Update the details in each tab.
You can select PREVIOUS to go back to review a previous tab or simply directly go to a tab to review and amend the properties.
You can see how to use the tabs in the Update Method wizard to edit details and properties by following the example scenario used to create a method. For more information, see Create a method.
 The arguments and properties you can update on the method are dynamic and can vary for each method as they are based on the specific client API you selected in the Add Details tab.
Manage the access groups for a method
You can manage the access groups that are related to a method.
1. Search for the required method, and then select Actions 
 > Manage Access Groups.
 To restrict method access based on the ownership of an object, select the owning groups to which you want to restrict access to in the Filter By Owner Or Owning Group. For more information, see Manage the access groups
for a role.
2. Set Restrict When In Workflow to True if you want to restrict method access to only workflow action recipients if the object is in a workflow.
 When set to True, and the object is in a workflow, the method is only available to users that are recipients of an action for the current workflow step.
3. Select the condition that must be satisfied before the method can be run if required. This condition must be satisfied when users in that access group try to run the method.
You can also use the Manage Methods action directly from the method.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Manage the interface definitions for a method
You can manage the interface definitions that are related to a method. For example:
1. Search for the method, and select Actions 
 > Manage Interface Definitions.
2. In the Manage Interface Definitions dialog, add, remove, or configure the interface definition for the method, as required.
Restrict access to methods in an access group
The access granted by any access group can be restricted to a set of owning groups by identifying them on the access group relationship. This enables the same access group to be used in different roles on data identified by their owning
group. In this way, you can restrict access to methods based on the ownership.
You can restrict method access based on the ownership of an object so that the user must be either the owner of the object or related to the owning group found on the access group. When an owning group is set on a role, all the method
functionality granted by that access group can be restricted to only the objects within that owning group. Using this approach, the role of a user can grant them permission to update objects that belong to one owning group, but not update
the objects that belong to other owning groups.
 To restrict access to methods using owning groups, see Restrict access to methods using owning groups.
At least one domain must be configured on the role or access group relationships; otherwise, the user cannot access any domain information.
For more information on managing owning groups, see Configure owning groups.
For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Further ways to restrict access to methods
The following describes what options can be used for access restrictions and how to configure them.
Restricting method access by design component
You can restrict access to methods based on the Integration Data Filter (IDF) selected for the method to create a relationship with an integration design component. This ensures that the method is only visible to the end user based on their
selected IDF when set in their scopes selection.
Restricting update access to configuration-controlled objects
A client API configured to update will not expose its actions methods on configuration-controlled objects unless the user sets the create configuration scope to the existing configuration of the object. For example, a document created in a
project cannot be updated unless the create configuration scope is set to that project.
Restricting method access by conditions
A method can be related to a condition that must be satisfied before the method can run. A condition can be configured on the method, and this condition must be satisfied when users in the access group try to run the method. For
example, the document update method may be available only to engineers on CURRENT documents, but an administrator may be condition that allows them to revise a SUPERSEDED document as well.
Restricting method access by object ownership
You can restrict method access based on the ownership of an object so that the user must be either the owner of the object or related to the owning group found on the access group. To do this, when the access group is related to a
method, set the option required in the Filter by Owner Or Owning Group column.
When an owning group is set on a role, all the functionality granted by that access group can be restricted to only the objects within that owning group. The methods on the access group are not automatically restricted in this way, the Filter
by Owner or Owning Group property needs to also be set on the method related access group.
When a method action menu is evaluated, the system first checks if the owning group filter flag is set on the method related access group. Then if set, it also checks if the role or access group relationship has an owning group. If the
selected object has an owning group, it must be one of those on the role – access group relationship for the method to be available to the user.
Restricting method access to object workflow recipients
You can restrict method access to only workflow action recipients if the object is in workflow. To do this, when the access group is related to a method, set the Restrict to workflow recipients property.
When set, this restricts access to the method when the object is in a workflow. When the object is in a workflow, the method is only available to users that are recipients of an action for the current workflow step. Information-only recipients
do not get the method even if they are recipients of the current step.
Display a method icon on the Actions menu
You can give prominence to method functionality using shortcut icons displayed when you open the Actions menu or displayed in a ribbon when you hover over any point of an object row in a results grid.
1. In HxGN SDx2, find the method, and select Update.
2. On the method update form, locate the Shortcut toolbar details section.
3. In the Shortcut icon box, enter the name of the icon to use as the method shortcut icon.
 Search for the name of the icon to use in System Administration > Icons. Do not use the filename.
4. In the Shortcut Display Text box, enter the text to be displayed.
5. In the Shortcut Sequence box, enter the number for the icon to appear in a sequence in the menu ordered from low to high. If the lowest number is one, it is the first icon to be displayed in the menu.
6. In the Shortcut Tooltip box, enter the text for the tooltip.
For more information on using action shortcuts, see Use the Actions menu to manage objects.
HxGN SDx2 uses an azure storage account that has content containers to store the icons for each tenant.
For more information on configuring custom UI icons for use with methods, see Configure a UI icon.
To display method UI icons in a feature set, see Create a feature set.
Configure forms
Each form can be configured to contain various components in a set structure defined for a specific type of function or purpose. Forms can be created, updated, copied, and reused for the different purposes. The individual component
sections of the form can be configured to display the various display item properties and options as required.
How are the components displayed?
Each form is made up of the following components.
1 - The form purpose
2 - Sections applicable to the form
3 - Display Items applicable to the section
All forms in HxGN SDx2 are composed of three main components:
Form
Each form acts as the framework to display the components you can configure for specific types of functionality or purpose, such as an object create from. You can configure how each form is constructed based on the class definition
selected for the form.
Sections
Sections contain display item properties or options to be used to build the structure of the form. Each section is created with a specific form purpose, which controls on which type of form it can be used and how it is structured. After
you create a form, you add sections to build the form to display the required property data or options.
Display items
Display items are used to present property or relationship data as boxes, options, or pick lists on a section. After you create a display item, you can add it to a section so that it can be included on forms that require the same
properties or data to be displayed. You can also control the display of items in combo boxes and the format of the display item type using a picture mask.
Existing sections and display items are stored in the database and can be re-used and applied to multiple forms that require the same display of property options or relationship data.
Any changes made to a section or a display item are automatically updated on every form in which they have been included.
Form generation process
The basic form generation process is as follows:
1. Create a form from a class definition. See Create a form.
2. Create sections for the form based on the form purpose. See Create a section.
3. Create display items for the section from the required property or relationship definition properties. See Create a display item.
4. Use Manage Display Items to relate display items to a section. See Manage display items in a section.
5. Use Manage Sections to relate sections to a form. See Manage sections in a form.
An easy way to understand how to create a form is to find an existing form similar to how you want your form to be constructed. When you find a form that matches, expand its sections and relate the required sections to your new form.
You can rearrange the order of each section or their display items to control the order in which they are displayed in your form.
For more information on controlling related sections or display items, see Manage related items.
You can control the access to sections and display items in a form by relating them to access groups. For more information, see Manage section access groups.
You can configure a section so it is only visible in a form in a selected configuration. For more information, see Configuration control of sections.
Form generation rules
The following basic rules apply to each form generated in the system:
A common Name, Description, and Display As details section is used on all forms
Additional display items can be added to sections to display the configuration in which a form is created and the the object classifications are located. For more information, see Configuration control of display items.
Related relationship definition items on linked classifications are included, but abstract relationships are not included.
Configuring forms
You can configure a form in the following ways:
You can create a new form, see Create a form.
You can update an existing form and edit its properties, see Update a form.
You can copy an existing form that matches your requirements and save it as a new form, see Copy a form.
To help improve your overall experience with the form, keep the number of sections and their display items to a minimum. When updating or customizing an existing form, evaluate if any sections or display items can be removed.
Consider the amount of data added into the form using lists or combo boxes. Fewer options help to reduce the time for the form to open.
You can create alternative forms from the same class definition for different object classifications. For more information, see Alternative forms.
You can select a Design Component to create a relationship for the form to an integrated design component. This ensures the form is only visible based on the selected design component.
Create a form
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Create Form.
2. In the Main Details section, type the name and description for the form.
3. In the Form details section, search for a class definition to be used as the basis for the form in the Class definitions box. to be related as the Integration Data Filters.
4. In Related Items section, you can search for and select the Design Components to relate to the form that are used as Integration Data Filters. This ensures that the form is only visible in an integrated environment based on the
selected design component. For more information, see Relate design components to forms.
You cannot overwrite an existing form with the same name. Choose a form name that has not been used before.
When you create the form and select a class definition, sections and their display items are not automatically added to the form and must be related to the form. For more information on managing sections, see Manage sections in a
form.
You can select the class definition and any related classifications by updating the form. For more information, see Update a form.
Update a form
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Forms.
2. Choose the form, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In MAIN DETAILS section, update the options in the Form details section, and select UPDATE.
4. What are the update form detail options?
The following table lists the options available for updating a form.
Property option
Description
Type of access control
The type of access control to use when updating a form, either Access Group or Interface. The
Type of access control must be set to Access Group so that the access groups are honored.
Hide ENS Multi Item Create
Option to override the inclusion of the ENS configured sequence Start at number and the
Number to create options to ensure users cannot create multiple objects or override the start
sequence number.
Hide Apply Button on Create forms in Web Client
Option when you want to suppress the Apply button when form is viewed in the Form purpose
options.
Class definitions
The class definition to be used as the basis for the form.
Classifications
Classifications are based on the selected parent class definition. For more information, see Create
a relationship between objects.
5. In Related Items section, you can search for and select the Design Components to relate to the form that are used as Integration Data Filters. This ensures that the form is only visible in an integrated environment based on the
selected design component. For more information, see Relate design components to forms.
Copy a form
Copying a form allows you to create a new form defined with the same class definition. However, the original form sections and display items are not copied. You must add and update the copied form with the required form sections and
display items.
1. Choose the form to be copied, and select Actions 
 > Copy Form.
2. In the Copy Form dialog, enter a new name and description for the form, and select COPY.
 For more information on adding sections and display items, see Manage sections in a form and Manage display items in a section.
Control ENS display in create forms
An Engineering Numbering System (ENS) allows project-specific identification formats to be used when creating new objects. You can control the display of ENS in object create forms using Relationship Builder by selecting or
terminating the relationship between the object class definition and the configured ENS definition.
1. Select Data Model Administration > Class Definitions.
2. Select the class definition, and click Actions 
 > Relationship Builder.
3. In the Relationship Items section, search for the ENS definition and relate it to the class definition to display ENS in the object create form. You can also terminate an existing relationship by clearing the ENS definition to stop the
display of ENS in the object create form.
 For information on how to use ENS in your system, see Using ENS.
Relate design components to forms
As you create or update any delivered or custom form, such as for methods or entry points, you can select an integrated design component in the Main Details tab for the form to create a relationship with a design component. By selecting
the design component, you can ensure that the form is only visible based on the selected design component that users set in their scopes.
 For more information on setting the Integration Data Filter in scopes, see Working with scopes.
Alternative forms
You can create alternative forms for different object classifications related to the same parent class definition. These forms can be used when you want to display properties specific to each classification for that class definition. For
example, the following image uses the Tag class definition that has two separate classifications Pump and Centrifugal Pump:
 This example shows the different relationship definitions used to navigate from the class definition to the specific classification objects in order to display the correct properties for each type of pump.
Suppress the Apply button on forms
You can suppress the display of the APPLY button on a form to stop any uncontrolled changes.
1. Search for the form, and select Actions 
 > Update.
2. Select the Hide Apply button on Create forms in Web Client option.
Forms for complex objects
Documents are complex objects made up of a master, revision, and version. Forms are always created for the lowest complex object. In this case, that is a document version.
Form sections can be created for the objects at each level of the complex object. For documents, each version being identified by $3, the revision by $2, and the master by $1.
The Create and Query methods are written with respect to the document version and retrieve the form related to the document version.
When the update command is run on one level of the complex object, the sections for the levels above are displayed as well. For example, update of a document revision displays the document master sections and the document
revision sections.
Hide the ENS Multi Item Create option
When you create or update an object, you can use the Number to create and Start at number options on the form. These options allow you to set the number of ENS objects to create and the starting number for the ENS sequence.
However, you can override these settings by selecting the Hide ENS Multi Item Create option on the object update form.
For example, if an ENS system has 8 numbered objects and you set the Number to create to 6 and Start at number to 12, six new objects are created numbered from 12 to 17. But, if you select the Hide ENS Multi Item Create option,
the six new objects will be numbered using the next available numbers in the ENS sequence, so would be numbered 9 to 14.
 If the option is not available on the update form, relate the SPFHideENSCreateOptions property to the object's class definition so that the option appears on the update form.
Configure sections
Sections are used in forms to display a collection of properties or relationships found in display items that contain defined lists or options. Having data displayed in sections makes it easier to group related properties for display in a form.
This allows you to make partial updates to the data in sections that already exist in a form.
Each section can be related to multiple different forms that requires the same display of properties, depending on the selected form purpose. You can:
Create sections based on the form purpose that is defined by the selected class definition for the form. You can then relate display items to a section for the selected form purpose. For more information, see Form purpose options.
Search for any created or existing sections and relate them to the required form using Manage Related Items. For more information, see Create a section, Update a section, and Manage sections in a form.
Any changes made to a section and its the properties are automatically updated on all forms on which the section appears.
A number of sections are delivered with the software based on standard class definitions, interfaces, and form purposes.
Sections can be used on forms for different class definitions based on the form purpose.
If you update an existing section, it does not delete its relationships to any forms in which it is included. It just updates the section details or its display items.
Create a section
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Create Section.
2. In MAIN DETAILS section, type the name and description for the section.
3. In the Display As box, enter the section name as it should appear on the form.
4. In Section details, select the options and values to the various properties as applicable, including the Form purpose.
What are the section property options?
The following table lists the property options available for each section.
Property option
Description
Is section visible
Option to make the section visible on the form.
Lower precedence section
Option to select a section that has a lower order of precedence for display of the section when
configured for different roles.
Property-level access control
Options to override the display items setting as:
PropertyLevelAccessControl - All display items in the section are made as read-only regardless of
what is configured on the display item.
Read only - All display items in the section are read-only regardless of their individual settings.
Read/Write - All display items in the section are editable regardless of their individual settings.
Form purposes
The type of form on which the section is to be based. For more information, see Form purpose
options.
Dynamic section driven from column set
Option to make the section dynamic and appear as a tab that is driven from the column set in the
form.
Collapse behavior
Options to determine whether the form displays the section in a collapsed or expanded mode. The
options are:
Auto And Initially Collapsed
Auto And Initially Expanded
Initially Collapsed
Initially Expanded (Default)
Indicate what level the section is in the complex structure.
Determines which object within a complex object you set the properties on.
If specified section will be presented in a tab on the form using the name or #nnnn resource string.
String in which you can enter a name for the tab, such as Collaboration.
Is Dynamic section?
Option to make the section dynamic and appear as a tab in a form. For more information, see
Defining dynamic sections.
 Form sections given a form purpose will display property options configured specifically for that form purpose. For more information, see Form purpose options.
Form purpose options
The form purpose defines the type of form for which the section has been created. Sections can be associated with more than one form purpose, so they can appear on different forms or versions of the same form.
Purpose
Description
Activate
A section to activate a reserved document. This option is different from create because some properties can be set to read-only during
activation. An example of a read-only property is Name.
Attach File
Section to be displayed to attach files on Attach forms.
Copy
Section to be displayed on Copy forms.
Create
Section to be displayed on Create and Copy forms.
Get Info
Section to be displayed on Properties or Details dialogs in forms. All display items are read-only so the same section can be used for
Update and Get Info forms.
HisRev
Section to be displayed on History Revision forms.
Query
Section to be displayed on Query forms.
Revise
Section to be displayed on Revise forms.
Sign Off
Section to be displayed only on Sign Off forms.
Update
Section to be displayed only on Update forms.
Upload
Section to be displayed on Upload forms.
 For more information on the type of form the section will be used in, see Configuring forms.
Update a section
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Sections.
2. Choose a section, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the MAIN DETAILS section, edit the section properties, and select UPDATE.
What are the section property options?
The following table lists the property options available for each section.
Property option
Description
Is section visible
Option to make the section visible on the form.
Lower precedence section
Option to select a section that has a lower order of precedence for display of the section when
configured for different roles.
Property-level access control
Options to override the display items setting as:
PropertyLevelAccessControl - All display items in the section are made as read-only regardless
of what is configured on the display item.
Read only - All display items in the section are read-only regardless of their individual settings.
Read/Write - All display items in the section are editable regardless of their individual settings.
Form purposes
The type of form on which the section is to be based. For more information, see Form purpose
options.
Dynamic section driven from column set
Option to make the section dynamic and appear as a tab that is driven from the column set in the
form.
Collapse behavior
Options to determine whether the form displays the section in a collapsed or expanded mode. The
options are:
Auto And Initially Collapsed
Auto And Initially Expanded
Initially Collapsed
Initially Expanded (Default)
Indicate what level the section is in the complex structure.
Determines which object within a complex object you set the properties on.
If specified section will be presented in a tab on the form using the name or #nnnn resource string.
String in which you can enter a name for the tab, such as Collaboration.
Is Dynamic section?
Option to make the section dynamic and appear as a tab in a form. For more information, see
Defining dynamic sections.
Any changes made to a section are automatically updated on all forms in which it is included.
You can also manage sections using the other column options, such as controlling visibility.
You can allow dynamic section access to the form using the Allow Dynamic Section content option.
Manage sections in a form
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Forms.
2. Choose a form, and select Actions 
 > Manage Sections.
3. In the Manage Sections dialog, search for sections to add to the form and add, remove, or rearrange the order of sections as applicable.
 When updating or customizing an existing form, evaluate if any sections can be removed to reduce the forms length. Also consider the amount of data added to the form sections that use lists or combo boxes that increase the time
for the form to open.
You can make each section dynamic so they display as tabs to reduce the forms length. For more information, see Defining dynamic sections.
For more information on managing display items in a section, see Manage display items in a section.
Manage section access groups
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Sections.
2. Choose a section, and select Actions 
 > Manage Access Groups.
3. In the Manage Access Groups dialog, search for the access groups to relate to the section and add, update, or remove access groups as applicable.
Access groups are only honored if Type of access control is set to Access Group. For more information, see Update a form.
If you want the section to be read-only for a specific access group, change the Readonly? option to True in Manage Access Groups.
The section is hidden from access groups that are not related to the section.
The section is visible to all users when no access groups are configured for the section.
Any change to the access groups for a section, will change who can view the section on each form in which it appears.
Hide sections
You can hide a section from display in a form so the display items are not visible. There are two ways to hide a section:
1. Choose a section, and select Actions 
 > Update.
2. In Section details, uncheck the Is section visible option.
or
1. Choose a form, and select Actions 
 > Manage Sections.
2. In the Manage Sections dialog, change the Visible column item property to False.
 For more information, see Manage sections in a form.
Defining dynamic sections
In large complex forms, there can be multiple sections and their display items configured for display in the form. Scrolling through a long list of sections and their display items to find a specific property or option can be time consuming.
However, you can define sections to be dynamic, so that they are displayed as a series of tabs across the top of the form. The tabs support all types of forms, such as update, query, details, and so on.
The section tabs are dynamically generated from the column items configured for the classified object type. Grouping the display items in dynamic section tabs can help reduce the form length, and arrange the display items in logically
grouped lists that can be easily managed.
Finding a display item this way is much easier than searching through a long list of sections as you can just select the section tab at the top to display the related display item properties.
 A form can only have one dynamic section for any given form purpose, unless the form has different complex object levels.
Create a dynamic section
You can define a form so that the sections display as tabs across the top of the form. The tabs are dynamically generated from the column items configured from the classified object's column set. To create a dynamic section:
1. Create or update a section, and select the Dynamic section driven from column set option.
2. In Dynamic Section Details, select the Is Dynamic section? option.
Hide an attribute column item from display in a dynamic section by choosing a specific column set, selecting Manage Column Items for Edit, and setting the Hide on Dynamic Form option on the column item to True.
You can configure column items to display only in a dynamic section when selected from a specific configuration. You choose the column set, select Manage Column Items, and set the Configs property option to the configuration for
each display item required.
Dynamic sections must be related to a form for the dynamic section to be displayed.
When you select the Dynamic section driven from column set option, the appropriate complex level must also be set.
Display items are dynamically generated in the same order as the column items and sorted by their order value. This order can be rearranged using the Manage Related Items dialog.
Display items are not generated for the column items when:
The property definition, edge definition, or relationship definition of a column item is related to a display item already on the form.
Any property definition for IObject is exposed.
The Dynamic property is set to True.
The edge definition UID and the property definition UID arenot set to Name.
The edge definition column item has multiple relationship definitions in its path definition.
Configure column items in dynamic form sections
You can configure related property attribute column items in a classification column set to display in a dynamic form section.
1. Configure a column set dedicated to a classification, and add the column items you want to display.
2. In Manage Related Items, relate the column set to the classification.
3. Search for the column set, and select Actions 
 > Show Column Items.
4. Choose a column item, and select Actions 
 > Update.
5. In the Dynamic form section box, select the form section required, such as Properties. The column item will now display on the Properties section as a tab.
If a column item is not related to a section tab, it will display on the Main Details tab, along with the default column items, such as Name and Description.
Each column item in a dynamic section tab is displayed in the order defined by the Order Value when you select Manage Column Items for a column set.
You can change the order sequence of the column items using Manage Related Items instead of sequencing many column sets individually. For more information, see Configure column sets.
Configure column items by configuration
You can configure column items to only display in a dynamic form section when selected from a specific configuration. This allows you to control the display of the column item properties in a form based on the selected configuration.
1. Select the object's column set, and select Actions 
 > Manage Column Items.
2. In the Manage Column Items dialog, select the column item Configs cell, and select the configuration from the list.
 For more information on managing related items, see Manage related items.
Dependent pick lists
In a section, you can use the Indicate what level the section is in the complex structure option to create a dependency between related items in complex objects. This allows you to control what is displayed in a pick list.
For example, a tag has a relationship to both a Unit and an Area, but there is also a dependent relationship between the Unit and the Area using the ISPFDisplayItemDependentItem relationship definition. When you pick a unit, the pick
list is restricted to only show those display items that are related to the dependent area. Therefore, if you pick Unit 01, only the items from Area 01A and Area 01B are displayed, and if you pick Unit 02, only the items from Area02A and
Area 02B are displayed.
Configure display items
Display items are configured to present data or options in a section on a form by assigning a property or relationship attribute to a display item. Newly created display items can be related to a new or existing section for inclusion on the
form.
You can create new display items or search for existing display items that you can re-use and relate to a section for a form. The options for each display item vary according to the display item type selected when you first create a display
item
When you select the display item type, the selection determines which additional properties and options are activated. You can add the following display item types to a form:
Interface definition - To do a new instantiation of optional interfaces.
Relationship definition - To display or update relationship definition attributes.
Property definition - To display or update property definition attributes.
Edge definition - To display edge relationship attributes only.
User Rel - To display or update relationship definition attributes and relate them to roles.
Create a display item
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Create Display Item.
2. In the New Display Item page, select the MAIN DETAILS tab, and enter a Name and Description for the display item.
3. In the Display As box, enter the display item name as it should appear on the form.
4. In the Display item details section, add values and select options as required.
What are the display item details options?
Some of the options available on the Create or Update Display Item dialog boxes vary depending on what type of display item you selected, such as an edge definition, relationship definition, interface definition, or property
definition.
The following table shows some of the corresponding options that are displayed and available. Their visibility depends on the Display item type selected and you access permissions:
Property option
Description
Default create value
For example, RESERVED
Default copy value
For example, RESERVED
Default query value
Default custom value
Default revise value
Size of label
The size of the label for the display item.
Size of field
The size of the field for the display item.
Display item type
The type of display item, such as RelDef, EdgeDef, InterfaceDef, PropertyDef, or UserRel. The
options displayed are based on the display item type selected.
What are the display item display types?
The option Display item display type appears when the Display item type selected is RelDef (a
relationship definition) or EdgeDef (an edge definition). This option controls the appearance and
behavior of the display item on the form. The available values include:
RelationshipFilter - This display item type displays the added results as chips, which can be
managed independently.
RelationshipFind - The type of control displayed depends on the cardinality of the
relationship definition.
Cardinality of 1
If the relationship definition has a maximum cardinality of 1, you can only assign one
value. Enter the search criteria, and populate the box with possible values, and then
select one value.
Cardinality of > 1
If the relationship definition has a cardinality of > 1, the list box is displayed without any
values. Enter the search criteria, and populate the box with possible values, and then
select one or more values from the box.
RelationshipFindMultiSelect - This option is the same as RelationshipFind. This option
exists to remain compatible with previous versions of SmartPlant Foundation.
RelationshipMultiQuery - This value is for Query forms. The values are populated
automatically when the form is opened. You can select more than one value from the box.
RelationshipMultiQueryFind - This value is for Query forms, and is similar to
RelationshipMultiQuery. However, the values are not populated automatically when the
form opens, and you must click Find to populate the box with possible values.
RelationshipNameDesc - The type of control displayed depends on the cardinality of the
relationship definition. This is similar to RelationshipMultiQuery, except that both the
names and descriptions of the items are displayed.
Cardinality of 1
The display item on the form appears where you can choose a value by name or
description. The values are populated automatically when the form opens.
Cardinality of > 1
The display item on the form appears as a list of owning groups.
Relationship Maintenance - This display item type creates tabs on the Details dialog boxes
to display relationships.
Relationship Definition - Determines which relationship is displayed on the tab when
configured.
 If you do not enter a value for Display item display type, the type of control
displayed depends on the cardinality of the relationship definition, such as:
Cardinality of 1
The display item is a drop-down list box populated when the form opens.
Cardinality of > 1
The display item is a list box with populated when the form opens.
 When UserRel is selected, you can select specific Roles within the system to control the
display of options and lists.
[Type of definition]
This displays a list of the definitions you can select based on the display item type. For example,
Issue State if the display item type is PropertyDef.
 You can enter a name directly in the box to search for a specific definition or search for all
definitions that contain the value entered. To see the total number of definitions applicable, enter
*** in the box.
Help text
The text to appear when the cursor hovers over the display item.
Dependent display items
This displays a list of objects you can select that have a dependency on the display item type to
control what is actually displayed.
What are dependent related items?
An object can have a relationship to two different objects and these two objects can also have a
relationship between them. For example, an external transmittal may have a relationship to a
company and to a controlling recipient. The recipient can also have a relationship to the company,
such that the recipient selected must belong to (and is therefore dependent on) the selected
company in order to review the external transmittal. The relationship creates a dependency
between related items in order to control what is displayed.
Property picture
A format for the display item data. Transforms the value to uppercase and matches the value for
any defined picture mask type.
What are picture mask types?
Picture mask types are formats for display item data. The picture property on a display item is
used for the masking in all cases, except for those properties that are date formats. For more
information, see Picture mask types.
Default unit of measure
The default unit of measure to be used in the form
Display units of measure
Option to display the selected unit of measure in the form.
Override display for combos
Determines what information is displayed in the drop-down list for the display item. The options
available are Description, Name, and NameAndDescription.
Roles
The roles that have access to the display item type being created.
Field length for input
The length of the input field in characters.
 If the display item type is set to RelDef or UserRel then the SPFRelDefDirection option is displayed. This indicates the direction that the relationship definition will be navigated: 12 (from end 1 to end 2) or 21 (from end 2 to
end 1).
 The options available on the create display item dialog boxes vary based on what type of display item you are creating or updating, such as for an edge definition, relationship definition, interface definition, or property definition.
Update a display item
1. Go to System Administration feature set, and select Display Items.
2. Search for display item, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the MAIN DETAILS tab, edit the details and options as required, and select UPDATE.
What are the display item details options?
Some of the options available on the Create or Update Display Item dialog boxes vary depending on what type of display item you selected, such as an edge definition, relationship definition, interface definition, or property
definition.
The following table shows some of the corresponding options that are displayed and available. Their visibility depends on the Display item type selected and you access permissions:
Property option
Description
Default create value
For example, RESERVED
Default copy value
For example, RESERVED
Default query value
Default custom value
Default revise value
Size of label
The size of the label for the display item.
Size of field
The size of the field for the display item.
Display item type
The type of display item, such as RelDef, EdgeDef, InterfaceDef, PropertyDef, or UserRel. The
options displayed are based on the display item type selected.

The option Display item display type appears when the Display item type selected is RelDef (a

Cardinality of > 1

 When UserRel is selected, you can select specific Roles within the system to control the
display of options and lists.
[Type of definition]
This displays a list of the definitions you can select based on the display item type. For example,
Issue State if the display item type is PropertyDef.
 You can enter a name directly in the box to search for a specific definition or search for all
definitions that contain the value entered. To see the total number of definitions applicable, enter
*** in the box.
Help text
The text to appear when the cursor hovers over the display item.
Dependent display items
This displays a list of objects you can select that have a dependency on the display item type to
control what is actually displayed.
What are dependent related items?
An object can have a relationship to two different objects and these two objects can also have a
relationship between them. For example, an external transmittal may have a relationship to a
company and to a controlling recipient. The recipient can also have a relationship to the company,
such that the recipient selected must belong to (and is therefore dependent on) the selected
company in order to review the external transmittal. The relationship creates a dependency
between related items in order to control what is displayed.
Property picture
A format for the display item data. Transforms the value to uppercase and matches the value for
any defined picture mask type.
What are picture mask types?
Picture mask types are formats for display item data. The picture property on a display item is
used for the masking in all cases, except for those properties that are date formats. For more
information, see Picture mask types.
Default unit of measure
The default unit of measure to be used in the form
Display units of measure
Option to display the selected unit of measure in the form.
Override display for combos
Determines what information is displayed in the drop-down list for the display item. The options
available are Description, Name, and NameAndDescription.
Roles
The roles that have access to the display item type being created.
Field length for input
The length of the input field in characters.
 If the display item type is set to RelDef or UserRel then the SPFRelDefDirection option is displayed. This indicates the direction that the relationship definition will be navigated: 12 (from end 1 to end 2) or 21 (from end 2 to
end 1).
 The options available on the update display item dialog vary based on what type of display item you are creating or updating, such as an edge definition, relationship definition, interface definition, or property definition.
Manage display items in a section
1. Go to the System Administration feature set, and select Sections.
2. Choose a section, and select Actions 
 > Manage Display Items.
3. In the Manage Display Items dialog, search for display items to add, remove, or rearrange their order in the section as applicable.
 You can configure multiple display items in each section, but keep the number of display items to a minimum to help reduce the time the form takes to display the section.
 For more information on configuring display items, see Configure display items.
Configuration control of display items
Configure display item relationship tabs on forms
You can configure display items so that they appear as a tab at the bottom of the form Details or Update dialog boxes and allow basic actions on the tabs. You configure the Details or Update dialog boxes in a form to display
Relationship tabs, as follows:
1. Create a new display item for a section.
 This can be a separate section just for Relationship tabs or it can be an existing section with other display items. Alternatively, you can modify an existing relationship type display item.
2. For the Display item type, select RelDef.
3. Select RelationshipMaintenance from the Display Item Display Type list.
4. Enter a relationship definition in the Relationship box.
5. Repeat steps 1 to 4 for every relationship definition you want to see on the form. A separate tab displays for each relationship definition.
Relationship tabs appear only on Details and Update dialog boxes. For other form purposes, the display item display type is set to the default, which is determined by the relationship cardinality.
Each tab heading contains a DisplayAs value that is entered on the display item, and it shows a count in brackets telling the user how many items are related.
The content of each tab is retrieved from the database only when the tab is selected.
Relationship tabs on Details forms
