Tell me more about the Integration Service State statuses
The Integration Service State status identifies the progress of a SharedGroup in the Integration Engine.
InCumulate - The SharedGroup is currently in progress in the cumulate step. For more information, see Cumulate.
Cumulated - The SharedGroup finished the cumulate step.
InCorrelate - The SharedGroup is currently in progress in the correlate step. For more information, see Correlate.
Correlated - The SharedGroup finished the correlate step.
Failed Correlate - The SharedGroup failed the correlate step. You can find an exception message for the failure. For more information about integration exceptions, see Troubleshoot the Integration Engine.
InConsolidate - The SharedGroup is currently in progress in the consolidate step. For more information, see Consolidate.
Consolidated - The SharedGroup finished the consolidate step, and the objects are in the consolidated design component.
Retry Consolidate - An object in the SharedGroup depends on another object because the incoming SharedGroup contains a mandatory relationship. The SharedGroup is automatically resubmitted for the consolidation step when
the required object arrives in the consolidated design component.
Failed Consolidate - The SharedGroup failed the consolidate step. You can find an exception message for the failure. For more information about integration exceptions, see Troubleshoot the Integration Engine.
InCompare - The SharedGroup is currently in progress in the compare step. For more information, see Compare.
 When the objects of the SharedGroup are in the consolidated design component and the SharedGroup finished the compare step, the SharedGroup is deleted from the system.
Use work areas to determine who can see objects
For objects not currently related to a work areas (such as objects shared internally to a project not associated with a work area), you can create a relationship to allow users (internal or external) access to view those objects. A task of the
main scheduler creates these relationships automatically.
1. Locate the Main Scheduler.
2. Select Actions 
 > Scheduler > Start to enable it.
 You can also create these relationships manually by finding the work area and applying the filter by doing the following:
a. Select Design Tool Integration > Work Areas.
b. For the applicable work area, select Actions 
 > Apply Filter to Work Area to create a scheduler task to relate the filter to the Common Schema objects shared for that work area.
 A notification in your inbox keeps you apprised of the status of the scheduler task and indicates whether the task was completed successfully.
What properties are included in this notification?
The following properties are included and tracked in the notification for this scheduler task.
SDXWorkAreaRelateItemsProgressStatus - The progress status of the relationships of items to the work area.
SDXWorkAreaRelatedItemsInProjectCount - The number of items in the project related to the work area.
SDXWorkAreaTotalItemsInProjectCount - The total number of items to relate in the project.
SDXWorkAreaRelatedItemsInAsBuiltCount - The number of items related in the as-built configuration.
SDXWorkAreaTotalItemsInAsBuiltCount - The total number of items to relate in the as-built configuration.
SDXWorkAreaRelateItemsFailureMessage - The message that appears when relating items to the work area fails.
Enable or disable the automatic reprocessing of objects that failed the Integration process
 You must have the System Administrator role to make this update.
1. From the System Administration feature set, select Scheduled Tasks.
2. Select SDxiHubAutoIntegrationSchedulerTask.
3. Select Actions 
 > Update.
4. Select the Enabled checkbox to enable or disable this scheduler task.
 When this checkbox is disabled, objects that fail while being processed by the Integration Engine are not resubmitted.
 Other options on this dialog box allow you to customize this scheduler task. For example, you can specify the maximum number of times an object will be resubmitted for processing in the Maximum reintegration attempt
count box.
For more information about reprocessing objects that failed the Integration process, see View and address Integration Engine exceptions.
Creating a report of incomplete relationships in the loaded as-built data
After you have loaded the as-built handover data and before you can start project work, you need to check for dangling relationships. A dangling relationship is where the object on one end of the relationship was loaded, but the object on
the other end of the relationship can't be found. This can happen for a number of reasons, such as:
An error occurred in HxGN SDx2 that prevented the missing object from being published properly from Smart 3D.
The missing object was not included in the Smart 3D database and was, therefore, not published.
The mapping between the object in Smart 3D and HxGN SDx2 was incorrect.
 If the dangling relationships are not resolved before the project work begins, you could encounter issues with claiming, releasing claimed objects, and merging data.
The following section explains how to create a report of dangling relationships.
Generating the Missing Relationships report
The report, created using the following steps, allows you to view all the dangling relationships found in a project. Once the information is gathered in the report, you can use the information to correct the issues in Smart 3D and update
HxGN SDx2 or delete the relationship completely.
1. Find the project you want to create a report for:
From the Business Administration feature set, select Projects, and query for the project.
2. From the project, select Actions 
 > View Missing Relationships.
 You can use the Export to Excel option to transfer the data results of the report to a Microsoft Excel® spreadsheet to make it easier to share with others.
Understanding the Missing Relationships report
The report that results from the steps above includes information to help you investigate the issue:
The name of the End 1 object.
The name of the End 2 object.
The name of the type of relationship that should exist between those two objects.
An Indicator to let you know which object is missing (End 1 or End 2).
The domain that the relationship belongs to.
The HxGN SDx2 configuration where the objects and relationship should exist.
 This report supports filtering and sorting like other list views in HxGN SDx2.
How do I configure Data Take-On?
Data Take-On monitors and maintains the data quality when data is being moved from one source to another, during project handover, brownfield data take-on, data loads, and migrations. Data Take-On manages the data for quality
assurance purposes, and provides reports that can be used for both greenfield and brownfield environment data to help minimize quality issues.
Supported CSV load sizes in Data Take-On
The following parameters must be kept under consideration while running a Data Take-On job.
The following parameters must be kept under consideration while running a Data Take-On job. However, these parameters will change regularly as we add more features and continuously enhance our solution. We recommend you to
revisit the documentation frequently to keep track of changes to these parameters.
The maximum number of rows in your comma separated values (CSV) file must not exceed 50,000, in case of documents and tags. The application notifies you if the number of rows in your CSV file exceeds this limit.
The maximum number of files that can be uploaded to a single document or all the documents that can be created as part of a Data Take-On job is 2,000. The maximum size of an uploaded file must not exceed 4GB.
The maximum number of files that can be attached as a zip file to documents must not exceed 10,000. Even when you are uploading multiple zip files, the total number of files in all the zip files must not exceed 10,000.
Data Take-On can run a single job at a time.
Before you start running any Data Take-On jobs, ensure that the client application is registered for your tenant. Otherwise, the jobs may not move to the next stage after Stage processing. Contact your system administrator for more
information. Also, see Configure a client application.
You must also ensure that any PBS items such as Area, Unit, and so on, listed in your CSV file, also exist in the domain specified in the job definition. For more information, see Load Configurations tab in Create a job definition.
For running a Data Take-On job, you must be having a valid organization and security code assigned to you by your administrator.
Configure Data Take-On mappings
Most Data Take-On mappings are configured out-of-the-box and help in creating objects such as plants, projects, areas, and so on, that represent your plant configuration. You may need to modify the out-of-the-box mappings sometimes to
meet your unique business requirements. Data Take-On provides Master (MSR) and Editable (HEX) copies of mappings to ensure that the master mappings are protected from unwanted side effects when you customize the editable copy
of your own mappings and to support any upgrade process.
Master Data Take-On mappings with the prefix MSR cannot be used in the system and are supplied only for reference.
Editable copies of the master Data Take-On mappings with the prefix HEX can be used and edited in the system.
The main Data Take-On mapping elements are import mappings, load mappings, rules and rule sets, and job definitions.
The import mappings, load mappings, rules and rule sets, and job definitions are available in the Data Take-On feature set. For more information, see Converting the HxGN SDx2 object to a Data Take-On job.
Master mappings (MSR)
In order that each master mappings (MSR) remains intact and unaffected, the ISPFNonEditableCopy interface definition is instantiated on each master mapping element. The ISPFNonEditableCopy interface is only realized by elements
used in the master mappings and prevents those elements from being referenced, edited, updated, deleted, or attached. When the master mapping is selected in the user interface, the edit/update option is not displayed.
You can select an object in the Relationships > Show Load Object Mappings option in the Load Definitions screen, and select Update to view the load mapping details of the selected object. However, you cannot update any of
the details.
Master mappings or their related elements must not be used while running a job using Data Take-On feature set in the Web Client.
Editable copy of the mappings (HEX)
The editable copy of the mappings (HEX) is supplied so you can customize the mappings. The editable copy does not contain or reference the elements that realize the ISPFNonEditableCopy interface. This allows you to edit the copy of
the mappings to meet your custom mappings needs, such as adding new columns, rule sets, and so on.
 In case of existing mappings, only the Master copy (MSR) of a mapping is delivered in case it has been changed in a new release. The Editable copy (HEX) is not upgraded to prevent undesirable consequences from the upgrade.
Using MSR or HEX prefix in custom mappings
When you create or edit your own custom mappings, we recommend you do not use the MSR or HEX prefix in the names of the mapping elements. If there are overlapping names and Object UIDs between delivered and custom
mappings, they may cause issues in the upgrade process, due to uniqueness constraints. Also, we recommend you do not use the MSR or HEX prefix in any of your custom XML mappings that you load into your system using the Import
Data feature set.
Custom mapping elements that must not have MSR or HSR prefix
Import Definitions
Job Definitions
Validation Rules
Validation Rule Sets
Staging Schema
Object weights for delivered mappings
The Object Weight value controls the order of loading of the object or error report. The lower the number, the earlier in the process the object is loaded or reported. For example, a class can exist without a tag, but a tag cannot exist
without a class. Therefore, a class has a higher priority and so a lower object weight.
You can view or edit the object weights in Data Take-On feature set in the Load Mappings page.
You must not use any of the object weights already assigned to the delivered class definitions of the staging objects.
Objects with zero object weight are not processed by Data Take-On. For more information on how to assign object weights, see Create load object mappings.

Using Data Take-On Administration
With Data Take-On, you can control how supplied data is imported, validated, and loaded into your database. Data Take-On consists of a set of components that manage the import of different types of files and validate the data for loading
into the HxGN SDx2 database.
 Data Take-On supports the following input file formats:
Comma Separated Value (.CSV) files
 We recommend using a text editor like Notepad to modify your CSV files to avoid any unexpected results.
After the data is imported into the staging area, the remaining Data Take-On components are fully configurable and optional. For example, a job might include just the import and validation of the new data and provide a report on the quality
of the imported data. You can control the loading of the data so that the load process is not initiated until the quality of the imported data is approved.
Data files are imported using an import definition, which specifies how the data is mapped to the database using existing class definitions, property definitions, and relationship definitions. The imported data is validated against defined
validation rule sets that can use the database as their basis, and the load mapping ensures that the data is mapped correctly into the database format. Job definitions combine all these components into one set of steps.
 Data Take-On does not process reference files. For example, if a document contains a reference to another file and the document is imported into Data Take-On, the software does not maintain any relationships to the
reference file when loaded into the database.
There are several components that are defined in Data Take-On and used to manage the movement of data:
Import process - Uses a definition to map data files to the staging area.
Validation process - Evaluates imported data against a defined set of rules to ensure the validity of the data.
Load process - Uses a defined mapping, based on the structure of the database, to manage loading data into the system.
Job definition - Links together various importing, validating, and loading components to define how a set of data will be processed by the Data Take-On job.
The UID definitions (unique identifiers) in the staging area must match the UID definitions in the database. UID definitions must be set on all objects that Data Take-On loads from the staging area. When you create your
mappings, you must set the correct UID in the import mapping by setting up the UID definitions for any object mapping. For more information, see Define import mapping.
The Data Take-On job runs the complete process using all the components to stage, validate, and load the data into the HxGN SDx2 database. For more information on loading data using Data Take-On jobs, see Loading data
into your system.
Set Data Take-On default options
The following options can be set at the site level and will be inherited by all objects created in Data Take-On. The options can be changed, as needed, when configuring and mapping the objects required for Data Take-On.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Data Take-On System Options.
2. Set a default value for the available options.
The following tabs are available in this dialog. Select the tabs to know more about the options.
IMPORT
You can set a default value for the following:
Character encoding
Illegal characters
Validation of column header text and order
Separator for multiple values, and text values that represent blank values
Validation of CSV file format before uploading in Data Take-On for processing jobs
Each import mapping object created in Data Take-On inherits these defaults, which you can keep or change, as necessary.
You can set default text values that represent property values in your CSV file, which will be treated as blank values so the properties will not be imported into the staging area during mapping. For example, you may have properties
in your CSV file that contain values like NA, N/A, NR, and None. These values will be skipped during import.
For more information on illegal characters, see Set illegal characters for import mapping.
LOAD
You can set a default value for the following:
Block object on load minimum severity - Set the minimum error severity level at which object load will be prevented. Select Error to block the loading of objects that return validation errors, or Warning to return validation warnings
in the validation report.
Block property on load minimum severity - Set the default minimum severity level at which the property load will be prevented. Select Error to block the loading of properties that return validation errors, or Warning to return
validation warnings in the validation report.
 This default value can be overridden on the load property mapping.
Suppress notifications for all jobs - This check box is selected by default to suppress notifications on all jobs. These notifications are from the notification subscriptions configured on any class definition (like create, and update
events).
 This global setting takes precedence over the Suppress Notifications check box in the Create Job Definition dialog. For more information, see Create a job definition.
JOB MANAGEMENT
Enter a search criteria to find the name of the column set, and select the column set to be used when displaying job information. For more information, see Configure column sets.
Set the wait time to the number of days before a crashed job can be deleted from the system. The minimum wait time value is 1 and the maximum value is 30. For example, if you have created a job today, and set the wait time to 1
day, then you can delete the job any time next day.
Select the checkbox to allow automatic cleanup of failed jobs or submittals or those awaiting approval after a specific time interval. This option is selected by default.
Set the wait time to the number of days before a job or submittal that is awaiting approval can be automatically cleaned up from the system. The minimum wait time value is 1 day and the maximum value is 365 days. For
example, if you created a job or submittal today, and set the wait time to 1 day, then the job or submittal is cleaned up automatically any time after one day. The default wait time is 21 days.
Set the wait time to the number of days before a failed job or submittal can be deleted from the system. The minimum wait time value is 1 day and the maximum value is 365 days. For example, if you created a job or submittal
today, and set the wait time to 1 day, then the job or submittal is cleaned up automatically any time after one day. The default wait time is 14 days.
The job or submittal can still be viewed, and the reason for failure is accessible in the Web Client.
The cleaned up job or submittal cannot be re-run or submitted for approval.
What happens during the automatic clean up of jobs/submittals that are failed or awaiting approval?
The failed jobs and those awaiting approval can be automatically cleaned up at periodic intervals by configuring the wait time for such clean up. During this process, the staging and landing tables for the failed/awaiting approval jobs
are deleted from the database. The ISPFConfiguration interface is removed from the job and it is not considered as a configuration object anymore. This ensures better performance during login and a healthy database over time.
 Jobs that are on hold or crashed jobs will not be part of the automatic cleanup process.
REPORTS
You can specify the:
Maximum number of rows to be included in a validation report.
Default validation report definition, which determines the type of validation report generated: Excel, text, or a custom format.
 A default Excel format is delivered with Data Take-On.
VALIDATION
Select the default rules for the automatic generation of rules.
In the Auto Generated Object Rules section, select the default object rules to be automatically generated for each object action.
In the Auto Generated Property Rules section, select the default property rules to be automatically generated for each property action.
In the Auto Generated Relationship Rules section, select the default relationship rules to be automatically generated for each relationship action.
In the Auto Generated Relationship Property Rules section, select the default relationship property rules to be automatically generated for each relationship property action.
 Before the relationship property rule is automatically generated, at least one relationship property mapping must be configured. One rule will be generated for all the properties of a relationship.
To know more about the specific actions supported in Data Take-On, see Actions.
 There is a naming convention for the validation rules that are automatically generated. For more information, see Naming convention for rule auto-generation.
In the Inconsistency Severity section, select the default error severity for inconsistent property values, invalid action combinations, and conflicts between multiple actions. By default, inconsistency checks run for all jobs, but they
can be disabled on the job definition.
In the Severity Levels section:
Choose the colors used for each severity level in the validation report. The defaults are red for errors and yellow for warnings.
Create additional severity levels or remove any unused levels.
In the Validation Defaults section, select default severity triggers for the following:
Property error propagation minimum severity - The minimum severity level at which the property error will be propagated to the object. Any error of higher severity, such as warnings, will also propagate up to the object.
 This default can be overridden on the stage property to target property mapping.
Missing identifier generation columns severity – The default severity level for an object or property that comes from a mandatory column but has no a value.
Missing UID columns error severity – The default severity level when one of the properties for the UID creation has no value.
Auto generate rule error severity - The default severity level for an auto-generated rule that fails validation. For more information about auto-generated rules, see Define import mapping.
Set illegal characters for import mapping
What illegal characters can I specify in import mappings?
When creating or updating import definitions, you can specify a set of illegal characters that cannot be used in imported data. Illegal characters can be any characters that you decide are illegal in your CSV file.
What happens if illegal characters are found in an imported file?
If Data Take-On encounters any illegal characters during the import process, the import operation fails with an error indicating that illegal characters were found.
If you are specifying any illegal characters that are outside the range of valid XML characters, those characters will not be exported.
 Use Character Map or Character Viewer application provided with your operating system to select the illegal characters and add them to the Illegal Characters box.
Define import mapping
Import mapping process
Data Take-On uses import definitions to map imported file data from existing column headers to the correct column headers for objects, properties, relationships, and relationship properties in the staging area. The import process uses the
import definition as a template. The import mapping is built using the existing physical column headers in the input data file, but also supports other column headers that are computed.
Data Take-On supports CSV files as the input file format.
 CSV files must be supplied in invariant format. If you import a CSV file that is not in invariant format, you must use computed columns to create an import mapping that converts numeric and date information into invariant
format. For more information, see Supported CSV file formats.
What is invariant format?
Invariant format is a property that does not change when a specified transformation is applied on a CSV file. For example, Data Take-On uses the invariant format for thousand separator “,” and for decimal separator “.”. So a value of 1.001
would always be interpreted as 1 with three decimal places even if your intention may have been 1001, as the decimal point was really a thousand separator. Data Take-On will not return any validation errors on properties using the
invariant format, but you run the risk of not getting the intended data into the system.
For more information on CSV files not in invariant format, see CSV files not in invariant format.
Import mapping creation
Select Create Import Definition from the Data Take-On feature set to create import definitions and import mappings. The details for import mappings are displayed in tabs:
Import Definition Details - Use this tab to enter the name, description, and other details of the import definition you are creating.
Import Mappings - Use this tab to enter the primary object details and to define the column headers for the import definition.
Summary - This tab displays a summary of all the column headers.
 Most of the import mapping is already done for you by providing default options. You can use 
 to edit the default values.
For more information, see Create an import definition.
Types of import mappings
There are four types of column header mappings you can create for each column header.
Object mapping - Maps the object data in the CSV file to the HxGN SDx2 objects in the database.
Property mapping - Maps the property data in the CSV file to the properties of the HxGN SDx2 objects in the database.
Relationship mapping - Maps the relationship data in the CSV file to the relationships between the HxGN SDx2 objects in the database.
Unique identifiers (UIDs)
 All UID definitions (unique identifiers) in the staging area must match the UID definitions in the database. UID definitions must be set on all objects that Data Take-On is loading from the staging area to the database.
When creating your mappings, you must ensure uniqueness in the Data Take-On import mapping by setting up the unique identifier on an object mapping. The system warns you if the number of columns selected to construct the unique
identifiers does not match the number of elements in the database UID definition.
 A mapping with an incorrect number of UID elements may not be incorrect. For example, if computed columns are concatenated, the UID warning can be safely ignored and the mapping saved with the different number of unique
elements.
Globally unique identifiers (GUIDs)
If no UID is configured on the class definition of an object in the database, and the UID of the object in the staging area exceeds 128 characters, a GUID (Globally Unique identifier) is generated by the system for that staging object to make
it unique across the staging tables.
This GUID is considered as the UID of the object. This ensures that no validation errors occur during job processing, even if the object UID exceeds 128 characters. However, a validation error is thrown if the UID is set on an object in the
database, and the UID configured on the class definition of the object in the staging area exceeds 128 characters.
Auto-generated load mappings
The load mapping in an import definition maps the imported objects, classes, and properties to the existing objects, classes, and properties in the database. This mapping information is automatically generated by Data Take-On when
import mappings are defined, to save you time when creating load mappings.
 When any import mapping is deleted that contains class definitions, relationship definitions, or property definitions that are no longer used by Data Take-On, those schema items are automatically removed from the database.
Auto-generated validation rules
Validation rules are automatically generated when import mappings are defined, to save you time when creating rules. For example, each time you create an object mapping for Check Exists, Data Take-On automatically generates a rule.
Validation automatically runs the rule to ensure the object exists in the database.
By default, each action that can be assigned to a column is related to one or more generic rules. When validation rules are automatically generated, each generic rule is automatically configured for, and applied to, the column. For more
information about the rules related to each action, see Validation rules and actions.
Create an import definition
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Create Import Definition.
2. Complete the tabs to create the import definition as follows:
a. Import Definition Details
1. Enter a name and description for the new import definition.
 Do not use MSR or HEX in the name of your import definition as these are reserved for the delivered mappings and may cause issues due to uniqueness constraints.
2. In Default Prefix, change the default prefix for the object, if required.
 Changing the default prefix will update the staging schema names for all the mappings in the import definition. For example, when you set DTO as the default prefix in an import definition, it will update all staging
schema object names to start with DTO. Thus, the staging schema name for an area or a unit will be updated as DTOArea or DTOUnit.
3. Select the Data Mapping or Document Mapping option as required.
4. In CSV File, either drag your files to the box, or browse to add the files from your system.
 If you skip uploading a CSV file, you have to create the column headers manually.
5. Select Next to move to the Import Mappings tab.
b. Import Mappings
 The options displayed vary depending on whether you selected Data Mapping or Document Mapping in the Import Definition Details tab.
Primary Object Details section
The primary object is the starting object for the import mapping. The other objects are mapped by navigating from the primary object.
1. From the Class Definition list, select the class definition of the primary object.
 If you selected Document Mapping in the Import Definition Details tab, select Document Master Class Definition or Document Revision Class Definition as the primary object.
2. From the Column Header list, select a primary column header for the mapping.
 If you selected Document Mapping in the Import Definition Details tab, select primary column headers for both the master and revision.
 To edit the primary object details, select 
. For more information, see Edit the primary object details.
Column Headers Details section
You can define the column headers for the import definition and map the column data in the CSV files to the existing objects, properties, and relationships in the database.
Column Headers - All existing column headers in the CSV file are displayed in the Column Headers list.
Mapping Type - This column specifies whether the column header in the CSV file is to be mapped to the primary object property, related object property, or the mapped related object property.
Primary Object property - Select this option if the column header in the CSV file is to be mapped to a property on the object that has been defined as the primary object.
 If you selected Document Mapping in the Import Definition Details tab, this option is displayed as Document Property. Any properties you select as primary column headers in the Column Header list in the
Primary Object Details section are non-editable during mapping.
Related Object Property - The column header in the CSV file is mapped to a property on any of the objects in the CSV file other than the primary object.
Mapped Related Object Property - The column header in the CSV file is mapped to a property on an already mapped related object. For example, you have already mapped Area as a related object. To map Area
Code as a property on Area, select this mapping type and the Related Object list is populated with a list of objects that are already mapped and you can select Area from the list.
Master/Revision - Select the required master or revision to map. You can also select both master and revision, if required.
Related Object - This column is enabled when you select the Related Object Property or the Mapping Related Object Property mapping types. Search for the required class definition using the Search Bar 
 and
select the class definition of the related object from the list that is displayed.
Relationship - Search for the required relationship between the primary object and the related object using the Search Bar 
 and select the relationship from the list that is displayed.
Property - Search for the required property using the Search Bar 
 and select the property from the list that is displayed.
You can create new column headers by selecting 
. For more information, see Create a new column header.
You can remove or edit a column header by selecting . For more information, see Edit a column header.
You can initially create your import mapping without any column headers and update them at a later time.
Uploaded CSV section
Select Uploaded CSV to view the contents of your CSV file in a grid.
 Scroll down to view the grid.
3. Select FINISH to create the import definition, or select NEXT to open the Summary tab where you can review the details of the import definition before saving or canceling it.
 To update a setting, select the relevant tab or use the navigation buttons.
How do I use the filter options?
You can apply the below filters to refine the results in the tabs.
1: Use this option to search for a particular object name or to refine the values. The search box uses the object's name as the default filter.
2: Use this option to toggle between the selected items and all items.
3: Select 
 to change how the filter works, and select 
 to choose the item the search includes, such as name, class definition, or property definition.
4: Indicates the number of items selected.
Edit the primary object details
The class definition and column header options are editable only when you are creating a new import mapping. because you will lose any related mappings if you edit these options after you create the import definition.
If you selected Document Mapping in the Import Definition Details tab, you can edit the primary object details for the document master and revision separately in the user interface.
1. To edit the primary object details, select 
 next to the Column Header list.
2. Change the Object Action to another selection, if required. For a list of object actions, see Actions.
3. Change the prefix for the object type if required, for example TG, in the Object UID Prefix box.
4. Change the unique identifier for the object, if required, in the Unique Identifier text box. The default unique identifier matches the existing column header name.
5. If the column header is required to have a value, select the Is Mandatory checkbox.
6. To add optional interfaces for the selected class definition, filter and select the required optional interfaces in the Optional Interface box. This option is available for object mapping with Create Update and Update actions.
The Optional Interface option allows you to select optional interfaces that are available for the selected class definition in the database. The selected optional interfaces are instantiated on the objects during loading.
For more information on optional interfaces, see Optional interfaces on created objects.
7. If you are mapping documents, change the default relationship between the document master and revision in the Relationship box.
Select 
 to clear the existing value.
The list is populated with the available relationships between the document master and revision when you enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
.
8. Change the Relationship Action for the document mapping to another selection, if required. For a list of relationship actions, see Actions.
9. Change the Staging Relationship Definition for the document mapping, if required. This text box is automatically populated by default based on the relationship between the primary and related objects.
10. Select SAVE to save your changes.
The Unique Key Definition and Unique ID Definition (UID) are set on all objects that Data Take-On loads from the staging area. These options are auto-populated with values that have been configured for the class definition
currently being mapped to and are non-editable. If the unique key and unique ID definition are not configured for the class definition, the options not populated.
The Staging Class Definition box is automatically populated based on the class definition of the related object and is non-editable.
Create a new column header
Column headers are used to map the data in the CSV file to the correct locations in the staging area and the database, and support a number of different column types. The column type you select when defining the column header
determines the appearance of the other options. Data Take-On supports the following types of columns:
Physical - Maps to a specific column in the input data source.
Computed - Generates a new column using data from other columns or sources, such as environment type variables. This provides the ability to use functions. For example, you can enter a function in the Computed API box to
replace original entries with new entries. For more information, see Use the Computed column.
Constant - Allows you to use a string constant.
Create a new column header
1. In the Column Headers Details section, select 
 to display the New Column Header dialog.
2. In the Name box, enter a name for the new column header.
3. From the Column Type list, select one of the following column type types.
Physical - In the Column Header Text box, enter the column header details.
Computed - In the Computed API box, enter a function.
Constant - In the Value box, enter a value.
4. Select the Is Multiple Values Column checkbox if any column in your CSV file contains multiple values. For example, if a tag has to be related to many secondary disciplines (such as MECH, ELEC, CIVIL), you can separate the
values in the CSV file using a comma, and then select this checkbox when creating the column header.
The Is Multiple Values Column option is supported for Physical and Computed column types only.
For Computed column type, the function can return multiple values. However, it does not support multiple values as input.
5. Select the Ignore properties with blank values option if you want properties with blank values in your CSV file to be ignored.
6. Select SAVE to save the new column header and view it in the Column Headers Details grid.
Edit a column header
1. To edit a column header from the Column Headers list, select , and select Edit Column Header.
2. In the Edit Column Header dialog, edit the column header details.
Edit mappings
 The options displayed vary depending on the type of mapping you select from the Mapping Types list.
Mapping Types section
1. From the Mapping Types list, select the required mapping type. For more information on mapping types, see Create an import definition.
 This option is disabled, if you are updating the import mapping after the mapping is created.
2. The Primary Object option is disabled by default. To modify the primary object, see Edit the primary object details.
Property Name section
1. Search for the required property using the Search Bar 
 and select the property from the list that is displayed.
2. If necessary, change the Object Action to another selection. For a list of object actions, see Actions.
 The Staging Property Definition box is populated by default. This name is automatically populated based on the class definition of the object and is non-editable.
Related Object section
1. From the Related Object list, select the related object that is populated when you enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
.
 Select 
to clear the existing value.
2. Change the Related Object Action to another selection, if required.
3. In the Object UID Prefix box, change the default prefix for the object type if required, for example DTO.
4. Use the Object Unique Identifier text box to build a unique object identifier by entering the appropriate column headers available in the CSV file you are uploading or by creating computed columns to specify how to identify the
object uniquely.
5. If the column header is required to have a value, select the Is Mandatory checkbox.
The Unique Key Definition and Unique ID Definition (UID) are set on all objects that Data Take-On loads from the staging area. These options are populated with values that are configured for the class definition currently being
mapped to and are non-editable.
The Staging Class Definition box is automatically populated based on the class definition of the related object and is non-editable.
Relationship section
1. From the Relationship list that is populated when you enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
, select the relationship between the primary and related objects.
 Select 
to clear the existing value.
2. Change the Relationship Action to another selection, if required.
3. Change the Staging Relationship Definition, if required. This text box is automatically populated by default based on the relationship between the primary and related objects.
 If your mapping is not saved, it could be because a staging relationship with the same name already exists. In this case, change the prefix in the staging relationship definition, from HEX to, for example, HEX1 or HEX2, and so
forth.
Update an import definition
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Import Definitions.
2. Choose the import definition you want to update, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the Update dialog, modify the mapping components on each tab as required. For more information on the mapping components for each tab, see Create an import definition.
4. Select FINISH to update the import definition or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then update the import definition.
Delete an import definition
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Import Definitions.
2. Choose the import definition you want to delete, and select Actions 
 > Delete.
Define load mapping
The load process uses a defined mapping, based on the structure of the database, to manage loading data into the database.
The data to be loaded must match the data in the database using any one of three parameters:
Unique ID
Unique key
Query definition
By default, Data Take-On identifies objects in the database using the UID constructed in the staging area and the class definition specified in the load map. Sometimes, however, the UIDs for a specific type of object in a database cannot
be constructed or predicted from the data provided in a CSV file. In that case, you can use a unique key or a query definition to specify how to identify the object in the database.
Mapping with Unique ID
Objects can be identified by the unique ID when it is specified in the class definition for both the staging area and the database . The unique ID is specified during the import mapping. For more information, see Define import mapping.
Example 1: A tag object with a unique ID “Tag”, .TagName is created as “Tag_P100” in the staging area and database, where Tag is the prefix and P100 is the tag name.
In this case, the Load Object Unique Key and the Load Object Query Definition are not required to be specified, as Data Take-On uses the Unique ID to identify and match the objects.
The above example contains the unique key with prefix and object property, but it may also contain properties expanded through relationships.
Example 2: A tag object with a unique ID, “Tag”, “+TagAreaRel.Name”, .TagName, is created as “Tag_Area1_P100” in the staging area and database. Here, the Tag is the prefix, Area1 is the name of the area to which the tag is related, and
P100 is the tag name. In this case, there can be two tags with the same name, but the area is the property that adds uniqueness.
Mapping with unique key
Objects with a unique key specified in the class definition instead of a unique ID can be identified by the unique key. You must ensure that the unique key value is specified in the Load Object Unique Key text box during load object
mapping.
Example: A tag object with a unique key, “Tag”, .TagName is created as “Tag_P100” in the staging and the HxGN SDx2 database tables or a tag object with a unique key “Tag”, “+TagAreaRel.Name”, .TagName is created as
“Tag_Area1_P100” in the staging and the HxGN SDx2 database tables.
Mapping with Query definition
Some class definitions may not have a unique ID or a unique key specified or you may want some more control in identifying the load objects by expanding the relationships and matching properties.
Example: A simple query definition can be constructed with tag name and name of the area to which the tag is related. For example, .Name, +TagAreaRel.Name. A more complex query definition would be required if the tag is related to a
document as well as the area. For example, .Name, +TagAreaRel.Name,+TagDocumentRel.Name. Data Take-On will query for an object in the database with the .Name as the name of the object having relationships to the area and the
document.
For more information on unique keys and query definitions, see Unique keys and query definitions.
Load Mapping
You can load data into the database by mapping the imported staging area data objects to the existing objects, properties, relationships, and relationship properties found in the database. You can manually configure the load mapping for
each object in the imported data, or you can automatically generate the load mapping from the database by selecting the Auto Generate Load Mapping option when you create a new import definition. The load mapping can be further
refined for each stage object to load object mapping by selecting specific property-to-property, relationship-to-relationship, and relationship property-to-relationship property mappings.
You can view, edit, or create load mappings from the Data Take-On feature set by selecting Load Definitions.
Load Definitions - This grid details all of the existing load mappings as a list with two columns, Name and Description. One load definition is delivered with HxGN SDx2, which can be updated as required.
 The Description column is automatically set to Auto generated if the Auto Generate Export Mapping option is selected for the import definition.
Load Object Mappings - Select any load definition from the Load Definitions grid and select Actions > Relationships > Show Load Object Mappings to display the details for a selected stage to load object mapping in five
columns; the stage object, the load object to which it is mapped, the object weight, the load object unique key, and the load object query definition.
You can access and refine the properties of a stage object in the list by selecting one of the property-to-property, relationship-to-relationship, or relationship property-to-relationship property mapping commands located on the Actions
menu. For more information, see Define load object mappings.
During import mapping, you can only use Check File Exists or Check Target Exists as an action on an object, you cannot use both.
To map the .UID property, you can use only No Action as an action on the object.
When any load mapping is deleted that contains class definitions, property definitions, relationship definitions, or relationship property definitions that are no longer used by Data Take-On, those schema items are automatically
removed from the database.
The object, property, relationship, and relationship property mapping names can include underscore and alphanumeric characters.
Define load object mappings
Update a load definition
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load Definitions.
2. Choose the load definition you want to update, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. Update the load definition components as required.
4. Select FINISH to apply the changes.
 You can review the details in the Summary tab before applying the changes.
Define load object mappings
The Load Object Mappings grid displays the names of all the objects stored in the staging area of Data Take-On and the load objects that the stage objects are mapped to in the database.
The Object Weight value determines the order in which objects are loaded and/or error reports are generated. Objects with lower weight values are processed or reported earlier. For example, a class can exist without a tag, but a tag
cannot exist without a class, so class must have a higher priority and therefore, a lower object weight.
You can filter for the data in each column to limit the objects to a manageable list. For example, filter for the objects with the highest priority.
You must specify the object weighting for each Load Object Mapping to ensure correct loading order. For example, the object weightings must be set to load the revision before loading the file to which it is attached.
Objects with zero object weight are not processed by Data Take-On. For more information on object weights set on the delivered load mappings, see Configure Data Take-On mappings.
Create load object mappings
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load Definitions.
2. Choose the load definition for which you want to create a load object mapping, and select Actions 
 > Create Load Object Mappings.
3. Complete the sections to create the load object mapping as follows:
a. Stage Object
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the object that you want to map to a load object.
2. Select an object from the search results to display it in the Stage Object box.
b. Load Object
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the object that you want to map to a load object.
 If the load object does not exist, you can enter a name for it in the Free Text box.
2. Select the load object from the results to display it in the Load Object list.
c. Details
1. Provide a description for the object mapping in the Description box.
2. Provide a value in the Object Weighting box to set the order for the object mapping. Objects with lower weights are processed first.
3. In the Load Object Unique Key box, type the unique key of the load object.
4. In the Load Object Query Definition box, type the query definition to find the load object.
4. Select FINISH to save the load object mapping.
 The software uses the UID to identify objects in the database during validation and loading. If the object cannot be found using the UID, you can search using either the unique key or the query definition. For more information
on unique keys and query definitions, see Define unique keys and query definitions.
How do I use the filter options?
You can apply the below filters to refine the results in the tabs.
1: Use this option to search for a particular object name or to refine the values. The search box uses the object's name as the default filter.
2: Use this option to toggle between the selected items and all items.
3: Select 
 to change how the filter works, and select 
 to choose the item the search includes, such as name, class definition, or property definition.
4: Indicates the number of items selected.
Update load object mappings
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load Definitions.
2. Choose the load definition for which you want to update a load object mapping, and select Relationships > Show Load Object Mappings.
3. Select the load object mapping you want to update and select Actions 
 > Update.
4. Complete the sections to update the stage object to target object mapping as follows:
a. Stage Object
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the object that you want to map to a load object.
2. Select an object from the search results to display it in the Stage Object box.
b. Load Object
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the object that you want to map to a load object.
 If the load object does not exist, you can enter a name for it in the Free Text box.
2. Select the load object from the results to display it in the Load Object list.
c. Details
1. Provide a description for the object mapping in the Description box.
2. Provide a value in the Object Weighting box to set the order for the object mapping. Objects with lower weights are processed first.
3. In the Load Object Unique Key box, type the unique key of the load object.
4. In the Load Object Query Definition box, type the query definition to find the load object.
5. Select FINISH to save the changes to the load object mapping.
Define stage properties and relationships to load object mappings
The stage to load object properties and relationships can be further defined as mappings between each object found in the stage object and the load object mapping. There are three mapping types that can be created and edited.
Create load property mappings
Edit load property mappings
Create load relationship mappings
Edit load relationship mappings
Create load relationship property mappings
Update load relationship property mappings
Create load property mappings
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load Definitions.
2. Choose the load definition for which you want to create a load property mapping, and select Relationships > Show Load Object Mappings.
3. Select the required load object mapping from the list and select Actions 
 > Create Load Property Mappings.
4. Complete the sections to create the load property mapping as follows:
a. Stage Property
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the property that you want to map to a load property.
2. Select a property from the search results to display it in the Stage Property box.
b. Load Property
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the load property that you want to map to a stage property.
2. Select a property from the search results to display it in the Load Property box.
 If the load property does not exist, you can enter a name for it in the Free Text box.
c. Details
1. Provide a description for the property mapping in the Description box.
2. In the Block Property on Load Minimum Severity list, select the severity threshold at which the property will be blocked from loading if it fails a validation rule. Any severity setting greater than this will also pass on. If the
setting is warning, then both warnings and errors pass on to the object.
3. In the Property Error Propagation Minimum Severity list, select the severity threshold at which the failure of the property will be passed on to the object and prevent it from being loaded. Any severity setting greater than
this will cause the property to be blocked. If the setting was warning, then both warnings and errors are passed on to the object.
 For more information about the severity level assigned to validation rules, see Define validation rules and rule sets.
5. Select FINISH to save the load property mapping.
How do I use the filter options?
You can apply the below filters to refine the results in the tabs.
1: Use this option to search for a particular object name or to refine the values. The search box uses the object's name as the default filter.
2: Use this option to toggle between the selected items and all items.
3: Select 
 to change how the filter works, and select 
 to choose the item the search includes, such as name, class definition, or property definition.
4: Indicates the number of items selected.
Update a load property mapping
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load Definitions.
2. Choose the load definition for which you want to update a load property mapping, and select Relationships > Show Load Object Mappings.
3. Select the required load object mapping from the list, and select Relationships > Show Load Property Mappings.
4. Select the load property mapping you want to update and select Actions 
 > Update.
5. Complete the sections to update the load property mapping as follows:
a. Stage Property
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the property that you want to map to a load property.
2. Select a property from the search results to display it in the Stage Property box.
b. Load Property
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the load property that you want to map to a stage property.
2. Select a property from the search results to display it in the Load Property box.
 If the load property does not exist, you can enter a name for it in the Free Text box.
c. Details
1. Provide a description for the property mapping in the Description box.
2. In the Block Property on Load Minimum Severity list, select the severity threshold at which the property will be blocked from loading if it fails a validation rule. Any severity setting greater than this will also pass on. If the
setting is warning, then both warnings and errors pass on to the object.
3. In the Property Error Propagation Minimum Severity list, select the severity threshold at which the failure of the property will be passed on to the object and prevent it from being loaded. Any severity setting greater than
this will cause the property to be blocked. If the setting was warning, then both warnings and errors are passed on to the object.
 For more information about the severity level assigned to validation rules, see Define validation rules and rule sets.
6. Select FINISH to save the load property mapping.
Create load relationship mappings
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Load Definitions.
2. Choose the load definition for which you want to create a load relationship mapping, and select Relationships > Show Load Object Mappings.
3. Select the required load object mapping from the list and select Actions 
 > Create Load Relationship Mappings.
4. Complete the sections to create the load relationship mapping as follows:
a. Stage Relationship
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the relationship that you want to map to a load relationship.
2. Select a relationship from the search results to display it in the Stage Relationship box.
b. Load Relationship
1. Enter a search criteria in the Search Bar 
 to find the load relationship that you want to map to a stage relationship.
2. Select a relationship from the search results to display it in the Load Relationship box.
 If the load relationship does not exist, you can enter a name for it in the Free Text box.
c. Details
1. In the Details section, provide a description for the relationship mapping in the Description box.
2. In the End1 Stage Object list, select the stage object required for End 1.
3. In the End2 Stage Object list, select the stage object required for End 2.
4. In the End1 to End2 Error Propagation Minimum Severity list, select the severity threshold at which an issue with End 1 will be passed on to the object on End 2. Any severity setting greater than this will also be passed
on, so if the setting was warning, then both warnings and errors are passed on to the object.
5. In the End2 to End1 Error Propagation Minimum Severity list, select the severity threshold at which an issue with End 2 will be passed on to the object on End 1. Any severity setting greater than this will also be passed
on, so if the setting was warning, then both warnings and errors are passed on to the object.
 Select the Reverse Stage Relationship Direction option to reverse the stage relationship direction, if required.
5. Select FINISH to save the load property mapping.
