Define unique keys and query definitions
Load object unique keys
When a unique key definition has been specified in HxGN SDx2, Data Take-On can then identify objects in the database. You can specify the unique key for the object type and enter it in the Load Object Unique Key box when you create
or edit the load object mapping.
The unique key definition is specified in the same way as in HxGN SDx2, but with a few changes.
Any properties used must be prefixed with a full stop.
Any environment variables must be surrounded by @ symbols
Any constants must be surrounded by double quotes"". Properties and relationships must be Data Take-On properties and they must have a load map defined.
For example,
The Unique Key Definition TG,Name becomes "TG",.Name.
The Unique Key Definition TG,CURRENTCONFIG,Name becomes "TG",@CURRENTCONFIG@,.Name.
The Unique Key Definition TG,CURRENTCONFIG,+SPFTagArea.Name becomes "TG",@CURRENTCONFIG@,+SPFTagArea.Name.
A formatting check is performed as you enter the unique key details. A green tick indicates that the data format is correct.
A schema check is performed as you save the load object mapping.
The supported environment variables are: @CURRENTCONFIG@, @CONFIGLEVEL1@, @CONFIGLEVEL2@, @CONFIGLEVEL3@, @CONFIGLEVEL4@, and @CONFIGLEVEL5@. These variables are not case sensitive.
Load object query definitions
If the HxGN SDx2 database does not have reliable unique keys setup, then a load object query definition can be used. The query definition is entered in the Load Object Query Definition box as a series of properties, which can identify
the object in the database along with the class definition. These properties can be retrieved by navigating the relationships.
The query definition is a comma separated list of these properties, with no limitation to number or order.
Properties are specified by a period and then the VTL property definition UID.
Properties that require navigating relationships should have the relationship information before the property. This is the relationship definition UID prefixed with a plus sign for navigation from end 1 to 2 and a minus sign for end 2 to 1.
If an object can be identified in the database by just its name and class definition, the query definition would be .Name.
If a tag object can be identified in the database by its name, the name of the related area, and the class definition, then the query definition would be .Name,+VTLTagArea.Name.
If a document object can be identified in the database by its name, its major, and minor revisions, the query definition would be .Name,.VTLSPFMajorRevision, .VTLSPFMinorRevision.
If a file object attached to a document can be identified in the database by its name, the name of the document to which it is attached, and the major and minor revisions of the document, the query definition would be
.Name,+VTLSPFFileComposition.Name,+VTLSPFFileComposition.VTLSPFMajorRevision,+VTLSPFFileComposition.VTLSPFMinorRevision,+VTLSPFFileComposition.VTLSPFDocVersion.
Query definitions should only be used after unique key definitions or UID matching as they have better performance.
You can specify the query definition parameters to be in any order, such as .Name,+VTLTagArea.Name can be +VTLTagArea.Name,.Name.
A formatting check is performed as you enter the query definition details. A green check mark indicates that the data format is correct.
A schema check is performed as you save the load object mapping.
Environment variables are not allowed in the query definition.
If both a unique key and query definition are specified, then Data Take-On will use the unique key to identify the objects in the database. If neither are specified then Data Take-On will use UIDs to match the database.
If you load from Data Take-On leaving the Unique Key and Query definition fields blank, the UIDs in the staging tables are used to match the UIDs in the database tables. Sometimes, however, the UIDs for a specific type of object in
a database cannot be constructed from the data provided in a CSV file. In that situation, you can use the unique key and query definition as an alternative to identify those objects.
If the property mentioned in the load object query definition is looking for the revision object, the query definition queries for the document revision object during the loading process, even when the stage relationship definition is
mapped to the version relationship definition in the database. Example: +VTLSPFFileComposition.VTLSPFMajorRevision.
If the subsequent relationship mentioned in the load object query definition is looking for the revision object, the query definition queries for the document revision object during the export process even when the stage relationship
definition is mapped to the version relationship definition in the database. Example: +VTLSPFFileComposition+VTLSPFNFDWDocRevTag.
In out of the box delivered load mappings, the query definition to identify the correct version object to attach the file on HEXSPFDesignFile has been updated to:
+HEXSPFFileComposition.Name,+HEXSPFFileComposition.HEXSPFMajorRevision,+HEXSPFFileComposition.HEXSPFMinorRevision,+HEXSPFFileComposition.HEXSPFDocVersion,.Name. In upgrade scenarios, this change will
be applicable only to MSR copies of load mappings. You have to manually apply this change on HEX load mappings.
Some class definitions may not have a unique ID or a unique key specified or sometimes objects cannot be uniquely identified when using multiple criteria in the query definitions. In such cases, if you know the actual UID of the
database object, you can identify the object by using the .UID property in the query definition. It will identity the objects for the generated UIDs.
Export file mappings
When you export files to a target system, the import mapping maps a file object type, (for example SPFDesignFile) and creates a property with a Check Staging File Exists action, which is attached with the full file path of the file stored in
SPFLocalFileName.
During the export process, all of the local staging files found are uploaded to the target system file services. The export process then creates all of the design files and saves the files into the vaults.
This mapping uses the Check Target System File Exists action, which ensures that the file is uploaded correctly using the path that is accessible by the target system server.
The target system loading will stop an export that contains an object with more than one property with a Check Staging File Exists or Check Target System File Exists action. This is because an object that represents a file can only
have one file attached to it in SmartPlant Foundation. Additionally, the export process checks for any updates to an existing file object and warns the user that those updates are not supported. These update warnings are ignored so that
the load can continue.
Define validation rules and rule sets
When do I need to create a validation rule?
Create a validation rule when you need to validate the data before you load it into your database. The validation rule ensures that the data conforms to the quality requirements, schema standards, and so on.
How do I ensure that imported data conforms to the object property schema and the relationship property schema?
The Check Properties Against Schema validation rule ensures that imported data conforms to the object and relationship property schemas. This rule can be auto-generated when configuring the system options.
How do I ensure that imported properties are valid on classified items?
The Check Properties Against Classification validation rule ensures that only those property definitions that are relevant to the object classification of the item can be imported. The rule checks for attributes on the column sets of the
classification items.
 The rule does not validate documents.
The rule ensures that attributes not applicable for a classification do not get loaded if they are not defined on the column set related to the object classification. If no attributes are found for the object classification
(SPFPrimaryClassification), the rule will search for a relevant column set along the hierarchy of the object’s classification tree till a column set is found and then validate the properties on the object against this column set.
For example, tags of type pipe and pressure transmitter will have different properties. A pipe will have properties like paint code and nominal pipe diameter while it does not make sense for a pressure transmitter to have such
properties.
Also, if the classification was not provided as part of the Data Take-On job for an existing object, the classification against which the attribution will be checked can be derived by the rule from the database object.
If the classification is shared by more than one classified item (for example, SDAAsset and SDAModel with FDWMaterialClassification), the rule is capable of detecting which attribution it needs to check against. For example, an
Asset is not validated against a Model column set.
The rule works in combination with Concurrent Engineering, where tags or assets might be claimed from As Built, Projects, and so on, and reclassified consequently. The rule checks for the classification of the object in the
appropriate configuration. For example, an asset can have different classifications in As Built and in Projects, and the rule checks for the relevant attributes for the classification accordingly.
Validation rules and rulesets
Validation rules verify that the data in an imported record meets the data standards specified for the database. Each validation rule contains a formula that evaluates the imported data in one or more fields and verifies that it matches the
existing criteria and hierarchy found in the HxGN SDx2 database. Each validation rule includes an error message that is recorded in a validation report when the rule returns an invalid value.
All the validation rules that will be used in a job are grouped into a set. You can create a validation rule set, and then create the individual rules that will make up that set. When you create your own custom mappings or make a copy, we
recommend you do not use the MSR or HEX prefix in the names of the mapping elements. For more information, see Configure Data Take-On mappings.
For a list of the existing validation rules and their actions supplied with Data Take-On, see Validation rules and actions.
 You can configure Data Take-On to automatically generate validation rules when you create your import mapping. For more information, see Configure the automatic generation of validation rules.
Configure the automatic generation of validation rules
Create a validation rule set
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Create Rule Set.
2. Complete the tab to create the rule set as follows:
Basic Details
1. Enter a name and description for the new rule set.
2. In the Rules box, enter a search criteria to find the name of the rule, class definition, or property definition required for the new rule set.
3. Choose one or more rules you want to include in the rule set.
3. Select FINISH to save the rule set, or select NEXT to open the Summary tab where you can review the details of the rule set before saving or canceling it.
 To update a setting, select the Basic Details tab or use the navigation buttons.
How do I use the filter options?
You can apply the below filters to refine the results in the tabs.
1: Use this option to search for a particular object name or to refine the values. The search box uses the object's name as the default filter.
2: Use this option to toggle between the selected items and all items.
3: Select 
 to change how the filter works, and select 
 to choose the item the search includes, such as name, class definition, or property definition.
4: Indicates the number of items selected.
Update a validation rule set
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Rule Sets.
2. Choose the rule set you want to edit, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the Update dialog, modify the rule set components in the Basic Details tab as required.
4. Select FINISH to update the rule set or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then update the rule set.
Copy a validation rule set
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Rule Sets.
2. Choose the rule set you want to copy, and select Actions 
 > Copy.
3. In the Copy dialog, enter the name and optional description in the Basic Details tab and modify other components as required.
4. Select FINISH to copy the rule set or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then copy the rule set.
Terminate a validation rule set
You can terminate a validation rule set from the system using Data Take-On.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Rule Sets.
2. Choose the validation rule set you want to terminate, and select Actions 
 > Terminate.
Create a validation rule
How do I decide whether to select a class definition or a relationship definition schema type when creating a validation rule?
When you create a validation rule, select the class definition schema type to validate objects and their properties, and select the relationship definition schema type to validate relationships and their properties exposed by their linked
interfaces.
What does the End for Error Propagation option in the Schema Type page do?
The End for Error Propagation option specifies either one of the ends of a relationship to be considered as invalid when the imported data fails to be validated against a relationship property rule.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Create Rule.
2. Complete the tabs to create the rule as follows:
a. Schema Type
1. Select either a Class Definition or a Relationship Definition option.
2. Enter a search criteria, and select the required class definition or relationship definition on which you want to base this rule.
b. Rule Type
Select the required rule type to add to the validation rule. For more information, see Validation rule description.
 The validation rule types listed in the Rule Type box are dependent on the schema type selected. The following rule types are not supported:
Check Value and UOM
Compare ENS Value
Compare Value
Double
Integer
Target System Cardinality
String Does Not Start With
String Not Equal To
Use the import data functionality to create a rule with one of the above rule types. For more information, see Import data.
Use the Regular Expression rule type instead of the String Does Not Start With and the String Not Equal To rule types. For example, the regular expression ^(?!valve).*$ would exclude strings that start with the
word 'valve' and ^(?!valve$).* would exclude strings that include the word 'valve'.
c. Basic Details
1. Enter a name and description for the new validation rule.
2. Enter the full error message to be displayed when the validation rule fails.
3. Choose a severity level to be assigned when this rule fails.
 The following table lists the properties that are available for selection in the Rule Details section based on the selected rule type.
Selected Rule Type
Property
What does it do?
Check Parallel Claim
Check Parallel Exclusive Claim
Checks to see if the item is claimed to a parallel project under the same plant and
only one configuration at a time.
Check Revised to Parallel Config
Check Revised to Parallel Configuration
Exclusive Claim
Checks to see if the document item has been revised to a parallel project under the
same plant and is claimed into only one configuration at a time.
Check Object Exists
Exclude Higher Configuration
Checks to see if the object exists in the database already except in the higher
configuration.
Check Relationship Exists
Checks to see if the relationship exists in the database already except in the higher
configuration.
Compare All Property Values For Object
Checks to see if all the staging area object property values are the same as the
database object property values except in the higher configuration.
Check Properties Against Schema
Allow Disabled Enum Entries
Checks for the disabled enums and reports the results in the validation report.
Check for Unmapped Properties
Checks if any of the properties of an object are not mapped to the database
properties and reports the results in the validation report.
d. Rule Details
 This tab is visible only if you selected one of the following rule type - Check File Exists, Check File Exists For Document Revision, Check For Cyclic Relationship, Check Properties Against Classification,
Check Relationship Exists, Date Time, Regular Expression, Staging System Cardinality or String Length.
Provide additional details for the rule, as necessary, based on the rule type selected.
If you selected the Staging System Cardinality rule type, see Relationship cardinality rules for more information.
For the Regular Expression rule type, the software compares the regular expression with the property value during validation. If the values are different, the software reports an error.
3. Select FINISH to save the validation rule. The new validation rule appears in the Rules list and displays the properties associated with the rule in the Properties pane. Alternatively, select NEXT to review the details defined for the
rule on the Summary tab and then save the validation rule.
 To update a setting, select the relevant tab or use the navigation buttons.
How do I use the filter options?
You can apply the below filters to refine the results in the tabs.
1: Use this option to search for a particular object name or to refine the values. The search box uses the object's name as the default filter.
2: Use this option to toggle between the selected items and all items.
3: Select 
 to change how the filter works, and select 
 to choose the item the search includes, such as name, class definition, or property definition.
4: Indicates the number of items selected.
Update a validation rule
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Rules.
2. Choose the rule you want to edit, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. In the Update dialog, modify the rule components on each page as required. For more information on the component properties for each page, see Create a validation rule.
4. Select FINISH to update the rule or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then update the rule.
 You cannot edit a rule that uses one of the following rule types:
Check Value and UOM
Compare ENS Value
Compare Value
Double
Integer
Target System Cardinality
String Does Not Start With
String Not Equal To
 Use the export data functionality to edit a rule that uses one of the above rule types. For more information, see Export data.
Copy a validation rule
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Rules.
2. Choose the rule you want to copy, and select Actions 
 > Copy.
3. In the Copy dialog, enter the name and optional description in the Basic Details tab and modify other components on each page as required. For more information on the component properties for each page, see Create a validation
rule.
4. Select FINISH to copy the rule or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then copy the rule.
 You cannot copy a rule that uses one of the following rule types:
Check Value and UOM
Compare ENS Value
Compare Value
Double
Integer
Target System Cardinality
String Does Not Start With
String Not Equal To
Terminate a validation rule
You can terminate a validation rule from the system using Data Take-On.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Rules.
2. Choose the validation rule you want to terminate, and select Actions 
 > Terminate.
 When any validation rule is terminated, the class definitions, relationship definitions, or property definitions belonging to the Data Take-On staging schema - that are no longer used by the Data Take-On import mappings or load
mappings - are automatically removed from the database.
Configure the automatic generation of validation rules
When you create your import mappings from the database, the validation rules are automatically generated. The system determines which rules it will run for each action, saving configuration time. You can configure which rules are
automatically generated for each action in the Data Take-On system options. The actions and rules that appear are configurable. For more information, see Rule auto-generation model.
For more information about the system default options, see Set Data Take-On default options.
Rule auto-generation model
Each action you see in the import mapping user interface comes from a specific enumerated list. The following three enumerated lists for actions are delivered with Data Take-On: VTLObjectActions, VTLPropActions, and
VTLRelActions.
All validation rules have the same class definition and realize a specific interface definition with properties for each rule type, such as IVTLCheckObjectExistsValidationRule.
For an action to appear in the Validation page of the Data Take-On Default Options dialog box, the enum must instantiate the IEnumEnumVTLExt interface. For a rule to appear, it must have the IVTLRuleSupportsAutoGen interface. The
rules must also have the IPropertyValidationRule interface to be available for property actions, the IVTLRelationshipValidationRule interface to be available for relationship actions, and the IVTLRelDefValidationRule interface to be available
for relationship property actions.
To ensure the creation of logical relationships, the relationship definition for VTLActionVTLValidationRule includes specific interfaces to relate the action to the rule.
The following validation rules are provided in the base configuration:
Item

Rule
Object
Check Exists
Check Object Exists,
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Object
Create Update
Check Properties Against Schema,
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Object
Delete
Check Object Exists,
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Object
Terminate
Check Object Exists,
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Object
Update
Check Object Exists, Check Properties Against Schema,
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Object
No Action
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Property

Check File Exists
Relationship

Target System Cardinality
Relationship

Compare Value
 The Compare Value rule ignores null values in the database if the object property action or the relationship property action is set to Compare Create, but respects the null values if the action is set to Compare Value. This is
because loading with a Compare Create action requires blank database values to be updated without altering any existing values.
The VTLActionVTLValidationRule relationship definition links the action to the specific rule interface definition.
For a detailed description of each validation rule, see Validation rule description.
For more information on setting the Data Take-On default options, see Set Data Take-On default options.
Naming convention for rule auto-generation
There is a naming convention for the validation rules that are automatically generated. Depending on the rule type, the rule name is a combination of the rule interface display name, class or property definition name, and relationship
definition name.
Rule type
Rule name
Object Rule
[Rule interface display name]-[Class definition name]
Property Rule
[Rule interface display name]-[Property definition name]-[Class definition name]
Relationship Rule
[Rule interface display name]-[Relationship definition name]-[Class definition name]
Relationship Property Rule
[Rule interface display name]-[Property definition name]-[Relationship definition name]
 The auto-generated rule name length cannot exceed 128 characters. In such case, the rule name is a combination of the rule interface display name and the global unique identifier (GUID) separated by a hyphen '-'.
Data Take-On rule severity levels
The IEnumEnumVTLExt interface definition provides the VTLReportingColour property for the rule severity levels to the two supported validation rules.
Define job definitions
A job definition is a combined set of components configured into a template for the staging, validation, and loading of data into the HxGN SDx2 database. The job definition manages the quality of the data to be loaded into the database.
You can define the import mappings, load mappings, validation rule sets, and delete rule sets.
When a job definition has been configured, it can be used by Data Take-On to load Data Take-On jobs containing data into the database. For more information on Data Take-On jobs, see Run a Data Take-On job.
To view, update, or terminate job definitions, select Job Definitions from the Data Take-On feature set. To create a job definition, select Create Job Definition from the Data Take-On feature set.
You create job definitions using a dynamic job wizard, which uses the workflow selected as the basis for the behavior of the job running through Data Take-On. When you create your own custom mappings or make a copy, we
recommend you do not use the MSR or HEX prefix in the names of the mapping elements. For more information, see Configure Data Take-On mappings.
Each job definition has a set of process steps that manages the import, validation, and load processes. Some process steps appear in the wizard only when a specific workflow has been selected.
Each job definition can be terminated using the Data Take-On feature set, but only if there are no active jobs attached to the job definition. For more information, see Terminate a job definition.
Each job definition is configured using the following components:
Basic Details - The job description name and description, as well as the workflow used to load data into the database.
Workflow - The defined set of steps that drive the behavior of the job running through Data Take-On. The following workflow templates are delivered with Data Take-On: DTO Loader and Translate Loader.
Load Configurations - The load configurations used to load the data into the database.
Import Definitions - The import definitions used to import the data.
Rule Sets - The validation rule sets used to validate and verify the imported data.
Validation Report Definitions - The validation report definitions used to create the validation report.
Method Details - The method details used to create a method to run the Data Take-On job.
Access Groups - The access groups that have permission to use the job definition to create a new job in Data Take-On.
Create a job definition
 The creation of a job definition and the tabs that are displayed during that process are based on the workflow selected. The following example shows creating a job definition using the DTO Loader workflow.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Create Job Definition.
2. Complete the tabs to create the job definition as follows:
 The tabs displayed vary depending on the type of job definition you are creating.
a. Basic Details
1. Enter the name and description for the job definition.
2. Select the workflow to be used as the basis for the job definition. For example, DTO Loader workflow.
 The workflow selected changes the tabs displayed.
3. Select the Report Inconsistent Property Values option to ensure that the inconsistency check is always run when the workflow selected contains the validation process step.
The inconsistency check processes all objects in the staging area, and when any two instances of the same property on the same object are found with differing values, an error message is flagged in the validation
report. You can then decide whether to propagate the error to the object.
This option is selected by default. If you clear this option, you stop the inconsistency check from running during validation, and you may get results that are not expected.
4. Select the Report Invalid Action Combinations option to report the number of invalid action combinations on an object by looking at the data from combined mappings.
For example, if an object is mapped for terminate, then there should not be any properties mapped on the object for create update. If any properties for create update are found to be mapped to the object, an error is
propagated.
This option is selected by default. If you clear this option, you stop the ability to report the number of invalid action combinations running during validation, and you may get results that are not expected.
5. Select the Report Multiple Action Conflicts option that checks for multiple actions on a single object.
This option is selected by default. If you clear this option, you stop the system from reporting on multiple actions running during validation, and you may get results that are not expected.
The user cannot choose the action that is taken forward. If multiple actions are found, the user is warned about which action is being taken forward. For example, if an object is found to have both an update and a
create update action, then the user is informed that the object is being taken forward with the create update action.
 The previous three options do not appear if the workflow does not contain a validation process step.
6. Select the Record Job Details option to store the job information, such as stage details, validation, load details, and so forth.
7. Select the Allow Blank CSV with Column Header option to attach CSV files with column headers but no data when creating a Data Take-On job.
 For example, there may be multiple CSV files to be loaded and some of them may not have any data. In such cases, selecting this option ensures that the job is still processed successfully.
8. Select the Is Enabled option to activate the job definition for use in creating jobs in Data Take-On.
 This option is selected by default. If you clear this option, this job definition cannot be used to create jobs in Data Take-On.
b. Load Configurations
1. Set the default options for new document version creation in the Document Version Configurations section:
Allow Version Creation - Select this option to create a new document version if the same file is available in the input CSV file during subsequent imports.
Copy Files from the Previous Version - Select this option to copy files from the previous version of a document while creating a new version.
What are the use cases for the Allow Version Creation and Copy Files from the Previous Version check boxes?
If you set the Allow Version Creation option to True and the Copy Files from the Previous Version option to True:

2. Set the default options for general configuration in the General Configurations section:
Suppress Notifications - Select this option to suppress all notifications related to the job definition.
 This option is enabled only when the Suppress notifications for all jobs check box is not selected in the Data Take-On System Options. For more information, see Set Data Take-On default options.
When should I select the Suppress Notifications check box?
When notification subscriptions are configured for events (like create or update) on any class definition and large amounts of data for that class definition has to be loaded using Data Take-On, the system generates a
huge number of notifications, which can slow down the job process. In such cases, select the Suppress Notifications check box to suppress all the notifications.
What happens if I change the Suppress notifications for all jobs check box in the Data Take-On System Options?
If the Suppress notifications for all jobs check box in the Data Take-On System Options is selected, all the notifications are suppressed, irrespective of the option specified in the Suppress Notifications check
box in the job definition. The various scenarios are explained below:
Suppress all notifications in Data Take-On System Options
Suppress Notifications in Create Job Definition
What happens
Selected
Selected
All notifications are
suppressed
Not selected
Selected
All notifications are
suppressed
Selected
Not selected
All notifications are
suppressed
Not selected
Not selected
Notifications are not
suppressed
ENS Behavior - From the ENS Behavior list, select the required behavior for ENS.
Honor ENS – Objects are created in the database using the ENS. This is the default option.
Select on load job/submittal – When creating a load job or a submittal, the user can select the option to honor or suppress the ENS for objects created in the database.
 The Honor ENS or the Select on load job/submittal options will work only if ENS has been configured on the class definition of the objects being loaded.
Suppress ENS – Objects are created in the database by suppressing the ENS and naming them according to the values in the CSV file.
 The Suppress ENS option on the load job/submittal form is enabled only if the Select on load job/submittal option is selected from the ENS behavior list.
Design Tool - Select the required design tool you want to load the data into, from the list.
Design Component - You can select the required design component if more than one design component is associated with the selected design tool.
 If only one design component is associated with the design tool, it is selected by default and is not displayed. However, you can view the design component in the Summary page.
c. Import Definitions
Enter a search criteria to find the name of the import definition.
Select the required import definitions to add to the job definition.
 You must select at least one import definition.
d. Rule Sets
Enter a search criteria to find the name of the rule set.
Select the required rule sets to add to the job definition.
 You must select at least one rule set.
e. Validation Report Definitions
Select the required report definitions to add to the job definition.
f. Method Details
1. In the Client API box, enter the name of the client API.
 The supported client APIs are CreateClassObjAndLoad and SDxCreateClassObjAndLoad.
2. In the Display As box, enter a name for the method as it should appear in the Data Take-On feature set.
3. From the Condition list, select a condition.
4. In the Supported File Type(s) box, enter the file name extensions of the supported file types that can be uploaded using the Data Take-On job wizard. Use a comma to separate the different file types.
 The supported file types are: CSV, IFC, NWD, and NWF.
5. In the Maximum Number of Control Files box, specify the maximum number of control files that can be uploaded using the Data Take-On job wizard.
6. From the Loader Classification list, select the required classification.
 To create a new classification, select the New Loader Classification option from the list and enter the name and description for the new classification in the pop-up window that is displayed.
 A classification can be associated with one job definition only.
7. Select the Show File Upload Page checkbox to show the file upload page in the Data Take-On job wizard.
8. Select the Show Configuration checkbox to show the configuration selection page in the Data Take-On job wizard.
For more information on Data Take-On jobs, see Run a Data Take-On job.
g. Access Groups
Enter a search criteria to find the name of the access group.
Select the access group to add to the job definition.
 You must select at least one access group.
3. Select FINISH to save the job definition, or select NEXT to open the Summary tab where you can review the details of the job definition before saving or canceling it.
 To update a setting, select the relevant tab or use the navigation buttons.
How do I use the filter options?
You can apply the below filters to refine the results in the tabs.
1: Use this option to search for a particular object name or to refine the values. The search box uses the object's name as the default filter.
2: Use this option to toggle between the selected items and all items.
3: Select 
 to change how the filter works, and select 
 to choose the item the search includes, such as name, class definition, or property definition.
4: Indicates the number of items selected.
Update a job definition
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Job Definitions.
2. Choose the job definition you want to edit, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. Modify the job definition components on each page as required. For more information on the component properties for each page, see Create a job definition.
 If you change a setting that affects other pages, for example by changing the workflow, then all relevant pages must be completed again.
4. Select FINISH to update the job definition, or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then update the job definition.
Copy a job definition
In the Data Take-On feature set, select Job Definitions.
1. Choose the job definition you want to copy, and select Actions 
 > Copy.
2. Enter the name and optional description in the Basic Details tab, and modify other components on each page as required. For more information on the component properties for each page, see Create a job definition.
 If you change a setting that affects other pages, for example by changing the workflow, then all relevant pages must be completed again.
3. Select FINISH to copy the job definition, or check the details in the Summary tab for all the components and then copy the job definition.
Terminate a job definition
You can terminate a job definition from the system using Data Take-On. However, you cannot terminate a job definition if it has any active jobs still attached.
1. In the Data Take-On feature set, select Job Definitions.
2. Choose the job definition you want to terminate, and select Actions 
 > Terminate.
Use function column type mappings
When creating a column header mapping, you can select the Computed column type that uses functions to match the imported data to the database configuration.
Administrator users must have programming or function creation experience to use this functionality. However, this has been made easier as the functionality is provided using an Intellisense style control, which streamlines the process.
When a CSV file contains data that is not in invariant format, for example, the CSV file uses a comma as decimal separator, you must create a computed column type to convert the comma to a decimal.
For information on configuring prompted attributes, see Configure prompted attributes on Data Take-On jobs.
Use the Computed column
When you select the Computed column type, you must enter a function as the Computed API box cannot be blank. For example:
1. Type func. in the Computed API box, and a drop-down list displays all the functions that are available.
2. Double-click the parameter Replace to select it, and then type the rest of the parameter string syntax, for example func.Replace([TAG_NAME],"PSS","EDW"). For more examples of computed API functions, see Functions.
Other input columns can be viewed inside each function by typing [ to display a drop-down list.
A green check mark indicates that the function is valid.
The following is a list of all the available functions for the Computed column type; each function has a set of parameters that can also be viewed.
Function name
Description
AddDefaultUOMIfMissing
Applied to a column with a UOM value. If there is no UOM supplied and the specified property maps to
a property that is scoped by a UOM type, then the output has the default UOM added to the value.
Otherwise, the value is left untouched. Default value is based on SI in the schema.
Concat
Concatenates a set of input strings.
ConvertToHash
Returns the SHA-1 (Secure Hash Algorithm 1) capitalized hash value for the input string. The SHA-1
algorithm computes a uniquely identifiable fixed hash value for the input string.
DateTimeColumn
Input value is converted to date time format and can include time zone, yyyy/MM/dd-HH:mm:ss:fff.
Decode
Looks for value matches and replaces them with a new value.
Divide
Divides parameters using a numerator.
GetDefaultUOMIfMissing
Applied to a column with a UOM value. If there is no UOM supplied and the specified property maps to
a property that is scoped by a specific UOM type, then the output is set to that default UOM. Otherwise,
the value is left untouched. Default value is based on SI in the schema.
GetDocumentRevisionsCountFromTargetSystem
Returns the number of revisions available for a document in the database.
GetFileName
Returns the file name from a file path.
GetJobDetails
Returns the value of the property specified in the input string.
GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem
Uses major revision code from the database.
GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem
Uses minor revision code from the database.
GetParentObjectClassDef
Returns the class definition of the parent object. A blank value is returned if the parent object is not
found in either the object name column of the input CSV file or in the database for all the possible class
definitions specified in the PossibleParentObjectClassDef parameter.
GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault
Replaces input values with the value from the database, and if the object specified in the QueryDef
parameter is not available in the database, the default value specified in the default value parameter is
returned.
GetTypingEnumValuesBasedOnClassification
Returns the Enum list level type for the specified classification level. If the classification UID is not
provided or is invalid, a blank value is returned.
GetValueFromTargetSystem
Replaces values with the value from the database.
IndexOf
Creates an index of parameters; part 1 is input string and part 2 is search string.
InvertedFilter
Checks for the property name in the inverted CSV file that matches the property name configured in the
computed column. The corresponding computed column is then evaluated and the property value is
imported into the staging area.
Join
Joins parameters with same separator.
Left
Input is from the left by the specified length in the computed column.
Length
Value in length as an input string.
Minus
Reduces initial value by another value being subtracted.
Multiply
Multiplies input value by a value.
PadLeft
Takes the input string and adds padding to the left using a padding character to match the string length.
PadRight
Takes the input string and adds padding to the right using a padding character to match the string
length.
RegexMatch
If matches a regular expression (such as uppercase characters) returns True, if not then returns False.
RegexReplace
Replaces existing regular expression characters (such as uppercase characters) in the name with new
characters.
Replace
Replaces existing characters in the name with new characters.
Right
Starts the count from the right by the specified length in the computed column.
Split
Splits the input string using a given character from the string.
SplitAlphaNumericSequence
Splits up an alpha numeric sequence into separate values.
SubString
Starts input string from sub string.
Sum
Adds values together so changes input values to a new value.
ToLower
Changes the input string value to lower case.
ToUpper
Changes the input string value to upper case.
Translate
Replaces a sequence of characters found in a string with another set of characters, a single character
at a time.
Trim
Trims a character value.
TrimEnd
Trims a character value from the end.
TrimStart
Trims a character value from the start.
YMDColumn
Input value is converted to the yyyy/MM/dd format.
Microsoft .NET methods calculate some of the computed columns. For more information on how some computer columns function, see Computed column .NET methods.
Comma decimal separated values in your CSV file must be surrounded by double quotes, for example, "123".
When a CSV file uses comma decimal separators, you have to create a computed column to convert the comma separated value to a decimal value. Create a computed column, map it to the property that was mapped on your
physical column, and run this computed column function Func.Replace([PHYSICAL COLUMN], ",", ".").
You can also make use of HxGN SDx2 environment- and session-related variables, for example, Job Name, UserName, and Current query configuration. These are accessible using the @ character. For further information, see
Environment variables for a list of all the variables supported.
When you use the Replace function to replace the @ symbol, Data Take-On considers the @ symbol as an environment variable and does not replace it. To replace the @ symbol, you must create a column of Constant type, and
give it the value of @. For example, Func.Replace([PHYSICAL COLUMN], "[CONSTANT COLUMN]", "1"). Here, this example replaces the @ symbol with 1.
When you use the GetParentObjectClassDef function, if the second parameter, ObjectClassDef, is not available in the CSV file, you can use another computed column to get the class definition of the object.
For example, if the CSV file for SPO/E system tags provides an object with the classfication Cooling Tower, we can get its class definition of 'SPXTagEquipment by using the computed column function:
func.GetValueFromTargetSystem("true", "#SPXTagClass,.Name=[TagClass]","-SPFClassMember+TagClassClassDef.Name")).
The two computed columns, GetValueFromTargetSystem and GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault, support multiple relationship expansions, but do not support edge definitions in the QueryDef parameter.
The three computed columns, GetJobDetails, Split, and SubString, return blank outputs if the input values are invalid.
Example 1: GetJobDetails – func.GetJobDetails([PROPERTY_NAME]) - If an invalid property name is provided as input, such as, VTLJobWorkflow. The correct value is VTLJobWorkflowName.
Example 2: Split - func.Split("TAG_NAME","_","2") – If the first parameter is the string itself, and the "_" character is used for splitting, the third parameter can be 0 or 1, but not 2.
Example 3: SubString - func.SubString("TAG_NAME","0","9") – The third parameter can be any integer from 0-7 as the string, TAG_NAME, consists of seven characters, not 9.
Processing the HxGN SDx2 objects through the DTO Loader workflow
Data Take-On allows the creation and updation of HxGN SDx2 objects, such as a plant, project, area, or discipline.
Data Take-On uses CSV files as input files to process and validate the imported data through the DTO Loader workflow. This ensures that when a new system is being set up, the administrator can control the type of data entering the
system and its integrity.
The first step in this process is to create a Data Take-On job.
 For any HxGN SDx2 object to be used to process data in CSV files, the IVTLJob, IVTLValidationJob, IVTLJobExportDetails, IVTLJobImportDetails, and ISPFConfigurationItem optional interfaces must be added. This schema is
required to process any type of HxGN SDx2 object, such as a plant or a project, through the DTO Loader workflow.
The DTO Loader workflow is specified as one of the arguments on the load method used for creating the Data Take-On job. This method is created as part of the job definition creation process.
For every job definition that is configured:
a corresponding load method is displayed in the Data Take-On feature set, which can be used to run Data Take-On jobs, based on your role permissions.
a job instance is created when the load method is executed, that will be attached to the configured workflow.
After the object is run through a Data Take-On job, the convert, stage, validate, and load steps of the DTO Loader workflow are executed automatically. You can monitor the Data Take-On job as it progresses through the workflow.
The following work process diagram illustrates the steps involved in processing the input file through a Data Take-On job using the DTO Loader workflow.
Run a Data Take-On job
Attach the DTO Loader workflow automatically
Monitor the Data Take-On job
View the job
Run a new Data Take-On job
You can run a Data Take-On job as described in Run a Data Take-On job. When setting up a plant or project, you can control the type of data entering your system and validate it by processing the data using the DTO Loader workflow
which is triggered when a Data Take-On job is run. The Data Take-On job contains one or more control files with all the details of the data that must be validated before entering into the system, like tags or documents and their file
attachments. Each Data Take-On job has a defined classification that determines which type of data must be validated using Data Take-On.
 If you upload more than one control file while creating a Data Take-On job, the control files are automatically associated with the respective import definitions by the system.
When a Data Take-On job file is uploaded, the attached files are transferred to a specified location where the files can be scanned for viruses before being loaded into Data Take-On. Data Take-On validates the quality of the incoming data
in the supplied files before they are loaded into the system.
Attach the DTO Loader workflow automatically
When you create an object to process CSV files, such as a project or an area, in the Web Client, the DTO Loader workflow automatically attaches to the object and runs through the workflow steps. The workflow uses the following process
steps to accomplish the conversion of the object into a job.
The following table provides the process steps used by the DTO Loader workflow.
Process step name
Description
Arguments
SDVJobConversionStep
Converts an HxGN SDx2 object to a Data Take-On job object.
None
SDVUnzipStep
Reads all the file references in a document and unzips any
zipped files that are not referenced.
None
SDVSplitCSVFileStep
Splits input CSV file using properties defined in the Import
Definition tab of the Create Job Definition wizard.
None
Import step
Runs the import of the supplied data to the staging area
database using a defined job definition.
None
Validate step
Runs the validation rules on the imported data and generates a
validation report. If there are validation errors, the validation
report must be approved before the workflow can continue.
Arg1: VTLValidationReportFile
Load step
Transfers and loads the data to the database. This step starts
automatically if there are no validation errors.
None
Monitor the Data Take-On job
You can use the My Data Take-On jobs page in the Web Client to monitor the progress of Data Take-On jobs you created in the current scope and view their summary. For more information, see Monitor the Data Take-On job progress.
View the Data Take-On job
You can view the summary of each job in Data Take-On by selecting View Summary from the Actions 
 menu of the selected job in Show My Data Take-On Jobs. For more information, see Viewing job progress and statistics.
Configure prompted attributes on Data Take-On jobs
Data Take-On allows you to enter prompted attribute or property values when creating a Data Take-On job, and map them to the required properties in the database using prompted computed columns.To support this feature in the Web
Client, you configure fixed or dynamic display items in sections and display them on the Data Take-On job form. You can then enter the property values in the display items when creating Data Take-On jobs using the Data Take-On feature
set in the Web Client.
For example, you can configure the description of a pipe or nozzle as a property value that must be specified when creating a Data Take-On job in the Web Client. The GetJobDetails computed column can be used to pick up the property
value from the Data Take-On job form and map this description to the long description property of the pipe or nozzle in the import definition.
Expose properties as fixed display items
If you want to expose the property as a fixed display item in the Data Take-On job forms for all classifications, you must create the following items:
A new property definition.
A new interface definition related to the Loader classification to expose the property to be displayed in the Data Take-On job form.
For more information on creating property and interface definitions, see Extend the existing stand-alone schema.
A new section related to the SDALoader-form so that the new property is displayed on the Data Take-On jobs for all classifications. For more information, see Configure forms.
A new computed column in the Data Take-On import definition which uses the GetJobDetails function. This allows the property to be picked up and mapped to the required property in the import definition. For more information on
using computed columns, see Computed column functions.
 After creating these items, you can view the newly generated section in the Data Take-On job form by navigating to the Data Take-On feature set in the Web Client.
Expose properties as dynamic display items
If you want to expose the property selectively, as a dynamic display item in the Data Take-On jobs for applicable classifications, you must create the following items:
A new Data Take-On job form related to the applicable sub-classification unless a form already exists. The form, however, must contain the ISPFDisplayOverride section, which is required to dynamically display the required column
sets on the Data Take-On job form. For more information on generating forms, see Form generation process.
A new interface related to the Loader classification to expose the required properties to be displayed in the Data Take-On job form. For more information on creating interfaces, see Extend the existing stand-alone schema.
A new column set and column items for the required properties to display on the Data Take-On job form. For more information, see Configure column sets.
A new job definition related to the new sub-classification. For more information on creating a job definition, see Create a job definition.
A new computed column in the Data Take-On import definition which uses the GetJobDetails function. This allows the property to be picked up and mapped to the required property in the import definition. For more information on
using computed columns, see Computed column functions.
 After creating these items, you can view the newly generated section with the new display items in the Data Take-On job form by navigating to the Data Take-On feature set in the Web Client.
Functions
Functions are programmed by you to change the original input data to match a specific requirement expected when data is loaded into a database configuration. Computed columns use functions.
You can use environment variables when you create computed columns. For more information, see Environment variables.
You can also create prompted API computed columns. For more information, see Configure prompted attributes on load jobs.
Computed column functions
The following computed column function examples show the original input and the output expected when data is imported to match a specific requirement in a database configuration.
 For information on using functions in the Computed column, see Use the Computed column.
Function name
Computed column sample
Input values and output results
AddDefaultUOMIfMissing
func.AddDefaultUOMIfMissing("DEVTag", "DEVWeight", [TAG_WEIGHT])
INPUT
If the values for [TAG_WEIGHT] are "123 " and "456"
OUTPUT
The outputs are 123 kg and 456 kg, as the default UOM is kg based on the SI in the schema
ConCat
func.Concat({"A","B","C"})
INPUT
If the values are "A", "B", and "C"
OUTPUT
ABC
ConvertToHash
func.ConvertToHash([Name])
INPUT
If the value for [Name] is “ABC Industries”
OUTPUT
The output is "170C0B71E9F68CC294A6614BAE3E7347C208EA48"
DateTimeColumn
func.DateTimeColumn([InputColumn],"Input value format","")
INPUT
Converts date or date time to invariant format to work with HxGN SDx2 and Data Take-On. For
example, the Input Value Format
"dd/MMM/yyyy-HH:mm:ss:fff"
OUTPUT
Output as invariant date format and time
"yyyy/MM/dd-HH:mm:ss:fff"’
DateTimeColumn
func.DateTimeColumn([InputColumn], "Input value format", "TimeZone")
INPUT
Converts date time to invariant format date time and time zone. For example, for the Input Value
Format and Time Zone
"dd/MMM/yyyy-HH:mm:ss:fff","Central Standard Time"
OUTPUT
Output as invariant date time format and includes time zone. For example
"yyyy/MM/dd-HH:mm:ss:fff","GMT"
Decode
func.Decode([Input column],
{"IfValueMatches1","SetValueTo1","IfValueMatches2","SetValueTo2"}, "DefaultValue")
INPUT
Looks at the input string and allows you to replace values; func.decode([inputcol],
{"N","North","S","South","E","East","W","West"},"Default")
OUTPUT
If input is N it is replaced by North, and so on. If input is not N, S, W, or E it returns to the default
value
Divide
func.Divide("100","10")
INPUT
Divides the numerator by the denominator; func.Divide(100,10)
OUTPUT
If 100 is the numerator and 10 is the denominator, 10 is the output
GetDefaultUOMIfMissing
func.GetDefaultUOMIfMissing("DEVTag", "DEVWeight", [TAG_WEIGHT_UOM])
INPUT
If the values for [TAG_WEIGHT_UOM] is "kg" and ""
OUTPUT
The outputs are "kg" and "kg" assuming that the default UOM is kg
GetDocumentLatestRevisionsFromTargetSystem func.GetDocumentLatestRevisionsFromTargetSystem("False",
"FDWDocumentMaster", [COMP_DocumentMasterUID])
INPUT
Takes the class definition for the unique identification of the document master, a true/false flag to
determine the configuration scope, and the document master UID.
OUTPUT
Returns the latest revision UID for a master in the identified configuration scope.
GetDocumentRevisionsCountFromTargetSystem func.GetDocumentRevisionsCountFromTargetSystem(“#SPFDesignDocMaster,.Nam
e=[DocumentName]”,”true”)
INPUT
Takes the QueryDef for the unique identification of the document master, and a true/false flag to
determine the search configuration.
For example, the input can be Name = Document1 and Ignore configuration = true.
OUTPUT
Returns the number of revisions available for Document1 in all configurations.
If the Ignore configuration value is set to “false”, then the count of revisions available for Document1
that are specific to the configuration selected during job creation is considered.
GetFileName
func.GetFileName([FilePath])
INPUT
Returns the file name from a file path. For example, if the file path value is
C:\Folder1\SubFolder1\File1.txt.
OUTPUT
File1.txt
GetJobDetails
func.GetJobDetails([PROPERTY_NAME])
INPUT
Takes the property name from the job as input. For example, VTLJobWorkflowName.
OUTPUT
Returns the value associated with the specified property. For example, Import Validate Export.
GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem
func.GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem([REV_SCHEME], [REV_ CODE])
INPUT
Takes the document revision scheme and revision code. For example, REV_CODE=1A,
REV_SCHEME=RS_Rev1A
OUTPUT
Returns the document major revision code specified in the input. For example, Major revision code =
1
GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem
func.GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem([REV_SCHEME], [REV_ CODE])
INPUT
Takes the document revision scheme and revision code. For example, REV_CODE=1A,
REV_SCHEME=RS_Rev1A
OUTPUT
Returns the document minor revision code specified in the input. For example, Minor revision code =
A
GetParentObjectClassDef
func.GetParentObjectClassDef([Tag],[TagClass],[ParentTagName],
{"SPXTagInstrument", "SPXTagEquipment"})
INPUT
Takes the object name (Tag), object class definition (TagClass), parent object name
(ParentTagName), and possible parent object class definitions (SPXTagInstrument,
SPXTagEquipment); func.GetParentObjectClassDef("Tag201","SPXTagEquipment","Tag101",
{"SPXTagInstrument", "SPXTagEquipment"})
For example, ObjectName = "Tag201"
ObjectClassDef = "SPXTagEquipment"
ParentObjectName = "Tag101"
PossibleParentObjectClassDef = {"SPXTagInstrument", "SPXTagEquipment"}
OUTPUT
Returns the class definition of the parent object. For example, checks if Tag101 (parent tag of
Tag201) is provided in the object name column of the input CSV file. This is possible if user is trying
to create the parent tag (Tag101) and child tag (Tag201) in the same CSV file.
If the object name is provided, gets the ObjectClassDef value for Tag101. This means that you have
the parent class definition from the CSV file.
If the object name is not provided, queries for Tag101 as SPXTagInstrument in the database;
If the parent tag name (Tag101) is found, returns SPXTagInstrument as the parent class definition.
If the parent tag name (Tag101) is not found, queries for Tag101 as SPXTagEquipment;
If the parent tag name (Tag101) is found, returns SPXTagEquipment.
If the object is still not found, a blank value is returned.
GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault( "true",”This is default tag
description”, "#DEVTag,.Name=[TAG_NAME]",".Description")
INPUT
Replaces input values with the value from the database, and if the object identified by the QueryDef
parameter is not available, returns the default value specified in the input string. For example,
TAG_NAME = "Tag01"
OUTPUT
This goes to the database and finds the DEVTag with the name "Tag01" and returns its description.
If the DEVTag, "Tag01" is not available in the database, then the description, "This is default tag
description" specified in the input string is returned.
GetTypingEnumValuesBasedOnClassification
func.GetTypingEnumValuesBasedOnClassification("FDWTagClassification",
[Comp_TagClassificationUID], "TagClass")
INPUT
Takes the class definition of the classification, the UID of the classification, and the property
definition UID of the Enum list level.
OUTPUT
Returns the Enum list level type for the specified classification level. If the classification UID is not
provided or is invalid, a blank value is returned.
GetValueFromTargetSystem
func.GetValueFromTargetSystem("true","#DEVTag,.Name=
[TAG_NAME]",".Description")
INPUT
Replaces input values with the value from the database. For example, TAG_NAME = "Tag01"
OUTPUT
This goes to the database and finds the DEVTag with the name "Tag01" and return its description. If
"Tag01" is not found in the database, then "NULL" value is returned.
GetVersionNumberForRevision
func.GetVersionNumberForRevision("#FDWDocumentRevision,.Name=[Document
Number],.SPFExternalRevision=[Revision]", "true"))
INPUT
Returns the latest version number of the document revision from the database.
OUTPUT
This goes to the database and finds the latest version number on the document revision.
 If the Allow Version Creation flag is turned on in the job definition, it finds out the version
number in the database and increments the number by 1 as an output.
IndexOf
func.IndexOf("ABCDEF","C")
INPUT
Finds the index of the search string within the input string. For example,
func.IndexOf("ABCDEF","C")
OUTPUT
The output is 2 because counting begins at zero
InvertedFilter
func.InvertedFilter([PROPERTY_NAME],{"DateTime",
func.DateTimeColumn([PROPERTY_VALUE], "dd-MM-yyyy", "UTC")})
INPUT
Checks for the Property name “DateTime”, in an Inverted csv formatted file and if it matches the
column name (“PROPERTY_VALUE”) then the DateTime column value is evaluated based on the
defined function DateTimeColumn.
OUTPUT
Output as invariant date and time format
"yyyy/MM/dd-HH:mm:ss:fff"
Join
func.Join({"T","A","G"},",")
INPUT
Joins an array of strings separated by a user-defined separator. For example,
func.Join({"T","A","G"},",")
OUTPUT
T,A,G
Left
func.Left([TAG_NAME],"5")
INPUT
Adds a number of input string characters to the output string starting from the left, matching the
number specified. For example,
[TAG_NAME] = PSS-105
OUTPUT
PSS-1
Length
func.Length([TAG_NAME])
INPUT
Length of the input string. For example,
[TAG_NAME] = PSS-105
OUTPUT
7
Minus
func.Minus("10","2")
INPUT
Subtracts the second value from the initial value. For example,
10 - 2
OUTPUT
8
Multiply
func.Multiply({"4","2"})
INPUT
Multiplies two values. For example,
4 x 2
OUTPUT
8
PadLeft
func.PadLeft([TAG_NAME],"A","10")
INPUT
Takes the given input string [TAG_NAME] and adds padding to the left using a padding character (A)
to match the string length given in the function (10). For example,
TAG_NAME = PSS-105
OUTPUT
AAAPSS-105
PadRight
func.PadRight([TAG_NAME],"A","10")
INPUT
Takes the given input string [TAG_NAME] and adds padding to the right using a padding character
(A) to match the string length given in the function (10). For example,
TAG_NAME = PSS-105
OUTPUT
PSS-105AAA
RegexMatch
func.RegexMatch([TAG_NAME], "[A-Z]")
INPUT
If uppercase characters in the tag name, returns = True, if no uppercase characters, returns = False.
For example,
TAG_NAME = TAG01
OUTPUT
TAG_NAME = TAG01 returns = True
RegexMatch
func.RegexMatch([TAG_NAME], "[^A-Z]")
INPUT
If no uppercase characters in tag name, returns = True. If uppercase characters exist returns =
False. (^ = string may not contain alphabetic characters A - Z). For example,
[TAG_NAME] = tag01
OUTPUT
TAG_NAME = tag01 returns = True
RegexReplace
func.RegexReplace([TAG_NAME],"-","_")
INPUT
Uses the second parameter to find the characters to replace and replaces them with the third
parameter specified.
[TAG_NAME] = "TAG-01-02-03"
OUTPUT
Where "-" means hyphen and we are replacing hyphen with "_" or underscore, so the output is
"TAG_01_02_03"
Replace
func.Replace([TAG_NAME],"P","E")
INPUT
[TAG_NAME] = P-105
OUTPUT
E-105
Right
func.Right([TAG_NAME],"3")
INPUT
Adds a number of input string characters to the output string starting from the right, matching the
number specified
[TAG_NAME] = PSS-105
OUTPUT
105
Right
func.Right([TAG_NAME],"5")
INPUT
Adds a number of input string characters to the output string starting from the right, matching the
number specified
[TAG_NAME] = PSS-105
OUTPUT
S-105
Split
func.Split("TAG_NAME","_","1")
INPUT
Takes a string and splits it on the given character. The output returned is determined using the
integer value provided
For example, in the string, TAG_NAME, to get NAME as the output, first argument is the string itself,
second argument is the “_” character in the string that is used for splitting, and the third argument is
the integer value of the index, that is “1”.
OUTPUT
The output is NAME because counting begins at zero
SplitAlphaNumericSequence
func.SplitAlphaNumericSequence([REV_CODE],"True","1")
INPUT
Takes a string value, a true/false flag to determine if the primary sequence should be returned and a
default value to be used in case the value is empty. Splits the string based on the change in
alphanumeric characters and returns the first or second part of the string depending on the true or
false flag
[REV_CODE]="A1"
OUTPUT
"A"
SubString
func.SubString([TAG_NAME],"0","3")
INPUT
Takes part of a string, starting from a given starting index character number, and continues for a
given number of characters.
[TAG_NAME] = TAG-105
OUTPUT
TAG
Sum
func.Sum({"1","2","3"})
INPUT
Adds the elements of an integer array, so 1 + 2 + 3
OUTPUT
6
ToLower
(Lowercase)
func.ToLower([TAG_NAME])
INPUT
[TAG_NAME] = PSS-105
OUTPUT
pss-105
ToUpper
(Uppercase)
func.ToUpper([TAG_NAME])
INPUT
[TAG_NAME] = pss-105
OUTPUT
PSS-105
Translate
func.Translate([TAG_NAME], "TAG-", "tag")
INPUT
This takes the first character of the StringToReplace parameter and replaces it with the first
character of the ReplacementString. It then takes the second character of the StringToReplace
parameter and replaces it with the second character of the ReplacementString and so on. If the
StringToReplace is longer than the ReplacementString then the extra characters are replaced with
blanks
[TAG_NAME] = TAG-PSS-123
OUTPUT
tagPSS123
Trim
func.Trim([TAG_NAME],"E")
INPUT
Trims a character value.
[TAG_NAME] = EIS-PSS-105E
OUTPUT
IS-PSS-105
TrimEnd
func.TrimEnd([TAG_NAME],"0")
INPUT
Trims a character value from the end.
[TAG_NAME] = 0001-PSS-10500
OUTPUT
0001-PSS-105
TrimStart
func.TrimStart([TAG_NAME],"0")
INPUT
Trims a character value from the start.
[TAG_NAME] = 0001-PSS-10500
OUTPUT
1-PSS-10500
YMDColumn
func.YMDColumn([InputColumn],"Input value format")
Takes date value of dd/MMM/yyyy and converts to yyyy/MM/dd
INPUT
30/Jan/2014
OUTPUT
2014/01/30
Document functions
There are two types of computed columns predominantly for use with documents: Database computed columns and Non-database computed columns.
Database computed columns
The following computed columns use information from the database so that the computed columns conforms to the database.
GetDocumentRevisionsCountFromTargetSystem
This computed column retrieves the number of revisions available for a document from the database. It uses these parameters:
QueryDef – Used to uniquely identify the document master in the databse. You can specify the class definition of the document master and the document name.
IgnoreConfiguration - Used to specify whether to include all configurations in the query or not.
This QueryDef searches for class definitions that realize the interface ISPFDocumentMaster.
If more than one document is identified by the QueryDef, then a cumulative count of all the revisions available for the identified documents is returned.
Example
In this example, the first parameter finds the object with the class definition SPFDesignDocMaster and name Document1. The second parameter searches in all configurations.
func.GetDocumentRevisionCountFromTargetSystem(“#SPFDesignDocMaster,.Name=’Document1’”,"true")
Scenario
To configure a rule that would result in a validation failure message for a document having more than one version, do the following:
Create an integer rule with minimum value set as zero and maximum value set as one. The Allow Blank Values option should be set to True.
Create a computed column with the following function:
func.TrimStart(func.GetDocumentRevisionsCountFromTargetSystem("#SPFDesignDocMaster,.Name=[DOCUMENT_NUMBER]","True"),"0")
GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem
Given a revision scheme and a revision code, the computed column retrieves the details for the revision scheme from the database, then splits the major revision code and validates that it conforms to the criteria for the revision code.
For example,
func.GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem([REVISION_SCHEME], [REVISION_CODE])
func.GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem([REVISION_SCHEME], [REVISION_CODE])
GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem
Given a revision scheme and a revision code, the computed column retrieves the details for the revision scheme from the database, then splits the minor revision code and validates that it conforms to the criteria for the revision code.
Errors
If an error occurs in either computed columns, the system displays the following error message:
ERROR resolving MINOR/MAJOR revision code REV_CODE using revision scheme REV_SCHEME
 This error is not used for any .Net exceptions. This error occurs when either the computed column produces an empty value, both the minor and sequences are numeric, or both the sequences are alphabetic.
For example:
func.GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_REV01", "A1") = A
func.GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_REV01", "A1") = 1
GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault
This computed column retrieves the value of a property from the database, and if the object specified by the QueryDef is not found in the database, returns the default value specified in the input string. It uses the following parameters:
IncludeHigherConfiguration – Used to specify whether to include a higher configuration in the query or not.
Default value – Used to specify the default value if the object specified by the QueryDef is not found in the database.
QueryDef – Used to find an object in the database. You can specify the class definition of the object and any property values found on that object or a related object. Any number of criteria can be added by separating them with a comma.
This parameter must be a constant string, but it can reference other columns within the string if you enclose each of those columns in brackets [ ]. For example, func.Concat({[TestColumn1], [TestColumn2], [TestColumn3]}).
ReturnDef - Used to describe the property to be returned from the database. You can specify the relationships to which to navigate, followed by the property you want to retrieve. Any number of relationships can be navigated. This
parameter must be a constant string.
You must use the database schema in the QueryDef and ReturnDef.
If multiple objects are found, either by the QueryDef or by navigating to the relationships, then multiple values are returned, each separated by a comma.
Example 1
In this example, the first parameter specifies whether to include items from a higher configuration or not. The second parameter specifies the default value to be returned if the object specified by the QueryDef is not found in the database.
The third parameter is specified to find the object with the class definition SPFDesignDocMaster and the UID Document1. The fourth parameter specifies to return the description of that object.
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("false","This is default document master description","#SPFDesignDocMaster,.UID='Document1'", ".Description")
Example 2
In this example, the same object is navigated using the SPFPrimaryClassification relationship from end 2 to end 1 to find the required UID property. If the object specified by the QueryDef is not found in the database, then the default string,
"Object is not found", is returned.
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("false","Object is not found","#SPFDesignDocMaster,.UID='Document1'","-SPFPrimaryClassification.UID")
Example 3
In this example, a match on the name is used to find the object, instead of the UID. The name DOCMASTER_NAME is used to match on from the column, instead of entering it manually. If the object is not found, then the default string,
"Object is not found", is returned.
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("false","Object is not found","#SPFDesignDocMaster,.Name=[DOCMASTER_NAME]","-SPFPrimaryClassification.UID")
Example 4
This example shows how multiple relationships can be navigated. -RelDef1+RelDef2.Name navigates RelDef1 from end2 to end1, and RelDef2 from end 1 to end 2. This returns the object name from the end of those relationships. If the
object is not found, then the specified default string value is returned.
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("false","Folder1 is not found","#SPFFolder,.Name='Folder1'","-SPFFolderDocumentVersion+SPFFolderSupportItem.Name")
Example 5
This example shows how to navigate through multiple properties and relationships. It traverses the description and originating organization name properties, as well as the area and files associated with the revision object's versions, to
locate the revision object. If the revision object is found, it returns the UIDs of the files attached to all the versions. If the revision object is not found, then the specified default string value is returned.
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("true", "File UIDs are not found", "#FDWDocumentRevision,.Description=
[CONST_DESC],.OriginatingOrgName='DC',+SDADocRevArea.Name='10',+SPFRevisionVersions-SPFFileComposition.Name=[COMP_FILENAME]", "+SPFRevisionVersions-SPFFileComposition.UID")
GetValueFromTargetSystem
This computed column retrieves the value of a property from the database. It uses the following parameters:
IncludeHigherConfiguration – Used to specify whether to include a higher configuration in the query or not.
QueryDef – Used to find an object in the database. You can specify the class definition of the object and any property values found on that object or a related object. Any number of criteria can be added by separating them with a comma.
This parameter must be a constant string, but it can reference other columns within the string if you enclose each of those columns in brackets [ ]. For example, func.Concat({[TestColumn1], [TestColumn2], [TestColumn3]}). If the object is
not found in the database, then "NULL" value is returned.
ReturnDef - Used to describe the property to be returned from the database. You can specify the relationships to navigate to, followed by the property you want to retrieve. Any number of relationships can be navigated. This parameter
must be a constant string.
You must use the database schema in the QueryDef and ReturnDef.
If multiple objects are found either by the QueryDef or by navigating to the relationships, then multiple values are returned, each separated by a comma.
Example 1
In this example, the first parameter specifies items at a higher configuration or not. The second parameter is specified to find the object with the class definition SPFDesignDocMaster and the UID Document1. The third parameter specifies
to return the Name of that object.
func.GetValueFromTargetSystem("false", "#SPFDesignDocMaster,.UID='Document1'", ".Name")
Example 2
In this example, the same object is navigated using the SPFPrimaryClassification relationship from end 2 to end 1 to find the required UID property.
func.GetValueFromTargetSystem("false","#SPFDesignDocMaster,.UID='Document1'","-SPFPrimaryClassification.UID")
Example 3
In this example, a match on the name is used to find the object, instead of the UID. The name DOCMASTER_NAME is used to match on from the column, instead of entering it manually.
func.GetValueFromTargetSystem("false", "#SPFDesignDocMaster,.Name=[DOCMASTER_NAME]", "-SPFPrimaryClassification.UID")
Example 4
This example shows how multiple relationships can be navigated. -RelDef1+RelDef2.Name navigates RelDef1 from end2 to end1, and RelDef2 from end 1 to end 2. This returns the object name from the end of those relationships. If the
object is not found, then the value, "NULL" is returned.
func.GetValueFromTargetSystem("false", "#SPFFolder,.Name='Folder1'", "-SPFFolderDocumentVersion+SPFFolderSupportItem.Name")
Non-database computed columns
This non-database computed column performs a similar function as the previous computed columns, but does not use SPFRevisionScheme or the database for validation.
SplitAlphaNumericSequence
This computed column splits a value based upon a set of simple defined rules to determine the minor and major versions of documents. It can be used to split any value and is not limited to just document revisions.
SplitAlphaNumericSequence(Input String, ReturnPrimaryValue, DefaultValue)
For example,
func.SplitAlphaNumericSequence([REVISION_CODE],"True","ERROR NO VALUE")
func.SplitAlphaNumericSequence([REVISION_CODE],"False","ERROR NO VALUE")
 The first example returns the first alphanumeric sequence (True) and the second returns the last alphanumeric sequence (False).
Document function examples
Example 1
RevisionCode = 1A, RevisionScheme = RS_Rev1A
Returns the major revision code:
GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_REV1A","1A" ) = 1
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("1A","True","") = 1
Returns the minor revision code:
GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_REV1A","1A" ) = A
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("1A","False","") = A
Example 2
RevisionCode = A01, RevisionScheme = RS_RevA01
Returns the major revision code:
GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_ RevA01","A01" ) = A
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("A01","True","") = A
Returns the minor revision code:
GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_ RevA01"," A01" ) = 01
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("A01","False","") = 01
Example 3
RevisionCode = A, RevisionScheme = RS_RevAlpha
Returns the major revision code:
GetMajorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_ RevAlpha","A" ) = A
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("A","True","") = A
Returns the minor revision code:
GetMinorRevisionCodeFromTargetSystem("RS_ RevAlpha"," A" ) = A
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("A","False","") = A
SplitAlphaNumericSequence("A","False","Error no value") = Error no value
Date and time functions
A date and time function string defines the date time value resulting from a formatting operation and defines the date time value required to convert one date and time string to the required date and time string format.
There are two computed column types for date time values:
