DateTimeColumn - This column is for the date and time for a specific time zone. The parameters are column, format of the input value, and time zone, for example, func.DateTimeColumn([InputColumn], "Input value format",
"TimeZone"). Using this format the input value is converted to SPFDateTime format, yyyy/MM/dd-HH:mm:ss:fff.
For example, the input value is 12/06/2015-09:00:00 and the input value format is "dd/MM/yyyy-HH:mm:ss", time zone is "Central Standard Time". Therefore, the input value is converted to SPFDateTime format, 2015/06/12-
14:00:00:000,GMT.
YMDColumn - This column is for the YMD format. The parameters are the column name and the format of the input value, for example func.YMD([InputColumn], "Input value format"). Using this format, the input value is converted to
the SPFDate format, yyyy/MM/dd.
For example, the input value is dd/MM/yyyy format (12/06/2015), which is converted to SPFDate format that is yyyy/MM/dd (2015/06/12).
The DateTime rule should only be configured on YMD or DateTime computed columns.
The time zones mentioned in the computed columns must match with the Microsoft Time Zone Index Values available in your system.
ConvertToHash function
The ConvertToHash function can be used to generate a unique hash code for objects when you have case-sensitive data in your CSV file, but you are loading to a case-insensitive database. The ConvertToHash function takes the input
string and returns a capitalized hash value using the Secure Hash Algorithm 1 (SHA-1) algorithm.
For a given object, the SHA-1 algorithm consistently returns a unique alpha-numeric value known as the hash code for that object.
For example, you may have two company names in your CSV file, ABC Industries and Abc Industries, each with different capitalizations. If you are loading the CSV file to a case-insensitive database, the load will fail. In this case, you can
use the ConvertToHash function to map the ConvertToHash computed column to the SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode property for the object.
func.ConvertToHash(“ABC Industries”) returns 170C0B71E9F68CC294A6614BAE3E7347C208EA48
func.ConvertToHash(“Abc Industries”) returns 98761FC6EB12227F5367A34379998F8055373E1A
How can I identify case-sensitive data in a case-insensitive database during the import process?
You can use the code samples provided below to create your own XML files and load into the database using the Import Data feature. For more information, see Create a load file.
ISPFCaseSensitiveObject.xml – Comprises the interface definition, ISPFCaseSensitiveObject, and the property definition, SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode.
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<Container SoftwareVersion="05.02.00.18" IObject_ContainerID="" Scope="Schema" CompSchema="SPFReferenceComponent" IObject_OBID="Auto" IObject_CreationDate="Now"
IObject_TerminationDate="9999/12/31-23:59:59:999" IObject_LastUpdatedDate="Now" IObject_CreationUser="superuser" IObject_DomainUID="Auto" IObject_UniqueKey="Auto"
IRel_DomainUID1="Auto" IRel_DomainUID2="Auto" >
<InterfaceDef>
<IObject UID="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" Name="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" Description="Case sensitive object" />
<IInterfaceDef />
<IInterfaceRole />
<ISchemaObj DisplayName="Case sensitive object" />
<IPropCategory />
<IEnumMetadata />
<IInterfaceDefExt />
</InterfaceDef>
<Rel>
<ISchemaObj />
<IObject UID="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject.Implies.IObject" />
<IRel UID1="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" UID2="IObject" DefUID="Implies" />
</Rel>
<PropertyDef>
<IObject UID="SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode" Name="SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode" Description="Case sensitive hash code" />
<IPropertyDef />
<ISchemaObj DisplayName="Case sensitive hash code" />
<IPropertyDefExt />
</PropertyDef>
<Rel>
<IRel DefUID="Exposes" UID1="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" UID2="SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode" />
<IObject UID="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject.Exposes.SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode" />
<ISchemaObj />
</Rel>
<Rel>
<IRel DefUID="ScopedBy" UID1="SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode" UID2="string" />
<IObject UID="SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode.ScopedBy.string" />
<ISchemaObj />
</Rel>
</Container>
HelperSchema-To-Imply-ISPFCaseSensitiveObject.xml – This code will optionally imply and realize the class definitions that require the hash value. You can specify the object class definitions and interface definitions in this file and
load it.
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<Container Scope="Schema" CompSchema="SPFReferenceComponent" IObject_ContainerID="" SoftwareVersion="05.02.00.18" IObject_OBID="Auto" IObject_CreationDate="Now"
IObject_TerminationDate="9999/12/31-23:59:59:999" IObject_LastUpdatedDate="Now" IObject_CreationUser="superuser" IObject_DomainUID="Auto" IObject_UniqueKey="Auto"
IRel_DomainUID1="Auto" IRel_DomainUID2="Auto">
<Rel>
<ISchemaObj />
<IObject UID="XXXX_InterfaceDef.Implies.ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" />
<IRel UID1="XXXX_InterfaceDef" UID2="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" DefUID="Implies" />
</Rel>
<Rel>
<ISchemaObj />
<IObject UID="YYYY_ClassDef.Realizes.ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" />
<IRel UID1="YYYY_ClassDef" UID2="ISPFCaseSensitiveObject" IsRequired="False" DefUID="Realizes" />
</Rel>
</Container>
During import mapping, you must select the ISPFCaseSensitiveObject optional interface for the object and the ConvertToHash computed column must be mapped to the SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode property.
For new objects, the database query definition in the load mapping must be defined as .SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode.
For existing objects, the load object query definition in the load mapping can be defined as .Name. The query definition can be changed to .SPFCaseSensitiveHashCode after all the objects in the database are assigned the hash
code.
Computed column .NET methods
Microsoft .NET methods calculate some of the computed columns. The following table provides further details on how some computer columns function:
Function
Description
Concat
Concatenates a set of input strings.
Divide
Divides the first operand by the second operand and throws an exception if the divisor is 0.
IndexOf
Returns the character position of the value parameter if the specified string is found, or -1 if it is not found. If the value is empty, the
returned value is 0 (zero).
Join
Returns a string that consists of the elements in the value delimited by the separator string. If the value is an empty array, the
method returns an empty string "".
Left
Sets the startIndex parameter to 0. See Substring for further details.
Length
Returns the number of char objects in the current string object. This function does not return the number of Unicode characters,
because a particular Unicode character might be represented by more than one char object (standard character).
Minus
Returns the difference between two double values.
Multiply
Calculates the product of its operands.
PadLeft
Returns a new string that is equivalent to this, but left-aligned and padded on the right with as many padding characters as needed
to create a length of totalWidth. However, if totalWidth is less than the length of this, the method returns a reference to the existing
instance. If totalWidth is equal to the length of this, the method returns a new string that is identical.
PadRight
Returns a new string that is equivalent to this instance, but left-aligned and padded on the right with as many padding characters as
needed to create a length of totalWidth. However, if totalWidth is less than the length of this, the method returns a reference to the
existing instance. If totalWidth is equal to the length of this value, the method returns a new string that is identical.
RegexMatch
Searches the specified input string for the first occurrence of the regular expression supplied in the pattern.
RegexReplace
Returns a new string that is identical to the input string, except that a replacement string takes the place of each matched string.
Replace
Returns a string that is equivalent to the current string, except that all instances of oldValue are replaced with newValue. If oldValue
is not found in the current instance, the method returns the current instance unchanged. This function throws an exception if
oldValue null is empty or null.
SubString
Returns a string object equivalent to the substring of the length specified in the length parameter, beginning at the starting index
number in the current String object.
-or-
Returns an empty field if the startIndex parameter is equal to the length of the current object and length is 0 (zero).
Sum
Returns the sum of the two operands.
ToLower
(Lowercase)
Returns the current string in lowercase.
ToUpper
(Uppercase)
Returns the current string in uppercase.
Trim
Passes a single character. Removes all occurrences of the specified character from the beginning and end of the string.
TrimEnd
Passes a single character. Removes all occurrences of the specified character from the end of the current string object.
TrimStart
Passes a single character. Removes all occurrences of the specified character from the beginning of the string object.
 For more information on Microsoft .NET methods and their operators, see https://msdn.microsoft.com.
Regular expression for evaluating multiple value columns in a CSV file
You can create a regular expression in a computed column and use it to evaluate the data pattern in columns which have multiple values in your CSV file.
Example
Scenario: The data in two multiple value columns need to be compared and validated.
MultipleValueColumn1 – This column contains multiple values which need to be compared with the values in another column.
MultipleValueColumn2 – This column also contains multiple values which need to be validated against those in the MultipleValueColumn1.
To evaluate the two columns, create the following computed columns:
RegExPatternForValidation - This creates the required regular expression pattern from the column, MultipleValueColumn2, in the CSV file:
func.Concat({"^(?=.*", func.Replace([MultipleValueColumn2], ",", ")(?=.*"), ").*$"})
ValidateColumnValues - This uses the regular expression pattern to compare the values in MultipleValueColumn1 and MultipleValueColumn2:
func.Decode(func.RegexMatch([MultipleValueColumn1],[RegExPatternForValidation]), {"False", "False"}, "True")
Result: If the values provided in MultipleValueColumn2 matches with the values present in the MultipleValueColumn1, the ValidateColumnValues computed column returns the value as True.
Validation rules and actions
The following table lists all the validation rules supplied with Data Take-On and the actions that invoke them.
 = Object 
 = Property  = Relationship 
 = Relationship Property
 = If the same object is found for Create Update locally, this is reported as True.
 = The relationship must also have the Create Update action on it.
 Only one Check Staging File Exists property action is allowed per object. If there is more than one, an error is raised and the loading is blocked. For more information, see Review load stage errors.
The Compare Value rule ignores null values in the database if the object property action or relationship property action is set to Compare Create, but respects the null values if the action is set to Compare Value. This is because
exporting with a Compare Create action requires blank database values to be updated without altering any existing values.
The Check Exist action is invoked for Staging System Cardinality and Target System Cardinality rules only when the user selects this action during a rule creation.
For more information on how to define validation rules and create rulesets, see Define validation rules and rule sets.
Validation rule description
The following table lists all the validation rules and their descriptions supplied with Data Take-On.
Rule name
Description
Check Claimed to Sub Configuration
Checks to see if a plant-level item has been claimed to the project.
Check Duplicate Issue Dates
Checks to see if there are revisions with duplicate issue dates for the same master document.
Check File Exists
Checks to see that a file exists in the specified path and that security has been set to configure access permissions.
Check For Cyclic Relationship
Checks to determine if the same object is set on both End 1 and End 2 of a relationship definition.
Check Object Exists
Checks to see if the object exists in the database already.
Check File Exists for Document Revision
Checks to see if any file is attached to the document revision if the document revision is not a planned revision.
Check Parallel Claim
Checks to see if the item is claimed to a parallel project under the same plant.
Check Is Claimable
Checks to see if the data object is claimable into the project and prevents the object from being loaded.
Check is Revisable
Checks to see if the document is revisable to the project and prevents the document revision from being loaded.
Check Revised to Sub Config
Checks to see if a plant-level document has been revised to the project.
Check Revised to Parallel Config
Checks to see if the document item has been revised to a parallel project under the same plant.
Check Properties Against Schema
Checks to see if the target property definitions are valid for the target object type or relationship type, and if the values are
valid for these properties, including whether required values are present.
Check Properties Against Classification
Checks to see if the target property definitions are valid for the target object classification provided in the CSV as a part of
the Data Take-On job or if they already exist in the database. The rule validates the properties based on the column sets
configured in the database for the classification, including if mandatory properties are present.
Check Relationship Exists
Checks to see if the relationship exists in the database.
Check Unique Key
Checks to see if a specific unique key already exists elsewhere in the system and cannot be implicitly claimed. This check
runs only on the database and detects duplicates in the source files.
Check Value and UOM
Checks to see if the unit of measure (UOM) of the object property is the same as the UOM configured in the database. It
also checks to see if the object property value is defined within a specified range of minimum and maximum values.
Compare All Property Values For Objects
Checks to see if all the staging area object property values are the same as the database object property values.
Compare ENS Value
Checks to see if the object name specified in the CSV file is the same as the object name computed by the rule using the
Engineering Numbering System (ENS) definition of the object class. The ENS definition is configured in the database.
Compare Value
Checks to see if the staging area object property or relationship property value is the same as the database object
property or relationship property value.
Compare Value Against Environment Variable
Checks to see if the staging area property value specified in the CSV file is available in the database using the
environment variable.
Date Time
Checks to see if an object property or relationship property must validate against some date and time, YMD, or DMY. Must
be in invariant format.
Double
Checks to see if an object property value or relationship property value has a minimum and a maximum value.
Integer
Checks to see if an object property value or relationship property value is defined within a specified range by a minimum
and maximum value.
Regular Expression
Checks to see if an object property value or relationship property value adheres to a specified pattern.
Staging System Cardinality
Checks for the cardinalities between the related objects.
String Does Not Start With
Checks to see that the value does not start with the given value.
String Length
Checks to see if the length of an object property or relationship property is within a specified minimum and maximum limit
or adheres to a specified pattern.
String Not Equal To
Checks to see that the value is not equal to the given value.
Target System Cardinality
Checks for the minimum and maximum cardinality values between related objects from the database.
The Check Duplicate Issue Dates rule cannot be used if the revision object is configured to be identified using a query definition.
The Check File Exists rule validates the existence of the file specified in your CSV file by checking in your Data Take-On application server file system or your database application file system, depending on the property action.
The Check File Exists for Document Revision rule validates the document revision and checks if any file is attached to it, unless the document revision is a planned revision. In the case of a planned revision, the rule does not
check for attached files.
The Check is Claimable rule is applicable only when data objects are loaded into collaboration projects.
The Check is Revisable rule is applicable only when document revisions are loaded into collaboration projects.
The Check Unique Key rule can be used in collaboration projects to avoid loading of duplicate data for non-claimable items.
The Compare Value rule ignores null values in the database if the object property action or relationship property action is set to Compare Create, but respects the null values if the action is set to Compare Value. This is because
exporting with a Compare Create action requires blank database values to be updated without altering any existing values.
The Check Revised to Sub Config rule and Check Revised to Parallel rule are not auto generated in the supplied system, but they can be changed to auto generate using the system options.
The Regular Expression rule allows you to create a regular expression by including object properties in the pattern. The object property text should exactly match the case of the object property in the database. Additionally, you
must add an ampersand (&) around each property for it to be parsed. For example, the following syntax will search for two digits, followed by a hyphen, and then an object property: ^(\d\d-&OBJ.<Property Definition UID>&)$.
The Check Properties Against Schema rule checks for:
The disabled enums and unmapped properties and reports the results in the validation report. You can use the Allow Disabled Enum Entries checkbox in the Edit Rule dialog box to determine whether the disabled enums are
to be reported in the validation report. The Check for Unmapped Properties checkbox in the Edit Rule dialog box allows you to check if any of the properties of an object are not mapped to the database properties.
The property values scoped by an enumerated list level type by matching them to the parent enumerated list values. For example, an instrument has two properties, Construction Status and Construction Status2.
ConstructionStatus is scoped by EnumListType, and ConstructionStatus2 is scoped by EnumLevelListType. The ConstructionStatus2 property value depends on the value of the ConstructionStatus property. If the input CSV file
specifies ConstructionStatus as New and ConstructionStatus2 as Relocate, which is not in the New list, the Construction Status2 property is reported as invalid in the validation report.
ConstructionStatus
(EnumListType)
ConstructionStatus2
(EnumListLevelType)
New
New
Existing
Relocate
Revamp
Temporary
The Check Value and UOM rule checks the UOM of the object property when the object property value is blank. You can use the Allow Blank Values checkbox to specify that the object property value is blank.
The Check Properties Against Classification rule depends on the attribution configured for the database. However, if no attribution is configured, the following options come into play:
The Allow any property option is selected by default in the Edit Rule dialog box and no properties are validated by Data Take-On.
If the Allow common properties only option is selected, the data will be validated against the common properties selected in the Edit Rule dialog box.
Common properties are shared by all classified items irrespective of their actual classification. For example, a tag can have common properties like Tag Status and Criticality Rating, which is applicable to all tags, whether they
are classified as a pipe or a pump.
 The object's classification must be provided as part of the import mapping, either as a physical column in the CSV file, or through a computed column which derives it from the database.
For the Compare ENS Value rule, the following points are to be noted:
The object name is computed based on the ENS item values and compared with the name provided in the CSV file. To compute the object name, the ENS item values are taken from:
the database for Constant and Environment Type ENS item types
the CSV file or the import mappings for Related Item, Property, and Function ENS item types
The ISPFENSRefDetails interface must be realized on the class definition of the objects being exported.
All the columns provided in the CSV file can be mapped to the respective objects, properties, and relationships, except the sequence number, for which a new property, ENSSequenceNumber, is exposed on the
ISPFENSRefDetails interface.
The ENS must be suppressed in the database as below:
by selecting the Suppress ENS checkbox in the Load <Object> form for running Data Take-On jobs or submittal jobs.
How is the ENS definition configured in the database?
The ENS definition is composed of various components called ENS items that can extract data from constants and other properties on the object or related objects.
Actions
When you define a column header in Data Take-On, you assign it an action. This action indicates what Data Take-On will do with the data imported from the related column in the CSV file. The kinds of operations supported varies,
depending on whether the column header is mapped to a primary or a related object, a primary object property or a related object property, relationship, or relationship property.
 The list of actions available for a property or a relationship is determined by the action selected for the object. For more information, see Load mapping actions.
Primary and related object actions
Data Take-On supports the following object actions:
Action
Description
Check Exists
Checks that the object exists in the database.
Create Update
Creates a new object and updates the existing object in the database.
Delete
Deletes an object in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the object in the database.
Terminate
The object is terminated in the database.
Update
The object is updated in the database.
Validate Only
No action is taken on the object in the database, but if errors are found then they are propagated to the related objects.
Primary and related object property actions
Data Take-On supports the following property actions:
Action
Description
Check Staging File Exists
Checks that the staging property file exists in the staging area.
Compare Create
Invokes the compare value rule, which creates an error if the database value is different. The database property is only overwritten if the value is not
set.
Compare Value
Invokes the compare value rule, which never changes the value in the database.
Create Update
Creates a new property and updates the existing property in the database.
Delete
Deletes a property in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the property in the database.
Terminate
The property is terminated in the database.
Validate Only
No action is taken on the property in the database, but if errors are found then they are propagated to the related objects.
 Using Data Take-On, you cannot delete or terminate the required properties of an object that exists in the database.
Relationship actions
Data Take-On supports the following relationship actions:
Action
Description
Check Exists
Checks that the relationship exists in the database.
Create Update
Creates a new relationship and updates the existing relationship in the database.
Delete
Deletes a relationship in the database.
No Action
No action is taken on the relationship in the database.
Terminate
The relationship is terminated in the database.
Load mapping actions
Load mappings are restricted by what actions you select when creating the object, as there are permitted combinations of actions. For example, the actions available for the Delete class definition mapping are Compare Value and No
Action.
The following table shows the supported combination of default actions available in Data Take-On.
 Only one Check Staging File Exists property action is allowed per object. If there is more than one, an error is raised and the loading is blocked. For more information, see Review load stage errors.
Relationship cardinality rules
Staging system cardinality
For this rule, select a class definition and a relationship definition for the rule, and select the direction of the class definition specified in the relationship. Two Boolean properties are used to enforce the minimum and maximum cardinality of
1. If neither the minimum nor the maximum cardinalities are set, no validation takes place.
For example, if you want to enforce only one primary classification, set both properties to True.
Enforce min cardinality of 1
Enforce max cardinality of 1
Therefore, if no relationship definition exists and the enforce minimum is set to 1, an error message is generated. If multiple relationships for the relationship definition are found and the enforce maximum cardinality is set to 1, an error
message is generated.
Target system cardinality
For this rule, select a class definition and a relationship definition to run the rule, and select the direction of the class definition specified in the relationship. The cardinality information is defined by the relationship definition in the database
and is used to perform the validation against the data in the staging area.
If the selected class definition is on End1 of the relationship, you must select End1 as the direction, and the rule will check for the cardinality from End1 of the relationship definition. To check for the cardinality from End2 of the relationship
definition, you must create a separate rule by selecting the class definition on End2 of the relationship and selecting End2 as the direction of the relationship.
Schedulers
The following schedulers are used by Data Take-On to process the Data Take-On jobs. We recommend that you turn on all four schedulers before running any Data Take-On jobs.
Job Cleanup Scheduler - Identifies jobs stuck in active workflows and failed jobs. After 24 hours, the scheduler fails the jobs that are stuck in a processing state and any jobs without a scheduler task. After the jobs are failed, the
scheduler deletes the staging tables in the database. In each case of these failed jobs, you must create a new job and reload the data. The default polling interval for this scheduler is 1,800,000 milliseconds. For more information about
failed jobs, see Set Data Take-On default options.
Export Task Scheduler - Loads the Data Take-On job data into the HxGN SDx2 database. This scheduler task is specific to each job and is deleted after the job is processed. The default polling interval for this scheduler is 600,000
milliseconds.
PBS Sync Scheduler - Replicates the Plant Breakdown Structure of a consolidated design component, based on the Common Schema, in any required tool design component in HxGN SDx2. The default polling interval for this scheduler
is 1,800,000 milliseconds. For more information, see Configuring schedulers.
Recovery Scheduler - Attempts to recover the jobs stuck in active workflows. The default polling interval for this scheduler is 3,600,000 milliseconds. For more information, see Recovery scheduler task.
If the following scheduler tasks fail, a notification is sent to your business administrator and system administrator. They should investigate the reason for failure and retry the failed task:
Job Cleanup Scheduler
PBS Sync Scheduler
Recovery Scheduler
Supported CSV file formats
Data Take-On supports the data import from two types of CSV file formats, which allows the same validation rules to be used regardless of the imported data file and the loaded data to be in the same invariant format as the database.
CSV file format.
CSV raw attribute file format (inverted file format).
 For more information on using computed columns and functions, see Use the Computed column.
Each CSV file format is explained as follows:
CSV file format
The CSV file format is a standard delimited text file that uses a comma, or other separators, to separate values for columns in a spreadsheet. For example:
This information is shown in the staging area as shown in the following illustration:
CSV Raw Attribute file format (inverted file format)
CSV Raw Attribute file format is used to load design properties. This is when a single object has many properties that makes it impractical to represent each property in its own column. Each row in the CSV Raw Attribute file format refers
to a different property definition, whereas the property definition is named in the column mapping for a standard CSV file. For example:
This information is shown in the staging area as shown in the following illustration:
CSV files not in invariant format
What is invariant format?
Invariant format is a property that does not change when a specified transformation is applied on a CSV file. For example, Data Take-On uses the invariant format for thousand separator “,” and for decimal separator “.”. So a value of 1.001
would always be interpreted as 1 with three decimal places even if your intention may have been 1001, as the decimal point was really a thousand separator. Data Take-On will not return any validation errors on properties using the
invariant format, but you run the risk of not getting the intended data into the system.
For more information on CSV files not in invariant format, see CSV files not in invariant format.
Invariant format CSV files
If the data in a CSV file is not supplied in invariant format, it is possible that Data Take-On will interpret the data as valid data and not return any validation errors when loading the data. Therefore, you run a risk of not getting the intended
data into the database. For example, submitting a value of 1.001 would be interpreted as 1 with three decimal places, when the intention may have been 1001, as the decimal point was really a thousand separator. Similar conditions can
occur with dates.
If you import a CSV file that is not in invariant format, you must use computed columns to convert the data to invariant format. This applies to all dates, time offsets, and numerics in localized format.
 Data Take-On uses the invariant format as defined by HxGN SDx2 as follows:
Date Time Format: yyyy/MM/dd - HH:mm:ss:fff
Timezone: GMT with no day light savings adjustment
Year Month Day Format: yyyy/MM/dd
Numeric format:
Thousand separator ","
Decimal separator "."
For more information on using computed columns and functions, see Use the Computed column and Functions.
Load objects configured with ENS in the database
The Engineering Numbering System (ENS) provides automated object identification, which allows project-specific identification formats to be configured as templates. These templates guide the user when creating new objects.
The ENS definition is composed of various components called ENS items that can extract data from constants and from other properties on the object or related objects. These components are configured in sequence with additional
delimiter characters to define the required format.
If a class definition is configured to use ENS in the database, then the object name is set by the ENS definition instead of using the actual name of the object provided in the CSV file during loading.
If the ENS definition is related to a property definition, such as Name, and this property definition is referred to in a UID or unique key, then Data Take-On fails to identify the object and leads to load failures. This is because the relationships
are processed as the UIDs or unique keys and these get changed according to the ENS definition.
 It is not recommended to run jobs in parallel when using ENS because it might lead to data corruption and unexpected results.
Configuration for a tag object
To map a tag object available in the CSV file with the tag object in the database, do the following:
Ensure the Tag class definition optionally realizes the ISPFENSRefDetails interface. This interface exposes the ENSRefName property to store the tag name from the CSV file.
<Rel>
 <IObject UID="Tag.Realizes.ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 <ISchemaObj />
 <IRel UID1="Tag" DefUID="Realizes" UID2="ISPFENSRefDetails" />
 </Rel>
Create an import mapping with a computed column, as shown in the example below.
Map the tag name from the CSV file to the ENSRefName property of the tag object.
Get the actual tag name from the database and map it to the Name property.
 The ENSRefName property definition is used to store the original name of the tag object from the CSV file and helps Data Take-On identify the tag object within the database during the update, delete, and terminate object actions.
For example, if ENS is configured on the Name property of a tag, such as [Current Config][-][TG][-][Sequence], and TAG1 is loaded into the database where the configuration is PlantA and 'TG' is a constant, then the name of the tag in the
database is set to PlantA-TG-00001 and the ENSRefName property to TAG1.
Example CSV file

TAG NAME

Tag
SDVTag

SDVFDWTagClassification

Computed

TAG NAME
Computed column for COMP_Actual_TS_Tag_Name
func.GetTargetSystemValueIfEmptyReturnDefault("true", [TAGNAME], "#Tag,.ENSRefName=[TAGNAME]", ".Name")
Computed column for COMP_Tag_Ref_Name
func.Concat({[TAG NAME]})
Configuration for a document object
If you have configured ENS for a document master, revision, and/or version, you can use the ENSRefName property on the ISPFENSRefDetails interface definition to store the document information from the CSV file. This interface is
realized by the FDWDocumentMaster and the FDWDocumentRevision class definitions. The document number value must be set on the master object’s ENSRefName property. The document number, major revision, and minor revision
must be set on the ENSRefName property of the revision object.
To map a document object available in the CSV file with the document object in the database, do the following:
Create an import mapping with a computed column, as shown in the example below.
Map the document name from the CSV file to the ENSRefName property of the document master object.
Map the document name and the revision code to the ENSRefName property of the document revision object.
Get the actual document name from the database and map it to the Name property of the document master and the revision object.
 The files attached to the document can be identified using the query definition, +SPFFileComposition.ENSRefName,.Name. This query definition needs to be modified, for your staging schema.

Job reports
You can use Data Take-On to store the information related to a job in the database. The stored information can be used as a metadata for the generation of reports. For this purpose, a new schema is provided to record the job information,
such as Import Details, Validation,and Load Details. If you delete a job from the database, only that job is deleted, its details are still stored in the database. This happens only if you select the Record Job Details option when creating a
job definition, and associate it with that specific job. You can find the job summary details in the staging area.
The following image shows the new class definitions and the corresponding property definitions that are modeled to hold job details.
The following class definitions are delivered with the software.
SDVJobSummary - Stores the basic details of a job, such as Import Stage Details and Validation Stage Details.
SDVJobSummarySeverityItem - Displays the severity items of a job, such as warnings, errors, and the count.
SDVJobSummaryTarget - Stores load stage details of the database.
SDVJobSummaryImportFile - Stores import file that is used to execute a Job.
Each class definition contains property definitions that allows you to define the properties of a job, such as name, description, and other properties. The SDVJobSummarySeverityItem, SDVJobSummaryTarget, and
SDVJobSummaryImportFile class definitions are associated with the main class definition SDVJobSummary.
The job details are stored in the staging area even after deletion of the job from Data Take-On.
To view job details, you must be working in a role associated with the DTOAdmin access group.
Select the Record Job Details option to store the job information such as Stage details, Validation, Load details and other job information.
For more information about Data Take-On, see What is Data Take-On?
For more information about running Data Take-On jobs, see How do I use Data Take-On to load documents and data?
.
Data Take-On troubleshooting
The following topics will help you troubleshoot any issues encountered while using Data Take-On.
Import
The following topics provide troubleshooting for issues that may arise during the import process.
Import mapping: Mapped related object property is not saved
Error message
Only column headers are saved. Mappings could not be saved because the parent mapping for the primary or related object is not saved. Ensure the parent mapping is saved.
Problem
During import mapping, the mapping for the mapped related object property is not saved.
This might occur if the parent of the mapped related object property is saved after the mapped related object property, or a staging relationship definition with the same name already exists in the system.
Solution
Save the parent of the mapped related object property first, then save the mapped related object property.
Replace the prefix in the Staging Relationship Definition box with a different value.
Validation
The following topics provide troubleshooting for issues that may arise during the validation process.
Invalid value for enum level on property
Error message
Value is invalid for enum level on target property
Problem
The above error is observed in the validation report after validation stage.
Cause
The target property scoped by the EnumListLevelType is mapped to an enum value. However, either the parent EnumListType is not part of the import mapping or it does not contain the enum type value.
Solution
You must ensure that the parent EnumListType is part of the import mapping and that it contains the correct enum type value. For more information, see Validation rule description.
Job Processing - CSV files
The following topics provide troubleshooting for issues that may arise when jobs are processed in Data Take-On Job Management.
Error when file object is loaded before the object to which it is attached
Error message
Object reference not set to the instance of an object
Problem
When loading files, an error occurs when you attempt to load a file object before you load the object to which it is attached, such as when you set the load object weight to load a design file before the design document that is related to the
file.
Cause
This issue occurs because the file exists in the database before the revision to which it should be attached.
Solution
You must set the load object weighting to load a file object before loading the file to which it is attached. For example, you must set the load object weight so that the design document will be loaded before the design file.
 You can adjust the load object weight by selecting Data Take-On > Load Definitions > Actions 
 > Create Load Object Mappings > Details > Object Weighting. For more information on setting the load object weightings, see
Define load object mappings.
Object weights have not been configured correctly
Error message
File CSV Validation Failed [one or more load object mappings have zero object weight]
Problem
Job cannot be processed in Data Take-On.
Solution
Ensure that the object weights are set correctly for load object mappings and that none of the weights is set to zero.
 You can adjust the load object weight by selecting Data Take-On > Load Definitions > Actions 
 > Create Load Object Mappings > Details > Object Weighting. For more information on setting the load object weightings, see
Define load object mappings.
Job is not created with error: Object successfully created, but failed to be attached to the workflow
Problem
You may get an error when trying to create a Data Take-On job in the Data Take-On feature set.
Object successfully created, but failed to be attached to the workflow
Cause
This error may occur in the following cases:
In the CSV file, there are rows with columns that do not match the column header.
The number of columns in the CSV file do not match with the import mapping columns created in Data Take-On.
Solution
You must ensure that the data in the CSV file matches the data in the specified import definition by comparing the CSV file and the import mapping columns in the import definition. You must correct the data in the CSV file and create a
new Data Take-On job with the corrected CSV file. For more information on import mapping, see Define import mapping.
Load fails with error: The request limit for the database is 2000/3200 and has been reached
Error message
The request limit for the database is 2000/3200 and has been reached
Problem
Unable to load data into the database.
Cause
The database resources (worker processes) are insufficient.
Solution
Contact your system administrator.
Load fails at stage 1: Transferring data
Problem
Loading process may fail while transferring data with the following error message:
User must be related to organization to continue with registration
Cause
If no tool signature is available for the tool and the configuration into which the data is being loaded, Data Take-On creates a tool signature and then runs the Data Take-On job. However, if the user running a Data Take-On job is not related
to any organization, the job fails with this error message.
Solution
The user running the job must be updated with the correct organization by the administrator.
Load fails with error: Transferring data failed
Error message
Transferring data failed
Problem
Loading process may fail during the transferring data stage with the above error.
Solution
You can get additional information by viewing the exact error message in the View Summary page from the Actions 
 menu of the selected job in Show My Data Take-On Jobs. For more information, see Viewing job progress and
statistics.
Load fails at stage 3: Loading data to database
Problem
Loading process may fail during the third stage of loading data to the database with the following error message:
Unique key or query def was not unique
Cause
This error occurs when the object you are trying to create already exists in the database.
Solution
Remove the duplicate object from your system.
Load fails at stage 3: Data is partially loaded into the database
Problem
The loading process fails during the third stage of loading data to the database. For example, you load 1000 tags, and the process stops after loading 500 tags.
Solution
Run a new Data Take-On job using the same CSV file. The 500 tags that were loaded successfully in the previous job will be updated and the remaining 500 tags will now be created.
How do I capture unstructured data?
When a large amount of unstructured and unintelligent asset life cycle data needs to be captured and organized, Data Capture can be used to capture, review, validate and convert the data to an intelligent and manageable format. By
automating these processes, you can significantly reduce operational costs and streamline your work processes.
Data Capture is an effective one-stop solution to:
Capture the unstructured data
Convert the unstructured data to a structured and manageable format
Validate the information
Update latest information
Search from large amounts of information
Integrate information by creating relationships and cross-references
View and manage the structured data from anywhere
What roles are available for use with Data Capture?
There are two roles available for use with Data Capture:
Data Capture Administrator - Users with this role can configure and manage Data Capture.
Data Capture Reviewer - Users with this role can review progress, revisions and data.
 To access the Data Capture feature set, the Data Capture Administrator role must be configured in the ConfigurationTop apart from other configurations.
What are the limitations when extracting content?
The following limitations apply when extracting content from the documents. However, these parameters will change regularly as we add more features and continuously enhance our solution.
Document classification
Only the Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) classification is supported for content extraction. To classify a document as P&ID, ensure that the EnumEnum UID in the document classifications provided in the starter pack is set
to EE83. For more information, see Use the starter pack to prepare your digital twin and Document classification mappings.
Other document classifications are not supported.
File limitations
Multiple files for a single document are not supported. Only one file per document can be processed.
Multi-page files are not supported. Only single-page files can be processed.
Files with a black background are not supported. Ensure documents have a light background.
Currently, navigation is not supported for a few design drawing file types, such as .dwg, .dgn.
Document limitations
Currently, we recommend you to limit the number of documents that need to be processed to 200.
Where do I start with Data Capture?
If you have many documents that need to be processed using Data Capture, this section provides some guidance on how to process them in an optimal way.
 Before you start using Data Capture, ensure that your tag and document classifications and mappings are correctly set up as described in Tenant setup overview, Document classification mappings, and Tag classification
mappings.
To make sure that the process runs optimally, we recommend that you start by processing a small subset of your P&ID documents and then check that they are correctly processed before moving onto the remainder of your P&ID
documents.
The procedure for getting started with Data Capture is described below.
1. Select a representative subset of your documents, for example, select 20 out of your 500 P&IDs.
2. Start the content extraction task for the documents as described in Extract content from the documents.
3. When the content extraction task completes, review the tags that were extracted from your documents before they are created. For more information, see Review the extracted tags.
While reviewing the tags, you might notice the following:
Tags are identified but are not fully qualified, that is, you need to include a unit in the tag name. To resolve this, configure tag naming rules. For information, see Configure Data Capture.
Off-page connectors (OPCs) are not identified correctly. To resolve this, configure examples of OPC names. For information, see Create an off-page connector (OPC) naming example.
Tags or OPCs are missing from the list of extracted tags. To resolve this, manually create any tags or OPCs that were not extracted from the document.
How do I manually create tags and OPCs?
1. At the top-right of the document, select Create new drawing representation 
, and create each tag or OPC as follows:
a. Select the area around the tag or OPC in the document.
b. Select whether you are creating a tag or OPC.
c. Enter the name in Fully Qualified Name.
 You cannot create a tag or OPC with the same name as another tag or OPC in the CET.
d. If you are creating a tag, select the required class.
 You can also specify an area and unit for the tag, if required.
e. Select Create.
 You can select another document in the CET, and continue to create tags and OPCs in that document.
2. When you have finished manually creating tags and OPCs, select End drawing representation creation 
.
The confidence score should be considered together with the number of tags extracted from the document. For example, if there is only one tag then it should be reviewed even if the confidence score is 100%.
4. If you need to create any naming rules before continuing with the extraction, reject the content extraction task and, once the naming rules are created, re-process the document(s). For information on how to re-process documents,
see Extract content from the documents.
5. When you are satisfied that the documents have been correctly processed and the tags were successfully extracted, repeat the procedure for the remaining P&ID documents.
How do I verify that tags are correctly hotspotted after the content extraction task is complete?
Once the content extraction task is complete, you can view the tags in the document to verify they are correctly hotspotted. For more information, see What happens after content is extracted?
Configure Data Capture
Before you upload documents and extract data from them, we recommend you define the tag naming rules and examples. Although, AI can identify and extract tags and off-page connectors from the documents, these rules ensure
extraction of fully qualified tags and correct off-page connectors.
Tag naming rule consists of multiple tag naming rule parts with separators in between if required.
Create a naming rule part
1. In the Data Capture feature set, select Create Naming Rule Part.
2. Complete the tabs to create the naming rule part as follows:
 The tabs displayed vary depending on the type of part you are creating.
a. Setup Details
1. Type a name for the naming rule part.
2. Select the type of part. The type can be:
Document Metadata Property to select a document property as the part.
Dynamic to set the part to be another non-constant value.
 The tabs that are available in the remainder of the wizard will change to suit the type of part selected.
b. Document Metadata
This tab is only available if you selected Document Metadata Property as the type of part.
Choose the required relationship from the list provided.
 Recommended relationships are indicated with 
.
 Select 
 to display the selected relationships.
c. Mapping
This tab is only available if you selected Document Metadata Property as the type of part.
Select whether a mapping is required and, if it is, choose the required mapping from the list.
d. Examples
This tab is only available if you selected Dynamic as the type of part.
Example parts help the system to know what to look for when identifying tags in your documents. The more example parts you add, the better the system will be at extracting tags from your documents.
Select 
 and enter an example of a part. Repeat to add additional examples.
3. On the Summary tab, check the details selected for the naming rule part.
 To update a setting, click on the applicable tab and update the setting, as necessary.
4. Click Finish to save the naming rule part.
Create a tag naming rule
A tag naming rule enables you to define the parts of a tag name, and relate them to existing objects in the database. Before you create a tag naming rule, you should create any required naming rule parts as described in Create a naming
rule part.
1. In the Data Capture feature set, select Create Tag Naming Rule.
2. Complete the tabs to create the tag naming rule as follows:
a. Setup Details
1. Type a name for the tag naming rule.
2. Select the type of tag.
3. To make the tag naming rule available to other users, select Active. You can leave this option cleared until you have finished making your changes and select it at a later time.
 For each configuration, there can be only one active tag naming rule for each tag type. When a rule is set to active, you are asked to confirm that you want to disable the previously active rule for that tag type.
b. Rule Builder
1. Select 
 and select the naming rule parts that make up the rule. See Create a naming rule part for information on how these are created.
 Only parts in the current configuration are available to be selected.
2. Repeat this to add any additional parts to the rule.
3. Select the required separator between each part.
You can reorder the parts in the rule by dragging them into the required order.
Select Optional to make that part optional in the rule.
A preview of the rule is displayed at the bottom of the page.
3. On the Summary tab, check the details selected in the tag naming rule.
 To update a setting, click on the applicable tab and update the setting, as necessary.
4. Click Finish to save the tag naming rule.
Create an off-page connector (OPC) naming example
Providing example OPC names helps the system to correctly identify OPCs when extracting content from your documents. The more examples you add, the better the system will be at extracting OPCs from your documents.
1. In the Data Capture feature set, select Create OPC Naming Example.
2. Complete the tabs to create the OPC naming examples as follows:
a. Setup Details
1. Type a name for the OPC naming example.
2. Select Active to use these examples to identify OPCs when extracting content from your documents. You can leave this option cleared until you have finished adding your examples and select it at a later time.
b. Examples
1. Select 
, and enter an example.
2. Repeat to add additional OPC examples.
3. On the Summary tab, check the details in the OPC naming examples.
 To update the examples, select the applicable tab and update as necessary.
4. Select Finish to save the OPC naming example.
Copy a tag naming rule or naming rule part
1. Select the tag naming rule or the naming rule part you want to copy, and select Actions 
 > Copy.
2. Modify the tag naming rule or the naming rule part as required. The options available in the various tabs are described in Create a tag naming rule and Create a naming rule part.
 If you copy one of the provided rules or parts, the non-editable interface is removed so that you can modify the copy.
Process the documents
After loading unstructured data as document objects, the Content Extraction workflow processes these objects and uses AI to identify and extract tags, off-page connectors, properties, and relationships. If tag naming rules are configured,
workflow ensures identification and extraction of fully qualified tags and correct off-page connectors.
 For users with the Data Capture Administrator or Data Capture Reviewer role, the Content Extraction Task Dashboard provides an overview of current content extraction tasks. As well as showing your To Do List, the dashboard
displays your content extraction tasks, all content extraction tasks by status, all pending content extraction tasks, the active tag naming rules, and the total number of extracted tags. For more information on home screen dashboards, see
Using an InsightView dashboard.
Can I extract content from any file type?
From the document classified as P&ID, content can be extracted from any of the following file types:
PDF
Drawing files
Microsoft Office files
.txt files
The server automatically converts any file type to a PDF and then extracts the content from them.
What are the limitations when extracting content?
The following limitations apply when extracting content from the documents. However, these parameters will change regularly as we add more features and continuously enhance our solution.
Document classification
Only the Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) classification is supported for content extraction. To classify a document as P&ID, ensure that the EnumEnum UID in the document classifications provided in the starter pack is set
to EE83. For more information, see Use the starter pack to prepare your digital twin and Document classification mappings.
Other document classifications are not supported.
File limitations
Multiple files for a single document are not supported. Only one file per document can be processed.
Multi-page files are not supported. Only single-page files can be processed.
Files with a black background are not supported. Ensure documents have a light background.
Currently, navigation is not supported for a few design drawing file types, such as .dwg, .dgn.
Document limitations
Currently, we recommend you to limit the number of documents that need to be processed to 200.
Prerequisites for extracting content
 Before you start using Data Capture, ensure that your tag and document classifications and mappings are correctly set up as described in Tenant setup overview, Document classification mappings, and Tag classification
mappings.
Ensure you have the following set up before extracting content from the documents:
Job scheduler
The PBSSyncScheduler scheduler task must be running successfully, to the defined schedule, to ensure that the Plant Breakdown Structure (PBS) objects of a consolidated design component are replicated in the DC design
component. This task looks for changes to the PBS structure within the consolidated design component and updates them in the DC design component. This synchronization is important for the content extraction task to be
successful, and to create appropriate relationships between the objects.
The VTLJobCleanUpScheduler scheduler task must be running successfully to clean up staging tables after data is extracted from documents to manage database size. This task also performs the following actions:
Automatically cleans up failed CET jobs after 14 days.
Identifies and cleans up CET jobs that remain in the "processing" state for more than 21 days, as they are considered stuck.
Rejects the Content Extraction Workflow when the CET job is stuck at the workflow step ImplicitDeleteStep or SDVExportStep for more than 24 hours, where:
It does not have a scheduler task.
The scheduler task is in Processing or in Failed state.
In such instances, we recommend you create a new CET job to re-process the content. As an administrator, you can configure the number of days after which scheduler task can begin the clean up process. For more
information, see Set Data Take-On default options.
The RecoveryTask scheduler task should always be in Ready state. The default polling interval is 60 minutes, where the scheduler runs every 60 minutes and checks for any CET jobs which are stuck or have failed at the
ExtractContentStep or at the previous workflow steps. If such jobs are identified, the scheduler re-runs the CET job to re-process the documents. For this scheduler to work properly, ensure that the Main scheduler is always turned
on. For more information, see Recovery scheduler task.
User role
To access the Data Capture feature set, the Data Capture Administrator role must be configured in the ConfigurationTop apart from other configurations.
Extract content from the documents
What are the limitations when extracting content?
The following limitations apply when extracting content from the documents. However, these parameters will change regularly as we add more features and continuously enhance our solution.
Document classification
Only the Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) classification is supported for content extraction. To classify a document as P&ID, ensure that the EnumEnum UID in the document classifications provided in the starter pack is set
to EE83. For more information, see Use the starter pack to prepare your digital twin and Document classification mappings.
Other document classifications are not supported.
File limitations
Multiple files for a single document are not supported. Only one file per document can be processed.
Multi-page files are not supported. Only single-page files can be processed.
Files with a black background are not supported. Ensure documents have a light background.
Currently, navigation is not supported for a few design drawing file types, such as .dwg, .dgn.
Document limitations
Currently, we recommend you to limit the number of documents that need to be processed to 200.
Can I extract content from any file type?
From the document classified as P&ID, content can be extracted from any of the following file types:
PDF
Drawing files
Microsoft Office files
.txt files
The server automatically converts any file type to a PDF and then extracts the content from them.
1. In the Documents feature set, select All Documents.
2. Select the required document(s) from the All Documents list, and select Actions 
 > Extract Content to create the Content Extraction Task (CET) object.
After the Content Extract Task object is created, it is attached to the Content extraction workflow which extracts content as tags, drawing representation objects, properties, and relationships.
To view the workflow for an object, select Relationships > Show Workflow.
When the Extract Content step in the Content Extraction Workflow has completed, a task is added to your To Do List 
 for you to perform the tag review. For more information, see Review the extracted tags.
To view the status of the Content Extract Task, do the following:
a. In the Data Capture feature set, select Content Extract Tasks.
b. Select the task you want to view, and select Actions 
 > View Summary.
Review the extracted tags
After a document content extraction task (CET) has completed, the next step is to review the tags that were extracted, and keep, edit, or remove them as required, before they are created in HxGN SDx2. When the document content
extraction task has successfully completed, a task is added to your To Do List for you to carry out the review.
1. In the To Do List 
, select the Content Extraction Task, and then select Actions 
 > Perform Tag Review.
 In the Perform Tag Review page, the Content Extraction Overview dashboard displays an overview of the task. If any documents failed, you can select 
 to view the failed documents and the error messages generated.
Select 
 to return to the list of successful documents.
 Any unsupported documents are shown as skipped in the Content Extraction Overview dashboard.
2. Select a successful document from the DOCUMENTS list to view the tags and off page connectors (OPCs) that were extracted from it.
 Each tag has a confidence rating indicating how confident the software is that it extracted the correct tag name and tag class. The tags are sorted with the lowest confidence rating at the top so that you can easily see the
ones that need more attention. A colored border is added to each tag shown in the document to indicate its confidence rating.
 You can select the column heading to sort the data by that column, or select 
to filter the data displayed.
3. Select the checkbox next to an item to view it in the document.
4. Update the extracted tags as required.
How do I update a tag?
1. Select Edit row 
.
2. In Fully Qualified Name, edit the name of the tag. You can also search for the required tag name.
The maximum number of characters that can be used for the Fully Qualified Name is 50.
If you enter a tag name that is the same as another tag extracted from the document, the two tags will be merged.
 After you have modified the name, the confidence is updated to show 100% but you can modify this if required.
3. Modify the Class, Unit, and Area as required.
 You can also update the Class, Unit, Area, and Confidence for up to 25 tags on the page at the same time, by selecting the required tags followed by Update all selected items 
 at the top of the column.
 If you update a tag that exists in multiple documents in the CET, the update is applied to that tag across all those documents.
5. Manually create any tags or OPCs that were not extracted from the document.
OPCs can only be created for P&ID documents.
Pan and Marquee Zoom are unavailable when manually creating tags and OPCs.
How do I manually create tags and OPCs?
1. At the top-right of the document, select Create new drawing representation 
, and create each tag or OPC as follows:
a. Select the area around the tag or OPC in the document.
b. Select whether you are creating a tag or OPC.
c. Enter the name in Fully Qualified Name.
 You cannot create a tag or OPC with the same name as another tag or OPC in the CET.
d. If you are creating a tag, select the required class.
 You can also specify an area and unit for the tag, if required.
e. Select Create.
 You can select another document in the CET, and continue to create tags and OPCs in that document.
2. When you have finished manually creating tags and OPCs, select End drawing representation creation 
.
6. If any tags or OPCs have been incorrectly extracted, remove them from the list to be created by selecting Terminate 
 for an individual row, or by selecting the required items followed by Terminate all selected items 
at the top
of the column.
7. When you have updated the lists of tags and OPCs as required, select Finish to complete the task.
 If you select to Reject the task, you must enter the reason for the rejection.
 You can also view the tags and OPCs from other user's content extraction tasks by searching for the extraction task and selecting Actions 
 > View Extracted Tags.
What happens after content is extracted?
The final step of the Content Extraction Workflow loads the data to the DC design component. From here, the data is consolidated to the Consolidated design component. When consolidating data from the DC design component, only
equipment and instrument tags are then consolidated to the Consolidated design component as common schema objects, whereas the other classification tags remain in DC design component.
Viewing and navigating the data
To view and navigate the common schema tags in the Consolidated design component, you need to update your query scope filter to select the Consolidated design component. For more information, see Filter data by design component.
 Currently, navigation is not supported for a few design drawing file types, such as .dwg, .dgn.
View and navigate tags
To view and navigate a tag,
1. Search for the P&ID document from which content has been extracted, and select Actions 
 > Relationships > Show tags.
2. Hover over a tag from the Results list, and select View Item in Document 
 from the shortcut menu ribbon.
 You can also view the tag in the P&ID file by selecting a tag from the Results list, and then selecting Actions 
 > View Item in Document 
.
View a continuation drawing
1. Search for the P&ID document from which content has been extracted, and select Actions 
 > Relationships > Show Document Off Page Connectors.
2. Select an off-page connector (OPC) from the Results list, and select Actions 
 > View continuation drawing to navigate to the corresponding continued P&ID file.
How do I connect Hexagon Digital Reality with HxGN SDx2?
Hexagon Digital Reality (HxDR) is a cloud native platform for geospatial data at any scale and using the Digital Reality connector, HxGN SDx2 can connect you to HxDR Assets. For more information about HxDR, see
https://hexagon.com/products/innovation-stories/hxdr <https://hexagon.com/products/innovation-stories/hxdr> .
Create an HxDR Connection
You must have a role related to the SystemAdmin access group to access this option. The following delivered roles are related to the SystemAdmin access group:
System Administration
Business Administrator
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
1. In the Digital Reality feature set, select Create HxDR Connection.
2. Enter the required Digital Reality Connection Details, and then go to Step 2 of the form.
3. Select the Project.
4. Select the Assets, and then select FINISH.
 Only one HxDR Connection can be created for each HxGN SDx2 configuration.
Update HxDR Connections
You must have a role related to the SystemAdmin access group to access this option. The following delivered roles are related to the SystemAdmin access group:
System Administration
Business Administrator
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
1. In the Digital Reality feature set, select HxDR Connections.
2. Select the object in the results grid, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. On the Update HxDR Connection form, select the properties and values to update.
4. Select FINISH.
View HxDR Assets
You must have a role related to the Data View access group to access this option. The following delivered roles are related to the Data View access group:
System Administration
Business Administrator
Discipline Engineer
Project Engineer
Viewer
Engineer
Planner
For information about the functionality available to a role, see the HxGN SDx2 Roles <https://docs.hexagonali.com/v/u/JGCuEhM~7gm9q8wykJGH2A> matrix.
1. In the Digital Reality feature set, select HxDR Assets.
2. Select an asset and select 
 View from the shortcut icon ribbon or the Actions menu.
Can I connect to third-party software?
The following documentation describes how to synchronize data and use the HxGN SDx2 connectors with third-party software:
HxGN SDx2 Connector for Plant Maintenance
HxGN SDx2 Connector for EAM
Learn more about configuring HxGN SDx2
Find sample data on Smart Community
To access the sample data on Smart Community, complete the following steps:
1. Go to Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> , and log on with your support credentials.
2. On the home page, select DOWNLOADS.
3. Select the required product support page link.
4. Select Accept on the Hexagon AB Legal Notice that is displayed.
5. Go to the Freeware Tools and Utilities or Content page on the product support page.
6. Download the required files.
 If you do not see the sample data you need, check back later. We are working on it.
Pre-defined mappings in HxGN SDx2
The following section describes the pre-defined mappings in HxGN SDx2.
System setup mappings
If you want to use your own hierarchy or structural hierarchy, you must map classifications to the delivered common schema.
 You need to define the initial usage of the system. The level of mapping required depends on the design tools you are using. You can map the classifications to the common schema based on your system requirements.
Why do I need to map the classifications to the common schema?
You need to map the classifications to the common schema for the following reasons:
The classification tree or hierarchy that you use represents your internal language and understanding.
The Common Schema is the common language used by the tools.
Design tools communicate and share data using this tool language, which is the common schema.
Mapping allows you to convert your classifications into the Common Schema, enabling effective communication between your hierarchy and the design tools.
Document classification mappings
 This mapping can be used only if the Create and Query scopes are not selected.
You need to map the document classifications to the common schema if you use design tools for documents and integrate them with HxGN SDx2. A document created by the design tool generates a Facility Data Warehouse (FDW)
document.
The document classification mapping connects the common schema with the document classifications and determines the classification of the FDW document. For more information, see Sharing documents and drawings.
Use Import Data to load the document classifications. For more information, see Import a load file.
You can map the document classifications by referring to the SDx2 Starter Pack available on Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For information on how to download sample files from Smart Community, see
Find sample data on Smart Community.
The table below explains the input formats required for the mapping:
Column name
Explanation
EnumEnum UID
The unique identifier of the enum value.
enumenum
The name of the enum value.
document classification
The name of the document classification.
document classification UID
The unique identifier of the document classification.
Mandatory
Indicates that the value is mandatory and must not be updated or deleted.
Tag classification mappings
 This mapping can be used only if the Create and Query scopes are not selected.
If you are using Data Capture, you can initially map the main levels or groupings of the classifications to the minimum classes required for your system, which are:
PipeRun
Equipment
PipingComponent
Instrument
If more detailed mapping is required later, you can map the additional levels as needed.
Use Import Data to load the tag classifications. For more information, see Import a load file.
You can map the tag classifications by referring to the SDx2 Starter Pack available on Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For information on how to download sample files from Smart Community, see Find
sample data on Smart Community.
The table below explains the input formats required for the mapping:
Column name
Explanation
Parent tag class name (Column 1)
The name of the parent tag.
Tag class name (Column 2 to column 5)
The names of the child tags.
Description
A description of the tag.
Enum value
The unique identifier of the enum value.
Mandatory
Indicates that the value is mandatory and must not be updated or deleted.
Mapping
The tag that is mapped to the common schema.
Submittal mappings
 Examples of submittal mapping CSV files are available for you to use and modify on Smart Community <https://smartcommunity.hexagonppm.com> . For more information, see Find sample data on Smart Community..
A physical column maps to a specific column in the input CSV file. There may be cases where a value that is required for a mapping is either not available in the physical file or the physical data supplied needs to be reformatted. In such
cases, a computed column can be set up.
We recommend that all mappings are prefixed. For more information on mapping, see Define Import Mapping.
The Relationship Mapping option is not enabled until at least two objects have been mapped.
MDR planning create mappings
The planning submittal must have the document names, title, planned issue purpose, and date. For each submittal, a new revision of the MDR document must be supplied in the first row with a new revision and issue date. For more
information on what can be done with a submittal, see Planning submittal.
