Configure HxGN SDx2 for performance
The following sections describe in detail how to configure HxGN SDx2 for performance.
Actions menu
Lookahead count
You can configure the relationship definitions and edge definitions to suppress the lookahead count in the Actions menu for selected expansions. This improves the performance of the Actions menu by reducing the load on the server.
You can configure the relationship definitions with independent values for each navigation direction using the HideNavigationCount12 and HideNavigationCount21 properties. For example, setting the HideNavigationCount12
property to True for the area to tags relationship definition stops the lookahead count from area to tags being displayed, but is displayed when navigating from tags to area.
You can configure the edge definitions using the HideNavigationCount property.
Manage the expansion of nodes
You can configure for all users how methods and relationships in the Actions menu expand or collapse using the Actions Menu Behavior system option. This improves the Actions menu performance by reducing the load on the servers
and database and by helping with scalability.
By default, the system expands the Actions node and collapses the Relationships node on the initial load of the Actions menu. For information on how to configure the setting, see Configure the system options.
Conditions
Conditions can often be complex to write, involving navigation across several relationships to get to the information required. This is not only administration overhead but also can introduce performance overhead. By exposing and
configuring any related items as properties on an object, you can use the properties to specify conditions that are simpler to write and maintain and to speed the display of shortcut menus.
 You can run a report to help identify conditions which may be slowing down performance by exhibiting superfluous complexity. For more information, see Complex Conditions Cost Report.
End1PropertyForEnd2Name and End2PropertyForEnd1Name are properties on a relationship definition used to propagate data between the objects on either end. End1PropertyForEnd2Name stores the name of the object on End2
for use as a property on the End1 object. The End2PropertyForEnd1Name stores the name of the object on End1 for use as a property on the End2 object.
RelDefPropagatesTo is a relationship definition that can be used on complex objects. It creates a relationship between two relationship definitions to propagate the relationship from a parent object to a child object. When
RelDefPropagatesTo exists on a relationship definition, the PropagateRelationship method propagates this relationship from the parent object to any child objects. By default, these properties and relationship definitions are configured to
propagate the value of the originating organization relationship into the OriginatingOrgName property. The OriginatingOrgName property holds its end object name or its parent object’s OriginatingOrgName property value. This
property is exposed on IObject.
When a relationship is created, or updated for any object, the software checks if RelDefPropagatesTo exists on the relationship definition. If RelDefPropagatesTo exists, it propagates that relationship to its children. It also checks if
End1PropertyForEnd2Name and End2PropertyForEnd1Name are set on the relationship definition. If the properties are set, the property value is stored on the other end object's property.
For example, on a transmittal, the SPFCFromInternalOrg relationship definition can be configured so that the name of the From organization is stored on the transmittal object. Since organizations control many objects, such as
transmittals, documents, tags, and more, the out-of-the-box environment is configured to write the organization onto a property on IObject.
Exposing the organization property makes it simpler to write conditions to navigate between objects and makes it faster for the system to evaluate these conditions. An example of a condition that is easier to evaluate is:
Obj.OriginatingOrgName = Env.USERORGANIZATIONNAME.
The three scenarios show when the property is set. The RelDefPropogatesTo relationship definition exists between SDADocOriginatingOrg and SDADocRevOriginatingOrg to propagate the relationship from the master to the revision.
Because this relationship exists, the ProgagateRelationship method propagates the relationship to the child objects.
Scenario 1
Updating the SDADocOriginatingOrg relationship does the following:
Sets the OriginatingOrgName property on the document master.
Synchronizes the relationship to the SDADocRevOriginatingOrg property.
 Any superseded revisions will also be updated.
Updating the SDADocRevOriginatingOrg relationship does the following:
Sets the OriginatingOrgName property on the document revision and version and on the file object.
 Any superseded revisions will not be updated because the relationship is specific to each revision.
Scenario 2
Updating the SDADocOriginatingOrg relationship does the following:
Sets the OriginatingOrgName property on the document master.
Does not synchronize the relationship to the SDADocRevOriginatingOrg property because the RelDefPropogatesTo relationship definition does not exist.
Does not set the OriginatingOrgName property on the document revision because another relationship definition exists to control it.
Updating the SDADocRevOriginatingOrg relationship does the following:
Sets the OriginatingOrgName property on the document revision and version and on the file object.
Scenario 3
Updating the SDADocOriginatingOrg relationship does the following:
Sets the OriginatingOrgName property on the document master, revision, and version and the file object because the revision relationship is not set to propagate the organization property.
Does not synchronize the relationship definition because the RelDefPropogatesTo relationship definition does not exist.
Entry Points
Entry points that result in slow queries can potentially be optimized, to guide users in finding the data they want to see in more efficient and intuitive ways that put less load on the system. Some examples of the available optimizations are
below.
 You can run a report to help identify entry points which could be reconfigured to guide users to more meaningful and performant queries. For more information, see API Entry Point Cost Report.
Change the default query criteria
The default query criteria for a new entry point is a ‘*’ wildcard. If you do not change this value in the search box, any query returns all matching data of the selected type, paginated according to the configured page size. This is unlikely to
return the results you want, so your next action is likely to refine the query, or filter the results. To prevent system resources from being consumed by superfluous queries that can affect other users, aim to get to your specific data with the
fewest clicks and queries..
The default criteria can be modified in the following ways, against individual entry points.
Set the default criteria to ‘Empty’ for a specific entry point
This is designed to discourage ‘*’ queries and encourage targeted queries. This is configured on the entry point as follows:
The Default Search Criteria is deliberately empty, and the empty criteria is applied to the entry point. This will result in removing all default criteria from the search control for this entry point.
Set the pre-populated default criteria to a value appropriate to a specific Entry Point
This will not only discourage wide queries, but also pre-populate potentially common prefixes.
This will pre-populate the search control when the modified Entry Point is selected.
Where ENS is used for the data type, the configured prefix may be a good starting point. Where ENS is not used, analysis of the data distribution for the specific data type may identify a large proportion of the objects of this type that share,
for example, common sets of characters. Configuring an appropriate default search string can both guide you (and save keystrokes) and prevent initial wide queries that you need to narrow down later.
 SQL queries, such as the following, can help identify common data patterns that you can use to create appropriate default search text:
select distinct(n), count(*) as cnt from (select substring (objname,1,2) as n from docobj) d group by n order by cnt desc
Peek tuning
‘Peek’ queries are made to the database as you enter criteria in the search box and return any matching data, prior to running the full query. The first four rows of matching data are displayed as follows:
This is a popular feature that users expect in a modern web application. When querying a large data set, you can tune the behavior for optimization purposes.
Peek invocation character threshold
Peek queries are made, by default, after you enter the third character into the search box. For some data types with longer prefixes, it may make more sense to delay the initial Peek query until you have entered more. The character
threshold at which Peek will first be invoked is configurable for each Entry Point.
If no value is entered, the default of three characters will be applied. The quantity of characters specified must be larger than three (we do not allow Peek after 1 or 2 characters) and less than 10 (since peeking beyond this point is
considered unhelpful).
Having a higher threshold means that fewer Peek queries are issued, and data suggestions are only requested when you have entered something meaningful to the data type.
Disable Peek
If Peek is too disruptive for a specific data type, you can turn it off completely, for each Entry Point, by selecting the Disable peek check box.
Query properties
You can use the column set for each Entry Point to control the available or default properties to query by. These can be reviewed and fine-tuned in the following ways.
Review / reduce the default selected query properties
The available query properties are displayed in the query control as follows:
You can choose which properties to include in the criteria, depending on the nature of their query. Default selections can be configured by an administrator so that users can avoid setting the same query properties each time they run a
search. However, this should be used economically to avoid queries that span properties, and potentially database tables, resulting in queries that are more complex than necessary. Where several properties are selected by default, you
should ensure that only the minimum set is defaulted, and evaluated with the following criteria:
Any selected properties in the query criteria will use OR (for example, “Name = ‘ABC’ OR Description = ‘ABC’). The Data Access Layer (DAL) is able to optimize AND queries by not repeating any navigations. However, any
navigations for OR queries will be repeated, resulting in larger, and therefore less efficient, SQL.
Criteria should be grouped to the same object where possible. For example, if using a Documents Entry Point to query against Name, Description, and Contractor Document Number, it is more efficient to move the Name and
Description properties to the same level as Contractor Document Number in the corresponding column set.
The DAL has AND criteria from the Entry Point and security rules for all parts of the document already. In this case, it can join into that criteria another AND, for example:
AND (Name = ‘x’ OR Description = ‘x’ OR ContractorDocNo = ’x’)
This is simpler than:
AND (Name = ‘x’ OR Description = ‘x’ OR <Navigation from version to revision>.ContractorDocNo = ‘x’)
This is improved further by the fact that these properties are on the XXXOBJ table so will not require joins to the XXXOBJPR table.
Any potential configuration changes must be run with SQL capture switched on so that the resultant SQL can be evaluated. Generally, smaller SQL is more efficient than larger SQL.
We advise that you check for repeated references to the same RelDef, in case configuration changes can be made to avoid it.
Default search properties are configured on the column set that corresponds to the Entry Point:
 Where the defaults have not been modified, Name will always be selected by default.
Remove expensive columns from the available list
Certain column items may be valid for the Entry Point’s column set, in that they need to be part of the results, but they may be inappropriate for querying against. Typically this would be relationship or edge-based column items. In this
case, to prevent users selecting an expensive column item to be part of the query criteria, individual column items can be configured to be removed from the criteria options, yet still be part of the data set that is returned. This is also
configured on the column set that corresponds to the Entry Point:
Identify the column item to suppress, and choose the ExcludeFromFilters option in the Web Client Filter Behavior column in the Manage Column Items dialog box.
Ensure column items are contextualized properly and efficiently
For documents in particular, column items must be contextualized properly and efficiently. For example, column items for the column set that corresponds to a Documents Entry Point must be contextualized to the Version. It is inefficient to
retrieve the Name, for example, from the Master since it involves navigating an EdgeDef unnecessarily. This is controlled from the path on the column item in the column set that corresponds to the Entry Point. The guidance in the Review
/ reduce the default selected query properties topic applies here too.
With document column sets, navigations are hard to avoid, since it is likely that properties from different parts of the complex object will be required in the result set. However, queries that use properties from these different parts will be
less efficient than those that query the same part of the complex object. You should consider this when selecting available and default filter properties.
Default operator
By default, all searches from the search box are sent using the Contains operator, meaning that implicit wildcards are added to the search. For example, a criteria of ‘CDE’ using Contains would return data named ‘ABCDEF’ since the
criteria is contained within the matching data.
You can choose to use the Is equal to operator instead, meaning that wildcards have to be manually specified. This is useful when looking for data that begins or ends with a given string, as opposed to being contained anywhere within it.
It is also useful for optimizing queries on large data sets where Contains is not needed.
You can change from Contains to Is equal to on individual Entry Points by selecting the Use Equal To Operator By Default check box.
Other optimizations
Review complexity of property comparisons
Some Entry Points use property comparisons to focus the data that is returned. These can be reviewed to make sure that any extraneous comparisons, particularly those involving a relationship expansion, are removed:
Redirect to search bar
This can help to prevent open queries from the home page. Entry Points can be exposed on the home page as tiles within a feature set. These can perform direct ‘single-click’ queries to return all matching data, with a * wildcard.
To prevent or discourage wide open searches, and help you get to your required data quicker, you can modify the Entry Point to redirect users to the search bar, rather than immediately performing a wide open query. This can be
configured for each Entry Point by selecting the Redirect To Search Bar check box.
This results in a change to the icon on the home page tile:
Column Set Sorting
Column sets can be configured to have a default sort order so that returned data is sorted in ascending or descending order by a selected column.
Sorting by columns, which requires a navigation, should be avoided if possible, as this can lead to less efficient queries.
Property comparisons
Property comparisons are defined on API Entry Points, search methods, and the Attach Workflow scheduler task, and these comparisons are used to query the database. Property comparisons require the use of a complicated expansion
of relationships to return the specific objects being queried.
What are the standard property comparison operators?
The standard property comparison operators are:
Operator
Name
Description
=
Equal to
The value on the left is equal to the value on the right.
>
Greater than
The value on the left is greater than the value on the right.
<
Less than
The value on the left is less than the value on the right.
>=
Greater than or equal to
The value on the left is greater than or equal to the value on the right.
=<
Less than or equal to
The value on the left is less than or equal to the value on the right.
!=
Not equal
The value on the left is not equal to the value on the right.
As an example, you build a property comparison query for versions starting around document revisions rather than the versions. The query looks at the properties on the object being queried rather than directly for the related properties.
So if a search method is configured to search for Current document versions, the method must first point to the document revisions and then only return versions that are set to Current.
For related objects at different levels, choose the item with the most relationships. For example, if you choose the Master, the classified levels 1 to 3 are relationships from the Master, so you can select the owning group from the
version.
You can configure a method in multiple ways and compare the results to identify which comparison provides the best response.
Search, query, and edge filtering
Search, query, and edge filtering are all controlled using the same property comparison mechanism which is configured as follows:
An argument on the Search and Query methods.
A property on the Edge Definition.
The following examples show the syntax criteria for various types of property comparison:
Properties on a document
You can add criteria to a document query to return only document revisions that match the specified revision states and exclude those undergoing change.
ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevState~=~e1WORKING|e1CURRENT|e1CANCELLED|e1RESERVED~ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevUnderChangeInSameConfig~=~False
 This comparison returns document revisions with various status that are not undergoing change.
Properties on an object
Properties on an object can be simply queried for in the form of InterfaceDefUID~PropertyDefUID~=~Enum value. For example:
ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevState~=~e1CURRENT
This provides support for !=.
ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevState~!=~e1SUPERSEDED
Properties on related objects
Properties on related objects are in the form of +/- RelDef to indicate the relationship direction. For example:
+SPFFileComposition~ISPFDocumentVersion~SPFIsDocVersionSuperseded~=~False
Environment variables and multiple criteria
Environment variables can be used with multiple criteria, as shown in the following examples.
The item owner is also the current username.
+SPFItemOwner~IObject~Name~=~ENV.Username
The Transmittal is valid for the user and matches the related company.
+SPFCToInternalOrg~IObject~Name~=~ENV.USERORGANIZATIONNAME
Multiple criteria can be specified that use AND ~ as a delimiter.
ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevState~=~e1WORKING~+SPFRevisionVersions~ISPFDocumentVersion~SPFIsDocVersionSuperseded~=~False
Alternate values can be tried for a single property.
ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevState~=~e1CURRENT|e1WORKING
Edge filtering
Edges are supported using the following format.
+EDG_edgename~interfacename~propertyname
Property criteria also supports 1:M (one to many) relationship testing.
+EDG_ItemContractUser~ISPFLoginUser~SPFLoginName~=~ENV.UserName
When criteria uses an = comparison operator, the string on the left has to equal the string on the right. However, when comparing relationships to objects, the = operator will do an ANY comparison. For example, an edge definition
can expand from the contract to multiple users, any of which can match the current user.
The wild card * is supported for use in a general wild card string, and ? operator is supported in a string for single character matching. For example, IObject~Name~=~A?*|B?* works if you want to filter on values starting with an A or
B, which are at least 2 characters long, and the ? operator ensures that the second character is present.
Wildcards are not supported with environment variables.
If you specify multiple values for a property comparison in an edge definition, you can use the NOT IN operator to filter out those results while matching the criteria. For example, +SPFFiletype~IObject~UID~NOT IN~FT_vecz|FT_binz
filters out all the files related to the document except the WebGL files.
Filtering by property that has no value
You can filter by an existing property that does not have a set value. For example:
ISPFEmailUser~SPFUsersEmailFormat~=~""
The following instruction helps to return objects that comply with the comparison. For example:
ISPFEmailUser~SPFUsersEmailFormat~!=
Filtering documents by owning group
Owning groups are modeled as relationships by being related to a document version.
You can filter the syntax to get the latest document version by the owning group on the version, and define an edge definition from the revision to the owning group. For example:
SPFDocRevDocVerOwningGroup~=~+SPFRevisionVersions,+SPFItemOwningGroup
 Expansion of this edge returns all owning groups on all versions of the revision.
You can set up the query filter to test against the ENV variable containing all the user's owning groups. For example:
+EDG_SPFDocRevDocVerOwningGroupObjectName~=~ENV.OWNINGGROUPSFORUSERINQUERYCONFIG
 This works despite the = sign and the fact that the ENV variable returns a set of owning groups.
You can include a second comparison to filter for only current or working documents. For example:
~ISPFDocumentRevision~SPFRevState~=~e1WORKING|e1CURRENT
 This works by only returning the working or current documents.
Filtering objects by dates
You can use the Now keyword configured on property comparisons to search for present, past, or future dates. You append the Now keyword with either a + <number> (+2) to find objects created in the future or a - <number> (-2) to find
objects in the past based on the project calendar dates.
You can query for objects that are older than 2 days using the creation date in the syntax. For example:
IObject~CreationDate~<=~Now-2
Reassign task page size
You can control the pagination value used in methods configured for the reassignment of user tasks to help improve reassign performance, especially when you have a large number of users and roles to be displayed in the reassign tabs.
Select the Pagesize setting in the relevant client APIs to specify a new page size. For more information, see AddRecipient client API and MultiStepReAssign client API.
 If you do not set a page size value, the default application page size value is used for the reassign tabs, which may effect performance if you have a large number of users and roles..
Scheduler
Scheduler usage
The scheduler manages the queue of background tasks such as the running of background reports and publishing documents.
If these processes are not required, then you can stop the Scheduler in the System Administration feature set. For more information, see Start or stop a scheduler.
Scheduler polling
The scheduler runs a query on the tenant schedulers at regular intervals to check whether they are ready to poll (the default is every 10 minutes). If there are tasks ready for execution, the scheduler sends them as Advanced Message
Queuing Protocol (AMQP) messages to the appropriate service for processing.
What is an AMQP message?
Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP) is an open standard application layer protocol to enable messaging clients and servers to work together seamlessly, regardless of their technology.
Scheduler processing
The scheduler processor picks up the AMQP messages and checks for tasks that are ready to be processed and sends them to any available services that are ready to receive and execute the tasks. The HxGN SDx2 application server
processes only the tasks that match the specified criteria.
Workflows
In HxGN SDx2, all workflow steps exist as part of a workflow template. Every workflow step is related to the template known as a workflow step definition which describes the behavior of each workflow step. The following enhancements
should be considered to help improve workflow performance when you create or customize workflow step definitions and their workflow steps.
1. Keep the number of workflow steps to a minimum
Each time you revise a workflow, a complete copy of the workflow is instantiated in the database. This instantiation takes time and includes a check on which automatic sign off steps will not be used.
You should consider:
Splitting large workflows into smaller workflows so they have the same number of steps spread over multiple workflows. We recommend a maximum number of steps in a workflow should be about 40. You can then chain them
together to create the full workflow.
Keeping each instance of a workflow smaller to help reduce the time it takes to instantiate the workflow. This avoids instantiating unnecessary steps, which can fill up the database with data.
2. Set process step workflow instances to delete the workflow on completion
Deleting the workflow instances automatically on completion can help stop the build-up of unwanted workflow history in the database. This can help reduce your database growth and maintain your system's performance.
To do this:
Select the Delete Workflows On Completion option when you create or update a workflow template.
This setting works asynchronously as a scheduler task to automatically delete completed or terminated workflow instances.
 For more information, see Create a workflow template.
3. Manually remove any existing completed workflow instances already processed by the workflow template
Removing any existing completed or terminated workflow instances already processed by the workflow template can help reduce your database growth and maintain your system's performance.
To do this:
Choose a workflow template, and select Actions 
 > Delete Completed Workflows.
This action works asynchronously as a scheduler task across the system regardless of the configuration, and deletes all newly completed and terminated workflow instances when selected.
 There is no specific action to only delete terminated workflow instances. For more information, see Update a workflow template.
4. Optimize workflow templates by merging separate process steps into a single workflow process step
This type of merge workflow process step allows you to define multiple code classes and parameters on to a single workflow process step with a meaningful name. This merged process step then executes a defined set of process
steps in consecutive order, saving time on completion. This helps performance by minimizing the number of available process steps and manages the database size by reclaiming space from previous workflow instances for tenants
that use workflows intensively.
To do this:
1. Create or update a Workflow Step Definition, and select the Workflow Step Type tab.
2. Set the Process class name to the MergeProcessSteps class name. This class name is set by default on workflows you create.
 For more information, see Create a merge workflow process step type.
5. Merge all completed workflow process steps and replace them with a single unified step
Merging similar process steps automatically into a single process step on completion helps reduce the database size significantly by deleting redundant steps for workflows that are used intensively.
You can merge process steps in two ways:
1. Go to System Administration > System Options.
2. Choose System Options, and select Actions 
 > Update.
3. Scroll to the Workflow options details section, and select the Merge process steps on completion option.
Or
1. Locate an existing workflow template that has yet to complete
2. Select Actions 
 > Merge process steps on completion option.
 For more information, see Create a merge workflow process step type.
6. Reduce the number of users and roles that need to react to a workflow step task
Any number of users or roles can be added to the Use Distribution Matrix as you create a workflow template. However, only users with specific roles to perform the action should be assessed to have direct responsibility. Keep the
number of users and roles who will be assigned to perform the workflow step task to a minimum. This can help to reduce the time the workflow takes to progress and complete when there are a smaller number of assignees.
To do this:
1. In the Create Workflow template page, select Add New Workflow Step.
2. In the New Workflow Step pane, enter the required users and roles in the Distribution and Assignment Details section using the Choose who needs to react and Use Distribution Matrix sections.
Or
1. Search for and select a workflow template, and select Actions 
 > Update.
2. Edit the users and roles in the Distribution and Assignment Details section as detailed before.
 Only users with specific roles to perform the task should be considered to react to a workflow step. For more information, see Create a workflow step.
7. Delete unclaimed parallel interactive steps when workflow steps are assigned to a group
Reducing the number of unclaimed parallel steps on completion when an interactive step is assigned to a group can help reduce the database size.
To do this:
1. In the Create Workflow template page, select Add New Workflow Step.
2. In the New Workflow Step pane, select the Distribution and Assignment Details section.
3. In the Choose who needs to react in a group section, specify the minimum number of users who must perform or respond to the task using the Assignment and Acknowledgement lists respectively.
If you configure the OneUserPerRole or OneUserPerStep group assignment control on the workflow step, you can use the Delete unclaimed steps on completion system option to delete any unclaimed parallel steps after
the workflow step is signed off. For more information, see Configure the system options.
To remove any existing unclaimed steps from your database, search for the System Options, and select Actions 
 > Delete Unclaimed Workflow Steps. This starts a scheduler task to delete the steps. You can then the
view status of the task by querying for it as described in Query for scheduled tasks.
 You can only use the Delete Unclaimed Workflow Steps option when the Delete unclaimed steps on completion system option is enabled.
 For more information on creating workflow steps, see Create a workflow step.
8. Remove unclaimed workflow steps from the database when a step is claimed
Workflow steps are initially assigned to all the users selected to action the workflow step task. When the step task is claimed by one assigned user, the extra steps are signed off and remain in the database consuming unnecessary
space. Removing signed off steps from an existing database can help reduce your database growth and maintain your system's performance.
The action to delete signed off steps is activated by setting the system option Clean up unclaimed steps, which removes the unclaimed signed off steps when the workflow completes. This action works asynchronously as a
scheduler task across the system and deletes the unclaimed signed off workflow steps after the step is claimed. This task ultimately prevents the issue from proliferating and results in a more efficient customer database and
optimized workflow tables by the clean up of unnecessary historical objects.
This task works by being periodically run to identify instances of historical unclaimed steps which can be cleaned up. The task will only run if the Clean up unclaimed steps system option is activated. When the task runs out of
legacy data to be cleaned, the task will terminate itself.
 For more information on configuring workflows, see Manage workflows.
Run system health check reports
Several reports are available that display a performance assessment of key configuration features which you can then use to identify where you can optimize your system.
The following reports are available:
Column Set Cost Report
This report quantifies the efficiency of the column sets in the system. It helps to identify column sets which may be contributing to slower queries by displaying quantities of data which could potentially be optimized.
Complex Condition Cost Report
This report quantifies the complexity of the conditions in the system. It helps to identify conditions which may be slowing down the opening of the Actions menu by exhibiting superfluous complexity.
API Entry Point Cost Report
This report quantifies the efficiency of the API entry points in the system. It helps to identify entry points which could be reconfigured to guide users to more meaningful and performant queries.
Forms Cost Report
This report quantifies the efficiency of the forms in the system for each of the form's purposes. It helps to identify forms which could be reconfigured to perform better.
Dynamic Forms Cost Report
This report quantifies the efficiency of the dynamic forms in the system for a specific classification. It helps to identify forms which could be reconfigured to perform better.
So that you can easily see where optimizations can be made, each report is ordered with the most costly items first.
Column Set Cost Report
The Column Set Cost Report quantifies the efficiency of the column sets in the system. When you run the report, it looks at every column set in your system and counts the number of:
Properties
Relationships
Edges
Each item is assigned a value depending on its performance load on the system, with Properties having the lowest value and Edges having the highest.
The total cost assigned to each column set is then calculated as:
(Count of Properties * Value of Properties) + (Count of Relationships * Value of Relationships) + (Count of Edges * Value of Edges)
How do I run the report?
Run the report using a GET request in Postman. The command to run the report is:
http://<server>/<site>/api/v2/SDA/GetColumnSetCostReport
The report is displayed in JSON format, and is ordered in descending order by overall cost. This means that you can easily see the column sets in which optimizations might be made at the top of the report.
 Postman is an API platform for building and using APIs. For more information on Postman, see www.postman.com <https://www.postman.com> .
What does the Column Set Cost Report look like?
The following is an extract from a Column Set Cost Report:
{
"@odata.context": "http://localhost/sdxserver/api/v2/SDA/$metadata#Collection(Intergraph.SPF.Server.API.ClientSupport.Types.ColumnSetReportDetails)",
"value": [
{
"ColumnSetName": "ColumnItemWithRelListEdit",
"ColumnSetDescription": null,
"ColumnSetCost": 58,
"ColumnSetPropertyCount": 7,
"ColumnSetRelCount": 0,
"ColumnSetEdgeCount": 17
},
{
"ColumnSetName": "SDADocumentVersion",
"ColumnSetDescription": "Document version list view",
"ColumnSetCost": 58,
"ColumnSetPropertyCount": 9,
"ColumnSetRelCount": 8,
"ColumnSetEdgeCount": 11
},
{
"ColumnSetName": "SCLBOutgoingActionCS",
"ColumnSetDescription": "Outgoing action",
"ColumnSetCost": 55,
"ColumnSetPropertyCount": 39,
"ColumnSetRelCount": 8,
"ColumnSetEdgeCount": 0
},
...
}
In this report:
ColumnSetCost is the overall cost calculated for the column set.
ColumnSetPropertyCount is the count of the number of properties.
ColumnSetRelCount is the count of the number of relationships.
ColumnSetEdgeCount is the count of the number of edges.
Complex Conditions Cost Report
The Complex Conditions Cost Report quantifies the complexity of all the conditions in the system.
 For more information about conditions, see Configure conditions.
When you run the report, it looks at every condition in your system and counts the number of each of the following types of operation in the condition:
Conditional operators
Relational operators (any relationships with a cardinality of * have a higher cost than other relationships)
Numeric functions
String functions
Database functions
DateTime functions
Each operation is assigned a unit cost depending on its performance load on the system.
The total cost assigned to each condition is then calculated as the total sum of the counts of each operation multiplied by their unit cost. For example:
(Count of Operation1 * Unit cost of Operation1) + (Count of Operation2 * Unit cost of Operation2) + ... + (Count of OperationN * Unit cost of OperationN)
How do I run the report?
Run the report using a GET request in Postman. The command to run the report is:
http://<server>/<site>/api/v2/SDA/GetComplexConditionsCostReport
The report is displayed in JSON format, and is ordered in descending order by overall cost. This means that you can easily see the conditions in which optimizations might be made at the top of the report.
 Postman is an API platform for building and using APIs. For more information on Postman, see www.postman.com <https://www.postman.com> .
What does the Complex Conditions Cost Report look like?
The following is an extract from a Complex Conditions Cost Report:
{

API Entry Point Cost Report
The API Entry Point Cost Report quantifies the efficiency of the entry points in the system.
 For more information about entry points, see Configure API Entry Points.
When you run the report, it looks at every entry point in your system and calculates the cost for each of the following in the entry point:
Type definitions
A query using an interface definition is considered more expensive than one using a class definition as it can potentially return more types of data.
Property comparisons
A comparison using property definitions is cheaper than one using relationship definitions, and both are cheaper than one using edge definitions.
Whether or not the Is Equal To operator is used
It is cheaper to use Is Equal To, therefore not using it adds a higher cost.
Peek searches
Using a peek search adds a higher cost than having it disabled.
In addition, the cost is reduced if the number of characters required to invoke peek is increased.
Redirects to search bar
If the entry point does not redirect to the search bar, this adds a higher cost.
If no default search criteria is specified, this also adds a higher cost.
Column sets
The cost of the column set is calculated in the same way as in the Column Set Cost Report. For more information, see Column Set Cost Report.
The number of results that would be returned by executing the entry point query.
This is optional and can be requested in the report if required. By default, it is not included as it can slow the performance of the report.
The configuration of an entry point that would return a large number of results should be carefully considered as it can have a higher impact on performance than entry points which return very little data.
The total cost assigned to each entry point is then calculated as the sum of these individual costs.
How do I run the report?
Run the report using a GET request in Postman. The command to run the report is:
http://<server>/<site>/api/v2/SDA/GetSPFAPIEntryPointCostReport
 Add the following to the Body of the request to include the number of results in the report:
{
    GetDBCount:true
}
The report is displayed in JSON format, and is ordered in descending order by overall cost. This means that you can easily see the entry points in which optimizations might be made at the top of the report.
 Postman is an API platform for building and using APIs. For more information on Postman, see www.postman.com <https://www.postman.com> .
What does the API Entry Point Cost Report look like?
The following is an extract from an API Entry Point Cost Report:
{
"@odata.context": "https://swi-vppmsdxacc4.ingrnet.com/PMCost3Server/api/v2/SDA/$metadata#Collection(Intergraph.SPF.Server.API.ClientSupport.Types.SPFEntryPointReportDetails)",

Forms Cost Report
The Forms Cost Report quantifies the efficiency of the forms in the system.
 For more information about forms, see Configure forms.
A form can be used for multiple purposes such as creating, updating, and viewing an object. The different sections that make up the form can behave differently depending on the purpose of the form, for example, a section can be read-
only when used for one purpose and editable for another, or it can be hidden for another purpose. For this reason, the report calculates the cost of the form for each purpose. You can choose to run the report for a specific purpose, or for all
purposes. If you run the report for all purposes, the overall cost of the form is the sum of the costs for each purpose.
You can also choose whether to include the number of items that will be returned by a relationship display item in the report. This is optional as calculating a large number of items can slow the performance of the report. This number is for
information only but it can help you to make an informed decision on whether to apply a display override.
When you run the report, it looks at every form in your system and calculates the cost of each of the following, for each purpose of the form:
General form costs
If a form is configured to only display for specific access groups or interface definitions, then an additional cost is added.
If the Allow Attribution Manager Content property is set to True, then an additional cost is added.
Dynamic form sections
If the Dynamic section driven from column set option is selected, then an additional cost is added.
Display items in each form section
Interface definition display items each add a small cost.
Property definition display items each add a small cost.
Relationship definition display items each add a higher cost unless:
It is dependent on another display item, then it only adds a small cost.
It has an override display type property set, then it adds a medium cost.
If the item’s cardinality is greater than 1 or is *, then an additional cost is added to the above cost.
Edge definition display items each add a higher cost unless it is dependent on another display item, then it only adds a small cost.
If the edge definition has property comparisons and path definitions, then additional costs are added as follows:
If there is a property comparison, a cost is added for each relationship in the property comparison plus a smaller cost for each property definition in the property comparison.
The path definition property value is split into its relationships. The cost is then calculated as ((a cost for each relationship) * PropComparisons property cost) + ((a higher cost for each * cardinality relationship) *
PropComparisons property cost).
User relationship display items add a higher cost again, unless it is dependent on another display item, then it only adds a small cost.
If it has any SPFDisplayItemRole relationships, then an additional small cost is added for each SPFDisplayItemRole relationship.
 An additional cost is added to these display items if they have a default value. It costs more if there are object expansions in the default value.
How do I run the report?
Run the report using a GET request in Postman. The command to run the report is:
http://<server>/<site>/api/v2/SDA/GetSPFFormsCostReport
 Add the following to the Body of the request to run the report for a specific purpose (in this example, Update), and to include the number of items that will be returned by a display item in the report:
{
    FormPurpose: "Update"
    GetRelDefDICount: True
}
The report is displayed in JSON format, and is ordered in descending order by overall cost. This means that you can easily see the forms in which optimizations might be made at the top of the report.
 Postman is an API platform for building and using APIs. For more information on Postman, see www.postman.com <https://www.postman.com> .
Dynamic Forms Cost Report
The Dynamic Forms Cost Report quantifies the efficiency of dynamically-generated forms in the system.
 For more information about forms, see Configure forms, and for information about dynamic form sections, see Defining dynamic sections.
A form is related to a class definition. For classified objects, a form can have alternative versions with different content for the different classifications of the class definition. It is therefore necessary to specify the classification to use as an
input to this report, as the dynamic content will be different for each classification. The report returns the cost of the specified classification and all sub classifications
The same form can be used for multiple purposes such as creating, updating, and viewing an object. The different sections that make up the form can behave differently depending on the purpose of the form, for example, a section can be
read-only when used for one purpose and editable for another, or it can be hidden for another purpose. For this reason, the report calculates the cost of the form for each purpose. You can choose to run the report for a specific purpose, or
for all purposes. If you run the report for all purposes, the costs for each purpose are added together.
Before the report can start calculating costs for a form, it needs to:
1. Identify the form to use for the classification
To find the form to use for a classification, the report considers the following in turn:
1. If the classification object has a form specified on the SPFObjClassForm relationship, that is the classification object form used.
2. If no form is found, it tries to get a form from its parent classification object.
3. If still not able to determine a form, it tries to get the class definition classification object from the SPFClassDefForm relationship. If this relationship is found, it gets the forms related to the class definition classification object.
4. If still no forms are found, it tries to get the class definition classification object from its parent classification object.
5. If no forms are found, an error is reported.
If multiple forms are found at any step, the report uses the most specialized form.
If the form's Allow Attribution Manager Content property is set to False, an error is reported.
2. Identify the classification column set
To find the classification column set, the report considers the following in turn:
1. If the classification object has a column set on the SPFColumnSetObjClass relationship, that is the classification object column set used.
2. If no column set is found, the report tries to get a column set from its parent classification object.
3. If no column sets are found, an error is thrown.
 If multiple column sets are found at any step, then the report uses the most specialized column set.
3. Divide the column set items into static and dynamic display items
While dividing the column items into static and dynamic display items, the report ignores the following column items:
Column items with the Hide on Dynamic Form property set to True.
Column items with the Dynamic Item property set property set to True.
Column items where the edge definition UID property is set, and the property definition UID property is not Name.
Edge definition column items with multiple relationship definitions in the path definition.
4. Identify the static display item report details
The report finds display items for each of the column item’s property definition, relationship definition, and edge definition. It then filters these display items with the classification object's form display items, ensuring that any
duplication in the underlying schema between the column set and the form are not counted multiple times.
 The cost of the static display items are calculated in the same way as for a static form report. For information on how these costs are calculated, see Forms Cost Report.
5. Identify the dynamic display item report details
Before the report can calculate the cost of the dynamic display items, it must identify the purpose.
1. If the column set item has sections on the SPFCLMColumnItemSection relationship, the report tries to identify the form purpose for this section.
2. If no form purposes are found, it gets the form purposes from the classification object form.
3. If the column set item does not have any section relationships, it creates a dynamic section, and the classification object form purposes are the dynamic section form purposes.
The cost of the static display items are calculated in the same way as for a static form report. For information on how these costs are calculated, see Forms Cost Report.
The cost of the dynamic display items are calculated as follows:
Property definition display items each add a small cost.
Each relationship definition display item adds a higher cost unless:
It is dependent on another display item, then it only adds a small cost.
It has an override display type property set, then it adds a medium cost.
If the item’s cardinality is greater than 1 or is *, then an additional cost is added to the above cost.
Each edge definition display item adds a higher cost unless it is dependent on another display item, then it only adds a small cost.
If the edge definition has property comparisons and path definitions, additional costs are added as follows:
If there is a property comparison, a cost is added for each relationship in the property comparison plus a smaller cost for each property definition in the property comparison.
The path definition property value is split into its relationships. The cost is then calculated as:
((a cost for each relationship) * PropComparisons property cost) +
((a higher cost for each * cardinality relationship) * PropComparisons property cost).
How do I run the report?
Run the report using a GET request in Postman. The command to run the report is:
http://<server>/<site>/api/v2/SDA/GetDynamicFormsCostReport
 Add the following to the Body of the request to run the report for a specific classification (in this example, FTC_equipmentclass_1), and a specific purpose (in this example, Update):
{
    ClassificationUID: "FTC_equipmentclass_1"
    FormPurpose: "Update"
}
 Adding the ClassificationUID is mandatory.
The report is displayed in JSON format, and is ordered in descending order by overall cost. This means that you can easily see the forms in which optimizations might be made at the top of the report.
 Postman is an API platform for building and using APIs. For more information on Postman, see www.postman.com <https://www.postman.com> .
Clear the cache for a tenant
Cache-related errors might unexpectedly occur for a tenant at multiple points during application processes or during an administrative stage as a system is being configured. If the implementation tenant develops a cache-related issue, you
can clear the cache to help fixed a faulted state. When you clear the cache, all the schema model metadata and administration data cached in the SDx2 Server application is removed for the entire tenant. Because of this, we recommend
clearing the cache carefully and infrequently.
 When you clear the cache, the cache is cleared from the SDx2 Server application for the entire tenant. This has a temporary performance impact to all users. Application requests that were previously cached will take longer to
return data as the tenant has to retrieve the original data from the database.
1. In the System Administration feature set, select Clear Cache.
2. Select Clear Cache to confirm you want to clear the cache, or select Cancel to cancel the action.
Individual services in the HxGN SDx2 solution may maintain their own caches, which will not get cleared by the Clear Cache command.
When you upgrade the database or reset a tenant, a Clear Cache is automatically applied.
Environment variables
The following is a list of descriptions for the environment variables found in HxGN SDx2. These environment variables can be used when creating conditions, security rules, ENS, and Data Take-On import mappings.
 If any environment variables are included in a security rule definition without an associated object value (as standalone statements), they will not be supported.
Environment Variable
Description
Example
ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERINCREATECONFIG
All valid access groups associated with the current create
configuration/user (user's name).
FDWAdmin,FDWDocViewer,FDWTagViewer
ACCESSGROUPSFORUSERINQUERYCONFIG
All valid access groups associated with the current query
configuration/user (user's name).
FDWBusinessAdministrator,SCLBAdmin
ALLDISCIPLINESFORCREATECONFIG
All disciplines associated with the current create configuration. If
no disciplines are found for the current create configuration, the
environment variable returns all the disciplines present in the next
higher configuration level.
DC_CV, DC_EL, DC_MACH, DC_MECH-N
ALLDISCIPLINESFORQUERYCONFIG
All disciplines associated with the current query configuration. If
no disciplines are found for the current query configuration, the
environment variable returns all the disciplines present in the next
higher configuration level. If no disciplines are found for the
current query configuration or any of the higher configuration
levels, the environment variable returns a blank value.
MECH-PR, MECH-N
ALLDOMAINSFORUSER
All domains valid for the current user.
InstD,SPFLLADesD,LLAD,SPFREFERENCE,ME
TASCHEMA,SPF,VTL

parent configuration. Disabled users are ignored.
HSV_DC, HSV_DE

create configuration. Disabled users are ignored.
HSV_DC, HSV_DE

role assignment in the current create configuration.

Working with Client APIs
You can manage Client APIs and view their related methods available in HxGN SDx2.
To view a client API, its details, parameters, or related methods, use the Search box to search for a specific client API or select the Client APIs search function in the System Administration feature set. To view the methods related to a
client API, select Actions 
 > Relationships > Show Methods.
Searching client APIs is only applicable to a System Administrator role.
You cannot create or update client APIs as client APIs require code changes.
Creating client APIs is not required to use the Extensibility functionality as HxGN SDx2 uses both a common WebClientExtensibility client API and a WebClientExtensibilityMultiple client API.
Client APIs
The delivered database contains a number of client APIs and methods that provide functionality that is supported in HxGN SDx2. This section contains information about these client APIs and the methods that use them.
AddRecipient client API
The AddRecipient client API allows you to add a user or users in a role to a workflow step that is in progress and attaches a new workflow for the recipient that progresses in parallel to the original step.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
Workflow Template Name
Parameter 1 description
Name of the appended workflow template
Parameter 1 type
String
Parameter 1 default
None
Parameter 2
Roles to display
Parameter 2 description
Comma separated role names that will be displayed in the Add Recipient dialog box. Use ALLROLES to display all the roles
in the system. If no role is specified, the roles of the logged-on user in the configuration of the object in the workflow are
displayed.
Parameter 2 type
String
Parameter 2 default
None
Parameter 3
Show roles
Parameter 3 description
If set to TRUE, the software displays the roles along with the users in the Add Recipient dialog box. If set to FALSE, the
software displays only the users of that role in the configuration of the workflow item.
Parameter 3 type
Boolean
Parameter 3 default
True
Parameter 4
Workflow Step Definition Name
Parameter 4 description
Specify the workflow step definition to identify the steps in ready to start state with the same step definition in the current
workflow.
Parameter 4 type
String
Parameter 4 default
None
Parameter 5
Relationship To Object (Optional)
Parameter 5 description
Specify the relationship definition name to append the recipient to the workflow item.
Parameter 5 type
String
Parameter 5 default
None
Parameter 6
Pagesize
Parameter 6 description
Controls the pagination during reassign. The page size set is applied to both the roles and the user tabs.
Parameter 6 type
Integer
Parameter 6 default
25
The following methods use the AddRecipient client API:
AddRecipient adds a user or users in a role to a workflow step that is in progress and attaches a new workflow for the recipient that progresses in parallel to the original step. This method contains the condition IsNotFirstStep, which
checks to make sure that the step is not the first step in the workflow.
AddRecipientToUser
AddRecipientWithSpecificRoles
SCLBAddReviewer adds a user or users in a role as a reviewer to a document that is in the QA review workflow. This method opens the Add reviewer dialog box which lists the users, based on the configured security rule. These
users are identified based on the workflow step definition specified in parameter 4.
The following methods add a user or users in a role as a reviewer or a responder to the current workflow. These methods open a dialog box listing the users based on the configured security rule and the parameter 3 value.
SCLBAddInternalReviewer
SCLBAddOutTQResponder
SCLBAddIncTQResponder
 If a default value for parameter 6 is not specified on the method, the default application page size value is used.
AdminEntryPoints client API
The AdminEntryPoints client API is used to configure entry points, which expose system objects to the user interface, such as tags or documents. Any relationship or edge definitions for the system object are automatically exposed and
require no further configuration. This client API uses no parameters.
The AdminEntryPoints method uses this client API to display the system objects in the user interface when called.
AllOverdueStepsReport client API
The AllOverdueStepsReport client API generates a report of overdue steps from all workflows attached to objects such as folders, documents, and so on.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
ViewDef name
Parameter 1 description
Specifies the name of the view definition on which the report is based.
Parameter 1 type
String
Parameter 1 default
None
Parameter 2
Output type
Parameter 2 description
Specifies the format in which the report results are displayed. By default, the report is displayed in XHTML format. This is the
only format supported in HxGN SDx2. If you enter a different format, it will be ignored.
Parameter 2 type
String
Parameter 2 default
None
Parameter 3
Template name
Parameter 3 description
Specifies the name of the template file stored in HxGN SDx2 into which the results must be loaded.
Parameter 3 type
String
Parameter 3 default
None
The AllOverdueStepsReport method uses this client API to generate a report of overdue steps from all workflows attached to objects such as folders, documents, and so on.

Configures the filters in the recipients column. By default, Name and Description filters are shown along with the Show

AssignMatrixRecipients method uses this client API to assign the recipients to rows in a document review matrix.
AttachWorkflow client API
The AttachWorkflow client API allows you to attach an instance of a workflow template to a document. Methods using this API can be used with multiple documents at once, attaching a different instance of the same workflow template to
each document.
The Allow edit after attach parameter on this method allows you to specify whether the software automatically opens the workflow definition for editing after attaching it to the document, allowing you to immediately edit information like the
order of steps or the step recipients. By default, this parameter is set to False.
By default, the Run in asynchronous mode parameter is set to NULL, which means that when you attach a single object to a workflow, the attach action is immediately processed synchronously, and you then must wait for the attach
action to finish before you can perform other tasks. When you attach multiple objects to a workflow, the software creates the relationships asynchronously in the background, allowing you to continue working.
By changing the value of the Run in asynchronous mode parameter defined for the method you are using to attach workflows, you can change this behavior:
When you set the Run in asynchronous mode parameter to True, both single objects and multiple objects are attached to a workflow asynchronously in the background. This creates a notification object for each attach action so
you can view the progress and continue working.
When you set the Run in asynchronous mode parameter to False, both single objects and multiple objects are attached to a workflow synchronously and you must wait for the attach process to finish before you can continue
working.
As mentioned previously, when the Run in asynchronous mode parameter is set to NULL, single objects are processed synchronously, but multiple objects are processed asynchronously.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
Not used
Parameter 2
Run in asynchronous mode
Parameter 2 description
Indicates whether the creation of the workflow definitions and instances of the relationships between the definitions and the
objects will happen in the background. When set to True, the process is completed in the background. When set to False, the
process will not happen in the background, and you must wait for the process to be completed before starting other tasks.
When set to NULL, multi-selected documents associated with a workflow will be processed in the background, but single
documents will not.
Parameter 2 type
Boolean
Parameter 2 default
None
Parameter 3
Workflow processing option
Parameter 3 description
There is no workflow selection in the create pages. HxGN SDx2 chooses the applicable workflow unless a named workflow is
specified. However, if more than one applicable workflow is found, the software does not attach any workflow to the object.
Parameter 3 type
String
Parameter 3 default
None
Parameter 4
User notification message
Parameter 4 description
Provide text that will appear in a confirmation dialog box when an object is automatically associated with a workflow definition.
Parameter 4 type
String
Parameter 4 default
None
The following methods use the AttachWorkflow client API to associate an object with an instance of a workflow definition.
AttWFlow
AttWFlowNoEdit
AttWFlowNoEditAutoName
AttWFlowNoEditAutoUID
AttWFlowNoEditInvalid

Description

If True, terminates the workflows attached to the object. If False, the object remains in the workflow.

True

CheckInFiles client API
The CheckInFiles client API is used to check in documents that have been checked out for editing.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
CheckIn Action
Parameter 1 default
CHECKIN
Parameter 1 description
Specifies the action to be performed on the document while checking in the file. The options are:
CHECKIN - The document is checked in.
CHECKINANDSIGNOFF - The document is checked in and signed off at the same time.
CHECKINANDPROMPTFORSIGNOFF - You are prompted to confirm the sign off.
If Yes, the document is checked in and signed off at the same time.
If No, the document is only checked in.
Parameter 1 type
String
Parameter 2
Column set to display checked out files
Parameter 2 default
None
Parameter 2 description
UID of the column set to display checked out files.
Parameter 2 type
String
Parameter 3
Reference not found action
Parameter 3 default
PROMPT
Parameter 3 description
Indicates what the system does if the file being checked in references other files that are not yet stored in the system. The
options are CONTINUE, ABORT, and PROMPT.
Parameter 3 type
String
Parameter 4
Update references to latest version
Parameter 4 default
True
Parameter 4 description
When set to True, updates child references to the latest version of the parent.
Parameter 4 type
Boolean
Parameter 5
Column set to display nested document references
Parameter 5 default
None
Parameter 5 description
UID of the column set to display nested document references.
Parameter 5 type
String
Parameter 6
Extract Content
Parameter 6 default
False
Parameter 6 description
When set to True, content will be automatically extracted when the file is checked in.
Parameter 6 type
Boolean

CompareWorkflowTemplates client API
The CompareWorkflowTemplates client API is used to compare and synchronize an editable workflow template copy and a related master workflow template. This client API uses no parameters.
The Compare Workflow Templates method uses the CompareWorkflowTemplates client API to allows you to compare and synchronize an editable workflow template copy and a related master workflow template. This action only
appears on an editable workflow template copy when it is out of synchronization with the master workflow template.

Parameter 8 description
There is no workflow selection in the create pages. HxGN SDx2 chooses the applicable workflow unless a named workflow is
specified. However, if more than one applicable workflow is found, the software does not attach any workflow to the object.
Parameter 8 type
String
This client API is used by many methods in the system to create copies of objects.
CopyTagNamingSystem client API
The CopyTagNamingSystem client API is used to create a copy of a tag naming rule. This client API uses no parameters.
The Copy Tag Naming System method uses the CopyTagNamingSystem client API to allow an administrator to create a copy of a tag naming rule.
CopyTagNamingSystemPart client API
The CopyTagNamingSystemPart client API is used to create a copy of a naming rule part. This client API uses no parameters.
The Copy Tag Naming System Part method uses the CopyTagNamingSystemPart client API to allow an administrator to create a copy of a naming rule part.
CopyToLocalFile client API
The CopyToLocalFile client API allows you to pull a local copy of files attached to documents.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
Show hidden files
Parameter 1 default
False
Parameter 1 description
When set to True, allows the selection of hidden (non-viewable and non-editable) files. Default set to False, those files do not
appear on the form.
Parameter 1 type
Boolean
Parameter 2
Copy Alternate
Parameter 2 default
False
Parameter 2 description
When set to True, only alternate renditions attached to the document are copied locally. Default is set to False, alternate
renditions are not copied locally.
Parameter 2 type
Boolean
Parameter 3
Alternate rendition files types to be copied
Parameter 3 default
None
Parameter 3 description
Specifies the file types of alternate renditions to be copied locally. File types must be separated by a semicolon.
Parameter 3 type
String
Parameter 4
ColumnSet
Parameter 4 default
CS_CopyToLocalColumnSet
Parameter 4 description
Name of the column set to be used to display the files to be copied locally. When the Copy Alternate argument is set to
False, then the default for the ColumnSet argument should be CS_CopyToLocalColumnSet. When the Copy Alternate
argument is set to True, then this argument should be CS_CopyToLocalAlternateColumnSet.
Parameter 4 type
String
Parameter 5
Filetype Filter
Parameter 5 default
None
Parameter 5 description
A comma-separated list of file extensions that is only visible to the user using Save Target As when selecting files to
download that are attached to an object. If left blank, all available file extensions are visible to the user.
Parameter 5 type
String
The following methods use the CopyToLocal client API to create local copies of the attached files and in some cases the related alternate renditions.
CopyToLocalFile
CopyToLocalAlternateFile
FIleDocRevCopyToLocal
FileDocRevCopyToLocalAlternateFile
SPFOpenXMLSaveReport
CreateAdhocQuery client API
The CreateAdhocQuery client API is used to create new reports by opening the Create Report dialog.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
Allow group sharing (True/False)
Parameter 1 default
True
Parameter 1 description
Indicates whether the report can be shared with an owning group.
Parameter 1 type
Boolean
The SPFAdhocReport_Create method uses this client API to open the Create Report dialog, allowing you to create a new report.

CreateENSDefinition client API
The CreateENSDefintion client API is used to create a new Engineering Numbering System (ENS) numbering format that you can use to identify each design or project-specific object created in your system. This client API uses no
parameters.
The following methods use the CreateENSDefinition client API to create a new Engineering Numbering System (ENS) numbering format.
CreateENSDefinition
UpdateENSDefinition
CreateEntryPointFromQFind client API
The CreateEntryPointFromQFind client API creates new entry points based on existing HxGN SDx2 quick finds. This client API uses no parameters.
The CreateEntryPointFromQFind method uses this client API to display the system objects in the user interface returned by the quick find when called and populates the form with existing configuration details.
CreateMethod client API
The CreateMethod client API is used to create new methods.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
Create Method UID
Parameter 1 default
True
Parameter 1 description
If set to True, the CreateMethod UID is used to create a new method.
Parameter 1 type
String
The CreateMethod client API is used by the CreateMethod method to create new methods. The default is set to True.
CreateNextSheet client API
The CreateNextSheet client API is used to create the next sheet number for a document.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
StartPosition
Parameter 1 default
None
Parameter 1 description
Identifies the start position for the sheet number in the current name. For example, for the name DOC123-01, the value for this
parameter would be 8.
Parameter 1 type
String
Parameter 2
Length (of sheet)
Parameter 2 default
None
Parameter 2 description
The length of the sheet number. This is the number of characters in the sheet number portion of the name. For example, for
the name DOC123-01, the value for this parameter would be 2.
Parameter 2 type
String
Parameter 3
SheetNo PropertyDef
Parameter 3 default
None
Parameter 3 description
Name of the property that contains the sheet number. This property must be exposed on the create form. (Optional)
Parameter 3 type
String
No delivered methods use this client API. However, you can use the CreateNextSheet client API to configure a method to create a new document sheet from the original document with a number sequence set using ENS.
For more information about documents with multiple sheets, see Configure documents with multiple sheets.
CreateObj client API
The CreateObj client API is used to create various types of objects.
Argument
Description
Parameter 1
ClassDef of object to be created
Parameter 1 default
None
Parameter 1 description
The class definition of the object to be created.
Parameter 1 type
String
Parameter 2
Not used
Parameter 3
Not used
Parameter 4
Not used
Parameter 5
Title
Parameter 5 default
None
Parameter 5 description
The title of the form that will be used to create the object.
Parameter 5 type
String
Parameter 6
File attachment configuration
Parameter 6 default
SPFDesignFile~SPFFileComposition~PROMPT
Parameter 6 description
File attachment configuration (optional) FileClassDef~FileRelDef~ReferenceAction~SeedFileTemplateDocName.
SPFFileComposition is the default relationship definition if none other is specified. The seed file document is optional.
Reference file not found actions are CONTINUE|PROMPT|ABORT.
Parameter 6 type
String
Parameter 7
Not used
Parameter 8
Workflow processing option
Parameter 8 default
None
Parameter 8 description
There is no workflow selection in the create pages. HxGN SDx2 chooses the applicable workflow unless a named workflow is
specified. However, if more than one applicable workflow is found, the software does not attach any workflow to the object.
Parameter 8 type
String
Parameter 9
None
Parameter 9 default
None
Parameter 9 description
Complex Graphs are used to indicate the objects derived from the server side that creates relationships to link the objects
together.
Parameter 9 type
None
Parameter 10
Not used
Parameter 11
Not used
Parameter 12
Additional property to set
Parameter 12 default
None
Parameter 12 description
Four ~ separated arguments (UID~Text~Method~Boolean) which set the property identified by the UID to True, and invoke the
specified method, when the button with the specified text is clicked. True value navigates to the next screen even when no
workflow is attached to the object.
The default value of the fourth argument is False.
For example, if the user specifies SDACreatedAsDraft~Save As Draft~MTH_SOPSPrepareChangeRequest, a Save As
Draft button appears on the form and when clicked, the value of the property with the UID SDACreatedAsDraft is set to True,
and the SOPSPrepareChangeRequest method is invoked.
Parameter 12 type
String
Parameter 13 type
Not used
Parameter 14 type
Not used
Parameter 15
Is file attachment mandatory
Parameter 15 default
False
Parameter 15 description

Boolean

Not used

Parameter 17 default
None

 When configured, the selected create form will be launched during the create object process.
Parameter 17 type
String
