#

# Prologue

This document provides a strategic overview of “Smart Handover” processes; the opportunities afforded by Intergraph’s SmartPlant Technology and provides examples which may be reproduced freely by the reader in the creation of a handover specification.

## Glossary and Abbreviations

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| CMMS | Computerized Maintenance Management System |
| EPC | Engineering Procurement Construction |
| EPIC | Engineering Procurement Installation Commissioning |
| EPISTLE | European Process Industries STEP Technical Liaison Executive |
| IMS | Information Management System |
| IS | Information System |
| ITT | Invitation To Tender |
| PISTEP | Process Industry STEP consortium |
| RDL | Reference Data Library |
| RFP | Request For Proposal |
| STEP | Standard for Exchange of Product Data |

## Acknowledgements

The information provided in Section 2 and associated Subsections of this document is based on the work undertaken by members of the PISTEP and USPI-NL consortia, and summarized in the EPISTLE Process Industries Handover Guides (References [1] and [2]). FIATECH Capital Facilities Handover Guide also provides important subject matter on this topic.

# Executive Overview

Information Handover on a capital project is an activity that has traditionally been low on the priorities of both the Owner-Operator (OO) and the Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC) company alike. Often OO’s believe the ownership of consolidation and integrity of the content to be the responsibility of the EPC and then wonder why the data turned over is organized by project codes and structures and not plant codes and structures, or that data is duplicated, mis-indexed or just missing. Equally, EPC’s typically do not allocate key engineering resources or impact their existing processes to provide the co-ordination tasks necessary to ensure cross functional data integrity is assured if it is not a funded activity. Or to look at the problem in another way, consider the systems and processes that EPC’s and OO’s have optimized their businesses for;-

* EPC’s - World class engineering project execution
  + FAST - Concurrent and distributed global engineering / procurement using Data-Centric Design tools
  + **SLOW – Consolidation of ‘traditional’ deliverables contractually required for ‘document-centric’ handover**
* OO’s – Productive/Efficient Operations/Maintenance
  + FAST – CMMS, ERP and plant monitoring using Data-Centric tools
  + **SLOW – Access to, and loading of these tools with human intervention via research and cross referencing of traditional deliverables of the handover**

Handover therefore is not about altering the systems and processes in place today, it is about closing a gap between these processes. There is a better way, a win-win for both parties, a Smart Handover. A funded activity running parallel to the main project, resourced adequately with tools and processes to identify, gather and cleanse the information received from multiple sources in such a way that it is ready on plant during startup and into operations.

It is not just for handover of Greenfield projects either, Brownfield turnarounds are equally affected by inefficient information handover. The lack of readily available engineering information in the operating facility is resulting in extended turnaround times, **causing lost production / revenue opportunity and higher capital expenses**, typically some 30% of the costs for modifications are involved in verifying and correcting the ‘as-built’ plant data.

“CoP recently did a major turn around and facility upgrade on Bayu Undan – this would typically have been a three month process – but was completed in one month – the ONLY reason that was possible was because of the high quality, detail and accuracy of the data handed across from the project.”

Source: Peter Rogers of CoP - Perth

# Capitalizing on a ‘Smart Handover’ Strategy

## From Point Solution to Continuous Process

Plant Handover has in the past usually been approached by an owner-operator as a discrete stage, a milestone, an event in a plant’s lifecycle where the physical asset along with its associated information and systems are turned over to the operator by the companies that designed and/or constructed it. Approached in this manner it is also normal that a budget is allocated by the owner-operator to the turnover of the information, which in the past involved the delivery of boxes of documents, CD’s and other media. Today budgets upwards of $10m are allocated on many Greenfield projects (with comparative budgets on brownfield projects) to categorize, identify core data and nameplate details, capture it, potentially cleanse/correct and purge duplication, and turnover the information readied for operations use. In line with this practice, most information technology offerings in the market today have focused on the reduction of this dollar amount and the time efficiencies associated with preparation and delivery supporting this handover event -- more often than not in accordance with some evolving industry standard practice. But that is not the whole story of handover. The implementation of incremental handover during the development phase of a project and continual verification can reduce these final handover costs to practically zero.

![Slide7](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

While the formal handover of the plant to the owner-operator exists as a discrete activity the information exchange associated with it should not be executed in the same manner. It is inefficient, wasteful and invariably executed at the most frenetic time in the plants lifecycle – around startup – when a good, reliable source of all the information is needed. To have this source ready at startup and into operations in the most efficient manner the information should be harvested continuously by the project execution body. Therefore information handovers should not be seen just as a one off event, they should be seen as continuous processes. They can be considered involved in every interchange between owner, engineer, supplier, fabricator, commissioner, operator through the whole lifecycle of the plant.

![Slide8](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

Virtually every conversation, every transaction involves some form of handover. It is the stuff of collaboration. But without defining some basic rules of language, content and mode of communication, no integration or exchange (and therefore handover) on a project could occur. That is not to suggest that handovers do not occur today in a coordinated manner. On the contrary, many individual handovers or exchanges occur between parties involved in capital project execution, often utilizing pre-determined and carefully negotiated point-to-point methodologies, sometimes involving a data warehouse or a custom database, to limit information loss during the transaction. While these may be practical in the short term they are not very efficient in the long term, and may lead to information degradation, poor referential integrity and quality, and may also involve eventual total information loss – with the obvious impact on operational efficiency. Where these handover practices do not exist, or have not been institutionalized (as in eCommerce) the traditional paper or e-paper deliverables exchanges still occur as the norm, and will continue to occur where appropriate to support regulatory oversight.

![Slide9](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

By having the information ready loaded into operations systems during commissioning and startup including standard operating manuals and training materials, not only does it provide the opportunity to shorten the startup schedule, but when approached in a continuous manner in the context of the whole life of an asset, efficiencies and productivity associated with handovers can dwarf the financial opportunity from a single project turnover event. Indeed it has been calculated by some analysts to be worth upwards of 10% of the whole life operating cost of the asset in savings. Intergraph therefore recommends a holistic approach to handover, or in other words a ‘smart handover’ strategy, which when executed in a scope broader than the single event will yield more benefit. It is as simple as that.

## Success Criteria

So what is a ‘smart handover’ all about? It is not only important that handover is seen in the context of a lifecycle information strategy by the O, but how should one specify it, where would one start, what are the opportunities, what are the pitfalls, how should the parties on a capital project communicate, what is it technically, how broad should the scope be, what would it cost… how do you characterize and measure success? These are many of the questions that will be handled in this guide. However this is not the only document on the subject. You should also read:

* Plant Information Management – A Strategic White Paper – available upon request.
* SmartPlant Enterprise – Information Management - available here <http://www.intergraph.com/literature/SmartPlant_Enterprise_Information_Management.pdf>
* SmartPlant Foundation – Intergraph’s Information Management solution – available here <http://www.intergraph.com/literature/SmartPlant_Foundation_flier.pdf>

Satisfaction of the criteria listed below will enable Owner-Operators, EPC’s and Suppliers to collaborate more effectively and ensure the smooth migration of data from engineering systems into operations systems (and back again) supporting efficient start-up of the facility, as well as future configuration and change management during operations and maintenance of the facility. The goal being to;-

1. **Reduce Time-To-Market** (**TTM**) – get the plant asset designed and constructed ahead of schedule, within budget and operating at capacity to meet a market window of opportunity.
2. **Maximize Time-In-Market** (**TIM**) – mitigate unplanned outages, minimize engineering turnarounds and obviate safety, hazard and regulatory risks which would otherwise halt production.
3. **Optimize Operating Parameters** (**OOP**) – make the most of the things you have least of [time, resources, and schedule] for the things you want more of [throughput, safety and profit].

As you investigate what Intergraph refers to as ‘smart handover’ you will realize that there are key criteria for a success which includes;-

* A specification that identifies content, sources and owners of information that will encompass any handover.
* A pragmatic methodology for the identification, capture and exchange of vital information.
* Ownership of the ‘arrows’. Most exchanges are characterized symbolically by two boxes joined by an arrow. The boxes typically represent a function that is ‘owned’ by someone in the business, the arrows typically are not. Many organizations see this ownership as the opportunity for a ‘Chief Content Manager’ (CCM).
* Focus on improving efficient handover of technical information between life cycle stages. For example, if technical information is not available and correct at mechanical completion, it has a potential to delay start-up **(TTM)** as well as hampering de-bottlenecking that invariably happens during this frenetic period – bringing the plant up to ‘nameplate’ capacity. If technical information is not complete and accurate during operations it can result in losses of productivity **(OOP)** at a later date, and at the very least, makes de-bottlenecking more challenging**.** Furthermore, opportunities exist during the project itself to capture and reuse strategic data to populate other enterprise systems, such as CMMS, Reliability, DCS, Mechanical integrity, etc. that directly effect profitability **(TIM)**.
* Practices for data and file formats that are harmonious to all parties (using multiple systems) engaged in the venture (the ‘smartness spectrum’ described later). This is particularly relevant for ‘brownfield’ operations where a process of continuous, as-needed improvement is the only cost effective route, and therefore the support of multiple formats simultaneously is a requirement.
* Adoption of the ‘smart handover’ early in the project cycle when the risk, cost and exposure is minimal, delay may cause rework and increase the ‘cost of quality’.
* Visibility, education and institutionalization of the requirement and need for information handover to all members of the venture – in other words, remove the information silos
* Tools that support not just highly automated but also highly integrated engineering. The potential of leveraging the value of the SPF repository to support integrated project execution including MoC, interface mgt., deviations/waivers etc. is an important bi-product of this activity that can be exploited.
* An efficient change management process that ensures that the quality and integrity of the information is never compromised.
* A flexible strategy for the storage, conversion and distribution of the information.
* Control, transmittal and receipt procedures that extend to a level more granular than that of the document exchange.
* QA/QC, exception reporting, validation and remediation of questionable information.
* Early access to information by O&M staff allowing them to better prepare maintenance planning, reliability regimes as well as feedback to the EPC design team.
* And of course, contractual and financial incentive for the parties engaged in the handover

## Methodologies for Smart Handover

The basis for a smart handover indicated above is that it is a process, not a one off event, where the earlier in the project that the process begins, the more effective and less resource intensive will be the handover. But what does this look like on a project, and how do the parties engaged organize the exchanges? The following illustrates three such models (of which there are many variants) of smart handover that Intergraph has been engaged in. The common thread in these examples is SmartPlant Foundation, deployed as an Engineering Data Warehouse (EDW) the central repository for data and documents that are in transition to other systems. It is also worth noting that not all plant data finds a natural home in the operations systems, but it is needed over the lifetime of the facility. Without proper management its integrity will decay over time – requiring it to be brought up to date through costly plant walk-down/as-building or other means.

### Data QA through ‘application integration’

In this example, front end engineering data (FEED) has been handed over from the owner (or their contractor partner) in the form of an SPF repository. In this way the engineering contractor (in this case responsible for engineering, procurement and construction phases of the project) gets an early start on the data integrity – naming conventions, nomenclature, system definitions etc. The engineering contractor integrates their applications together through this SPF EDW utilizing the SmartPlant Schema. This ensures that exchanges between the tools use consistent terminology and content.

![Slide14](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

During the engineering phase the integrations (utilizing SmartPlant Adaptors) are tuned to support consistency of data that is shared between the applications. But the OO will not want all the data generated (multiple design iterations etc) by the EPC, and indeed the EPC will not want share some data (protecting their IPR etc). Therefore as the project draws to a conclusion the scope of the data exchanges provided by the SmartPlant Adaptors sharing data with the EDW will need to be adjusted as illustrated in the diagram below.

![Slide17](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

### Data QA through simultaneous ‘data integration’ by EPC

In this example the EP and CM contractors may utilize their existing tools in their traditional EP and CM execution processes. But since they may have a contract to provide a consolidated data handover at the end of the project regular exports are made to an EDW that is shadowing the process. In this way there is little or no impact in the way of tool adaptation or training before the project execution begins. However, since there is no direct integration with the tools, as in the application integration example above, it is only the data from multiple sources that is being integrated together, hence the term ‘data integration’ as opposed to ‘application integration’.

![Slide15](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

Therefore two different management mechanisms need to be established. Firstly to validate the data from the multiple sources as being correct and consistent, and where this is not the case to provide an exception report notification to the source owner of the data for correction and re-submittal. Secondly whereas the application integration example automated the management of the data overlaps, the shared data, in this example the overlaps would not be managed automatically but would either need policies set up to ensure that only data that was entered in to the EDW was from the correct ‘owning’ source as in the illustration below.

![Slide18](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

### Data QA through simultaneous ‘data integration’ by OO

This example is almost identical to the one above with the exception that the OO has taken the lead in aggregating and consolidating the data in their own SPF EDW. This is particularly useful in global projects with multiple joint venture partners that cannot harmonize project tools and need to share with an OO. It is also useful for Brownfield exercises where the EDW may already have been established as a result of previous projects. This reduces the burden often associated with plant walkdown verification prior to project inception.

![Slide16](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

## The Smartness Spectrum

It is a popular misconception that there will be a single common form of all data, unified into a single common data model that all applications involved in the plant life cycle will use now and on into the future. While it is obviously a fine vision that we must work towards, it is not yet wholly practical. For instance it is not mindful of what has gone before. It would be prohibitively expensive for an operation to upgrade and convert all its data simply to have it on the common platform ‘de jour’.

No, in today’s world the handover strategy must be mindful of the following hard truths;-

* Information will never be harmonized into single common form…
* Duplication will exist…
* Concurrency is inevitable…
* Security is paramount…
* Inconsistency will occur…
* Time & Cost will override all other concerns above…

In recognizing these facts we can then define a strategy that will support these often uncomfortable truths and set then ourselves upon a path of gradual improvement.

Consider a project that has multiple collaborators – not an uncommon scenario today. Can we insist that all members of this partnership utilize the same tools, the same version of the tools, and the same reference data within the tools? Even if we could (which would often negate the inherent skills and value each brought to the table) could we agree common naming schemes, formats, reference data – the content of the interchanges, as well as documents?

Or, consider a Brownfield operation that has a legacy of one type/format of data, would they have to uplift it all to take advantage of new methodologies for information management and new tools to enhance the productivity of their workforce?

The answer to both of scenarios is a system, like SPF that can support a wide spectrum of formats, all of which do not need to be in the most intelligent of forms. Obviously the smarter the form, the more that can be accomplished by the tool, but that alone is not a reason for a mass conversion exercise.

![Slide3](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

![Slide4](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

# SmartPlant Enterprise and Smart Handover Example

The **SmartPlant Technical Specification Guide** below is a detailed outline of the deployment considerations for the use of the SmartPlant Foundation and the SmartPlant suite of engineering tools and the required deliverables.

It is a starting point for our OO and EPC clients to quickly and efficiently develop parts of the HO specification relevant to their project.

Those who wish to use the information below may freely copy and paste from the Microsoft Word document into their company’s RFPs and RFQs. The specification language is written with the intent of providing all technical information needed to specify the SmartPlant solution in its entirety or as single tool components.

This will ensure that the data can be reused by the OO downstream from the engineering phase. SmartPlant Foundation is considered a required part of the handover strategy, while the SmartPlant engineering tools are considered “plug and play,” depending on the scope of the project (as indicated above).

The **SmartPlant Technical Specification Guide** covers only basic technical requirements, and in order to develop a complete solution for Plant Information Management and in particular for handover from engineering to operations, the specific client business requirements need to be considered as well. Therefore, Intergraph PP&M recommends to complement the use of the **SmartPlant Technical Specification Guide** with a more detailed “project assessment” of the present situation and a "Gap analysis” as described above to provide the optimum deployment of Intergraph **SmartPlant Enterprise.**

The **SmartPlant Technical Specification Guide** outlines in detail the deployment considerations for the use of the SmartPlant Foundation and the SmartPlant suite of engineering tools as well as the required deliverables at the end of a project.

It is strongly suggested that SmartPlant Foundation and all the SmartPlant design tools be pre-configured to the way your company wishes to finally receive the technical information at the pre-FEED timeframe. This is to include the plant/area/unit, vault names, and even down to the definition of the datasheets, especially the datasheet formats, or CAD symbols used to seed the new project.

## Overview

The greatest business benefit comes from having as much information as possible in a structured data form. However, unless the contractor creates the original information in the SmartPlant suite or even SmartPlant Foundation, it would be very difficult and costly for the OO to capture it later on. Therefore, it is essential that the contractor abide by this guide.

In addition to complying with the standards described in this document, the contractor must also comply with all other applicable standards specified by *XX* in other documents.

*XX* will decide what types of documents and data will be delivered progressively. The contractor is required to transfer data and documents to *XX* when requested and should allow for a minimum of four (4) incremental data dumps per key engineering activity. Client may comment on deliverables in the SPF repository and contractor may be given access to retrieve comments/mark-ups

The above data handover process is required to provide a mechanism for a meaningful information change history. *XX* will identify the type of information to be delivered at each stage or delivery milestone. Delivery is considered to be SmartPlant Foundation populated with the data or documents, and the relationship be made to the metadata relationships (tag, system, model, manufacturer, etc.).

## Purpose of this Document

This document defines the requirements for handover of both documents and data from EPC contractors, subcontractors and equipment vendors to XX during the <XYZ> project.

Descriptions of the necessary documents and data are provided, together with specification of required delivery formats. In addition, this document details XX’s and EPC contractors’ responsibilities for delivery and quality assurance during the handover process.

## Information Handover Philosophy for the <XYZ> Project

It is XX’s intention to implement an information management system (IMS) to manage all documents and data during the life of the <XYZ> facility. The XX intends to maximize the total integration of this project by utilizing best practices and leading proven technologies.

Based on this philosophy, all correspondence and information transfer between the EPC contractors and XX shall be electronic. Paper transmittals shall not be utilized unless specifically approved by XX. XX data, if required by the EPC contractors, shall be accessed from externalized interfaces to XX systems – notably CMMS, ERP, DCS etc – via application interfaces, secure portal or secure ftp locations.

The objectives of the IMS are as follows:

* Manage data and documents over the full facility life cycle; from design to decommissioning via an incremental handover methodology
* Integrate XX, Contractors and Suppliers information systems to remove delays and bottlenecks
* Provide a common platform for accessing and exchanging data and documentation throughout the project
* Provide a basis for seamless transfer of plant data from XX Project Team to Operations.
* Provide a basis for population of XX operations systems including ERP, RCM etc.
* Integrate engineering, project management, materials management, construction, interface management, pre-commissioning and commissioning, installation, hook-up, start-up and operations to provide superior decision support
* Integrate with the Contractor and XX management systems to reduce cost, provide visibility and allow risk evaluation/mitigation
* Eliminate handover complexity and cost between each phase of the project
* Provide the most recent revision of data and documents to all parties, when and where it is needed to avoid unnecessary errors
* Remove the complexity, cost, and delays of paper documents and manual distribution, review, and approval systems

The EPC contractor shall prepare and execute a plan for interfacing with XX’s IMS in accordance with the requirements detailed in this document. He shall also provide input, for XX review, to improve this approach, maximize the efficiency, and optimise the cost of system implementation

## Information Quality

### Information Quality Responsibilities and Deliverables

EPC contractor is responsible for the quality of the work it performs under the EP and/or CM contract. EPC contractor will take all reasonable measures to ensure that data received from the sub-contractors, suppliers and vendors is a) complete and b) consistent across the <XYZ> project scope. The effort required in compiling, re-formatting the data so received, and making consistent is deemed to have been included in the contract. EPC contractor will employ a Chief Content Manager (CCM) to coordinate these responsibilities. This is seen by XX as a key role in the project and should report directly to the EPC Project Manager.

XX requires EPC contractor CCM to be responsible for electronically transferring documents and data in the required format (see section 1.0.2) to XX’s internal system integrator who will manage the process of populating the IMS.

EPC contractor’s CCM will produce a comprehensive Information Management Plan (IMP). Contained in the IMP will be the Data Quality Management Framework (DQMF) document which will be created in conjunction with XX in order to demonstrate understanding of hand-over scope, requirements and quality issues. The IMP and DQMF will detail EPC contractor’s internal data gathering, data quality management processes and hand-over procedures.

The IMP\DQMF will be regarded as an evergreen document, maintained and revised during the <XYZ> project to further clarify and detail hand-over scope and requirements where required. Through the CCM identified handover requirements will be distributed to respective data stakeholders.

Data ownership remains with the responsible function\discipline based on the lifecycle state of said data.

### Content Handover Guide (CHG)

XX will provide all EP and CM contractors engaged on <XYZ> project a comprehensive, consistent set of information and data taxonomies, structures and tagging conventions. The compilation of these necessary and conditional parameters is the CHG. The purpose of the CHG is to govern detailed requirements for the handover of information on assets delivered under <XYZ> project, in terms of scope, structure, schedule, quality and standards.

The CHG details the handover of project asset information required for the entire lifecycle of an asset from Basis of Design (BoD) studies through to Abandonment, with a strong focus on the operational phase of that lifecycle. The scope includes all assets that may require work during any part of its lifecycle (where an asset is typically an item that can be uniquely identified with a serial number – as opposed to bulk/stock/inventory items).

The CHG does not cover the actual process of developing, generating or validating the data which is to follow project defined standard work-processes.

The list below summarizes the required information EPC contractor will handover:

* Tag / Equipment numbers, service descriptions, parent-child hierarchy details and structured (process and equipment) data sheets.
* Mechanical (static, rotating), Electrical, Instrumentation, Piping, Civil, HVAC, HSE, movables and specialty equipment data
* Equipment Manufacturer, Serial number, Model & Parts and related attributes
* Documents (including factory certificates and Vendor data / documents)
* Document Links to Objects (Tag, Component, Unit, Area, Site, BOM)
* Loop topology for instrumentation, fire & gas and telecoms.
* Electrical topology data describing the electrical distribution network
* 3D models
* Schematic diagrams
* Spares Lists
* Purchase Orders and related attributes
* Certification / Certificates (e.g. test certificates for relief valves, lifting gear etc.)
* Data Quality Management Framework
* Agreed Financial and Cost information

### Information Quality Validation Tools and Procedures

Within the IMP\DQMF, the CCM will determine, define, explore, document, publish and implement a robust, encompassing yet not intrusive tool set and methodology to continuously monitor and validate all EP and/or CM data. The base line for the Information Quality validation tools and procedures will be the information standards defined in the CHG. The intent will be to not adversely impact existing, proven work processes but to augment them with the generation and distribution of Exception Reports for data owner’s timely correction in advance of data hand over to XX.

### Data Quality Management Framework (DQMF)

The EPC contractor shall provide within the DQMF proposed methodologies for ensuring that the information requirements as specified by XX will be met. The DQMF document will contain and address the following:

* Detailed definitions of entities and attributes as understood by EPC contractor;
* Tag naming standards for the various types of equipment;
* For data fields with limited value domain: lists of permitted values;
* Validation procedures to validate the quality of data values;
* Units of measure;
* Specification of software tools to execute the validations;
* Consistency checking tools and procedures:
* Integrity checking tools and procedures;
* Completeness checking tools and procedures;
* Detailed procedures for handing over data, receiving error reports and correcting errors;
* Detailed planning /scheduling of the hand-over processes;

In particular, the EPC contractor’s plan shall incorporate answers to the following:

* To what extent does the EPC contractor use professionally supplied IT systems, and what is the level of customisation?
* How are consistency and quality of the EPC data maintained?
* How does the EPC contractor manage tag numbers and align it with equipment specification data?
* How does the EPC contractor propose to guarantee data integrity across applications (e.g. from 3D model to P&ID to Equipment List, to Line List and Instrument Index)?
* What is the EPC contractor’s strategy for data change management?

The EPC contractor shall include comprehensive details of information system (IS) infrastructure and workflow processes. Details of the workflow processes should indicate whether, for example, each engineering discipline manages their own data or has a corporate system imposed.

### Division of Responsibility

EPC contractor will be responsible for validating the quality and completeness of data required for handover to XX. This will be performed in a non-intrusive manner transparent to data generators/users so as not to impact engineering and construction schedule.

Data ownership remains with the responsible function\discipline based on the lifecycle state of said data. CHG requirements will be identified to respective data stakeholders.

The CCM will regularly generate and distribute CHG Exception Reports to the relative data stakeholders, allowing for resolution prior to handover to XX.

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Quality Issue | Description | Responsibility |
| Accuracy | The degree in which the data provided reflects the status of the plant ‘as-built’ | EPC contractor, accepted by XX |
| Consistency | The compatibility of the same type of data from different sources | EPC contractor, accepted by XX |
| Completeness | How much of the required information is available | EPC contractor, accepted by XX |
| Timeliness | The availability of up-to-date information | EPC contractor, accepted by XX |
| Clarity | Clear and Common understanding of the information content | EPC contractor, accepted by XX |
| Accessibility | Where, how, and to whom the information should be available | XX, with indexing by EPC contractor |
| Relevance | The usefulness of the information in the context of the activity | XX |
| Cost | The cost involved (inherent) in maintaining information quality | EPC contractor |
| Integration | The flexibility to manage information irrespective of the medium in which it is held | XX |

### Data Ownership

Ownership of data during the plant lifecycle is an important issue. In terms of the EPC phase, it must be clearly understood by all parties that the EPC contractors are responsible for the quality and integrity of documents and data – including subcontractor and equipment vendor data – that is handed over to XX. The XX will assume ownership of the documents and data upon completion of the EPC project (i.e. following plant commissioning acceptance and the receipt of electronic as-built documents and data).

The EPC ownership of the documents and data during their phase of the work is not affected by the requirement to transfer such information to XX at defined milestones (see Section 1.0.8).

## The Role of the IMS During the EPC Phase

In order to streamline the information handover process, it is intended that XX’s information management system will be populated during the EPC phase, and that the contractual responsibility for providing the required information as defined in this document will reside with the EPC contractor.

XX’s intention is that all data and documents will be managed within an integrated environment, such that users shall have access to all documents and data held within the Information Management System (IMS) via a user-friendly, graphical / web interface. The precise locations of documents and data within the IMS shall be transparent to the users, who will interrogate the IMS using familiar search criteria (e.g. Tag Number, Document Number, Equipment Number, Discipline, etc). Additionally the navigation structures, such as plant breakdown structure and work-breakdown structure provided by the tools will be used to aid location of data.

It is not XX’s intention for the EPC contractors to use the information management system as a ’live’ system during the normal execution of their work, but that will not be discouraged if it is seen to support the aims of the handover as described here with no impact on cost or delivery schedule to the core project. Instead, they will be expected to continue to use their standard software design applications, and shall start transferring information into the IMS when it is ready for XX review or at other times as required by XX. The process of delivering documents and data at intervals during the project will provide XX with early visibility of the engineering data and will help to ensure that any quality problems are rectified early. For example, it will provide opportunities to check data integrity in terms of the validity of data values and units (i.e. are the values within a valid physical range and do the units of measurement comply with XX standard). It will also provide opportunities to check that data formats comply with XX’s requirements as defined in this document.

In projects with multiple EPCs then the IMS shall be the platform used for exchange of data between contractors.

Where XX shall review and comment information deliverables then the EPC shall access comments/mark-ups via the IMS.

## Intelligent versus Non-intelligent data

If there are some rules that define associations and relationships between data and document entities, such that it is possible to select an information item (e.g. a pump on a P&ID) and navigate to other related information, then the information is termed “intelligent”. Intelligence is a prerequisite for providing the key functionality within information management systems, which optimise the use of intelligent data via a plant data model. The plant data model is aligned with the plant breakdown structure and tag hierarchy to facilitate navigation through the data repository. The ability to navigate between data and documents shall be maintained within the core of the IMS via defined relationships between tag, equipment and document numbers.

In terms of intelligent entities, XX will utilise intelligent P&ID and instrument loop diagrams and single-line diagrams on the <XYZ> facility.

As part of the work scope, EPC contractors will be responsible for providing XX with the Tag–to-Document–to-Equipment relationships, suitably aligned with the tag and document numbering philosophies that XX will provide.

## EPC Handover Details

This Section forms the basis for the EPC information handover, and is split into 3 major parts:-

* General Requirements
* Detailed Requirements
* Native Format Requirements For Intelligent Applications

### General Requirements

EPC contractor shall provide all documents and data as specified herewith. Documents and data shall be handed over from the EPC contractors to XX during the EPC process (see Section 1.0.8). In addition to handing over internally generated documents and data, EPC contractor shall be responsible for handing over information from subcontractors and equipment vendors. The handover information will consist of electronic documents (e.g. drawings and datasheets) as they become available plus exports of the data represented in them. The format for the exported data is discussed in Section 1.0.2.

The detailed requirements for handover are presented in the following Sections, and are based upon the information contained in References [1] and [2], suitably customised for XX <XYZ> Project.

#### Information Status

In presenting the handover requirements in Section 1.1, the following classification has been used, following the scheme outlined in Reference [2]:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Key** | **Description** |
| M | Mandatory requirement (i.e. necessary in order to retain operating licence) |
| E | Essential requirement for plant operations |
| S | Supplementary requirement (i.e. information is of interest, but not essential for plant operations) |
| O | Optional requirement (i.e. not required during operational plant life) |

#### Handover Formats

In presenting the requirements, the following key has been used to classify the information handover formats:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Key** | **Description** |
| S | Structured Format - independent of the software application that created it and adhering to a well-defined model (e.g. XML, DXF, CGM, RTF) |
| N | Native Format - dependent on the software application that created it (e.g. SmartPlant P&ID, SmartPlant 3D, MSWord, MSExcel, AutoCAD/MicroStation[[1]](#footnote-1)) |
| I | Image Format - scanned image (e.g. TIFF, PDF). Do Not Use Without Specific Approval or as specifically requested in Subsection 1.1.1 |
| H | Hardcopy Format. Do Not Use Without Specific Approval or as specifically requested in Subsection 1.1.1 |

It should be clear from this Table that Structured and Native Formats represent “intelligent” data, whereas Image and Hardcopy Formats characterise “dumb” documents.

In addition, EPC contractors should adhere to the following XX preferences for Native (N) Deliverables:

#### Native Deliverables

Deliverables required by the contractor will include the following documents created with the following software engineering tools (see Table 1.2-1). Contractors should utilize the full functionality of these software applications to create other document types. SmartPlant Foundation will be the delivery mechanism. Not all of these documents will be published into SmartPlant Foundation but will be required to be loaded for navigational purposes (note the term publish refers to data/documents exchanged via a SmartPlant Adaptor utilizing the SmartPlant Loader, load refers to data/documents that are not exchanged via SmartPlant Adaptor and are loaded via the SmartPlant Foundation Loader).

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Contractor Deliverables and Authoring Tools** | |
| **Document Type** | **Design Tool Source** |
| Equipment Datasheet | Aspen Tech Zyqad® or SmartPlant Foundation |
| Process Flow Diagram | Aspen Tech Zyqad or SmartSketch® |
| P&ID | SmartPlant P&ID |
| Line List/Line Designation Table | SmartPlant P&ID |
| Master Equipment List | SmartPlant P&ID |
| Instrument Index | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Instrument Specification Sheet | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Instrument Loop Diagram (ILDs) | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Alarm & Controller Set-point Summary | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Process Data Sheet | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Dimensional Data Sheet | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Cable Schedule | SmartPlant Instrumentation and SmartPlant Electrical |
| Junction Box Reports | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Calculation Sheet | SmartPlant Instrumentation (optional) |
| Telecommunications | SmartPlant Instrumentation |
| Electrical Motor Schematics | SmartPlant Electrical |
| Single Line Diagram (S.L.D.) | SmartPlant Electrical |
| ISO Drawings | SmartPlant 3D or Plant Design System® (PDS) 3D |
| Ortho Drawings | SmartPlant 3D or PDS 3D |
| HAZOP Studies | HAZID |
| Piping Specs  Standard component definitions in the following disciplines Piping  Instrumentation  Electrical(incl. Cables)  Steel Structure  HVAC | SmartPlant Reference Data |
| All other 2D CAD drawings (Foundation, Plot Plants, Mounting details, Cable Tray reports, Mechanical Layouts, Architectural, Site Layout, etc.) | SmartSketch® |
| Requisitions for purchase  Supplier lists  Purchase orders  Material delivery schedules and status  Inspection schedules  Spare part lists  Construction progress | SmartPlant Materials |

#### Datasheet Requirements

Wherever possible, data should be handed over in compliance with XX’s standard datasheet format – see also SPF Datasheets. As a minimum requirement, native non-graphical data should be delivered in MSAccess or MSExcel format. Electronic documents should be delivered in MSWord format. The preferred format should include a parsable XML content representation of the datasheet via an agreed schema for load into SmartPlant Foundation.

#### Document Requirements

Subsection 1.1.1 provides a summary listing of the handover requirements for <XYZ> project. All documents shall be submitted in N or S format, but XX will entertain alternatives from the EPC based on efficiency or cost optimisation. All documents generated during the EPC project must comply with XX <XYZ> Document Numbering System that is provided as part of the EPC RFP package (See Reference [4]).

#### Data Requirements

Subsection 1.1.1 provides a summary listing of the handover requirements for <XYZ>. All items associated with S (Structured) and/or N (Native) formats are to be interpreted as data deliverables and shall be handed over in accordance with the format instructions given in Subsection 1.0.2. In addition, EPC contractors must adhere to the data attribute requirements presented in Subsection 1.1.3.

#### Plant Hierarchy (Breakdown Structure)

It is XX’s intention to build an asset-centric information management system. In order to accomplish this, the Plant Hierarchy (or Breakdown Structure) must be firmly established at the start of the EPC project, since it will be the primary driver for classifying data (i.e. for locating equipment within the <XYZ> plant). The Plant Hierarchy shall subsequently provide one of the main user-entry points into the information management system, and will facilitate “drill-down” type functionality as a means of navigating the plant. All tags, equipment, documents and data must relate explicitly to the Plant Hierarchy. The EPC contractors shall be contractually obliged to comply with this requirement.

The Plant Hierarchy will present the structure and inter-relationships between the elements that define equipment locations (e.g. Project, Unit, Train, System, Sub-system, Area/Location, etc), and for the <XYZ> project will be defined as follows:

Project

|\_\_\_\_Area

|\_\_\_\_Unit

|\_\_\_\_System

|\_\_\_\_\_Sub-system

#### Tag Hierarchy

The Tag Hierarchy describes the associations between tags, and is expressed in the form of parent/child relationships. It is possible to have multiple Tag Hierarchies based on a logical categorisation such as discipline (e.g. Maintenance, Electrical, HVAC, etc). Such multiple hierarchies provide additional benefits to users of information management systems by focussing on information areas that are most relevant to particular users (or disciplines).

The Tag Hierarchy links with the Plant Hierarchy to provide the basis for interrogating the information management system, and for the <XYZ> project is described in the TAG system specification document that is provided as part of the EPC RFP package (See Reference [5]).

#### Tag-Document-Equipment Relationships

The engineering design tools are usually abandoned at the end of the project and the “paper” based deliverables are all that is handed over to the plant owner. For the <XYZ> project, it is XX’s intention to maintain the engineering design data during the complete plant lifecycle; and in particular to re-use the original design data during plant re-vamps (where possible).

In addition, it is XX’s intention to implement an information management system centred upon an engineering data warehouse that will provide access to all <XYZ> information and will seamlessly interface to external systems, notably the plant maintenance management system. A schematic illustrating the relationships between Tags, Documents and Equipment is shown below.

![tripod](data:image/jpeg;base64...)

In order to achieve the necessary degree of user functionality, and in particular to manage documents and data in a seamless manner, XX shall require access to the Tag-Document-Equipment relationships that are established during the EPC project.

Consequently, EPC contractors are required to generate an electronic record of the associations between Tag Numbers, Document Numbers and Equipment Numbers as part of their work scope. SPF (preferably) or MSAccess shall be used to create this relationship register.

In addition, EPC contractors are required to handover all application related files associated with Native Format deliverables, such that XX are able to subsequently use the native files in the native applications, should they choose to. For example, if the EPC contractors use PDS for creating P&ID, using MicroStation as a Native Format, then all related application files (including reference files) must be handed over in addition to the main P&ID in order to facilitate subsequent use of the native MicroStation files within PDS.

XX provided legends, CAD standards (line types, fonts and layering structures etc) shall be followed.

#### Handover Timing

XX expects data to be provided as scheduled to enable XX and the EPC contractors to work in partnership to continuously improve the quality of the documents and data as the project progresses. This approach will ensure that XX’s goal for seamless, quality-assured electronic document and data handover at each phase in the project is achieved. It will also enhance the management of interfaces between EPC contractors.

Data will be taken-on at milestones during the EPC cycle: these times may not necessarily coincide with set time periods, but should relate to significant points on the engineering activity list, a typical example of which is shown below.

![](data:image/x-wmf;base64...)

For each information submittal, EPC contractors shall transfer the associated data and documents to XX.

Key milestones for documents and data delivery will be:

* Issued for review (IFR)
* Issued for approval (IFA)
* Issued for construction (IFC)
* As-built
* As-accepted

Payment to EPC contractors will be tied to this information delivery in accordance with XX conditions set out in Section YYYY of the RFP package.

Vendor document receipt and acceptance by XX to be a prerequisite for final payment to suppliers and X% retention dependent of acceptance of final documentation shall be included in purchase terms.

The document and data handover process is required to accommodate change history, and should be automated as far as practicable to allow subsequent updates at regular intervals.

#### Data Validation

The EPC contractors will be responsible for validating their data and documents (including subcontractor and equipment vendor information (Reference [3]) prior to handover to XX.

In particular, the validation will break into a number of rules:

1. All Systems, Discipline and Area codes must align with the Plant Breakdown Structure (see Subsection 1.0.5).
2. All descriptions shall be uppercase and contain no special or null characters
3. All Tag Numbers shall follow the numbering procedure as supplied by XX
4. All data attributes must comply with the minimum requirements presented in Subsection 1.1.3).
5. Referential integrity - all relationships (tag-tag, document-document tag- document etc..) shall be valid.

The XX’s designated internal systems integrator (see Subsection 0.2.6) will work with the EPC contractors’ data handover CCM to oversee the data validation process, in order to ensure that data is handed over at the appropriate milestones (as defined in Subsection 1.0.8) and that payments to EPC contractors can be made.

#### Equipment Vendor Data

In order to maximise use of its information management system, XX requires handover of both engineering design data and equipment vendor data.

Equipment must be treated separately from tags in order to subsequently maximise the functionality of XX’s information management system in terms of tracking and auditing. The distinction between equipment and tag allows information associated with an equipment item to be tracked as it is moved around the Plant e.g. during refurbishment, routine maintenance etc.

If the EPC contractor assigns his own Equipment No to a physical piece of equipment, then he should also capture the Supplier’s Serial No.

Where applicable, and particularly in the case of equipment items, the data shall be handed over in XX’s chosen datasheet format (see Subsection 1.0.11). The EPC contractors will be contractually responsible for meeting these requirements, in line with the details presented in Section 1.1 and associated Subsections.

In particular, all data must be validated in accordance with Subsection 1.0.9. Failure to comply with this condition will adversely impact the EPC payment schedule, in that payment will be withheld until XX are satisfied that the data has been properly validated and is in a suitable format for uploading into the information management system.

#### Datasheet Format for Handover

XX will provide a corporate datasheet format that EPC contractors must use for data handover. Failure to comply with this requirement will adversely impact the EPC payment schedule. See also SPF Datasheets.

#### Units of Measurement

All handover information must comply with XX’s units of measurement schema, a copy of which will be provided to EPC contractors prior to contract award.

#### 3D Model Representational Requirements

a) Exact geometry:

Lines

Pipe supports (at least those that are > 2")

Valves dimensions only, appearance represented by envelope

Cable Tray

Ducting

Structural Steel/Nodes

Risers/Caissons

Underground Piping

Terrain model

b) Approx Geometry (envelopes)

Main Equipment (except for nozzles/connections to be exact geometry)

Handling/Access routes

Pipe Supports

Welds (NB: field and shop welds to be distinguished by differing symbols, have differing inspection requirements)

Valves

Instruments

Cable Routing Nodes

Electrical Field Equipment

Junction Boxes

Escape Routes

Fire Fighting Equipment

Anodes

Doors/Windows/Ceilings/Floors

### Detailed Requirements

| **Required Information** | **Description** | **Status** | **Format[[8]](#footnote-8)** |

| Process Flow Diagrams (PFD) | Details major items of equipment, primary lines and valves, operating temperatures, pressures and mass flows | **E** | **S** |
| Utility Flow Diagrams (UFD) | Details major items of equipment, primary lines and valves, operating temperatures, pressures and mass flows | **E** | **S** |
| Process and Instrument Diagrams (P&ID) | Showing in schematic form the equipment, piping valves, tagged instruments, piping specification (& breaks) and line numbering for the process, utilities and loss control systems | **E** | **N** |
| 3D Model | 3 dimensional CAD model | **E** | **N** |
| Principal Equipment List | List of major process equipment items | **E** | **N** |
| Power and Utility Summary | List of power and utility loads and requirements | **E** | **N** |
| All Project Philosophies |  | **E** | **N** |
| All Project Studies (generated in the EPC phase and later) |  | **E** | **N** |
| Cause and Effect Charts | Process cause and effect charts | **E** | **S** |
| Process Calculations | Design calculations for all process conditions and process equipment selection | **E** | **S** |
| System Design Basis Reports | Document outlining the basis for the design of the facility, and comprising all process design philosophies i.e. environmental philosophy, drainage philosophy, ventilation philosophy, isolation philosophy, plant availability / downtime assessment and relief / blow down philosophy etc. The philosophies detail the reasoning and assumptions made in plant design and are required as reference documents for the optimisation / modification of plant | **E** | **N** |
| Tie-in Schedules | Schedule of interfaces between Project Stages, Contractors and Systems | **E** | **N** |
| Supervisory Level Operating Procedures for all Process / Utility Systems (Shall include Steady State, Start Up, Shutdown, and Transient Operation) | Comprises operating information and procedures needed for the safe and efficient operation of the process plant and utilities | **E** | **N** |
| Mass Balance | Chart summarising mass flow rates at key points within the process and utility systems | **E** | **S** |
| Fluid and Substance Data | Details the composition / makeup of process and produced fluids | **E** | **N** |
| Relief and Blow down Data | Sizing data for the relief and blow down systems | **E** | **N** |
| Surge and Dynamic Analysis Data | Details and results of any analysis carried out to verify design of process plants | **E** | **N** |
| HL / LP Interface Details | Set of calculations verifying plant and equipment protection downstream of pressure letdown locations (the register of safety related devices refers to it) | **E** | **N** |
| Process Simulation Model | Computer based process flow model used in the design | **E** | **S** |
| Criticality Resume | Assumptions and reasoning behind the allocation of criticality ratings to plant and equipment. Needed for reference to support modifications | **E** | **N** |
| Process Safeguarding Drawings | Diagrams showing pressure and temperature trips, blow down, pressure relief, isolation, emergency shutdown valves and HL/LP interfaces | **E** | **N** |
| Process Data Sheets | Describe the operating parameters (temperatures, pressures, service conditions and operating fluid characteristics) of the equipment in the form of sketches and tables, and are the basis of the performance specification for the equipment | **E** | **N** |
| System Isometrics | Fabrication isometrics combined to represent a complete system. Required for system familiarisation and as a training aid | **E** | **S** |

The drawing enables maintenance personnel to function test, maintain and find instruments loops without the need to refer to numerous related wiring diagrams. | **E** | **S** |

| Shutdown Logic Diagrams | Provides an overview in graphic form of the "and", "or" and other logic functions of the shutdown systems, and the sequence in which initiation events occur. Required for trip testing, system fault finding, maintenance testing and modifications | **E** | **S** |
| Instrument / Control System Power Distribution, Grounding Interconnects Drawing | Schematic diagram of instrument power supplies and earthing arrangements | **E** | **S** |
| Alarm / Trip Schedule | Schedule of all alarms and trip settings including those in vendor packages | **E** | **N** |
| Cable Schedule | Ref 2.1.3.2.1 | **E** | **S** |
| Cable Routing Drawings | Details the routing of cables throughout the facility between the plant and equipment, modules, etc, including transit locations  Routing nodes to be included in 3D models | **E** | **S** |
| Testing and Commissioning Procedures | Includes procedures for each type of equipment to be tested and commissioned | **E** | **N** |
| Overall Control System Block Diagrams, I/O Loading & Cabinet Layouts |  | **E** | **S** |
| Detailed Procedures for Control and Shutdown System FAT |  | **E** | **N** |
| Failure Modes Effects Analysis for Shutdown Systems |  | **E** | **N** |
| Standard Instrument Details | Dimensioned drawings of instruments and supplier technical and installation details | **E** | **S** |
| Instrument Location Drawing | Shows the physical location of all equipment | **E** | **S** |
| General Arrangement Drawings | Shows general arrangement and locations of instrumentation and associated items on skids and within panels and cabinets. Annunciator panels GA shall include front fascia details along with full alarm text and tag numbering | **E** | **S** |
| Control Schematic | Gives a pictorial representation of the main elements and functions of a control circuit / system with input / output sources (switches, relays, etc) and their relationships. Details logic functions of the circuit / system | **E** | **S** |
| SCADA / PLC / DCS Details | This documents the SCADA / PLC / DCS implementation philosophy including configuration details of inputs, outputs, logic and screen graphics, and software revision status. This will include hard copy software documentation where the software system is not self documenting | **E** | **N** |

| F&G Location Drawing | Shows the physical location of all equipment. Shows the exact position of detectors / alarms; and for detectors, shows their orientation and fire area covered | **E** | **S** |
| General Arrangement Drawings (instruments, field mounting arrangements, panels) | Shows general arrangement and locations of instrumentation and associated items on skids and within panels and cabinets. Annunciator panel GAs shall include fascia details along with full alarm text and tag numbering | **E** | **S** |
| F&G Schematic | Gives a pictorial representation of the main elements and functions of a control circuit / system with input / output sources (switches, relays, etc) and their relationships | **E** | **S** |

#### Telecommunications

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Required Information** | **Description** | **Status** | **Format[[11]](#footnote-11)** |
| Telecommunications Equipment Data | See required data attributes in Subsection 1.1.3.5 | **E** | **S** |
| Loudspeaker Loop Diagrams | Details the loudspeaker connection and information on the designated loop. Required for automated PA interrogation system fault finding | **E** | **S** |
| Cable Schedule | Ref 2.1.3.2.1 | **E** | **S** |
| Cable Routing Drawings | Details the routing of cables throughout the facility between the plant and equipment, modules, etc, including transit locations | **E** | **S** |
| Testing and Commissioning Procedures | Includes procedures for each type of equipment to be tested and commissioned | **E** | **N** |
| Overall Control System Block Diagrams, I/O Loading & Cabinet Layouts |  | **E** | **S** |
| System Drawings | Details the telecommunications system and how it interconnects into the overall communications network | **E** | **S** |
| Single Line Diagram | Details how equipment is connected into a system to include comprehensive data thread and telephone trunk records | **E** | **S** |
| Block and Level Drawing | Details transmission levels within systems and through networks | **E** | **S** |
| Termination Drawing | For equipment panels, junction boxes, etc, Identifies all connections and cable terminations at input / output rails. Required for removal / hook-up purposes and modifications | **E** | **S** |
| Wiring Diagrams | Details all points of interconnecting wiring showing cable numbers, core colours and termination points | **E** | **S** |
| Telemetry ID Listing | Database model listing input and output points of plant instrumentation interconnections to telemetry systems | **E** | **S** |
| IDF Records | Details intermediate distribution frame connections in tabular form to show the origin and destination of individual system channels i.e. cable head and jump ring terminations | **E** | **N** |
| Radio Licence | Statutory requirement for radio operation for display on facility | **M** | **N** |

| Intermediate Stage Architectural Drawings | General drawings for construction details of buildings (draft) | **S** | **S** |
| Completion Stage Architectural Drawings | General drawings for construction details of buildings (final) | **E** | **S** |
| Completion Stage Architectural Specification | Contains contractor detail design based on initial specifications | **E** | **N** |
| Technical Interfaces | Drawings and documents describing interfaces between architectural and other contractor scopes | **E** | **S** |

| Power System and Load Transfer Philosophy | Power system operation and the philosophy of load transfer | **E** | **N** |

| Block Diagrams | Similar to Single Line Diagrams above, but detailing the relationships in single line form between items of equipment, control panels, distribution boards | **E** | **S** |
| Schematic Diagrams | Gives a pictorial representation of the main elements and functions of a control circuit for electrical equipment with input / output sources (switches, relays, etc) and their relationships. Details logic functions of the circuit | **E** | **S** |
| Wiring Diagrams | Details electrical wiring on all plant, equipment, boards, boxes and panels including interconnection / termination details for interface and hook up | **E** | **S** |
| Electrical Interconnection Diagrams | Details of cable numbers and terminations between vendor packages, equipment, and particularly at contractor interfaces | **E** | **S** |
| Electrical Equipment Arrangement Layouts | GA s of main electrical equipment, including switchboards, transformers, large loads, etc | **E** | **S** |
| Lighting and Grounding Layouts | Layout of lighting and grounding on facility | **E** | **S** |
| Cable Tray Layouts | Layout of main cable tray routes on facility | **E** | **S** |
| Power Layouts | Layout of main power distribution on facility | **E** | **S** |
| Electrical Layouts | Layout of main electrical loads on facility | **E** | **S** |
| Building Electrical Layouts | Layout of electrical equipment and lighting within buildings | **E** | **S** |
| Lighting Fixture Schedule |  | **E** | **N** |
| Panel Schedule |  | **E** | **N** |
| Conduit Schedule |  | **E** | **N** |

| Heat Tracing Isometrics |  | **E** | **S** |
| Load List | Tabulation of electrical loads which the electrical system must accommodate including detail of individual distribution board loads | **E** | **N** |
| Legends and Symbol Lists | As used in all drawings | **E** | **S** |
| Standard Power Details | Typical installation details | **E** | **S** |
| Standard Lighting Details | Typical installation details | **E** | **S** |
| Standard Ground Details | Typical installation details | **E** | **S** |
| Standard Cable Tray Details | Typical installation details | **E** | **S** |
| Cable Routing | Details the routing of cables throughout nthe facility between plant and equipment, modules etc. including transit locations | **E** | **S** |
| Grounding Drawings | Detail grounding connections for plant and equipment and between modules and dissimilar structural elements | **E** | **S** |

#### Mechanical

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Required Information** | **Description** | **Status** | **Format[[14]](#footnote-14)** |
| Plot Plans | Plan view of area showing exact location of main items of equipment | **E** | **S** |
| Equipment Location Plan | Showing next level of detail from plot plan | **E** | **S** |
| General Arrangement Drawing | Dimensional drawing indication overall extremities of plant or equipment in plan, elevation or sectional view | **E** | **S** |
| 3D Model | 3 dimensional CAD model | **E** | **N** |
| Vessel Detail Drawing | Sectional or exploded view drawing detailing main fabrication / assembly information - design parameters (pressures, temperatures, manufacturing codes, etc), materials, wall thickness, lining, internal fittings, test details of major process and utility vessels | **E** | **S** |
| Vessel Design Calculations | Prepared in order to prove that the item of equipment or system selected meets the design criteria | **E** | **N** |
| Critical Weld Procedures | Required for critical plant and equipment where non-standard (exotic) materials and methods are used (e.g. vessel lining, sour service). The procedures may not be industry standard or readily available thus resulting in significant costs (for procedure development / qualification and production loss) if repairs etc. required at short notice. | **E** | **N** |
| Standard Weld Procedure | Non-critical weld procedures | **E** | **N** |
| Performance Curves | These are charts detailing the design performance envelope and limits for major rotating machinery - turbines, crane/auxiliary diesels, compressors, transfer, fire and WI pump sets | **E** | **N** |
| Alignment Specifications | Manufacturers' recommended alignment readings and tolerances needed for re-coupling of major rotating machinery following overhaul | **E** | **N** |
| Start-up Logs for major rotating machinery | Running logs taken on first start-up /commissioning of major rotating machinery. Of particular importance are running hours for scheduling over speed tests and initial oil changes | **E** | **N** |
| Condition Monitoring Baselines | Set of vibration and SPL readings and oil/grease analysis results for first operation/run-up of rotating machinery | **E** | **N** |
| Lubrication Schedule | Details the lubrication requirements (oil, grease, etc) for all plant and machinery including detail of lubricants, quantities used and recommended change frequency. The schedule will be a rationalisation of lubricant grades/types so as to minimise offshore stockholdings  (Required for input to ERP system) | **E** | **S** |
| Access Manuals | Document detailing the methods and procedures for all mechanical handling activities that will take place during operations. Detailed procedures for removing plant and equipment for maintenance purposes, lifting/handling equipment to be used, load paths and layout areas | **E** | **N** |
| Pressure Vessel Certificate | Certificate issued by Certifying Authority is specific to pressure vessels and lists the salient data - manufacturer, type of construction, test pressure rating etc. | **E** | **N** |
| PSV Test Certificate | Records details of inspection of PSV - required to demonstrate fitness for purpose | **E** | **N** |
| Lifting Equipment Certificate | Issued by competent third party inspector covering all portable and fixed lifting gear | **E** | **N** |

| Line Schedule Data |  | **E** | **S** |
| Piping Support Standards |  | **S** | **N** |
| Pipe Support Details |  | **S** | **N** |
| Pipe Support Schedule | Tables defining special / critical support type (static, pre-loaded, etc.) and tag number | **S** | **S** |
| Piping GA Drawings | Drawings that provide plan and/or sectional views, highlighting the routing of dimensioned pipe work. To include general arrangements of special pipe supports | **E** | **S** |
| Fabrication Isometric Drawing | Isometric view of pipe run detailing configuration, orientation and necessary details (dimensions, material requirements) for manufacture and assembly | **E** | **S** |
| Piping Specification | Provides piping materials definition, design pressures and temperatures, pipe nominal bores and wall thickness, fitting types and applications and material test criteria | **S** | **S** |
| Piping Special Items Specification | Definition as per the piping specification but for non-standard items not included in line item above | **S** | **N** |
| Design Calculations | Prepared in order to prove that the item of equipment or system selected meets the design criteria. For special pipe support will include details of load settings | **S** | **N** |
| Pipe Stress Pack | Report containing pipe stress evaluation results and recommendations (including calculations, stress isometrics, support type and acceptable load criteria) for specific critical piping configurations | **S** | **N** |
| Valve Specification | Provides valve type, material definitions, design pressures and temperatures, operating service conditions actuation type, end connection and dimensional details | **S** | **S** |
| Insulation Specification | Provides equipment application information (pipe, vessel etc.) material type, thickness requirements, services conditions, personnel protection and fireproofing | **S** | **N** |

#### Corrosion Control

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Required Information** | **Description** | **Status** | **Format[[17]](#footnote-17)** |

| Material Selection Drawings | Schematics showing material selection on process | **E** | **N** |
| Material Selection Philosophy | Details reasons and assumptions made in the choice of materials for construction of structure, plant and equipment. Essential reference document should modifications be necessary due to change in process conditions (e.g. increased H2O etc) | **S** | **N** |
| Corrosion Control Philosophy | Forecast of plant condition based on hazards, operability and corrosion effects. Must be in place before start-up and is basis for corrosion monitoring programme | **S** | **N** |
| Painting Specifications | Details protective coatings / paint systems, application methods (including preparation) and where applied. Required to support fabric maintenance programme | **S** | **N** |
| Baseline Corrosion Readings | Required for critical process pipe work and vessels and are base readings for corrosion monitoring records | **S** | **N** |

| **Required Information** | **Description** | **Status** | **Format[[19]](#footnote-19)** |

| Hazardous Area Classification |  | **E** | **N** |
| Register of Safety Devices | Listing and detailed description of integrity protection devices including relevant basis and design information and service conditions. All items on this register must be flagged in the Master Equipment List and on P&ID | **E** | **S** |
| HAZOP Report and Project Response | Report on the findings of Hazard and Operability Studies for all hydrocarbon and main utilities systems including HAZOP team worksheets. Required as reference documents when modifying / optimising plant performance to support operations Safety Case submissions | **S** | **N** |
| Active Fire Fighting system P&ID | Details in schematic form the active fire fighting systems, equipment (fire pumps, deluge, monitor and halon skids) piping, valves, tagged instruments and their relationships | **E** | **S** |
| Fire Fighting equipment Layout Drawings | Drawing detailing the location of all portable / semi-portable fire fighting equipment (fire extinguishers, crash kits, hydrants & equipment, hose reels, breathing apparatus) | **E** | **S** |
| Life Saving Equipment & Escape Route Drawing | Drawing detailing access routes and means of escape in emergency and the location of all lifesaving equipment (escape sets, first aid boxes, showers, etc) | **E** | **S** |
| Passive Fire Protection Drawings | Structural and module layout drawings detailing the passive fire protection I.e. firewall / boundary fire protection ratings | **E** | **S** |
| Hazardous Source Data | Details all individual point sources for emissions, discharge inventories and corresponding zone classifications | **E** | **N** |
| Passive & Active Fire Protection Philosophy | Philosophy summarising the means of fire protection both passive (firewalls) and active (manual, foam, monitor, deluge/sprinkler systems, etc) | **E** | **N** |
| Active Fire Protection system Data | Design data and information that forms the basis of the performance specification for the active fire fighting systems (manual, foam, monitor, deluge/sprinkler systems, etc) | **E** | **N** |
| PHSR Reports | Report on the findings and actions issued following the Project Health and Safety Review exercises. Same justification as HAZOP reports | **S** | **N** |
| Recommendation Monitoring System | An audit trail of recommendations from safety reviews, HAZOPs and incidents, status of actions | **S** | **N** |
| Equipment Failure Information | Details of the design life, failure modes and causes for critical items of equipment within the hydrocarbon, main utilities and safety systems. Used in predicting plant availability and identifying critical equipment for maintenance monitoring purposes | **E** | **S** |
| Hazardous Substance Data Sheets |  | **E** | **N** |
| Emergency Procedures Manuals | As defined for Facilities Operations and for Pipeline Operations | **E** | **N** |
| Record of Survey | Report on survey of Fire Fighting and Life Saving equipment | **S** | **N** |
| HIPS Dossier | Reliability, availability and design dossier for all High Integrity Protection Systems | **E** | **S** |
| Test Procedures |  | **S** | **N** |
